author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Add a pinch of asafoetida (called Hing in Hindi) to the mix in step 6 -- it's an essential ingredient that adds flavour to the sambhar.  The recipe is fine otherwise.  (By the way, you can save yourself some labour by using sambhar powder that you can buy at any Indian store:  MTR is one of the better brands.)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is an abomination…nothing at all like Sambhar, beginning w the choice of dhal used, as others have pointed out. At least have the courtesy of calling it “riffing on…” or “inspired by”. Ms Lakshmi is best served by sticking to domains of her expertise - fashion and make up?

author: Julia Moskin

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was good, but not worth the time and effort to make. It took me a good 1.5 hours and required multiple steps. I even went to Indian food store for tamarind, fresh coconut and chana dal. I couldn’t really taste the tamarind or coconut and frankly the flavor was similar to a much similar dal I’ve made in past. I’ll likely stay with simpler dal with similar flavor profile and skip this more complicated recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
@Tammy this is not really a sambhar recipe. Sambhar has an unparalleled tangy flavour of tamarind and coconut is complete unnecessary and not even usual. 
When I make sambhar I can make it in under an hour tops, even faster if I already have cooked lentils (pigeon peas - toor dal - are usually used but you could sub with other red or yellow lentils although the flavour won’t be exactly the same). Google it and you will find better recipes to convince you of the delicious flavour of the authentic dish!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @CR Actually, AI’s calculation is wrong.  There are 36 teaspoons in 3/4 cup. 36 divided by 48 is .75 tsp.  So, 3/4 tsp of sugar in each mini muffin.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made these! I loved the story about the school chef who came up with the recipe. Like the author said, these are not cupcakes calling themselves muffins. They are subtly sweet, so you don't feel bad about eating 5 or 6, and you can in their miniature size. For more sweetness, you could sprinkle sugar on the tops. The taste of these muffins reminds me more of a bran muffin than a pumpkin muffin, which is ok for me because I like both flavors. I baked mine for 13 minutes. They are like tiny fluffy pillows! I made one substitution because I didn't want to have to run out to the store: used sour cream instead of Greek yogurt.

Stayed moist for a couple of days. Served with unsweetened whipped cream along with poached apricots. Careful not to overcook.

commentBody:
This was wonderful! Everyone who tried it loved it. I didn’t have a 9» pan so used an 8», and it was looking nicely browned with edges pulling away from the pan at about 40 min, but the knife I used to test done-ness was not clean, so I took internal temp and it was 210 so I took it out. When I flipped it over after sitting some of the topping stuck to the pan, but it came out easily and looked fine stuck back on the cake. I couldn’t find apricots so made the poached fruit with frozen peaches and it was a very nice addition. 
This was a delicious dessert!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Beautiful cake. Will make again. I had never used cardamom pods before. Splitting each pod, removing the seeds, and grinding the seeds added more time to this recipe than I expected. The scent is wonderful but it’s slow work. If you are using a mortar and pestle to grind the seeds, it is much easier to achieve a fine powder if you add a pinch of granulated sugar before grinding—the internet gave me that tip and it worked! Finally, I served this with a dollop of Bonne Maman apricot jam and a dollop of whipped cream. Sensational taste combination with the pistachio and cardamom. Enjoy!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This cake is absolutely delicious, but the directions are completely baffling. I think it may be an issue of the original restaurant recipe being scaled down for a home cook. 

I used some of the other suggestions:

I halved the amount of fresh cardamom.

I baked the cake for 35 or 36 minutes.

Reducing the freshly ground cardamom was definitely necessary, and It produced a lovely fragrant cake. If I had used the full amount, the cake would have tasted like soap.

I started checking the bake at 30 minutes, resulting in a cake that was moist with a wonderful texture. If I had baked it the full 40 minutes, it would have been dry. 

Also, we couldn't taste the pistachio flavor at all. While I was making the batter and then baking it, I could smell and taste the pistachio aroma, but none of us could taste it once the baking was finished.

I may switch to ground almonds next time since almonds are cheaper.

The second time I made this, I used Cup4Cup brand gluten free flour, and it worked perfectly. I truly didn't notice a difference with this cake.

To save cleaning dishes,  1) I melted the butter in the cake pan, 2) ground the cardamom in a spice grinder, and then added it to the flour and nuts, and 3) after grinding the initial quarter cup of flour with the nuts and cardamom, I added the remaining flour, baking powder, and salt directly to the food processor and ground it all again (didn't sift it separately).

This is definitely a keeper!

commentBody: @VaGirl Could always use a tsp of rose water. Thanks for the idea!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Very flavorful but dry. I left it in oven for 40 min but should have pulled it sooner. I used cardamom seeds that I removed from the pods (tedious!!) and ground in spice grinder. The cardamom flavor was too dominant — I’d tone it down if I were to make again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this for Thanksgiving dinner following the recipe exactly. It looked absolutely gorgeous. Everyone praised the cake, but although I didn’t bake it the full 40 minutes suggested, I personally found it a bit too dry. I served it with vanilla yogurt, which helped. Ice cream would probably be better, but I didn’t have any.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Loved putting this recipe together.  It smelled and looked great.  Could be my taste buds but the cardamon was quite overpowering.  I would make this recipe again but with half the cardamon.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Cake was really dry. Recommend taking out early.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Why won’t a springform cake pan work ? Because it might leak ?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Vinita I ignored that remark, used a springform as I thought it might come in handy to get the cake, and especially the almond layer, unharmed and that’s exactly how it went. So by all means, use one. Can’t think of a reason not to!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Did anyone try reducing sugar?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This cake was absolutely delicious and was a big hit at a family gathering. My only issue was my cake pans are rather dark so the finished product was very dark on top and the almonds were not light brown as in the photo, but were chocolate dark after 40 minutes (which is when I first checked for doneness).  I fixed this by toasting more almonds and re-covering the top. I recommend checking earlier and/or be sure to use a light-colored cake pan.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Just put the cardamom seeds in with the pistachios and they'll get ground fine

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This worked great with King Arthur gluten free flour. Served it with whipped cream and that Plum/Bay/Honey Compote from Melissa Clark’s Corn Scones. Really so delicious!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I had only salted pistachios, so I removed the salt (as much as possible) by shaking them in a jar then skipped adding the pinch of salt; it worked beautifully. I also didn’t have slivered almonds so I used walnuts for the top and although not as pretty, it tastes great. I might reduce the sugar next time by a quarter cup.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: It’s a truly delicious cake. I used pistachio flour so there was no need to blitz. I only had a springform pan so I skipped the topping part (as I used baking paper on the bottom) - I would appreciate an alternative topping recipe for people like me. I served the cake with Greek yoghurt mixed with a spoonful of orange flower water.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
@Kasia

I used a springform pan and it worked well, no leaking or problems at all. The almond topping is delicious and a good textural contrast

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious! One of the best cakes. I added only 2/3 cup sugar and will add less next time. Dropped in 2 tablespoons of pistachio cream and 1 teaspoon of almond extract during beating of the egg mixture. Added 1/4 cup butter and 1/4 cup light olive oil instead of 1/2 cup butter.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Weird. Followed the recipe exactly, everything looked fine, but it "fell" in the middle, while rising nicely around the edges.  I haven't had that happen to a cake in years. The instructions didn't say whether to grease the sides of the pan, so I didn't. Could that have been the reason?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was so lovely. Halved the sugar and cardamom. Added 1/2 tsp of rose water, could easily up to 1 tsp. I only had whole almonds so I put some in the food processor to get smaller pieces. Next time will add some rose petals on top - feeling inspired.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Has anyone substituted a neutral oil for the butter to reduce the dryness of the cake?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Gluten Free- Made with King Arthur GF measure for measure, turned out great.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I assume you should use a pastry brush to coat the sides of the cake pan in butter as well, yes?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Should the almonds be raw or toasted, with or without the skin?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
@Sar
The recipe calls for sliced almonds which I’ve only ever seen with skins intact. The skins are negligible though since the almonds are sliced so thin. As for toasted vs raw, I’d go with raw as they will get toasted while baking in the oven.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is phenomenal and easy, it’s going to be a regular at my house.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: There are no instructions on how to prepare the pan. Is it needed, or does the cake come easily out of the pan.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Cake came out easily

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @TKi greased the side of the pan.    Cake popped out.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Terrific! I wouldn't change anything in the recipe (I purchased whole cardamom and ground the seeds=lively, full flavor). Three quarters of the cake is in the freezer, I'm hoping it will taste nearly as delicious as the cake does freshly baked.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is yummy and worth the effort of grinding up whole cardamom seeds.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Except those look --for all the world-- like sliced almonds... !!?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Jilbob that’s because they are. Pistachios in almonds on. Read the recipe

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Is there a version of this that might sub olive oil for the butter?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @pz266 Try adding rose water to whipped cream.   Better yet, grind saffron to a powder and bloom in rose water for 15 minutes; add to cream; whip.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Lovely flavor, but as others have stated, rather dry. I’m sure it wasn’t over baked. I’m thinking of using it as a base for a Trifle, because it’s too tasty to waste.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This worked well with King Arthur gluten free flour, although maybe I would have taken it out at 30 or 35 minutes. (My oven may also be running a bit hot.) Really wonderful cake!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: tip - do a quick pulse or two in a coffee  grinder to remove or loosen cardamom husks @LizPerry

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Just made this, following the suggestion to substitute half of the flour for almond flour. Even though my uncooperative oven overcooked this, it still turned out absolutely fantastic. Will absolutely be putting in my ‘special dessert’ repertoire.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Burlap & B have a grinder lid you can add onto a bottle of their cardamom.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Heavenly. My new favorite cake.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Added ~1/2 t orange flower water to the batter, and glazed the top with apricot jam. Still, didn't love it. The cardamom is very strong (duh!), but the bigger problem was that it was very dry. Baked (in my calibrated oven) for 40 minutes, color was perfect and just starting to pull away from the edges of the pan. If doing again (unlikely), maybe increase the eggs/add a bit of yogurt or oil for fat to make it a bit more moist?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Great easy recipe, and absolutely delicious, a great flavor combination for something a bit different as a dessert cake. Mine only needed 30-35 minutes in the oven, and even then it was a tad dry. So the apricot sauce and/or some whipped cream is perfect.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I had such high hopes fpr this recipe, as it seems to hit many of my favorite notes.  However, at 40 minutes the cake bottom was very overbaked.  Why not share other ways to test for doneness, such as internal temp?  I may want to try again in the future and bake at a lower temperature

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: All that having been said, the flavor is delicious and the addition of the poached apricots makes it even better.  I substituted a small amount of flour with almond flour and the interior of the cake was not dry at all.  I think that starting to test for doneness early in the bake (around 25 minutes) and monitoring closely to avoid scorching will result in a fantastic cake.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Very dry, not worth the effort.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Excellent, easy, elegant. I used pre-ground cardamom and the flavors still sang. Agree with other comments that 40m is long: I'm certain 30m would have been sufficient and would have improved the texture.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: What about substituting cake flour for white flour?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: We've made many Lebovitz recipes - all are well tested - but this one was good, not great.  Per comments, used 1T rose water (not enough to make a difference) and cooked for about 32 minutes and should have taken it out a minute or two earlier.  The poached apricots were also pretty good (used white wine rather than sauterne), and the drizzled poaching liquid on each serving helped moisten the cake.  The almond topping was beautiful.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Served with rhubarb compote—excellent! I took the cake out at 37 minutes and should have taken it out a few minutes earlier. Still, fantastic flavor and very pretty.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This cake is an amazing find. One of the best things I've ever baked.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: It does not take as long to bake as the recipe says. Mine has consistently finished in just under 3O mintues in a 9" round pan. Delicious recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made as written. As much as I love cardamom, I actually felt that the flavor was overpowering. Serving with apricot jam really helped to offset this flavor, but still felt it was too strong. The cake also seemed a bit too sweet to me.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I had to use coconut oil instead of butter because my MIL is sure that butter is killing her. None-the-less, the cake was delicious if a bit dry. Forty minutes at 350 degrees was too long. My other substitution was using roasted lightly salted pistachios instead of raw unsalted nuts. That may explain the dry crumbly texture. I thought the dessert worked anyway - but I was glad for the cup of tea and fruit compote on the side.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Awesome. The only round pan I hd was a spring form. Some of the top stuck but I was able to stick it back -- partly because with 1/2 cup of AP and 1/2 cup of almond flour it was sticky moist -- just perfect.  I also added 2 T of rose flower water -- thanks again Scharkita. :) My butter and sugar didn't whip up fluffy --(butter too cold?) but it turned out great regardless.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’ve made some other NYT cakes that use olive oil oil instead of butter, and was wondering if that might be a solution for this cake. Mine did turn out dry. Does anybody have any ideas how to sub olive oil oil for butter?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The bake time is way too long on this recipe. I found it very dry so the second time I made it I added 1-4 cup of sour cream and I baked for only 30 minutes. The cake was moist and baked through. Regarding the cardamom, next time I’m going to cut to 1/4 teaspoon. In the recipe as written, the cardamom is completely overpowering, making the cake unbalanced in terms of flavour profile.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I only had a springform pan and the butter leaked out during cooking but the cake came out great anyway. Also reduced the cardamom by half, i thought it would overwhelm the pistachio. It was perfect that way. Next time i will wrap the pan in foil.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
The way that I was taught to make an egg cream, (in my father’s Bronx candy store)was to put the syrup in first, then the milk and before you stir it, you add the seltzer.
This way, when you stir, you get a lovely white foam on top of the chocolate milk mixture.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I may have grown up around the corner from that candy shop ;)  fond memories

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
@Marc
Exactly. The term egg in egg cream refers to the white foam on top. No white foam, not an egg cream. Also U-Bet is the ONLY chocolate syrup. Anything else is heresy.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @ArtM u-bet uses corn syrup these days unfortunately but I was able to find a natural sugar one a few years ago.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Why is it called an egg cream when there is no egg?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: It's a soda drink au creme, reshaped in a New York accent into Egg Cream...it never had egg...no matter what any expert tells you. Growing up in New York City, we called acorns, "egg corns"..there ya go!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Rube and no cream, either.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Rube the frothiness of the carbonized milk and seltzer that gave it the name. Eggy cream or aux crème. Depending what part of society you came from cause in my youth I had egg creams in da Bronx and egg creams on Park Ave. Both were equally good.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Growing up in Maspeth, Queens in the 1960’s, our family had a soda man who delivered our sodas and seltzer, in the blue bottle. U-bet syrup of course, I remember a splash of milk.  Very versatile, you can always add ice cream to make an ice cream soda.  Great memories.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Never mix the milk and syrup.   Syrup first then milk then seltzer poured in over the back of a spoon. Then mix to get a white frothy foam on top. And never drink with a straw. You need to drink it through the foam.  
This a a new york luncheonette real egg cream 
And there are no eggs

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Hollie. Yes, this is exactly how I made them working in a NY diner. Customers loved them.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If you buy U-Bet syrup during Passover, it will be made with sugar, as it was originally, not corn syrup.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Susan what a great tip.  I have a bottle of Fox’s and IT’S NOT THE SAME!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I also grew up in a candy store.  This was in Brooklyn. And as mentioned by another commenter we didn't stir the syrup and milk together but added the seltzer hitting the side of the glass and then stirred it all together resulting in the white creamy foam on top.  But really, however you make it, it's the greatest drink ever!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
There's an ingredient missing: aslightly stale pretzel rod from a cylindrical clear plastic holder with a red lid, sitting on the counter. The very salty, crispy pretzels were the perfect complement to the very beverage.
My dad regaled me with stories of his father's drug store in the Bronx which had a counter for malts, ice cream sodas and egg creams. They called my grandfather "Doc," and he was held up at gun point many times. One of my fondest memories of my dad, him making us fountain treats

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Recipe may be accurate, but not the same as having it properly prepared at a soda fountain, with the proper seltzer at the counter. My first choice would be a coffee egg cream, The delight of growing up in The Bronx and Manhattan. An early addition of the Daily News and an Egg Cream before heading home. Thanks for the trip down memory lane, too bad for those who are too young to have the simple pleasures of life.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My Dad told me the thing about egg creams is that there's much more Seltzer than there is milk because years ago the cost of milk was much more than the cost of seltzer. There was also this thing about allowing the seltzer poured in off the back of the spoon, I suspect that's presumably to minimize foaming and preserve the carbonation but I don't really know.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Neil. Yes, I worked in a NY diner and we made egg creams by pouring seltzer on back of a spoon.  I never drank one but the customers said they were the best egg creams.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: How to Spend Your Babysitting Earnings, Brooklyn circa 1959. An egg cream with a pretzel rod and the latest Superman comic book - twirling around a gently worn stool at a Luncheonette.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Why is it called "egg cream"? Wikipedia has good answers: 1) badly pronounced (from the French) "et creame" 
2) an expensive version had egg & cream in it 
3) no one remembers

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
A real NY egg cream must have a WHITE FOAM head, not chocolate.
The method to get this a first the chocolate syrup, then the milk on top, and then pour the seltzer to get the white foam. Then, stir the milk and chocolate syrup to get the dark mix under the white foam head.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My mom made these at home for me while I was growing up in LA in the 70s. With U-Bet syrup and with seltzer delivered by the ‘seltzer-man’, in ancient glass siphons, in heavy wood crates. Thanks for the memories…

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Fox also makes a vanilla syrup to make vanilla egg creams.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My dad grew up on NY’s lower East Side. We’d drive there from Cleveland (before I-80!) every summer to visit his mom and sister, and my aunt always brought in dobos torte for snack or dessert. Once or twice we’d all eat out at a deli.  My dad and I were the only egg cream fanatics. In Cleveland, the deli’s (what’s left) have chocolate phosphates. In Phx, I’ve introduced the glories of the egg cream to my grandsons. Chompie’s there makes a pretty good one.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The absolute best egg cream was made when the wide mouth jar of Fox's U-Bet was empty. I filled the jar 3/4 full with milk, screwed the top back on and shook like crazy. Seltzer was then squirted into the already frothy mixture. Drank it straight from the jar.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Fondly remember my parents making these for my brother and I when we were kids. We grew up in Michigan, but my parents were both transplanted New Yorkers. I’m going to have to get some seltzer and make these for my son now! Such good memories!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Growing up in Brooklyn, Egg Creams were my favorite treat. For $.15 it was pure heaven. And, whenever I returned to NYC, I would treat myself. Years ago my husband bought me an Egg Cream glass from Juniors-in Brooklyn and aside from being one of the best gifts it inspired me to indulge my Egg Cream passion. Today, I make several a week, using plain Polar Seltzer (small cans) and quench my thirst. Have to say, however, not a fan of UBet syrup, preferring Hersheys chocolate syrup.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I grew up working in my father’s candy store on 188th St. in the Bronx. I was taught that the original was made by L. Auster on the lower east side using his special syrup that included eggs. I am always upset by the claim that a proper egg cream is made with Fox’s U-BET THIS IS INCORRECT. We used DIAMOND brand syrup—Fox’s was the supermarket brand - nothing like the real thing…My father, Dave, had 2 partners-Jack (whose nephew is Robert Abrams),and Charlie - each had their own method.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: There's nothing wrong with a vanilla egg cream, made with Fox's U-Bet vanilla syrup. Yum.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Original formula Fox’s U-bet without corn syrup is only available around Passover

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
My father-in-law grew up in Brooklyn and made wonderful egg cream sodas with, of course, no egg. 
But when he took his sons on very early fly-fishing jaunts, he'd add a couple of raw, his backyard chickens' eggs "for nourishment". As he aged, and eating became less attractive and harder, those egg egg cream sodas became his daily breakfasts. He lived to be almost 97 years old.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Hold up...so egg creams contain....No egg?  Who knew??

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Happy to see Fox's U-Bet recommend.
(For truly authentic Egg Cream, get the Seltzer from Brooklyn Seltzer Boys, bottled in old-fashioned siphon bottles)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I never made a chocolate egg cream at home before and purchased the recommended Fox's Ubet Chocolate Syrup. I give it a firm "Meh". It might have been good in one of its previous versions, but the current official product is definitely average, and rather artificial tasting with a weird aftertaste. Also overpriced. Will try to find a higher quality syrup and make it again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Exactly. There is no magic in U-Bet, and there are much better syrups around. I know this is heresy, but it's true. It's like the people who claim, despite obvious evidence, that the repulsive Ray's is the best NY pizza.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: On my way uptown on Sundays, after volunteering at a midtown museum, I stop at Tom's Diner (of Seinfeld fame) and get a chocolate egg cream for the ride home. The quality of the treat varies depending on who makes it. This past weekend's was particularly great(fabulous foam on top), but even a middling one is still terrific--a perfect blend of great ingredients and the memory of the first one my mother bought me in Brooklyn when I was a little girl.  Spinning around on the fountain's stool between sips added to the taste and the memory .

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
White White Foam!!!   That indeed is the key that makes it an EGG cream.
If you add chocolate syrup to the milk and then stir it - it will be a chocolate foam topping. 
If you add syrup & milk and seltzer, then stir it, it will still be a chocolate foam.
1- Add the milk
2- Pour in the seltzer from a c02 cartridge bottle (that creates the strong stream, which then creates the WHITE FOAM).
3- NOW, add the syrup THROUGH the white foam.
4- Then using a long narrow spoon, gently stir into the syrup!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I started making these as a healthier alternative to milkshakes. 
2 tbsp half and half, a long squeeze of Hershey’s Simply 5 syrup (doesn’t contain high fructose corn syrup) and 8oz of plain seltzer. Not exactly the original recipe but just as tasty

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I agree re: Hersheys. And as I am now a diabetic, it is one of the few delicious treats b/c it is not made with a ton of chocolate and still tastes great~

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I NEVER mix the syrup & milk together until I begin to add the seltzer. I pour the syrup down the center of the milk (the way I remember the soda jerks of my Brooklyn childhood doing) so the syrup is at the bottom and the milk stays pristine white. The whole idea of the egg cream is to make the head as pure and stiff and white as a meringue. I stir from the bottom so as to keep the layers separate.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I worked at a luncheonette in New York when I was 15 in 1970. I made the best eggcream sodas!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Grew up in NYC (Queens) and I've lived in Fairbanks Alaska for 55 years.  Over the years I  would ship Fox's U-Bet here to make a great egg cream. Cost, who cares, its a great nostalgic treat. But now I have found Torani's Dark Chocolate syrup available locally to be a passable substitute. Don't get me wrong, I still have bottle of U-Bet in my freezer next to my fresh frozen Alaska salmon.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
You have to pour the seltzer over the spoon.  Wars have been fought over pouring on the front or back of the spoon.

Ed from Flatbush

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My 93-year-old father was a soda jerk when he was a boy in Brooklyn. he used to make hundreds of chocolate egg creams. I saw the recipe when they posted it and immediately said next time I see him I will make those for us and he enjoyed it so much. I hope it brought back a little of his childhood. They were great, and we use the right chocolate syrup, also from Brooklyn!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I am one of those weird people who do not like chocolate. I make vanilla egg creams with milk, seltzer, and vanilla extract. Devine!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Egg cream...French for "e cream" or "with cream"

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: How in heaven's name would a French phrase find its way into the name for an old-time, working-class [i.e. long before there were "foodies"] soda fountain beverage? Particularly an irrelevant one, since the drink is *not* made "with cream"? I don't pretend to know the real origin of the name, but the "comes from French" theory is perhaps the most ridiculous.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Michaelangelo. Just Google “why is an egg cream called an egg cream” and you’ll find possibilities based on presumably more than gut reactions.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: There are probably many ways to make an egg cream.  The candy store in my neighborhood started with a layer of whole milk in a tall glass, then added a burst of seltzer to create the "egg" foam midway up the glass, then into the foam, pumping shots of chocolate syrup, then mixing furiously as you add seltzer until the white foam has risen to the top of the glass. You are supposed to drink the egg cream fast, before the mixture settles, through the white foam, in say a New York Minute! Delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Or you can sub maple syrup. And much less milk.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Milk first. Then seltzer using the back of spoon technique. Then chocolate syrup goes right thru the foam to the bottom. Vigorously stir. This method preserves the white top. No longer use UBet as they don’t use sugar anymore. Try Trader Joe’s organic chocolate syrup.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Needs tweaking. A straight sided 12-16 oz.glass. 4 ozs. of milk is too much. Reduce to 3 or 2. Gauge amount of syrup to taste. It can be a little less that 3 TBs. Depends on how you want your last sip to taste. Syrup in glass, then milk, then 2/3 of the seltzer. Stir to mix - gently add rest of seltzer. Foam layer should be white. A transition layer of light brown underneath. The bottom should be darker. Add pretzel rod and consume immediately. Sip so all layers are tasted as one.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Direct from my father’s Brooklyn candy store at Caton and Flatbush Ave. The goal
is to get a foamy white top. The secret is to use semi-frozen whole milk which has ice crystals in it. Add the U-Bet chocolate syrup and the milk. Add seltzer, pouring onto a spoon into the glass. Then stir fast. Enjoy. Best in the world.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Haven't lived in New York for over 40 years. When living in the South my dad would send me Fox's U-Bet by the case. Now in the midwest, I order from Amazon. A craving since my childhood when the soda shop price was 5 cents (7 cents for large!). 
BTW, for vanilla flavor, Starbuck's syrup works, but not nearly as well.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I put the syrup in the glass first (only Foxes UBet please) then with the can of cold seltzer in one hand and the milk container in the other, pour the liquids in simultaneously. A quick stir provides the foam. That along with a salted pretzel rod is the best snack ever!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Born in New York City, the chocolate egg cream quickly became my all-time favorite drink, especially in the summer. Thanks for spreading the good news, Phil!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Who had the best? Undoubtably, Creamy Egg Cream on Bell Blvd. You'd sit at the counter like it were your favorite bar and order the usual, along with a pretzel rod. You'd polish it off and ask for another.
The menu was short at Creamy, but man, did they ever know how to make an egg cream. Each ingredient--all three of them-- stood out separately, but came together as a lovely symphony.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Ingrediants necessary are:
1- Yoo bet Chocalate
2- Whole Milk
3-Brooklyn Seltzer from the Brooklyn Seltzer in Brooklyn.
You need to order the Seltzer and each bottle has a $60 deposit
The Seltzer is $10. Only delivers in NY and Brooklyn

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My dad was a soda jerk in NYC while going to college in the 30s and he used to make Egg Creams all the time. but when his seltzer bottle finally gave up he stopped. I tried one using club soda and it was just not the same. When we went to visit his brother my dad found a seltzer bottle that worked, he asked his nephew to pick up a bottle of Fox's when he went to work in the city and that night after dinner the Egg Creams were back.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Stock up on Fox's U-Bet around Passover, when it's made with cane sugar.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Sammy's Romanian, up to the time they closed, used to allow their diners to make their own chocolate egg creams. They'd bring out bottles of seltzer and iced-milk, and a chocolate sauce dispenser, and you made the egg cream to suit your own tastes - a bit more of this, a bit less of that - and it was excellent. Not as exciting as the ones Sam the Candy Store Man used to make for us 80 years back for $.07 each here in Greenpoint. The candy store is still there, but no more egg creams.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Where in Greenpoint? My regular, usually on the way home after playing in McCarren Park right across the street, was the Park Luncheonette (which appears in Martin Scorsese's "The Departed," pretending to be in Boston) on the corner of Driggs and Lorimer. The spot is a pretentious restaurant now.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I learned to make these from Joe Leibowitz and Bob Ludwig when they hired me as a young lad of sixteen at their candy store on 75th and Columbus ave in Manhattan during the sixties. I made a million of them and Marc is right...put the syrup in first, then the milk and before you stir it, you add the seltzer. Nothing better and today I still send bottles of Fox's UBet syrup to my grandchildren.  I introduced egg creams to them a long time ago. Thanks to Amazon I can still get the syrup delivered

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
with vanilla syrup it becomes a white egg cream - also delicious,

but not as sweet as U-Bet syrup

the seltzer should not come from a can!

in the old days, it was delivered in 'seltzer bottles'

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Blast from the past!  Whisking up egg creams was my specialty at my high school job at the 86th St. and Bay Parkway tobacco/soda shop in Brooklyn.  I knew it was good when it had a nice white head!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The MOST important optional add-on here is the scoop of chocolate ice cream to make an ice cream soda! It's the only thing that can improve on an egg cream. NOT to be confused with an ice cream float which is just coke or root beer with a scoop of ice cream.  If I want to order a true ice cream soda I usually have to describe what it is exactly or they will just make a float.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If you wanted a drink with eggs you got an Orange Julius. Originally made with beaten egg whites for that unique froth. Born and raised in Kings County, near Erasmus Hall and I assure you eggs were never used in an egg cream. Also, if you ordered an egg cream it was vanilla unless you ordered a chocolate egg cream. The default was vanilla. At least in the 1950-1970’s in my neck of the woods.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: BTW that photo of the drink you called an egg cream looks more like a glass of chocolate milk with seltzer added. Not an egg cream.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Best and earliest egg cream memories: sitting on a stool in the late 50s at the small counter, sipping one while staring at those tall clear glass containers with pretzel sticks and penny candy dots stuck to rolls of waxed paper strips at Lil’s candy store  (I guess it sold other stuff too) on Parkside Ave. near Prospect Park. Good times!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: For a better-looking egg cream, hold off on the chocolate syrup. First add the seltzer to the milk and stir briefly, creating a bright white head. Then pour the syrup through the head, and when it settles on the bottom, stir again. The head will stay bright white (you'll scarcely see where the syrup passed through it) contrasting with the chocolate-colored liquid. Or do it as Marc's father did in the Bronx (where I was born with the egg cream gene). Either way, you want the bright white head.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Substitute vanilla syrup & you have a vanilla egg cream. At least you did in the luncheonette I worked in 55 years ago. A scoop of ice cream for the ice cream soda.Ah, nostalgia!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
If all else fails, visit a diner, they’re likely to make you one. 

I order mine, vanilla, not too sweet, hold the straw ; )

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I just made one with what I had at hand (and it’s 100 here, so I’m not going out). I have a Sodastream so used fizzy water from that, homemade chocolate syrup and whole milk. Whatever it was, it hit the spot!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My first egg cream was at a diner in State College, PA, 1976, shared with my first true love. I don’t know what kind of chocolate was used. Since it was central PA, probably Hershey’s. It was delicious and imparted a sweet memory.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Not being from NY or having grown up in the heyday of egg creams, I made it as instructed and it was a delight. There was still a light foam on top.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Is it truly seltzer water? My mother used to make these and it was milk and cream soda. It was kinda like a melted root beer float (I love a melted root beer float!) It’s hard for me to imagine using seltzer water instead.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My Pop was born and raised in Brooklyn, NY. He would be appalled that you left out the most important ingredient: the egg!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: No egg in an egg cream.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I lived on chocolate egg creams growing up in Brooklyn in the late 50s and 60s. There was never a suggestion that there should be egg in it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Most refreshing drink ever. The name scares away the uninitiated.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Still one of my favorite treats. If you get the right counter man at Tom's on Broadway (the Seinfeld coffee shop)--perfection!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This delicious drink was available at any soda fountain joint in Brooklyn for only one cent more than a glass ok Coke at 5 cents. I had one a ting I could scrounge up six cents in the 1950s.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Creamy Egg Cream on Bell Boulevard in Bayside, Queens sustained me during the dreary days of high school. If you could drink ten they wouldn’t charge you.  I don’t know anyone who made it past 5.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’m sure this is sacrilege, but I make my egg creams with Diet Coke/Coke Zero. Old time seltzer in squirt bottles is not readily available and I find the fizz from the colas makes a better brew than bottled “seltzer” or sparkling water. Plus no extra sugar. Try it and let us know!

author: Julia Moskin
Julia Moskin
commentBody: Dave's Luncheonette, Canal Street and B'way, was my place for egg creams. Another era long gone in ever-changing Manhattan. Happy memories and a twinge of bittersweet nostalgia for me.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
A properly made egg cream is truly one of life’s great pleasures.  Fox U-Bet makes other flavored syrups so if you cannot have chocolate, there are options.  If they ever bring back their cherry syrup, an egg cream made with that and Vintage brand’s cherry seltzer is amazing.
If you can’t get Fox U-Bet, use Hershey’s.  NEVER use Bosco for the syrup.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I use a half a cup of soy milk and two cups of flavored seltzer for an everyday treat.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I play around with adding spices like cinnamon or some smoked hot pepper, or almond extract. Not the classic egg creme but tasty.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My father was a counterman for years. It's Fox's U-bet followed by a splash of seltzer..pour milk 3/4 to the top and fill the rest with seltzer while stirring. End of story.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Nostalgia for a cold summer beverage!  My cousin & I at times had the run of our grandparents’ tiny house, referred to as a bungalow, on City Island, a lovely & unique part of the Bronx.  If my memory serves me right we made and drank copious amounts of egg creams on warm summer days.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The egg cream of my childhood was made with a homemade chocolate syrup that my Grandmother made. I wish that was one of the recipes that she handed down to me!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: No eggs?  That is one of life's great mysteries.  I worked at a luncheonette on Long Island, NY in the 60's and was "required" to add the seltzer using the fountain gizmo which shot it out at great speed helping to create the wonderful, creamy foam top.  Our specialty was the Vanilla Eggcream - a shot of vanilla syrup, milk, and seltzer - done.  Yum.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The vanilla syrup sounds good, I like cream soda .

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Instead of seltzer try your favorite cream soda.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I make these drinks all the time, but I stopped using U-Bet after I read the ingredients.  Try other chocolate syrups that do not contain corn syrup. A can of Trader Joe's seltzer, though not as fizzy as one might like, is okay and the right size for one drink.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I've been using a couple of big tablespoons of vanilla ice cream (slightly softened) instead of milk, it's really good!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Yes, ice cream sodas are good, too. That's what you're making, not an egg cream.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I wonder if Torani vanilla syrup can sub for chocolate syrup? I might try that for a vanilla egg cream.  I use Hershey’s Simply 5 Syrup for my chocolate egg creams. The Simply 5 doesn’t contain HFCS.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I was thought they an egg cream should have a WHITE frothy head. 
Put the milk in first
Add the seltzer from a siphon ora soda dispenser in the old fashion candy store. 
Finally the syrup. 
Stir 
Enjoy.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I’ve been waiting for this for years. I grew up in Riverdale, where my pal Marc and I would go to the luncheonette on the corner of Greystone and 238th for, of course, the best egg cream I’ve ever tasted. They used Diamond chocolate syrup (RIP), made in West Farms off the Cross Bronx, which I still think beat Foxes U-Bet, because it had milk solids in it for a creamier flavor. 

I later sold egg creams out of a cart in Central Park, and used the technique I learned from the experts at the luncheonette: pour in the milk, then seltzer to get a good head of white foam, THEN squirt in the syrup and carefully stir to keep the foam white. This produces the most delicious egg cream. Try it! (And one myth about the name is that the brown circle of syrup in the white foam looks like an egg)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: You get a purer white foam if you add the chocolate after the milk and seltzer

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I love egg creams. I use a lactose free chocolate milk and seltzer. Quick, easy and delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: What about almond or soy milk?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: sure why not! still would taste great, try to use one with a bit of fat in it

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The main problem is that home seltzer makers don't adequately carbonate

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: As a waiter for several years at Sammy's Roumanian Steakhouse - I became expert in egg cream making.  There, we were schooled in the perfect method to acheive a white foamy top above the chocolately soda: milk and selzter first, then the chocolate.  My favorite method to add Fox's u-bet was to stand on a chair, pouring from as high as I could reach.  Always a show stopper and a guaranteed tip booster ;)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I've heard that the term egg cream is a corruption of the phrase "ekht creme," or "real cream."  No matter.  If you can't get hold of the kosher for Passover Fox's U-bet, Hershey's Simply 5 (made with cane sugar) is a decent substitue.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I've done crowd tasting egg cream events. Fox these days looses to Hershye's.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have always added the syrup last..you get the best white top...

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Buffalo, Fall 1969. 6 freshmen from Brooklyn go to the Blu Galaxy diner across Main St from SUNY campus. We order a mix of sandwiches and 6 egg creams. The young waitress, whose flat “A” accent amuses us, as our Brooklyn accent amused her, looks puzzled. “What’s that.?”  We describe the ingredients and how to make it. “Oh, that’s a chocolate phosphate.”  “Sure,” we say, laughing over the name and her accent. She puts in the lunch order, and goes to make the drinks. Continued next

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: In addition to the steps in this recipe, the way I was taught to make an egg cream (mostly from experience drinking them in soda fountains during the late 50s and 60s) was that the chocolate syrup should be introduced into the glass so it forms a streak down the side, a streak that doesn’t get mixed in - and then you drink it from that side of the glass. Yummmm!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Chocolate phosphate contd: after a while we realize it is taking too long for the egg creams. I go to the front counter to investigate. She has 6 glasses with syrup, milk and seltzer and is vigorously stirring them. When the foam rises, she is scooping it out of each glass. “What are you doing,?” I ask. “Adding soda water,” she replies. “ you don’t want all that foam.”  “Oy” was the only response.  We taught her how to make a proper Brooklyn egg creams and became regulars.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Use strawberry quick to make a very attractive pink one. And if it's a special occasion, a splash of vodka wouldn't hurt.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: It occurs to me that it is kind of like a chocolate milk spritz minus the prosecco. I bet if it was marketed it as a spritz it would become a thing among annoying trend followers ;)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: In 1966 when I moved into my own apartment I surveyed about 3 or 4 candy stores for the best egg cream.  My choice used Pure Maid chocolate syrup, which I was able to buy in Brooklyn after a longish subway ride.   Pure Maid is long gone, so it's Fox's now.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The old candy store version--Fox's U-Bet Chocolate Syrup (has to be the Kosher for Passover one, so it contains sugar, not corn syrup), next would be the seltzer, then last is the milk--all mixed with a long spoon. Where the water used in the seltzer is from is important too--for the best taste, use NYC water and make the seltzer yourself. If you cannot make it yourself, Vintage Cosmetic seltzer comes closest to what used to be available out of the NYC candy store fountains.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Here's an even better idea:  Use regular old Hershey's Chocolate Syrup and fresh milk.  No need for seltzer (at least if you're from the south).  Mix in the amount of chocolate that best suits your taste (you'll get a feel for this after one or two times).  Drink.  Enjoy.  Repeat.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: That's chocolate milk: not the same thing.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Or pour orange soda over a helping of vanilla ice cream to get a creamsicle that's not on a stick.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I love egg creams.  I've been enjoying them for over 60 years.  The recipe is nice, but no substitute for getting one at a fountain, candy store, etc.  I no longer live in NYC, but when I come back I still have 4 places I know I can get them.  Some day, I will have my last egg cream.  I will still love it.  May U-bet chocolate syrup endure. It is a piece of history, and more importantly, it's perfectly delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I've settled on an incredibly delicious egg-cream recipe.  1 or 1.5 oz half-and-half, 1-2 Tbsp chocolate syrup, mix with a spoon, then fill 'er up with very cold and highly carbonated seltzer.  The half and half makes it creamy even though you're not using much.  The drink is light, frothy, and very delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: An egg cream is the best remedy for a hangover.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I always preferred Vanilla, not Chocolate. 
Once, in Oregon, a soda fountain employee tried to make me an egg cream with ICE. Outrageous. I taught him the proper way to make a genuine NY egg cream.

commentBody: Has anyone tried this with soy or oat milk?

commentBody:

Kaaren Powers, San Jose, Ca.

author: Julia Moskin

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: EVOO’s smoke point (temperature at which the oil breaks down, oxidizes, and releases dangerous free radicals) is only 350-410 deg F. Medium-high cooktop temp is 400-450 deg F. The longer the oil’s in the pan, the more likely the oil will hit if not surpass its smoke point. I’d recommend either sautéing at a lower cooktop temp (especially if you’re using cast iron) or use a higher smoke point oil. It’s just basic science.

author: Julia Moskin

author: Julia Moskin

author: Julia Moskin

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I like the addition of mint with the parm.  This is where to break out the  fresh, good olive oil.  I had to make sure not to over-salt the pasta water since it’s going back into dish for texture.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I didn't have snap peas and I forgot to get green peas out of the freezer (will be added to leftovers tomorrow.  I had Manchego cheese and grated that over the salad.  Everything else was as shown in the recipe that I halved. I was aware of some comments on saltiness, also on blandness.  I used salt once when sautéing all the vegetables and of course used it in boiling the pasta.  It was perfect for me, the pasta I love and a good assortment of vegetables. I can always add spice or chicken too.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I appreciated how veg-forward this dish was, but in the end, I wanted it to be a proper pasta with a sauce and not a pasta salad. While eating it, I found myself wishing I had kept more of the pasta water and built a sauce for the dish with extra Parmesan. I probably won’t make this again, but if I did, that’s what I’d do.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Use arugula instead of kale.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Added extra lemon just because I like the acidity. A fresh, healthy, and lovely salad.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Not a fan of kale. Any suggestions for substitutions?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Linda—Spinach.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Linda yes, kale is icky but we like mustard, collards, broccoli raab or leafy bok Choi or any type  if Choi

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I read through the recipe as written and decided to make a few changes. I live in Tucson and already have a bounty in the garden at the end of March, so I picked fresh English peas and threw them in the pan without parboiling them first and they were sweet and yummy. I used a very large bowl of fresh Italian parsley rather than kale or chard. The recipe is called a salad, but says to serve warm or room temperature. It benefited from being in the fridge for 2 hours before eating. Further, mint didn't sound good to me so I topped it with freshly chopped garden basil, halved those cherry tomatoes, drizzled with EVOO, S&P and it was delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is fantastic. Nice use of the kale leaf and stem. I let the veggies sautee a bit longer to get more sweetness. The lemon is absolutely essential here (although I didn't use Meyer lemons). Refreshing and very spring-y.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used baby spinach in place of Kale. Delicious!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this tonight and served with the NYT Ritz cracker/cheddar encrusted chicken cutlets. A perfect combo. The barely sautéed shallots and garlic add depth of flavor to the pasta salad that negates the need for a heavy dressing, leaving the veggies crisp and fresh tasting. It’s one of the best pasta salads we’ve had and tastes like spring!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was simple but delish!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
We tend to eat clean and vegan which is why I was I was excited for this recipe, but WOW is it bland bland bland and we used excellent olive oil to boost it along with the Meyer lemons. 

Even cheese cannot save this dish. 

It needs some red pepper flakes, mash up some of the peas first maybe with some cream (not my favorite idea but gosh this needs something - maybe ask the chefs at Che Fico for an idea), or throw in the towel and add some pancetta. We have a lot of leftovers so I shall experiment and get back with an update.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Shallots, garlic, lemon, 2 kinds of cheese, mint and black pepper should not result in a once, twice, three times bland result. I don't think any dish "needs" red aper flakes, but I like them and, apparently, so do you. A vegetarian dish certainly does not require pancetta. Also, thanks for not getting back with a bland (mashed peas and cream?) update.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Fun to make and I loved the crunch of the veggies. Next time however, I need more flavor. Maybe more shallot, salt and lemon?  Nice addition to a summer dinner with homegrown tomatoes.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If I make this again, I will use a smaller Pasta

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I thought this was really subtle but very tasty.  I used a meyer lemon and could taste it which was nice.  I used the juice of the whole lemon. I subbed baby spinach for kale to use up what i had in the fridge.  Finally I added a half teaspoon of red pepper flakes to give it some oomph without changing the flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I want to add that Meyer lemon and plenty of mint really make it sing.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: yummy!!!

author: Julia Moskin

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I thought this was great - the lemon made it especially delicious. Really easy to add whatever’s on hand. It makes a ton - great size for sharing.

author: Julia Moskin

author: Julia Moskin

author: Julia Moskin

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This was excellent. Added shrimp for more protein. I pushed the vegetables aside and briefly cooked the tomatoes in the same pan for 1 minute just to soften them. 

Adding pasta sauce was necessary to make it saucier.

author: Julia Moskin

I made this and it doesn't compare to my normal recipe.  The strawberries didn't add a lot of flavor but competed with the banana.  The passion fruit seeds added a little crunch but the crunch should have been from the vanilla wafers which for some reason were crushed.
The pudding part was actually ok.

author: Julia Moskin

commentBody:

And you have all of the ingredients in your pantry and fridge.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
@pat in oregon
But none of the nasty boxed preservatives and loads of chemicals found in boxed powders, which serve to MIMIC real food ingredients, without having ANY real food in them.  Many serve no serious function except to thicken or prevent ‘clumping.’  Some only prevent ‘rancidity’ or its accompanying odour, which identifies its years of storage on the stores’ Warehouse or Home Pantry Shelf!  We can ALL do better than that!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Elizabeth Watt, GP, AB. Canada you completely missed the point of this comment, which is that making it yourself from real food ingredients is just as easy as making the garbage from the boxed mix. It’s anti-box, not pro.

Then made it a second time with cooked down strawberries and rhubarb (no bananas or passion fruit), which was yummy too. I think it would also be excellent with cherries or peaches.

author: Julia Moskin

commentBody: This recipe is so delicious! Highly recommend! I made a batch with banana and a second batch with blueberries to replace bananas. Both were devine!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: One word delicious..very creamy and easy to make

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I was skeptical of M, but this is a simple, easy to make delish dessert. It’s elegant as well. 
Mine will have a few crumbled graham crackers, on top,  from my pantry.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Tried it.  Very easy.  I love the addition of strawberries to banana pudding.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I had two leftover egg yolks and a couple cups of milk well past the sell by date so I had little to lose by trying this. I added another tablespoon of cornstarch to make up for the missing egg yolk. My previous recipe was with a double boiler and I thought I would give this a try as it looked easier. It seems to be setting up properly in the refrigerator so I guess it worked. The remaining question is whether it will separate and get watery.

 Can I name my variation after my kid?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was absolutely delicious and so easy to make. We hosted a dinner party for 6 people and it was the perfect amount to prepare in fancy glasses for each person. I decorated it with some mint and followed the recipe as is. Definitely would not change anything about it and will be making this throughout the summer for a light and refreshing dessert.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So delicious I had to make another batch to share with friends.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made shortbread to use, in place of vanilla wafers (don’t live in US), and think I’ll do that going forward as it was super yummy!!! Also didn’t have whipped cream - I imagine it would make the dessert feel decadent - will definitely try with whipped cream in future. Thank be tall a lovely dessert and very easy to make!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Megan is amazing. I am so glad to read the article about her, and so happy to have this recipe. I have made banana pudding the traditional way for many years. This is a nice addition to the classic recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Absolutely delicious! My whole family ate them and asked for more

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I love this easy to follow recipe that yields delicious results!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The recipe was quite good -- I skipped the mint. As for the description itself, well, the best food writing evokes the wonder of ingredients. I can't quite say I've ever reflected on the "sting" of lemon and strawberry before, though.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I answered my own question about the single egg. 

This recipe is exactly a half recipe of that in Joy of Cooking for blancmange. Except that recipe with 4 cups of milk uses two eggs. Thus one egg would be a substitution here. I have successfully used that recipe and maybe that’s why I checked as it sounded familiar

I don’t know how common this particular recipe is,  but it’s possible that Megan’s grandmother had a copy of Joy of cooking.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Most desserts follow the same measurements of ingredients, unlike entrées. Read and compare the recipes for cookies, cakes, and breads.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Good starting point, but it was a little eggy for me. I combined this recipe with a pastry cream recipe because the directions for the other recipe are more complete and I was curious about the differences. I added an extra tablespoon of butter, which I liked, and next time I would add more vanilla. I might cut down on the sugar a bit. 

Fine tuning the directions:

At the end, I suggest stirring with a spoon, not whisking, to prevent the pudding from splitting (separating). I stirred in the vanilla and butter before I poured it into the serving bowl to keep the bowl cleaner. If you don’t want a skin, stir it very gently every ten minutes or so for around forty minutes. But be careful to prevent splitting. 

Also, I put a paper towel across the top of the bowl (not touching the pudding) before pudding the lid on it to absorb moisture, per Maida Heatter’s directions. That was the right move.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: You can use parchment paper in lieu of plastic wrap

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Since the plastic wrap is used to prevent a skin from forming on the top of the pudding as it cools you want to use something that you can gently and easily cover the pudding. It's much easier to manipulate plastic wrap than parchment paper or wax paper.  And plastic wrap stays were you put it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Barb H The idea is to use less plastic in general and single-use plastic in particular. I wish NYTCooking would stop with all the instructions using plastic. People managed perfectly well making custards etc., before the petroleum industry got everyone hooked on plastic. Wax paper can also be used to prevent a skin.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’m glad someone mentioned other fruits. I was wondering if they would be successful. I’m looking forward to making and eating some of the Lili pudding!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Wax paper or buttered Parchment would also suffice as a cover to prevent a ‘skin’ forming on the Pudding, as they are compostable.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Can I substitute a whole egg for the three egg yolks? Otherwise this looks like a fun recipe. I made a similar cocoa pudding from an old Hershey’s cookbook (no egg: more cornstarch) and it would be nice to have a good vanilla pudding recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Has anyone made this in advance? Does it keep for more than a day?

commentBody: Yes it does! Had it in the fridge for about 5 days total, tasted just as good on the last day

commentBody: @Dawn Yes - it turned out great for me using cashew milk but I’m sure any non-dairy milk would work. It’s really the eggs that hold it together

commentBody: This turned out sweet and gloppy for my taste but could be my mistake thinking it was a dessert for all ages. Reading again - maybe it’s just intended for kids? It has a strong “nursery food” vibe.

commentBody: Can I substitute arrowroot for the cornstarch? More healthful.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made it tonight after recipe came out this morning since there’s such a buzz about her,  and honestly it was good, not great. One important note is that this might feed her family of 10 (including eight kids) but I’d say it feeds four hungry adults.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I love stroganoff and make it often -- this is OK, but nothing special. Probably needs more herbs & seasoning! and trust me on this folks: no matter how good the yogurt, it won't taste the same as with SOUR CREAM. If Mrs. Ballerina Farm has an unlimited supply of homemade yogurt, she can (or could) also have SOUR CREAM.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Lola Montez I agree with you! I wanted to try this with yogurt, but it ended up too sour for me with the mustard. Sour cream makes it more creamy. It was still tasty, but I doubt I’ll make it this way again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Wine. It needs a whole bottle of wine. I know, I know. She doesn't drink wine. But most of us do.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The 3-star ratings say it all. This is a solid recipe if cooking for a child’s serving size and palate. For us adults in the room, Sam Sifton’s and Craig Claiborne’s recipes (on this website) are classics for a reason. Much more accurately scaled for an adult serving size and developed for an adult’s palate. But, oh those Sourdough Herbed Noodles! Haven’t made yet but it was worth reading through this good but not great recipe to discover another cool use for my sourdough starter. Thank you Ms Ballerina Farms!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: A good recipe but I usually add a couple of tablespoons of tomato paste to the saute and prefer sour cream to yogurt. A splash of Worcestershire sauce might also boost the umami if it seems a little dull.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Martha the recipe already does include a Tb of Worcestershire sauce :)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The umami in this recipe is dull because a really tasty stroganoff is 2 lbs of mushrooms to 1 lb of beef, not 2 lbs of beef to 6 ozs of mushrooms. Sure, there should be beef and beef broth, but it is the sour cream and mushrooms that make the dish (and not onion, mustard, or Worcestershire sauce, which just add noise).

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Love this recipe! Incredible user friendly

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe is quite tasty. We all enjoyed it. I asked the butcher to cut the beef for stroganoff which was a big time saver. While sautéing the it, I needed much more butter than was recommended. Perhaps the beef I used was too lean? I used button mushrooms, but I will mix it up next time. I imagine the leftovers will be divine as the flavors meld. We will see tomorrow.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Agree with other reviewers. I made this last night for our household of adults and it was pretty meh. Overall bland and lacking in any interesting flavor development that would make it more palatable for non kiddos. It needs garlic, shallot, sherry, wine, chives, tomato paste, maybe a bit of soy sauce for more umami and layered flavor. Sour cream would certainly make for a better mouth feel than Greek yogurt. Overall, this is fine if you’re making food for children that like simple, basic food. I’d avoid serving this to fellow adults without a bunch of doctoring to the original recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: It lacked depth of flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Just from the looks of this it is far too beef-forward.  I use 3/4 pound of thinly sliced beef (put it in the freezer for a bit, then salt and pepper it at least an hour before you sauté it) and a pound and a half of sliced crimini mushrooms.

No mustard. No onion. No Worcestershire sauce. No flour. No herbs. Absolutely no Parmesan cheese. Yikes.

I also tried making this with yogurt when I was in Japan and couldn’t get sour cream—sorry to say it turned out awful.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this for my family and they loved it. I used 2 tbpl Worcester and sour cream and it was delicious. I agree that the portions are a bit smaller than posted (or maybe we just eat much more) but it fed four hungry adults with some to spare.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Yum. Like the sourness, and the jalapeno I sautéed with the onions was the perfect kick. Out of my Knorr msg beef bouillon and the chicken one subbed nicely. Mmmsg.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Simply delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: With the addition of wine and tomato paste as suggested here by others, the recipe turned out very good. If I make it again, I’ll braise the meat in the oven for a while instead of just letting it simmer on the stove top.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Used vegetable broth, leftover tritip for one person, extra mushrooms, altered to 3T mustard, 2T Worstershire

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: It really seemed like the meat needed to be cooked for a much shorter time. It wasn’t very flavor able,needed much more

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I love this recipe and love that it uses yogurt instead of sour cream, which is more protein and also adds probiotics. I am making it with sirloin today, but have made it with other cuts in the past which have all turned out wonderfully. Huge fan.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This is a pretty good weeknight recipe that I can have on the table in about 45 minutes and it uses one pan. I keep presliced sirloin (buy in bulk at Costco and cut/vacuum seal) which cuts down on prep time, all I have to do is chop an onion and the mushrooms.

I definitely don't feel this has to simmer for 90 minutes. 20-30 seems fine.

I add the step of deglazing the pan with 2-3 T of white wine after the mushrooms and onions cook. 

And, as others have said, sour cream definitely adds. Sometimes I use a mix of sour cream and yogurt, just depends on what I have.  

I serve over egg noodles that cook in 3 minutes. If I'm feeling fancy, said noodles might get buttered and parslied.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This was fine. I made exactly as directed. A three-pound package of ground turkey at Fred Meyer (one of our Kroger brands) was only a couple of dollars more than the in-pound package, so I doubled the recipe: two loaves fit perfectly in a 9x13 pan.

Kids loved it, and I sent home the extra loaf with one of the couples. Maybe because I also made beef mushroom gravy from scratch to go with the mashed potatoes?

The leftovers made aa really good sandwich for lunches on thick wheat bread, with a bit of steak sauce and mayo, along with lettuce and tomato.

I think it’s a bit sweet, so will try subbing in zucchini for the apple next time (even though I used a Granny Smith).

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I make stroganoff with half a pound of thinly sliced ribeye (salt and pepper an hour in advance), a pound and a half of sliced cremini mushrooms, beef stock, spices, and sour cream. 20 minutes of simmering is plenty. This recipe is like lots of beef with some flecks of mushrooms. Love the fresh noodles, though. If buying packaged, I buy Manischewitz—they are about triple the price but totally worth it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Don't forget that the salt from her tears adds lots of flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Does this call for feed sourdough, or can sourdough discard be used?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Carol “discard” is just what you do with it, not a specific state of activity. It wouldn’t matter in this case how active your starter was.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @AMcG It does make a difference, discard is hungry starter, and active starter is going to act differently

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This wasn’t good.The dough is very wet and extremely hard to work with even after dusting copiously with more flour. I often make fresh pasta and was excited to try sourdough, but my family didn’t like it and it took me twice as long to work with as a regular pasta recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Love this recipe

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: A performative recipe that is mediocre upon execution. 0 stars if I could.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This isn’t good.  There is no reason for pasta to have rise, so the sourdough makes no sense.  This is definitely one of those internet recipes that was made to get clicks but not to actually eat,

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Carol-- I can't imagine that the starter would need to be well-fed. Since pasta needs no leavening, my guess is that the starter (as opposed to plain water) provides some flavor, and perhaps alters the texture somewhat-- it's fermenting properties probably does something positive to the gluten in the flour, too, making it more digestible....but that's just a guess.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I made this recipe as is (okay. I added some additional seasoning) and it was tasty. I usually find the comments semi helpful, but this time they were downright obnoxious. If you find the taste of bacon too strong, don’t make a recipe that includes bacon. If anyone bothered to read the copy that comes with the recipe, they’d clearly see the words, “Coq au vin blanc…..is very different from the classic coq au vin”. Oh and my sauce didn’t curdle when I added the heavy cream.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: @Bruce H Mine didn’t curdle either. Good recipe!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Sorry for a negative report here -- made this to recipe, but by the end it was trying too hard: too much of too many big flavor hammers (bacon, mustard, thyme, shallots, wine, even red pepper flakes on top) & the cream curdled as it was reducing.  Did the dish need all 3 fats, bacon+oil+butter?  The result has a strong flavor but overpowering, not great.  It could be far more subtle, perhaps with 1/3 the amounts on most taste ingredients.  Needs revising, I think.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Bacon sub? Chop some portobello mushrooms into 1/4” dice, and sauté in a bit of oil till they’ve released water and the pan starts to dry. They cook down into meaty chewy bits perfect for this dish. Want some smoke!? Add a pinch or two of smoked paprika, or a couple drops of liquid smoke at the beginning. Garlic powder is a nice addition too.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I was skeptical of this recipe from the start, but my wife and primary dining customer loved it.  So what do I know.  It was straightforward to cook.  Next time I'll make the full sauce recipe since it's so good, I thought 1 1/2 c of wine was too much, but I was wrong.  I served this with angel hair pasta and a crisp green salad

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: This is very tasty, but it has nothing at all to do with coq au vin.  Instead, it's a play on the basic French combinations of wine and cream, with the fabulous underflavors of mushroom and bacon.  Think more along the lines of Julia Child's wonderful recipe for poulet au porto.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Maybe nothing to do with coq au vin, but certainly with coq au vin blanc. They are not the same thing. You say it's instead a play on the combo of wine and cream. That's what coq au vin blanc is! It's not "blanc" just because of white wine, but also "blank" because of heavy cream.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: This is a lot of trouble for Meatballs. I agree, it has nothing to do with Coq au vin. Commenters really don’t have to try so hard to fix recipes that are just not worth making. There are many much better meatballs on this site.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: This was awesome! I subbed ground duck leg and breast for the chicken, fresh porcini for the shiitake and served it with a healthy topping of thinly sliced just-in-season white Alba truffles!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious, but the oceans of cream and vats of wine were utterly unnecessary.  I used about 1/2 C of wine, a gluggette of cream, and extended the sauce with chicken stock.  Just as yummy, and far less of a heart-attack-bomb.

Still, five stars for excellent flavor.  Will definitely make again!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I sautéed a shallot and garlic and added them to the meatballs. I used fresh oregano because that's what I had, and about 1/3 cup of crème fraîche instead of heavy cream. I also used just one cup of wine and one cup of chicken stock, and cremini mushrooms. The end result was quite good

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious but I also put sauteed shallots and garlic in the meatballs.  The meatballs ceratinly needed more than a pinch of salt and pepper.   The sauce flavor was great. Will be making this again, for sure!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: That was delicious. I cooked the meatballs separately in the oven at 425° and added garlic to the meatballs. I left the bacon in the pan, added the mushrooms, shallots wine, and then added 2 cups of water and a cup of orzo. Cooked till orzo was tender, added in meatballs from the oven. Mixed in Dijon and cream till warmed and gobbled it up!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Oh, my dairy also curdled. If you forget to turn down the heat before adding the cream as I did (which isn't mentioned in the recipe) here is how to solve that: mix in some cornstarch and let sit over low heat, mixing frequently, until the curdles aren't noticable. Voila, presentable once again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I followed this recipe using the turkey substitutes.  rolling the turkey meatball in oil covered hands was brilliant, unbelievably moist. I liked the sauce and adding water was good. I did half cream and half milk, so it wasn’t so heavy. The Dijon mustard was a great addition in my opinion, maybe not coq au vin but definitely French.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Followed according to recipe but added a sautéed shallot and clove of garlic to the meatballs, along with about a tsp of garlic powder, .5 tsp onion powder, .5 tsp paprika, and some extra salt in response to the comments that many found the meatballs themselves bland. I needed a little extra panko and let the meatballs chill in the fridge for about an hour after forming them and they held their shape very well. I thought this was delicious!! Used a mix of shiitake and cremini

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: There was way too much fat and way too much liquid! Could’ve ditched the butter entirely and added half the wine and just a splash of cream. I need to stop trusting these recipes blindly. I ended up adding a can of navy beans and a bag of spinach to soak some of it up and ladling off a bunch of the liquid.
