author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody:
I loved this and so did our guests. I cooked this for/with good friends, which is our idea of a good time, and it’s a good recipe for that. You can put guests to work or do a lot of it ahead of time.

One note: we saw no reason at all to add water and then cook to reduce.

Serves with wide egg noodles, was a hit! (We also considered wild rice or mashed potatoes.)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I did not like this.  It would have helped if there was a measurement for the shallots - their flavor (and that of the mushrooms) was overpowering.  A lot of work for a bland dish.  Served it over rice, which helped.  Using so much wine and water instead of stock did not produce a good sauce.  I had to add a large amount of chicken bouillon concentrate to make balance flavors.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: This was good, but overly complicated and if I make it again I'll tweak it slightly.  First, the meatballs did not hold together well in the skillet.  Next time I'd bake them in the oven and just throw them into the sauce at the end.  We also found the Dijon a bit overpowering with all the other strong flavors.  I might try a splash of lemon or a dash of Worcestershire instead, or just reduce the Dijon to a teaspoon.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: really liked this receipe.  very modifiable,  i did chanterelles instead of shitakes,  i  could easily do this with ground turkey as well.  or even chicken thighs.  the only thing i would say is to not add all the liquid at the end all at once - you might end up with a sauce that takes forever to reduce.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I made the recipe as directed the first time. The second time, I switched over to Julia Child's Coq au Vin recipe for the sauce: no mustard, red pepper or heavy cream - just a beurre manié to thicken.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Great potential, felt the sauce was pretty sharp using a California sauv blanc. Maybe reduce the wine some, and replace the water and removed wine with chicken broth? Or at least use a lighter-bodied wine. I made the meatballs in a convection oven with a heavy spritz of olive oil; frying meatballs is so fussy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: The sauce was absolutely delicious. I used half and half instead of heavy cream since that is what I had on hand. Otherwise followed directions as written. The advice to reduce the heat before whisking in the dairy was very helpful. I served over buttered egg noodles with a side salad. Next time I would consider doing sautéed chicken breast instead of meatballs. They were pretty bland.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Turkey bacon?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: The powerful flavors of the sauce didn't make up for how dry and tasteless the meatballs were.  If I were to make this again, I would use more panko and flavor the meatballs with onion, thyme, or something more than salt & pepper!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I was already planning to make ground beef meatballs using a tomato-based sauce.  Thought I'd try this recipe instead.  But since I was using ground beef, I substituted red wine for white.  The flavor was strong, but I thought it was acceptable.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: First try, I followed the white wine and water reduction. Added the cream and all curdled. I questioned the step and my instincts and yep, all curdled. Tossed out and started over. No more wine so I used Chicken broth. Note, thick bacon was not tender, I would suggest thin bacon and cook it to crispy. definitely not enough gravy for the ratio of meatballs. Three stars.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: This was a great recipe!  Ground chicken made the meatballs lighter than other meats and the sauce was wonderful. Highly recommend making as directed.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: To avoid curdling, use crème fraîche instead of heavy cream. And to avoid dairy, Belsoy makes a very useful soy-based substitute.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I punched up the meatballs a bit with garlic powder, onion powder, thyme, and some crushed red pepper; used a spoon of bacon grease to brown the meatballs since I didn’t have bacon in the fridge; and used whole milk in place of the heavy cream for the same reason. Served with egg noodles and a green salad. To my taste, this was delicious and made for a quick and easy week night dinner.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Use Shallots not onions, add MSG and plenty of salt to chicken to give it some flavor, and serve with mashed potatoes.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious!!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I thought this was really good. However, the prep time was an hour. The NYT estimates are not for the usual cook. I’ve been a home cook for many years! I substituted tarragon for the herbs and it was a great decision. Served with egg noodles and a green salad.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: This was very good.  I love meatballs.  I added a teaspoon of both garlic and onion powder since so many reviews said they were bland.  Other than that made as directed.  (Doubling the sauce portion so I could serve it over pasta. I will definitely make again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Mostly thought it was great-- but the meatballs themselves could have used a little more seasoning. If I make it again I'll toss some herbs or garlic or leek in there.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Good stuff! My sauce did break, but I attribute that to trying to hold it on low for too long while I was finishing up the rest of dinner, so that’s on me. If I have one complaint, it’s that while the sauce was excellent, the meatballs themselves were a bit on the bland side — my pinch of salt proved insufficient, and more precise measurements might welcome. Will happily make again!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I usually follow a recipe to the pinch and dash. But I was trained in France and couldn’t help but make a few small changes: shallots in before the mushrooms. I used chicken bone broth instead of water. And I did 1/2 heavy cream, half fat free 1/2 & 1/2. The  results were delectable. Delicious! Divine!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody:
Made this and it was amazing.

I would recommend taking a little more time with the meatballs. I added 1/3 cup of fresh grated parmesan to the mixture because the texture was off.

As a personal preference, I only made ten meatballs and put them into the sauce seven minutes before the mushrooms, they cooked perfectly, but if you do this make sure to add some additional water so your sauce doesn't over-reduce.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: My husband and I enjoyed this recipe so much. Our family was highly motivated to find new chicken recipes when our beloved Hindu daughter-in-law joined the family. We discovered that the Kitchen Aid meat grinder attachment worked really well! We grind boneless thighs and make chicken bolognese, Laotian lettuce cups, and teriyaki meatballs. Perhaps some of the negative reviews might improve if they ground their own chicken.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Use pancetta, bacon is too overpowering,add MSG to meatballs to add some flavor, other than that it is a good solid recipe for a winter evening.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I found that there wasn’t enough sauce. Add more stock?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I make this with orzo, which I cook in the sauce. It’s really, really delicious. It can get a little goopy, but there’s nothing I love more on a cold day than super savory and delicious goop!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: bacon flavor was overpowering - pancetta would be better I think. subbed half and half for heavy cream and it did break at addition but then the sauce formed up. served on a brown stoneware plate which did not do any visual favors - could use some additional garnish for a color pop. will make again!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody:
We have a dairy allergic household so I subbed in a combo of evoo and more bacon grease for butter and chx  stock for water in the sauce prep. Added more stock along the way. 

For the cream I’ve learned that the mix of Cocojune plain yogurt with Oatmilk creamer to make a cream consistency works really well as a cream substitute. I don’t boil I but it all came together warmed up at the end on low heat. You lose some mouth feel and flavor imo which is why I use my homemade stock in lieu of water above. 

Did the meatballs with dry spices and dried thyme as indicated in comments, and cooked in oven while I made the sauce and sides of pasta and steamed green beans. 

Everyone loved it. Even my no mushroom  teens.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: This was yummy but the end result was too thin. I think I’ll omit the 1/2c water if I make it again. Served it on noodles.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Ugh! This was a disappointment. I felt cheated of my time, calories and shiitake mushrooms. Got a nice box of shiitakes from Costco and wanted to try something new. Seemed like it should yield something tasty with protein, but the flavors just didn’t come together. The meatballs were bland, the sauce ingredients battled until stalemate, which is basically the flavor of the entire dish. Completion was a Pyrrhic victory that I get to relive each lunch until I get through the leftovers.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I made it just as written and it was wonderful! There certainly wasn’t too much liquid but you need to make sure to cook the liquid down before adding the meatballs and mushrooms. All my guests raved about it

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I found the meatballs flavorless, the sauce watery, and the amount of thyme overpowering. I love NYT cooking but this recipe was not for me.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I’m allergic (alpha-gal) to red meat.  Suggestions on how to get that bacon chew and taste without bacon?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Maple syrup, chicken skin/fat, smoked paprika?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Turkey bacon is worth a try.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Not possible despite many, many suggestions.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Chicken bacon, precooked, by Al Fresco actually tastes like pork bacon. Best when heated in skillet or microwave to get that crispy texture that crumbles. Allow to cool slightly before trying to crumble.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Thank you,Dixie,  the bacon sub! I will be using it in other dishes as well!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Possible to make this without dairy?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Sure, grind to a powder some oats and short grain rice with water (1 part oats, 3 parts rice and 90 parts water)  stirring constantly,  and cook until thickened and fold in!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Sorry, typo, 6 parts water and add more as needed once it begins to thicken.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night as written and agree it needs simplifying and revising. Too much wine and cream. Perhaps chicken stock to replace some of this. Also the meatballs were bland. I would add more flavor to the meat mix and broil them. Cook everything else in the skillet and add meatballs at the end. Not sure bacon is even necessary?? Just add more herbs (sage?)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: This works just as well with less fats, and without cream. I used just 1tsp olive oil to roast the shallots, and lean strips of bacon. Then sweated the mushrooms. Added the white wine, and thyme and reduced. I could have added chicken stock as well, for more depth. If too liquid in the end, use a bit of corn starch. And voilà! A lighter sauce but just as tasty. Cream is optional.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: If you ignore the meatball recipe (instead make a panade and season with whatever you like: I used a minced shallot, garlic, lots of umami seasoning, and plenty of fresh minced herbs) this is delicious. Only changes I made to the sauce were more umami, chicken broth, and starchy water from the egg noodles I served it with to make the most of the sauce. Would definitely make again with those changes but not as written. Served with garlicky wilted spinach and aforementioned egg noodles.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Generally speaking, I don't cook with cream and I think bacon in any dish is overpowering. I forwent the bacon and used olive oil instead. I substituted the cream with a cup of yogurt and a tablespoon of tomato paste. There were less steps involved, and it felt like a healthy meal. It was delicious!!!!!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I can't imagine depriving myself of bacon and heavy cream. I wouldn't call your results a healthy meal, I'd call it a punishment.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: My family really enjoyed this and it was pretty easy. I agree with the writer who said it is nothing like Coq au Vin but it is a great homage to those French essentials of butter, cream and thyme. I skipped the red pepper flakes which don’t feel French at all and doubled the thyme. I did not salt anything along the way as I was worried about it being too salty once everything was combined. Add the salt at the end and it was great. Great for the weeknight and sent it to my college daughter to try

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I read this recipe in the morning. No reviews yet but sounded tasty. I bought all the required ingredients and cooked a couple of days later. By then some reviews had appeared. Not great but I persevered with the recipe as written. Disappointing. The meatballs are bland and could use some seasoning independent of the sauce. The sauce was overwhelmed by the Dijon mustard at the end which should be cut in half and I like mustard. See Julia Child for coq au vin and chicken fricassee.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody:
Made a few shortcuts, added wine and heavy cream directly with the meatballs and onion mushroom, it reduced nicely in 8 minutes, served it with stripetti which does not have so much taste, really nice. Made whole strips of bacon, a whole one for each, and one in the sauce.
It turned out very well, served Riesling with the dish, same as I used for the cooking.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: We liked it very much and the cream didn't curdle. I had the heat down low and whisked it right away. The only change I made was I used unsalted butter. I see no point in buying a package of salted butter for just 2 tablespoons. I served the meatballs with brown rice to soak up the yummy sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: This was absolutely delicious. I made it to the "T" and wished I had doubled the sauce. The family laughed at me when I said I could bottle it and drink it, lol! Served with a lemony kale salad. This sauce kissed my salad on the plate, and bam! I now want to make this again to have the sauce as salad dressing! Definitely recommend!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I used store-bought frozen chicken meatballs to save time, and sauteed a whole yellow onion, thinly sliced, along with the garlic and shallots.  I waited to add the mushrooms until the onions were tender, so the onions and shallots were melted by the time I was through.  I’d leave out the red pepper next time, but it was otherwise a huge hit.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: This is really good. Just make it and enjoy it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I had a difficult time getting my chicken meatballs to look like meatballs. Maybe add a little more bread crumbs? It was a lovely dish to enjoy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Ok, I was super skeptical of this recipe given it’s from  Tieghan Gerard and I have…feelings. That said, I followed the recipe almost exactly as is (except I omitted the bacon) and it worked surprisingly well. The meatballs, although basic, assembled easily and the sauce infused everything with flavor. We served over buttered egg noodles and it was excellent.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I thought this was really good. My meatball mixture was too soft and sticky so I added more breadcrumbs let it absorb in the refrigerator for half an hour. Also, cooking the bacon in strips and THEN chopping it is so much easier and does no harm to the recipe. Next time I'll probably add some softened minced onion and/or mushrooms to the meatballs to give them a little more flavor and moisture, but this recipe is a great starting point. No leftovers!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: This was absolutely delicious, a wonderful weekend meal - took a little too long for a week night. The flavors were bold and complemented each other nicely. My only critique was that I didn't need the water added with the wine, it took too long to cook it off after. Added more salt and pepper than was called for, served with baguette for the sauce and the nyt fennel, apple and celery salad that was crisp and clean and a perfect complement to the warm, fatty flavors of this dish.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Excellent, rich, flavorful.  Make a double batch and freeze half... a wonderful to find in your freezer on a winter night.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I made this and used a chefs mixture of mushrooms and my husband really liked it.  I did not have ground chicken so I used ground veal and would make it again!  Turned out great!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Do not cook meatballs all the way through when you first sauté them.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful!I used turkey bacon and extra mushrooms.  Rreeeaally good...everyone in my family loved it-that's a rarity!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I did a riff on this, and followed commentator François's method. It became a different recipe where I used up leftover bits and bobs. I had frozen turkey meatballs, sauteed them in a little olive oil. I added sliced white button mushrooms,then shallots and garlic. I used vermouth and chicken stock. In went some frozen chopped spinach, cooked couscous, and the mustard. Finished with sour cream and some crumbled feta cheese. Delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: So good!  I didn't have bacon and it was still great.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Great flavors! I'm a long time fan of pork with cognac cream mustard sauce and apples and this was in that neighborhood. I do think the meatball was a little delicate for the turning...next time I will pan fry jus enough to get color and then finish in the oven.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: My husband and I really enjoyed this recipe, and the shiitake mushrooms really made the dish. I didn’t have bacon so I omitted it, and used full fat whole milk from the farmers market instead of cream. I did get some curdling in the beginning but whisked it out and it became smooth and thickened in the end, as per the recipe. The sauce was delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Excellent.  Made it 3 times now, always good.  I'd suggest making it as written first and then adjust to taste the next time -- despite all the changes noted in the reviews.  We (a group of 3-4) found the taste and texture of the meatballs to be excellent.  Used chicken each time, didn't have cream but used yogurt a few and whole milk one other.  With yogurt, let it cool down before whisking in -- even the whole milk curdles a bit, but with a little whisking and simmering, it smooths out.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: This has simple flavors and dish you can rely on to satisfy  your hunger. I simmered the white wine on medium low and added the heavy whipping cream (I used room temperature whipping cream) slowly and stirred for 3 minutes before I added the meatballs. Taste the sauce before you add the meatballs to see if you should add a pinch more salt or pepper. My sauce did not curdle and I used turkey instead of chicken.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Mastering the Art of French Cooking by Julia Child et al. has “Old-fashioned chicken fricassée with wine-flavored cream sauce, onions, and mushrooms (Fricassée de poulet à l’ancienne)”, which is essentially a white-wine / creamy variant related to coq au vin blanc.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: This was tasty, but I feel like it took too much time and had too many steps--the outome wasn't worth all the time invested. Also--my meatballs did NOT cook well in the pan--got stuck to it and left behind way too much meat. If I make this again, I'll bake the meatballs in the oven while I make the sauce. Also -- served with rice -- not a great pairing. Will try mashed potatoes or pasta if I make again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Made it, liked it. But an “easy skillet dinner” it was not. And that’s fine, just a big lift on a weeknight. It takes time because you are building the flavors, first the bacon, then the chicken, the mushrooms/garlic/shallots - the fond at the bottom when I deglazed the pot made quite a sauce, wine needs to reduce etc. My sauce also broke a bit (my fault, I subbed half and half for cream and it was just too hot when I added it). What I really liked was, except for the ground chicken, I had all the ingredients in my cabinet. Served it over cauliflower mash - which was pretty darn tasty. Nice change of pace for me.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody:
Wow this was delicious! Not sure I understand the complaints about this recipe. The bacon was such a small amount that it just lent a slight smokiness and blended into the overall taste...the butter was minimal and helped moisten the veggies...and nothing was curdled or overly saucy.
The only slight modification is that I didn't have scallions so used onion, and added a handful of grated onion to the meatballs and threw in a little garlic powder.
Will for sure make this again!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: This was meh at best

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I cheated and used premade Impossible meatballs, and it was SO GOOD. I skipped bacon because I don’t eat meat, threw in a few dashes of smoked paprika instead. I upped the garlic (because I always do), but otherwise stuck to the recipe. It’s definitely going in my make again folder!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Very involved, but a good recipe and fun if you have the time. I found there to be a lot of liquid at the end and added a bit of corn starch to help things thicken up, but made as written otherwise. Served with basic mashed potatoes, and it was quite good.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I was a bit underwhelmed with this dish. Thought it very bland as cooked. I think if I try it again I will use ground turkey.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely terrific recipe. It got rave reviews from my family. I didn't use the bacon as the wife doesn't eat pork or beef and it didn't seem to matter. Subbed portobellos for the shitakes and still great. Make sure to allow the wine/water stage to reduce enough. I rushed it and had to add salt at the end. Oh, and the heavy cream didn't curdle. And, we get it, some of you know a bit about French cooking. It says "blanc" in the recipe description for a reason. Get over it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Yummy, I skipped bacon and it was still good.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I was hoping for a lot more from this dish.  In my mind, I'd imagined all the great flavors that befit Coq au Vin, but in a weeknight version.  Sadly, this was more like a waste of wine than anything I'd attempt to make again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: A helpful kitchen tip... have Bread at hand in the kitchen because before you even serve the meatballs you'll be sopping up the sauce and eating it direct from the pan.  That said, the meatballs themselves were ok, but not special. Good but not great.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe....no curdle with cream addition...always helps to manage the temp. Delivered as described....wonderful with a pile of mashed potatoes and big salad !

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: I add a teaspoon or two of allspice and about a half teaspoon of clove to the meatballs. Adds some depth.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Maybe I’m crazy, but I swear I made this recipe a couple of months ago and it’s changed since? This came out really soupy and I definitely don’t remember dumping out the mushroom mixture or adding so much liquid (almost 3 cups between wine, water and cream). I’m also fairly certain I saved this recipe when I made it last time and this time I couldn’t find it in my saved recipes?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: The sauce was good, but the egg/breadcrumb binder was very noticeable in the texture of the meatballs. I didn’t care for that. The meatballs were a bit bland, as well. Don’t plan to make this again. Too much prep time for a dish that I found to be just so-so.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: This recipe has been panned by a few, but I thought it was delicious! The only things I changed: I sauteed a few mushrooms, a clove of garlic and a shallot then added to the meatball mix. I only used 1/4 c heavy cream and used 1 c chicken broth (replacing the cream and the water) and it was very good. My husband was over the moon! Added to my Go-To recipes.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: Really delicious sauce but I found I didn’t love the taste or texture of the chicken meatballs, would try again with beef meatballs.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Coq au Vin Blanc Meatballs Recipe
commentBody: This was one of the better New York Times recipes. Most of them I find need a lot of help to help labor and substance. Added parsley thyme and sage to the meatballs. I used chardonnay and one and a half cups of heavy cream no water. The meatballs were great. And the sauces to die for one of the best I've ever made. It was just outstanding.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: The recipe makes way too much frosting. You could cut in half. And double the coffee. I used instant decaf. But the cake itself is so moist and wonderful, you could drop the frosting all together!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I am wondering why we've all had such differing experiences. I cooked the recipe exactly, other than slightly extending the cooking time. Cake is great IMO - actually the texture I expected and wanted, not too dense but not light and cakey, either. Exactly the right amount of frosting, also, and no need for extra liquid at all.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Use three eggs and use 1/4 buttermilk with 1/4 coconut oil to get a less dense cake.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I made 12 cupcakes and with the leftover batter, I covered the bottom of a pie plate.  No frosting.  It's really good - reminiscent of ginger bread.  My variations:  1/4 olive oil plus 1/4 room temperature butter instead of coconut oil, 1 cup of AP flour plus 1/2 cup of ground hazelnuts for the flour, 1/2 t of kosher salt instead of Himalayan pink salt.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I read the recipe this morning and made it immediately. The cake was so dense. The toothpick came out clean, but when I cut it the cake was almost like a baked pudding.  There was too much frosting, too sweet. Very disappointed.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: As some suggested, I added an egg. I also subbed 1/2 the coconut oil for buttermilk and my cake is not dense. Also halved the icing and used fresh brewed espresso so tweaked the frosting to accommodate the extra liquid.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I made this cake yesterday and followed the directions exactly, though I did need to cook it a bit longer (a might just be my oven.)  I was uncertain how all the flavors would meld (coconut oil, cardamom and coffee?) but the sum total was fantastic, and it got rave reviews from everyone.  I will definitely make this again!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I made this just as listed re the ingredients. I used an 8" springform pan and Baker's Joy spray instead of the square pan with parchment, and it came out great. We found the icing too coffee-intense for our taste. I think a tsp of espresso powder (I used King Arthur brand) would be plenty, but I'll play it by ear next time.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
This cake is easy and great! It is wonderfully dense and rich, so don’t expect a pumpkin or banana bread texture.

Made as directed but with heaping measures of spices.
Baked in a 9” springform pan and was done in 25 minutes.

For the frosting, used 4 oz reduced fat cream cheese, 4 T. Butter, and a heaping cup of powdered sugar. Started with 1 t. espresso powder and added another 1/2 t.
The texture was perfect, no need for extra liquid.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
I thought this was quite good! Texture of a pumpkin bread or carrot cake. The frosting is delicious, I'll probably steal it for other cakes as well.

I'm allergic to coconut so followed some other commenters and replaced it with half olive oil, half buttermilk. Otherwise followed the recipe for the cake. It took a fair amount longer to bake than indicated but my oven may be running cold.

For the icing I dissolved some espresso powder in vanilla and then added to taste, so I think I did a bit less of both than the recipe called for. Excellent flavor though, next time I'll probably try adding closer to the recipe amounts (but would probably still start out with 1tsp espresso and go from there).

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Anyone try melted butter instead of melted coconut oil?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Great cake! Followed the recipe precisely. The only change I made was in prepping the pan: I used Baker’s Joy on an 8x8 square pan rather than deal with greasy, unwieldy parchment paper. I loved the frosting and found it a suitable amount. Then again, there are “frosting people” and “not frosting people” in this world, and I am frosting people. Frosting people are my people.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Not sure about this — the frosting tasted good, but it broke and looked really ugly. Perhaps my butter and cream cheese were too soft? After reading all the comments I feel like there are better pumpkin recipes out there, but hopefully if I try the frosting again it will work better. I like the idea of coffee icing on pumpkin cake/bread.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: The frosting recipe is missing milk or cream.  It requires some liquid in addition to the vanilla.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: You are alone (twice!) in saying that the recipe is missing liquid. I note that you do not say the frosting is missing liquid, so I'm assuming you are not speaking from experience. I and other readers did not find that additional liquid was needed for the frosting. One reader did add espresso to the frosting and modified to accommodate the "extra" liquid.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Oops! My mistake. The egg/flour ratio is the same as the NYT brown butter pumpkin bread recipe. The difference is that this recipe uses twice as much pumpkin.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This is possibly the best cake recipe I’ve ever made. It’s light and fluffy but also has some good weight to it. The spices come through wonderfully (I subbed pumpkin pie spice for the cardamom and ginger), and it’s not too sweet. I did not make this particular icing this time but will next time!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
So far, people are complaining that the texture is too solid. The “pumpkin bread with brown butter and bourbon” on this site uses the same amount of fat (but it’s butter not oil) and a similar amount of flour, but has four eggs instead of two. 

I think I’ll use the other cake recipe, but use this frosting!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: We liked this cake. I used King Arthur espresso powder in the frosting. If you don't want to buy salted butter, you can use a stick of unsalted butter and 1/4 teaspoon of salt.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
Cake came out dense and flavorless. Frosting came out WAY too sweet. I was hoping they would balance each other out when put together, but they did not. The taste of the frosting overwhelmed the cake. I like the concept, though. So I am going to try again, and here are some adjustments I think would help:

- double the sugar and spices in the cake batter
- an additional egg and/or leavening agent in the cake batter
- a tangy cream cheese-coffee frosting instead of buttercream

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: The texture of this cake reminds me of sweet potato pone. As long as you're not expecting a light cake, it tastes fine. Could use extra spice and extra coffee. I made 3/4 the frosting and it was still too much for an 8 inch single layer!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
I made a Frankenstein cake that turned out fantastic- the espresso pumpkin combo is quite delicious…. The nyt cooking Sheet Pan Pumpkin cake is a great recipe- this pumpkin cake is too claggy (in the GBBO sense of the word) imo. I changed the butter to cream cheese ratio to make it more cc heavy, so half a stick of butter and 4 ounces cc. 

Also- I have found decaf instant espresso at the store- helpful hint if you are looking to give this to kids or anyone sensitive to caffeine

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This recipe makes a dense, moist pumpkin cake, similar to pumpkin bread. The icing is mind-blowing, and, in my opinion, just the right amount. I immediately started thinking of other things I might put the icing on-- chocolate cake? Brownies? Honestly, I could just eat it with a spoon.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @Pamela agree this frosting is divine and I too think it would be wonderful with Nigella’s chocolate cake

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I needed to increase the baking time by a good ten minutes.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This was a five-star recipe for me. I loved the squishy, dense texture of the cake & the frosting was excellent. We had extra too so we just froze the leftover frosting and used it on something else later. Very rich & moist & flavorful!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I was surprised, with all the spices in this cake, how it turned out so bland for me.  Soft, moist, but so, so bland.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Doubled the recipe and baked in two 9in round pans. had no issues. Cake was very dense and moist! Kept it in the oven for a little over 25 minutes. Both the cake and the icing were very good however I wasn’t the biggest fan of the two together (everyone else loved it!) next time I would  probably do the pumpkin cake with a regular cream cheese icing.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, dense moist cake, super satisfying. Made with gluten free cup-for-cup flour with no problems. Definitely would make again. I eased up on the amount of frosting, subbed a bit of greek yogurt for tang as we had no  cream cheese. The tanginess helped cut the sweetness very nicely.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I also had to add about 10-12 minutes to the cooking time. I agree that the level of spice is mild, so when I made the cake again (because it’s SO good), I heaped my spoons with the spices and the flavor came through so much nicer in the 2nd cake.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: So good!! I did make some adjustments as per the comments— added one more egg, subbed half the coconut oil with yogurt (didn’t have buttermilk or milk), and almost doubled the spices. In the frosting, I reduced the sugar to just one cup, added a little salt, and used just 1 tsp espresso powder, 1 tsp decaf instant coffee. Baked 10 min longer (40 min), still came out underdone in the center.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I would love to make this cake but live outside the US, where canned pumpkin purée does not exist. Has anyone tried making this with fresh pureed pumpkin? I imagine the latter would be much more watery, and wondering if I just need to resign myself to my potential experiment not working.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: You just have to roast the pumpkin in the oven before you purée it - it should work perfectly!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @C. I did this (didn’t roast, cooked a kabocha squash in the instant pot) and it turned out perfectly

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
I made this as-is and it was GREAT! What a fun and sturdy little cake. As many others have noted I baked it for 5-10 extra minutes. My other notes (mostly for me—you’re all expert enough to know these things) are:
-the butter (for the frosting) really needs to be at room temp or blobs form
-let the cake cool COMPLETELY completely or the frosting wilts ;)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I baked this recipe according to the instructions. When it came out of the oven the toothpick came out clean. I let it cool and made the frosting recipe. The frosting was extremely loose and pourable. The cake was underbaked and pudding-like on the inside. I will be recreating this recipe and making altercations the next time I’m craving a pumpkin spice cake.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Followed directions to a T. The volunteers at the food pantry loved every bite. (I’ve made this three times now and it has turned our lovely each time.)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I chopped up a Trader Joe's Dark Chocolate Coffee Buzz Bar and added to the batter. It paired fantastically with the coffee frosting.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: We really liked this cake! Two things to remember - do NOT put the cake in the fridge! This changes the texture of the cake from dense to unpleasantly thick.  Only put the frosting on when you're about to serve, or the pieces you intend to eat. (When you put the frosting on top, you have to refrigerate it.) The frosting is AWESOME!!!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Can you substitute extra virgin olive oil for the coconut oil? Would it not come out right?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
It could affect the taste, since both olive and coconut oils have distinctive tastes. I think the coconut enhances the sweetness of the pumpkin, while the olive would come through with its own taste. If I was going to sub another oil for coconut, I'd use a neutral tasting oil rather than olive oil.

Made this and had only 29 oz cans of pumpkin, so I froze the leftover canned pumpkin in tablespoon sized dollops on a cookie sheet.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: The frosting recipe needs liquid. Such as milk OT cream.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This was a simple recipe to follow and we found the cake delicious but the coffee frosting was over powering. Next time I would make it with a maple cream cheese frosting.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: It needed about 35 to 40 minutes to bake, maybe because I used fresh pumpkin. With half the quantity of frosting, it’s delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Just took it out of the oven after 35 minutes. Looks set but when i tried to lift it out of the pan, it fell apart. Suggest adding "until toothpick comes out clean" to the directions. I'm putting it back in the oven. Sigh.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I upped the spice measurements by about 1.5x for personal preference but otherwise made the cake recipe to a T in a glass 8 inch square pan and it came out AMAZING! Perfect combination of density, moisture, and flavor. The frosting tasted amazing but turned out a little more runny and less fluffy than I’d like. Next time I might add more powdered sugar or use less butter to get a fluffier texture.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Fluffy and quick. Made without the frosting, but I don't think it would have bumped up the recipe from 3 to 4+ stars. The sort of thing I would make again to bring to a bake sale - because it is fine - but not actually keep for myself

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Definitely a keeper. It's a moist, dense spice cake ... not as dense as I thought it would be based on comments. I halved the frosting, but will do the full amount next time, a maximalist topping to a cake that works even without the frosting. It also needed way more bake time.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This is a lovely cake. I didn't have cream cheese for the frosting, so added a tablespoon of Kahlua. It freezes well.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I made this today and everyone loved it! It was delicious! I added an additional egg as others suggested. For the frosting, I used half of what is listed, and it was more than enough. The cake is good without frosting too!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Had to bake the shortbread closer to 30 minutes, but this was delicious! Came out great. Yes, this will make a little excess whipped cream, so be careful if you end up absent-mindedly eating the extra like me and getting a stomachache!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This was just okay. A little dense for me, and mine was still undercooked after 30 minutes. I liked the coffee pairing with the pumpkin, but I wished it were lighter texture.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Made this with the extra egg and half the coconut oil and it was kind of fluffy. Will be making this cake again absolutely loved it!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: This was wonderful. I was amazed at how complimentary the coffee was to the pumpkin flavor. It will become a Fall classic in this house.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Hi—would these work as cupcakes?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Made it with half olive oil, half butter instead of coconut oil and halved the frosting as suggested. It was plenty for an 8 inch cake. It came out well, but I think the recipe meant for us to dissolve espresso powder in a tbsp of water. It’s grainy if you don’t and you still need a dribble of water to hold the frosting together. It was fine though, good espresso taste.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Do you think it would work in a Bundt cake pan?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: You would have to double the recipe. It's not a lot of batter.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I added dark chocolate and it goes well with the pumpkin. The cake has a nice blend of spices and the coffee icing is the perfect topper.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Definitely, definitely needs more baking time. 45 minutes bare minimum. I might say ramp up the spices as well.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: Lovely taste but too dense. As others suggested, might try adding an extra egg next time to get a more spongy outcome hopefully. The buttercream is the real star in any case, it pays to be generous with the amount of coffee you add.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody:
Made the recipe as written (relied on the grams) and was delicious ! My instant coffee didn't melt into the frosting at first but we let it rest a few minutes and it worked out perfectly.

We did the cake in a 9'' pan so it's not as high as originally intended, but still good. The cake is a dense cake, but nothing too heavy, just not a fluffy cake. Wouldn't switch any ingredients.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: @C. yes, it will be watery so you'll want to bake, puree, then drain the pumpkin

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I’m usually way too lazy to ice cakes and I don’t need the sweetness, but this icing had a perfect latte taste so I’m thrilled to have tried it. (I used decaf instant Nespresso gold.) The cake also retained the pumpkin flavor which tasted great with the latte. Many pumpkin dessert recipes drown out the pumpkin with spice, but that didn’t happen in this cake.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Pumpkin Spice Latte Cake With Coffee Frosting Recipe
commentBody: I substituted butter for coconut oil because I don’t like the taste. The toothpick came out clean at 25 minutes but the cake was nowhere near finished. I keep testing it but if it isn’t good after the next check, I’ll dump the cake and make chocolate cupcakes for the coffee frosting which I added more coffee too. So disappointed.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Don’t be fooled by the small number of ingredients. Achieving a creamy, clump-free cacio e peoe is nearly impossible due to the extremely narrow temperature range  required for success, Fortunately there is an easy trick. Whisk together a cup of water and perhaps a Tbs of cornstarch and heat until thickened. Then add your pasta water and cheese and you will get a perfect emulsion every time. Yes, I suppose it’s cheating, but my understanding is that even restaurants do this.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Measuring cheese in a cup is a fools errand.  There is just too much variation in graters. Then how loosely do you pack it?  Just weigh the cheese.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody:
This dish has a tendency to seize up and become a big mess. 

I suggest googling Ethan Chlebowski's video for his foolproof, scientific preparation method. It's been successful for me both times I've made it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I am in Italy now, and have been here for quite some time. Here, they use Pecorino semi - Stagianato  unlike the rock-hard pecorino sold in the US (very aged). Garnish with Romano. The softer, younger pecorino doesn't require all this laboratory work to melt - and stay perfectly melted.  Aged cheeses do not want to melt or emulsify.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Take the short container of Locatelli from the store and two tablespoons freshly ground pepper, dump it over the freshly drained pasta, stir with tongs and add water if necessary.   Serve with the bottle of Primitivo you've already been drinking because of how easy it is to make this. And done.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Love the ingredient list/proportions like all get out....as for technique my preference is to follow Deb Perelman's method of creating a thick cheese/pepper paste with room temperature water.  This can be done hours in advance.  The hot pasta gets the paste dollopped over the surface. Tossing lets the cheese melt uniformly, with added amounts of the starchy pasta water added as needed, until achieving the most glorious Cacio EVER!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I hope the author reads this. Many of us have been cooking from the Food Section multiple times a week for years because almost without exception the recipes are foolproof. Here's one that obviously isn't as evidenced by the sad accounts of wasted expensive cheese.  It's fun to read about traveling chef's darting from the airport to the hottest destination restaurant and I appreciate how generations of a culinary family have perfected this dish but give us Jeff's failsafe technique.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Grate the cheese with a microplane for the fluffiness the recipe requires and you'll find the numbers do add up.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: This may be heresy, but here's what I do: stir the pecorino and pepper together in a large glass bowl. Once the pasta has been boiling for a few minutes, take some of the water and add it to the cheese/pepper mixture, just enough to get it to a paste. Drain the pasta, pour it onto the cheese paste, stir it up with a wooden spoon, and serve. The key is a finely grated pecorino. I get a good melt, and it's delicious and super easy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Yes. I agree. I did a variant of this last night.I did stir the stuff together with a fork, and waited until I could see the starch in the water (a benefit of using a skillet to cook it). The starch of the pasta matters. Then with water remaining I put it in. No gloppiness at all.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Your method is the same method as this recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: No salt in water makes for very bland noodles..  cheese alone can't be sufficient to make it salty enough.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: The recipe serves 4-6, which means each person is getting 2-3 oz of cheese, plus whatever they sprinkle on at the table. That is PLENTY of salt.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: When making this dish, I mix my pecorino and black pepper in a separate bowl.  I fill a large bowl, large enough for the pasta and mixing, with boiling water.  Once the pasta is done cooking and drained, I empty the bowl with boiling water and add in the pasta.  I then slowly add in the pecorino and black pepper mixture tossing all the while to making sure the pasta evenly coated.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: A cup and a half of water is WAY WAY  too much. I made Cacio e pépé soup.  Not a smooth sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Where id you come up with cup and a half of water? Recipe calls for 1/2 a cup and then used only as needed.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: @Gerry: Step 3 calls for 1-1/2 cups of water; Step 5 calls for 1/2 cup.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: In the original recipe, you only use Pecorino. Parmigiano is a foreign ingredient (being from Parma). It is clear though that adding it makes much more easy to prepare since Pecorino has an irresistible tendency to clump.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody:
I made this dish using the recipe printed with the article on the Roscioli food empire and took the cooked past out of the water with tongs. What a mess: the sauce never clung to the pasta
and ended up in gluey webs.
To make a successful cacio e pepe pasta: drain the cooked pasta in a strainer and dry in a clean
dish towel and THEN add it to the sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody:
As this recipe was published to illustrate a "tip" for a smooth pecorino cheese sauce I would rely on food science and add just a small bit of starch to the water. The starch can be in the pasta water (which may not be sufficient depending on the volume of the water used), or a bit of potato starch, corn starch, or even a small amount of flour dusted over the grated cheese.
This prevents the proteins from binding into long, gloppy strands and the fats from separating out and pooling.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Converting oz to cups of grated cheese depends entirely on how finely grated it is. With a fine rasp grater, one gets much more volume than with a coarse shredding.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: But weight remains constant whether a solid chunk or grated. Volumes would be different.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Yes, I’m one of those who didn’t make this so ignore if you choose. I have used Martha Stewart’s technique for this and have never had anything but excellent results. In her write up she specifically calls for Grana Padano because its softer and has non clumping qualities when stirred into a starchy/water /butter mixture in the pan. Pecorino Romano is added at end as well as cracked pepper and lemon zest. So is it authentic? beats me, but tastes fantastic, seems much easier & a no failure rate.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely agree with Jeff below about Deb Perelman's (Smitten Kitchen) method. Add room temp water (only a bit) to grated pecorino and, with an immersion blender, make a smooth cheese paste. Toss with hot pasta. Perfect, foolproof, simple, and delicious every time. It's absolutely the best (I'd say the only) way to make Cacio e Pepe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I just watched this being made at Rascioli in Rome on CBS's "The Dish," where the chef blended the pepper, cheeses and water in a jar using an immersion blender! :)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Agreed that three ingredients do not an easy recipe make! Making a paste with lukewarm water beforehand is now my go to step!!! Thanks to all for sharing how tender Pecorino can be!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I am so disappointed in this recipe that I am moved to actually post a note.  If the temperature “window” for making this recipe successfully is so narrow to avoid the cheese seizing up - as noted by another person - then the recipe is seriously deficient in not specifying the temperature needed for success.  I’ve now wasted $25 of cheese for this disgusting outcome.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Go to YouTube and watch Alex, the French guy, make this. After that, watch him make an omelet with Jacques Pepin, and Carbonara with Luciano Monosilio in Rome.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Somewhat counterintuitively I’ve found that not using a microplane and grating the pecorino on a box grater (think like store bought shredded cheddar cheese thick) helps avoid clumping. It makes the cheese melt more slowly which I think helps give it more time to emulsify before clumping up.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: This is the first time a Times recipe (and I’ve made many) has failed me, and it did so catastrophically. Block of clumped cheese in cloudy water. Apparently the temperatures required are VERY delicate and precise, the texture of the cheese critically important. Why not TELL us that?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: This was a total failure. My cheese mixture started clumping before the pasta was even done cooking. I knew to mind the heat and kept it super low. The cheese turned into an inedible glob that was tinted purple by the peppercorns.  I didn’t even want to feed it to the dog. Back to YouTube I go.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: The cheese proteins will often do this under any heat. You might consider adding a pinch of corn starch or potato starch (mix into the grated cheese). This helps keep the proteins from clumping. A small amount of acid also can help.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I think part of the issue is that you don't need to prep the cheese sauce BEFORE the pasta is ready, since it only takes 2-3 minutes to do it. If you try to get the cheese sauce right before the pasta even starts, it's going to be impossible to maintain the consistency you want (if you do get it...) while the pasta is cooking.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: It absolutely depends on the brand of pecorino and parmigiano, your grater, the temperature of the pasta water (did it sit for one minute or a minute and ten seconds?), and so many other things. The headline "How to Make a Perfect Cacio e Pepe, Every Time" (written by Sifton himself? who knows...) is a red herring. Cacio e pepe is an art. It takes lots of pain to get right. Embrace it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: OMG  i tried to make this and it was an utter fail!  I have been cooking for my family for 40 years.  i try new recipes every week.  this sounded so good and i followed every direction. ended up with a glopy disgusting mess that i threw in the trash and heated up a jar of sauce for dinner!  very disappointed!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I have the same issue with this recipe as I do of several of the Times recipes. Short on specific details. More photos, and more precise process instructions would increase people's chance of success, at the cost of making the recipes a little less sexy. What would be sexy for me would be having a recipe turn out great- which did not happen here. My cheese clumped into a hard puck and while my family reluctantly ate what I could salvage out of it, we ended up nicknaming the dish Clumpio e Pepe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: This is a regular weeknight dinner for us and we use a food processor to grate the cheese for several patches at a time.   We store it in weighed bags in the freezer.  Sometimes we add small pieces of smoked chicken and a touch of garlic.  I like less pepper so hubby adds more to his plate.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: What a disaster!  As soon as I added the 1 1/2 cups of warm water to the cheese, all of the cheese clumped into one big ball.  Nothing I tried worked to smooth it out.  I usually have such good luck with NYT recipes and I'm disappointed.  Looks like some folks had good ways of making this work.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody:
After 1 clumping mess I thought to combine the peccorino with some pasta water in my Cuisinart smart stick jar. 
1) boil water in a 2 quart pot, add pasta, dried for 8 minutes, fresh for 3 or 4 minutes uses.
2) drain pasta, reserving a cup of pasta water, and put into a heated bowl. I used pasta water, but hot tap water will do. 
3) using smart stick combine peccorino and pepper with enough reserved pasta water to make a "crema"
4) add crema to pasta, mix, add more pepper, possibly  and serve.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: On CBS's "The Dish" the chef at Roscioli in Rome blended the pepper, cheeses and water in a jar using an immersion blender, so you're spot on!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Holy OOEY GOOEY mess, Batman! The clumped cheese was coagulated enough to be added to the space shuttle for glue. I was so disappointed because not only did I buy a brand new mortar and pestle, successfully crushing the peppercorns to bits, but my careful grating of the pecorino Romano to a “fine texture” seemed to follow the recipe. And follow it, I did. But alas, the cheese was not having it…it wanted to make a beefy glob instead of melting beautifully into a sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Like so many before me, cheese clumped badly and way too much pepper. The hunt continues...

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody:
"When the sauce is thick and forms strings like glue, it is ready." 

No, that means it's ruined, not ready.  The water can't be so hot that the cheese gets stringy.  My tip (among the hundreds other ones here) is to scoop out (approx) a cup of starchy pasta water a few minutes before the pasta is ready and let it come to room temperature.  Have the cheese paste ready to mix with the drained pasta.  Then, add the pasta water a tablespoon at a time, but NOT right out of the boiling pasta pot.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody:
Once upon a time there was a foolish husband who thought cooking a Cacio e Pepe for his pregnant wife would be nice; it would remind her of their honeymoon. But instead of preparing a lovely dish to eat on the porch, he cook a monstrous wet clumpy heap that obliterated their cheese supplies and neither of them could eat. 

This recipe is not for inexperienced cooks.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody:
In step three it’s really important to very gradually add the cheeses to the water and I did not do this when I made it last night, but I will do it next time. I did get clumping strings of cheese because of incomplete emulsification.

Bring the water to a simmer and then whisk in the cheeses and pepper slowly over low heat in order to get the emulsification needed. I might add a little milk to the water.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Try like this: 0.00: throw bronze-drawn spaghetti in a little boiling water with a little salt;1’: incorporate 15-20 cc cooking water/35 g pecorino romano to form a sort of "ricotta"; 2’: grind pepper in a non-stick pan and heat; 4’: put spaghetti in the pan with the pepper and continue cooking as if they were a "risotto"; 12’-13’: remove the pot from the heat when spaghetti are still very moist and progressively incorporate the pecorino by shacking them with energy until it forms the cream…

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody:
The thing that kills it is adding too much - i.e., virtually ANY - water after you've already combined the pasta with the "pecorino cream." 

First, let the pepper toasting skillet cool almost completely before attempting to make the sauce. Whisk the cheese, pepper and water vigorously, and don't overheat. At most, kind of wave the pan over the burner. THIS is the stage at which it's almost safe to add a little more water, in hopes of avoiding a too-dry product in the end.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I wanted to believe this is the “fool-proof” recipe it promised to be. I love Julia Moskin. Sadly, it’s not as promised. I’m a half-decent cook, but I followed the steps exactly as described, but ended up with clumps. Perhaps I need specific temps to make this work?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I don’t think I will ever try to make this at home after having it in Rome. My sister and I were there in May and her friend recommended we eat at Il Ciociaro a family owned restaurant. They serve it piping hot Table side tossed inside the wheel of cheese! Everything we had at this “ mom and pop” restaurant was divine, but you really cannot improve on their Cacio e Pepe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: This recipe couldn't NOT have gone worse for me. The cheese/water mixture clumped into a ball and was inedible. I tried smoothing it out with cream, but then it was gritty/gooey and just plain awful. This is the second cheese/water preparation I've tried, and will never do it again!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Well, I wish I had read the comments before trying to make this.  It tasted good, but it certainly wasn't creamy and it made a complete mess of the pan.  I will test some of the ideas below next time to see if I have better luck!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: A major fail for me.  I think adding the water was a mistake, turned the cheese into rock hard dumplings.  Just not good and I love cacio e pepe.  There are much better recipes out there.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: TRUST THE PROCESS! That’s the only thing missing from this “recipe.” At first I thought, “oh no. Here we go again. Another watery, clumpy mess.” Just keep the heat LOW, and keep stirring. It will happen. If you trust the process, you will have a very authentic cacio e pepe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I find good success tossing the cheese onto cooked pasta, then adding pasta water, and using tongs to stir the pasta as it heats back up.  I try to cook the pasta in so little water that by the time it's cooked there are only a few tablespoons water left.  So very starchy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Quarter the amount of peppercorns, this is far too much. I made the mistake of following the recipe to the letter and the result was almost inedible.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: In Step 3, is the pot for the cheese and water still on high heat?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody:
terrible recipe
all the cheese clumps in one big ball

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I definitely would eat at a Roscioli family restaurant, but I don't think I would use this recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I agree that Ethan Chlebowski’s YouTube video on the science of making cacio e pepe is very helpful.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Having read the comments and tips .. I’m going to look for a restaurant that serves it!  And send it back if it’s too watery, salty or clumpy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Not salting the pasta water?  I'd love to know what other restaurants in the area think of this.  Place sounds like a tourist trap, but for chefs.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: What's a salumeria, please?  I couldn't find it in my dictionaries .... a salami shop?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Not hard to find on the World Wide Web: "A Salumeria is defined as a cured meat shop or Italian deli. Salumeria features many different kinds of cured Italian meats such as sausage, salami, prosciutto, honey ham, and many other cold cuts."

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: To those who want to cut recipes in half because leftovers are not possible, when making, save another cup of your pasta water, put in fridge. Pour into small skillet , add pasta, stir, add a bit more cheese, no problem. Is it as good as first night, who knows, tastes great and is gone next day.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I think it turn out ok, but if I were to make it again I’d cut the pepper down to around 1 to 1.5 tbsp and probably use all pecorino romano with the parm saved just for serving.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Pure folly! Ended up with a glob of melted cheese floating in water. All was fine until I placed the nonstick pan over low heat. Should have read notes first, but for some reason jumped in and what an uneatable gooey mess.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Wow.  Dreadful results.  I'll remember to leave this one to the folks with enough empty evenings on their hands to master it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Did you ever try to put the cheese and the pepper directly on the pasta?  Why do we need all this water?  A bit of olive oil or butter and it is delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: 100% agree. I'm never trying the "magic" cheese/water preparation again. When I next go to Italy, I'll let them do it this way. ;-)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Cooking the pasta in unsalted water is so inconsistent w/all the other recipes for the Roman Big 4 pasta dishes, this seems wrong. In my experience, pasta cooked in unsalted water always tastes like it's missing something, no matter how much salt or sauce you use in the finished dish.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: This recipe calls for 11 ounces of HIGH SALT cheese. It doesn't need more salt, unless you've been smoking a pack of cigarettes a day for the past two decades.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: The point of cacao e pepe is that it's a quick and easy, one-pan dish. Indeed, José Andrés's vegetable cookbook includes a version created by his daughter in college that's made in the microwave in a pyrex measuring cup. I've never had issues with lumps, no matter how tipsy I am when I put it together. This is unnecessarily complicated, IMHO.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I learned to make traditional Cacio e Pepe from YouTube video by Chef Riccardo Camanini. The technique is not difficult with just a little practice the Cacio e Pepe is classically elegant. It doesn't seem like weblinks can be posted in these comments so just Google: "Cacio e Pepe by Riccardo Camanini: Through The Eyes of The Chef" and see what you think. Highly recommended.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Potentially great. But I had two problems. I ended up with little tiny blobs of romano in a creamy sauce, rather than just a creamy sauce. Maybe not grated fine enough? Also, I will reduce the pepper next time. It was overwhelming.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Disappointing. Followed the recipe precisely. Two issues, I think: a cup and a half of water is too much, and the cheese/water mixture is on low heat for too long; it begins to separate and results in gloppy chunks of cheese in a watery broth. Tasted good, but not what I had planned.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: OK. I’ll be pilloried for this, but it was WAY too much cheese sauce. Otherwise tasty (and very easy as written), but too much sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody:
Apparently, my skills are not up to my skill level. I followed the instructions faithfully and ended up with pasta sitting in the pan with clumps of melted cheese. Just like the recipe said it would if I didn't drain the pasta well enough. Except I did - there wasn't a lick of water left on it when I did the transfer.

I'm sorry, water aside, dumping hot pasta on poorly rendered cheese (Step 3) results in blobs. Not in the "squeeze a drop between your fingers" result described.

My mistake.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Made this as directed tonight without any alterations. The biggest thing to focus on: not adding any water whatsoever hotter than 145 degrees Fahrenheit. I added hot tap water to the grated cheese and peppercorn (the hottest our tap gets is 120), then moved that over to the skillet on the lowest setting on our gas stove stop. Reserved water as directed (reserved a cup, used 2/3) but made sure reserved water temp was between 140 - 145. First bites smooth, last bites a lil stringy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Recipe was a total fail. As others have noted, the sauce seizes up.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: There are so many types of pecorino! I dont think it’s necessary or authentic to use parmiggiano or anything other than pecorino from sheep’s milk; “cacio” means sheep. The best to use for cacio e pepe is Pecorino Sardo; from Sardinia, where pecorino originated. Even though this is a Roman dish, the younger softer but still firm and not as sharp Sardo works better, as opposed to Pecorino Romano which is more aged, firmer, sharper and MUCH harder to melt; better for garnishing on the plate.

author: Julia Moskin
