recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Cacio does not mean sheep in Italian, it means cheese in some dialects. Sheep in Italian is pecora. And it is indeed authentic to use both cheeses in Rome.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Other than what Ren H has pointed out, I feel I should mention that Pecorino Romano, the one the origin of this recipe mandates, originated in Rome proper and in Lazio, the Italian region where Rome is located. It was only when the growth of the Romano industry made the land available for proper sheep grazing in this region insufficient, that the industry partially moved to Sardinia. Those producers still make Pecorino Romano in the island, which is quite different from the local Pecorino Sardo.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: A  bad recipe  Water in the cheese turned it to rubber, it had potential but in the end it hit the bin    Such a shame.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I would love to see a video of this recipe to see what it takes to make it work. My husband makes cacio e pepe and carbonara regularly. They always come out perfectly emulsified, creamy. He decided to try the method in this recipe and it was a complete disaster.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I cut the amount of cheese in half and made about 3/4 lb of Spaghetti.  It’s still way too much cheese. The good thing about the recipe is that using warm water prevents the cheese from clumping. Next time, less cheese!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Would sodium citrate help the melting? It works for Mac and cheese.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: The ancient Romans did not have sodium citrate. They just figured it out.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Horrible recipe…either not tested or not enough info to make it.  Needs more info on how to make “crema”….. following instructions produced a clump of cheese…ughh

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: We were very excited to eat this dish, but two tablespoons of peppercorns does seem very excessive. Our mouths and stomachs were on fire after eating this, and we could not finish. Please reconsider when publishing this kind of recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: A cup and a quarter of water added to the cheese makes a pecorino soup not a paste.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I am a big fan of NY Times Cooking.  This, however, was an utter disaster for me and a sad waste of pricey cheese.  I will seek out another method.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Even when you follow the instructions precisely, the cheese tends to clump together.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Like others here I was not able to achieve desired consistency in the cream.  1.5 cups seems like way too much water compared to what I’ve since watched online.  I was able to get the cheese clump to melt down once added to the pasta but required some rigorous tossing and stirring.  Pasta took the brunt of this unfortunately.  Tasted great and like the touch with the toasted peppercorns, but presentation was not what I hoped.  Thanks to all who posted recommendations here.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: become clumpy. It tasted great, but the cheese clumped up and was chewy. Read comments for suggestions.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Cheeses are much too strong here. The Casio a Pepe that I’ve had have more of a subtle cheese flavor and are delicious and scrumptious. This is a dish where the cheeses over run the pasta. Don’t know Roscioli would feel about you saying this is their recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Don't know how/why you think you know that this is not the Roscioli recipe. The accompanying article explains that the family has had to adapt their recipe to cheeses that are available in the United States.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: The Perfect Cacio e Pepe Recipe, According to Science https://nytimes.com/2025/01/24/science/cacio-e-pepe-recipe-corn-starch.html?smid=nytcore-android-share

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Here is the best recipe…6 tablespoons of virgin olive oil, 2 cloves of garlic cut up, 1 1/2 of freshly crushed peppercorns, 1/2 teaspoons of crushed red pepper, 3/4 cup of Parmesan, 3/4 cup of pecorino Romano. 1 lb of bucatini. When pasta is cooking sauté the garlic, pepper (red and peppercorns). When el dente, transfer the pasta to the sauce, sprinkling in the cheese and toss and toss. Put the pasta in a bowl so the sauce on the bottom of the pan goes on the top. Do NOT use extra pasta water.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody:
When  my mom was ill, my dad (both were from Italy) would prepare the spaghetti.
heat up some  olive oil (sometimes with a little  butter) add some garlic and black pepper
 and serve it  up with some grated Parmesan   cheese on top. Badda Bing badda bomm!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody:
The Milk Street approach has worked well for me: use a large shallow pan to heat a couple cups of water (only 2-3 cups, not a large pot full as usual for pasta!). Once boiling add the pasta laying flat on the bottom of the large pan. Using tongs or spatula, keep moving the pasta in the shallow boiling water.  About 3-4 minutes in, begin adding some of the finely grated cheese and ground pepper. 

The objective is that the pasta absorb the most of the liquid; the rest is the sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: The way we found to avoid clumps is pretty easy.  We grate the cheese a couple of hours ahead of time and let it sit out to warm to room temperature.  Set aside a cup of water, too, so it gets to room temp.  While the pasta is cooking, start working an immersion blender on the room temp cheese and gradually add the water until the cheese becomes a thick creamy sauce. Toss the pasta with pepper broth first, then add the thick cheese sauce and toss like a madman.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: The quality of the pasta is very important. You need to use an artisanal pasta, probably from Italy. The box white pasta on supermarket shelf’s in US does not have the starch content to make this work.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Yeah it does.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Surprised the author didn’t mention the article from this spring—Italian food scientists showed that cornstarch acts as a molecular buffer, forming a gel network that keeps cheese from clumping. Just a teaspoon can make the sauce silky and stable. Science wins again!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I work hard on this and it was a big dud! I’m a pretty good cook, bought expensive ingredients at the Italian speciality store- followed the receipt but the cheese did not emulsify. It was like glue- or more like mozzarella on a pizza. Clumps in the pan around the pasta. I have a lot left over and would like to somehow salvage it. Any tips are appreciated. But I think this goose is cooked!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Ugh I should have read the comments first as this is indeed a very tricky recipe and my result mostly went down the drain. The sauce was a clumpy mess, and it would be useful for the recipe to arrive with a warning that this is a deceptively tricky endeavor.  Never again!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: In my experience, 1 ounce (weight) of grated pecorino equates to roughly 1/3 cup (volume).

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: If you’re having difficulty with the ounces to cups ratio, Google it. All kinds of conversion measurements are available if you just use the right language when you Google. Try it. It’s easier than worrying about your measurements.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody:
Weigh the cheese like they tell you.
Put the blade on the bottom of food processor then spin it up and toss in the cheese chunks a few at a time. Don’t worry about the volume, the weight of the intact cheese chunks and the ground cheese is the same.

Add some sodium citrate, aka melting salt. The correct weight is googleable. Mix it in and also the cracked pepper.

Cook the pasta in less water than normal. Spoon a little pasta water into the cheese and it will make a cream smooth sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: So what size is “a very large nonstick skillet or pot” how frustratingly unclear. 12” skillet? 6 qt pot?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Can I successfully cut the recipe in half? I take it that ‘leftovers’ are not going to be possible to eat.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody:
Cooking for only two people, can I successfully cut this recipe in half?  
I take it that ‘leftovers’ are not going to be possible.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Love this recipe , Anyone has a good gluten free brand pasta ? cheers

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I really like the Jovial brand.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: How much cream?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: None, the cheeses make the sauce creamy

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I cook the pasta in a much smaller amount of water so you have starchy water at the end to use. I put the almost done pasta in the frying pan with the warm pepper and some water to finish cooking. I then add the pasta to the cheeses that have been made into a loose paste in a bowl and toss quickly. Seems to work for me.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I tried this and it clumped up. I now make sure I shake the pasta free of all water after cooking and then toss them in a mix of grated cheese, salt, pepper and about 1/4 cup of cream that I have heated up and mixed in advance.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I love cacio e pepe & as others have noted, it is very difficult to get right. I followed this recipe TO THE T (micro grating & weighing the cheese; using the timer; low heat on the cream mixture). And it still ended up gluey. At the time I added the pasta, the cream sauce was part liquid, part melted cheese. I tossed the pasta for at least 5 minutes (I wish I had not used fully cooked pasta) and it got better, but it never became an emulsive sauce. I think I'll just have to wait to go to Rome.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: The grate of the cheese has to be super fine which really helps to avoid the lumps of cheese. I ought to know…..I’ve made every mistake possible

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I make a pretty good Cacio e Pepe by boiling (with salt!) & draining the pasta, reserving 1/3 C cooking water.   While pasta is in the colander, I heat butter, water and pepper in the cooking pot.   My recipe actually calls for an egg yolk too but I never use it.   Then I toss in the pasta, getting it good and wet.   Then I gradually put in 1 C of parm.    The dish is delicious, nothing wrong with it at all.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: The flavor of this recipe is really good. That being said, mine turned out with clumps of cheese and too much liquid in the pan even though I drained the pasta so that there was not a drop of water. The "cream" was runny before the pasta was even added. I'll try this again using less water when making the cream.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody:
So in the true spirit of NYTimes commentators, I ignored a lot of the recipe, combining the tricks here with the tricks of another cacio e pepe recipe--which makes it all in one skillet. 

I wanted the starchy water & also maybe a little oil/butter. This doesn't have it. Making the cheese sauce ahead, and then melding with remaining water creates perfection, even the half-hearted way I did.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: https://youtu.be/q8kTWNwUD88 Nonsense. This is the only way to make this dish. It’s perfect.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I put a TBSP of flour in about 1.5 cups of water and warmed/whisked that, and added it to the cheese mixture as I was initially heating it - seemed to solve the clumping problem, with no change in taste.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe to a T and still had cheese clumps. I'm still searching for a go-to recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Cheese glopped and clumped. I imagine that I got it the water/cheese paste too hot. Not a success.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Professional chefs may disdain the microwave, but I've had consistent success using it to achieve creamy cheese emulsions. Whisk the cheese into the hot liquid and heat until thickened and smooth.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I'm not a big fan of Parmesan. Would the recipe work equally as well with Romano?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: "Equally as well" will never be achieved with substitutions that aren't suggested in the recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I experienced perfect cacio e pepe at Evelyn's Table in London some years ago. The restaurant has an open kitchen and I watched it being made. This procedure was followed: https://seriouseats.com/spaghetti-cacio-e-pepe-recipe

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Karen's note pre-sages the NYT article about an academic study on how to prevent cheese coagulation in cacio e pepe!   Look for "The Perfect Cacio e Pepe Recipe, According to Science" dated January 24.  Just dissolve cornstarch in warm water in the ratios specified and blend it with the cheese...

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Chris Kimball's group came up with the cornstarch fix years ago. I've had consistent and perfect success using Nathan Myhrvold's approach of adding a bit of sodium citrate. This information is searchable on the internet and sodium citrate is available on the internet. A little bit goes a long way. It doesn't change the flavor of the recipe as the pecorino is so strongly flavored that it really overwhelms. All that being said making foolproof cacio e pepe at home on a whim is cool.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: If you follow this recipe to the letter the sauce will clump. I don’t know what the author is going for here - maybe adding cooled pasta? The pasta itself has too much heat. I’d go with the emulsion technique.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: The Milk Street website has an excellent fool proof recipe for this dish.. I think there is a video that goes with it.  It's all about cooking the pasta in a low skillet with a specified amount of water, cheese and pepper added toward the end.  Also, its recommended to use bronze extruded pasta.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Pasta water is the only water I use in Cacio e Pepe. The starch keeps the sauce creamy. The key patience. I put the cooked pasta, cheese, a pepper and at least a cup (or more) of pasta water in a sauce pan together. You keep stirring/tossing while the sauce cooks down. It’s perfect creamy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Don't try to make this dish. You will mess it up. Go to Rome and enjoy it there. Seriously.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I’m going to try this recipe out of curiosity, but my consistent, go-to cacio e pepe recipe and method is Alton Brown’s. Made in my home 15-20x/year.  It has only ever failed when I had to substitute cheeses based on what was on hand. Microplane to grate, and use a scale on metric setting. It has been consistently successful

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I didn’t have pecorino but had some Tomme de Savoie. Substituted 1/2 for 1, added a tablespoon of butter and kept Parmesan the same, it made achieving a good cheesy stringy consistency really easy while keeping a really rich taste. Yes that’s surely cheating but eh at least no corn starch like another writer suggested :-)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I got rid of all my non-stick cookware. (i.e. teflon and pseudo-ceramic).  Can NYT Cooking include tips for using other than the recommended nonstick pan or pot?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Most of us have and like non-stick pans.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: I haven't used this technique exactly, but I have made others. Making cacio e pepe is simple the way that making bread or pizza crust is simple. Very few, easy-to-combine ingredients, but there is an art and an alchemy to it. I once watched an interview with an acclaimed Japanese sushi chef, who said, "I spent seven years learning to make rice". Buck up, little troopers, it's a journey.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Nonstick skillet manufacturers warn not to heat their products over high heat when empty

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: Problem is regardless of the label on the package you probably don’t have the ingredients. Bocatini for the pasta.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: There is an art to mastering this "easy" recipe --- and I obviously haven't mastered it yet. Matters are not helped by an uncooperative glass top electric stove. But the family proclaims this the best new pasta dish I've made in ages (I THINK that was a compliment), so it'll be practice, practice, practice. Lots of good tips in the comments here on how to achieve success.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: This just didn’t work. I followed instructions to the letter and it was a gloppy, watery mess. You’re better off getting into Roscioli and enjoying it there—otherwise it’s a massive waste of cheese!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: The biggest failure in the instructions for this recipe is stating to use the same pot or pan that one just used for toasting the peppercorns…. Has to cool to room temperature first.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody:
This is an awful recipe. Sticky clumpy terrible mess.

Remove it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: This is an annoying comment because I haven’t  made this particular recipe, BUT: I have had this at Roscioli in Rome with a wine tasting! They used rigatoni and there was an abundance of delicious sauce at the bottom of the bowl for wiping up with bread. So my suggestions are to make the sauce looser than you think, and to serve with bread and a Barolo, of course.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Roscioli Roman Cacio e Pepe Recipe
commentBody: In the end, I got a hunky chunky cream sauce. I wish I would have looked at the Comments to see this is a common problem with this recipe, and that there are ‘fixes’ to address them.  Thankfully enough of the Pecorino did blend well leaving the Pasta with a good flavor, albeit not what it could have been (or what I was hoping for …).

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If you’re supposed to have a half a cup of onions after reducing and cooking them for that long, you definitely need to start with more.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is a traditional Pakistani dish but it’s usually made with beef shank instead of oxtail, wheat flour instead of chickpea so it’s perfectly acceptable to substitute those.  There are variations in spices as well, try adding a teaspoon of powdered fennel with the other spices, it’s heavenly.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Loved this recipe! Mine came out super greasy (maybe the oxtail I had), but I ended up with a jar full of yummy beef fat for cooking other dishes. Definitely worth refrigerating overnight.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Like Robert, I am confused by temperature and roasting instructions in Steps 2. Do you roast the ox tails at 400 degrees for 45 minutes, then reduce heat to 245 degrees and remove the oxtails to add to sauce you have been preparing, then  place the entire dish back in the oven  to bake at 245 degrees for 5-6 hours? It makes sense to roast ox tails initially at high temperature and use a low temperature for the 5-6 hours stewing. But 45 minutes to complete steps 3, 4 & 5 is optimistic!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Just did it, cut the onions, started them sautéing, cut the garlic, actually pureed the ginger and onions together since ginger wasn't blending well, and when this was ready actually had 2 minutes left on the roasting

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Just made this and it was an adventure. Many steps and prep but ultimately the 4plus hours, allows for clean up and the aroma wafting thru the house. Absolutely gorgeous!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Step 2 is a bit confusing:
Do you mean preheat oven to 400°, then lower oven temperature to 250° when you put in the meat, and roast for a total time of 45 minutes?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Roast at 400 for 45 minutes, then turn the oven down to 250 and take out the oxtails. Pour the onion mixture over and return to the oven.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I roasted the oxtails at 400 for 45 minutes, then lowered the temperature to 250, keeping the oxtails in the oven. After I completed Steps 3, 4, and 5 in a LARGE Dutch Oven, I put the oxtails into the Dutch oven, surrounded them with sauce, and put everything together into the oven at that 250 temperature for 4 - 6 hours.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: no, roast for 45 minutes while doing the other steps, take out and lower temp to 250, add the combined spices/purees/water to the roaster, cover tightly and put back in cooler oven for 4-6 hours

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Oxtails yield a lot of fat. Since there's also a half cup of ghee, should the large majority of the solidified fat be removed after refrigeration or it traditionally mixed in after heating?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I skimmed off some fat the next morning after refrigerating the pot overnight (I did Steps 3,4,&5 in a large Dutch oven and then transferred the roasted oxtails into that and put everything back into the oven at 250 for 4 - 6 hours) but the oxtails give off a lot of fat while roasting.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
One of the most satisfying recipes. !Accolades from the whole itslian family. 
Tasted just like India!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Any advice for making this with lamb shanks instead?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is a family favorite. We make this overnight. Oven temp at 225, all ingredients in a large lidded Dutch oven. Typical cook is around 12 hours.  Absolutely heaven. If you are using the right cuts it will render a lot of fat which can be lifted off after refrigeration.  Oxtail is great, so are short ribs, as well as beef shanks which release a ton of marrow during cooking.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Anybody try this in a pressure cooker?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: In her accompanying article, "How to Make Great Beef Stew? Here’s a World of Wisdom.", Ms. Moskin references J. Kenji López-Alt for his "unstinting research" on beef stew. In the article she cites, Mr. López-Alt recommends using a single piece of meat (not cubed), and cooking with the lid ajar, which, he says promotes "extra browned flavor" and "tenderer meat." Elsewhere he says to cook stew either on the stovetop or "in a 200° to 250°F oven...with the lid slightly ajar."

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Having made Brisket based stews.  My advice is get the fattier second cut. After all it will be in the oven for almost 3 hrs. A lean piece of meat will turn into leather.  Second forget the tomato paste or tomato sauce.  These two ingredients will produce the deadening "Betampte fleish" translated to "tasty meat" meat--forget it--you will fall asleep at the dinner table.  
My suggestion white wine (never red) mushrooms and real Roma tomatoes in addition to the onions.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Coming from a traditional Jewish American home, this recipe is pretty authentic except for the thyme. I doubt my European born mother even knew what thyme was let alone be tempted to add it to her beef stew. Ditto for the tomato paste-we didn’t use anything tomato (I think they weren’t readily available in Czechoslovakia where my mom was brought up). She would have used the paprika only.
That said, a really nice basic recipe-simple and highlights a great piece of meat.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Don't be surprised at how much brisket shrinks during cooking...

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I don't think my Jewish grandmother and mother knew from thyme, but they did know about onion soup mix, which is, I guess, the secret ingredient to my family's brisket? I know hundreds of other Jewish grandmothers and mothers for whom it was the secret, too.  So, I guess it isn't so secret.  Also, it didn't need to be Liptons, the store brand was just fine.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Whole brisket is broken down into a flat cut and a point cut. The point cut is fattier and, in my humble opinion, the only cut worh using when making pot roast, corned beef (easy to brine) and pastrami (cormed beef that's spiced and hot smoked). I find the flat cut to be dry when pot-roasted and lacking the flavor of the point cut in everything else.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I agree about leaving out the tomato paste and thyme.  My recipe (actually, my grandmother's and mother's recipe) has me skipping the pot-on-the-stove step.  I just put the brisket in a pan, fat side up, cover it with sliced onions, then some Lawry's seasoning salt and paprika.  My mother often crumbled up a few gingersnaps to flavor the juices.  Add mushrooms and carrots alongside partway through cooking.  The key is to cover the pan TIGHTLY with foil. Cook for about 3 hours. Wonderful!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If this is cooked a day in advance, the cooled slab of brisket can be then be thinly sliced against the grain and added to the reheated pot . It is a nicer presentation and can be eaten with a fork.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Cook halved, peeled potatoes in the gravy for the last hour of cooking. I braise the whole piece of brisket for two hours, slice it, and put it back in the gravy with the potatoes. And no thyme or tomato paste. Just lots of garlic, onions, and paprika.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Whatever ingredients you use, refrigerate the cooked brisket overnight and slice it when cold. It’s so much easier that way than trying to slice the meat when hot.
And as another commenter noted: brisket shrinks considerably when cooked!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I have enjoyed this type of Jewish cookery from time to time, as a gentile.   Briskett is one of those things if done properly is a revelation.   I'd go with the tomato paste.    It not only is tomato flavor enhancer, it makes things rich.

However, if you are using parsley for green garnish, why not throw in two handfuls of peas for more veggie substance?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: You can throw in peas if you're not following the Ashkenazic Passover laws (no legumes). But not if you are, and not if your guests are.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made it as written. Except I doubled the carrots, we like carrots. Didn’t add potatoes as my husband makes the best garlic mashed potatoes ever, so that will be our starch. Definitely best if left to sit til the next day. Will probably cook some peas, too.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Margery, my grandmother used gingersnaps in the tomato sauce she made for stuffed cabbage. I never heard anyone else mention it!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Mine, too!  It's the secret ingredient!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Rachelnot all ginger snaps are pareve, so if you follow the kosher dietary laws… I have also used candied ginger in place of the cookies, but ginger snaps were my mother’s go to for any sweet and sour recipe

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Smear the tomato paste on the meat after browning. Meat will usually shred if cut against the grain after 3 hours of cooking. Cut 1/2 way thru after 2 hours of cooking and return to pot.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This is what my German mother and aunt who were born in the German part of Serbia in 1937 would call Hungarian Goulash.  As another reader stated, there would be more paprika and no tomato.  Also no carrot.  I loved this growing up and would often request it on my birthday.  Also can be made with chicken.  Always served over kulski noodles at our home growing up.  
So interesting to see this recipe.  Thank you.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I'm a sucker for pot roast and being from the Caribbean I've always made it with rice on the side and beans in with the roast.  When I saw this recipe suggested mashed potatoes, I was gobsmacked that it never had occured to me.  Cooked my pot roast this week with garlic mashed (my favorite) and let me tell  you, I will not make pot roast with rice and beans again!! The mashed potatoes take the roast to a much more cozy dimension. Very content with this recipe!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: 300F was way too high a temp for this size brisket. It made it to temp in an hour and a half and tasted like leather. Highly recommend going for 200F and just leaving it in until it hits 170/180 depending on your preference for rarity.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is a stew! It was never, ever, supposed to be eaten rare. Brisket is a tough lean cut full of connective tissue. As the recipe says, cook for 3 hours or until the meat is tender.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: First cut will never be dry if cooked low and slow tightly covered as recipe indicates . I skip the browning and smear mustard and spices on the brisket before putting it on the bed of onions and garlic. Still comes out fantastic

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Secret ingredient , Lipton onion soup mix, to taste, and save the pot dripping s and end cuts for midweek borscht...

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This somewhat resembles the recipe I learned from my mother which no doubt came from her Galician mother. My technique is simpler. I use one tomato, not paste, and simmer the brisket on the stove, with the lid on, in the Dutch oven, turning often. A handful of raisins (or a little box) gives it the sweetness. I don't add water; by the time it's 2/3 done, the shrunken meat is swimming in its own water, which I reduce to gravy and cook the slices in for half an hour or so. Yum!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Any thoughts on how to make in a slow cooker? Thanks.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this recipe exactly as written aside from adding 2 extra onions and some white button mushrooms. It’s delicious! I made the whole thing in my Dutch oven and covered it in a 300 degree oven for 3 hours exactly. The brisket came out tender and delicious. I took the advice of other commenters and let it cool and sliced the beef cold the next day. It’s sublime. Served it over simple mashed potatoes with some fresh parsley. It’s got that slight tangy sweetness from the tomato paste and carrots

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My Jewish mother made brisket two ways: 1. The onion soup packet, some water, sliced onions; or 2. A can of cranberry sauce and a bottle of Heinz chili sauce…a tasty Jewish BBQ recipe!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This is my new favorite pot roast recipe. I recommend sticking with a chuck or top roast (fat = good) deglazing the pan with some red wine to give the meat and gravy that extra flavor boost, add mushrooms at the start of the roast and tripple the carrots (big chunks). 

Boil your potatoes in milk instead of water to save all that starch and lightly season. Let the gravy do the work!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Absolutely delicious! I included quartered potatoes and more carrots, and substituted red wine for the water. This was the best pot roast I've ever made!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: How long should you reheat the brisket the next day and at what temp?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Also cute chuck roast into a few chunks. Added 1tbsp beef bouillon too. Added cornstarch slurry to gravy to thicken at end

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made as written to christen a new Dutch oven. It’s a lovely and simple recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Dumb Q; no S & P until the end or am I doing what would seem a normal season as go?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I had a 2 pound eye of round roast, so I thought I would make half a batch. But I did end up using two carrots and three medium yellow onions. No tomato paste but added 1 1/2 teaspoons of paprika as well as the bay leaf and thyme. Instead of water I used a can of beef broth and thickened the gravy with a tablespoon of flour at the end. Half a bag of wide egg noodles. Delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Big "no, no" on the mashed potatoes made with butter and milk to go with a beef brisket. We did small potatoes with the beef gravy and chopped herbs on top.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I'm curious about the preparation of mashed potatoes or noodles to go with this if you can't use dairy with the meat for a kosher meal (as I understand it).

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: What dairy is essential to either mashed potatoes or noodles?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I think the present availability of vegan dairy products to replace the milk/cream and butter usually involved in making traditional mashed potatoes must be a huge advantage!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The Jewish beef stew is similar to Tuscan beef soup & stew except that we used added a shin bone & ate the marrow on the side.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I always use a flat cut and cook it for 4 hours. Nothing compares to a slow roasted Brisket enhanced with onions, garlic and the sweetness of tomato and carrot. I use a chili sauce and some brown sugar at times as well to add a touch of BBQ taste. 
Brisket is our go to for both Passover and Hanukkah.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Super tasty.  After three hours the brisket was still tough. 4lb brisket with healthy amazing amount of fat.  Needed another two hours.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My mother-in-law made this for holidays but did not include tomato paste, thyme or carrots. Just extra paprika, and she used dry vermouth instead of water, with a single piece of meat. My father-in-law would slice it after cooling with an electric knife to get the slices nice and thin.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If I remember correctly, my mother cooked brisket for an hour in a pressure cooker.  The meat was always tender, but the vegetables turned to mush.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I've found that tough cuts of meat take much more time to tenderize than vegetables to cook through.  When pressure cooking, it seems to help to cook the meat at least halfway through, depressurize, add vegetables to the pot, and repressurize to finish cooking.  That way both meat and vegetables are cooked optimally.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is pretty much the same recipe my mother made except she rubbed the brisket with the garlic and paprika before browning. Definitely no tomato paste or thyme. I make this every year, but my addition is to add half water and half red wine and cook it until the same wooden handled fork that my mother used goes in easily.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious but I would use more carrots.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Cook a single piece of brisket two hours, remove from oven to slice thick, then return the slices to oven to cook for the last hour. The meat will be easier to slice before full tenderness and the slices will absorb  liquid, making them juicy and tender.

And please, no tomato paste (this is not an Italian dish) nor thyme. Lots of carrots, cut thick, for sweetness, lots of yellow onions, and patience.

Ladle some cooking liquid into a sauce pan and thicken with arrowroot.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Mimi was the real deal! Have made countless PRs and (almost) always use my home-made beef stock instead of water. Or, in a pinch, a weak "Better Than Bouillon" stock is a great substitution. BTB can be a bit salty if it's made too strong. Definitely the Point Cut, or a combo of Chuck and Short Ribs. It takes a dab hand to correctly roast a chicken, but anyone can make a PR!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Added parsnips because I had them. Yum

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I'm very appreciative for this recipe. We weren't blessed with a Jewish grandmother, but my husband has a tomato allergy so with a simple swap of anchovy paste for the umami aspect and we had a wonderful beef stew that everyone enjoyed together. I did take the recipe's suggestion to try it with cubed stew meat, highly recommend.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’ve always read that you take your pan off the heat before adding paprika to anything, otherwise you risk burning it and having it turn bitter.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My first generation Jewish-American mother always skipped tomato paste and added quartered potatoes to the pot, a welcome break from the ubiquitous mashed potatoes she often served. I skip tomato sauce and add a little Worcestershire sauce, which really  adds a lot of umami. I also use more carrots, so that everyone can have at least one whole carrot per serving. Browning the meat slowly and well is key to the best flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Julia, can we please get a slow cooker version of this recipe?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My Jewish mother taught me to use Manischewitz and Ketchup :-)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Absolutely perfect, tender, flavorful, tastes just like my grandmothers. I love that the rich flavor comes from simple ingredients and time. Don’t cut the cook time short and reheating the next day really makes a difference. I don’t think I’ll ever make pot roast any other way again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious. I added mushrooms after the onions had sautéed, and I added crumbled ginger snaps as my mother did. My brisket was very fatty and I baked it at 325 for 2.5 hours. The result was wonderful. Can't wait to serve an even tastier version tomorrow.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I would like to make this in a crock pot. Thoughts?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this recently, and it was fantastic. I did however make two small adjustments. I dropped the temp to 275°, and cooked it a little longer. I also subbed the water with equal parts beef stock and red wine. Plain water just seems wrong.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this with an instant pot instead of a dutch oven.  The results were pretty close to the expected results but my brisket could have used another 10 more minutes under pressure.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Add a little ketchup and ginger ale take out the thyme and throw in potatoes and you (basically) have my Grandmother's recipe. Browning before braising is essential!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My mother added chunks of potatoes for the last 40 minutes of cooking. I agree, no tomato paste, no thyme, just water. onions, green peppers, salt, and paprika. I now add a bit of tomato paste and a little red wine. Maybe I'll try thyme, since it one of my favorite herbs.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The sides suggested may be just fine, but my mother always served brisket with kasha!  In my mind it's one dish: brisket and kasha.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My mother used Lipton onion soup mixed with ketchup and mustard.   Slathered it over the 'double brust' brisket, put it in the pan with about 1/2" - 1" of water and roasted for several hours. Sliced, refrigerated and rewarmed following day to serve.  It's as good or better than all the gourmet briskets I've cooked over the decades since I left home.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My family recipe is very similar, but we add an apple cut up, a couple of tablespoons of honey and a package of Lipton’s Onion Soup. My husband’s favorite! Delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Can this be done in a crockpot?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Look for a crockpot recipe. There are plenty out there.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: How long would you cook in an Instant Pot?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Stringy, tough, and fatty -- among the least attractive attributes for a meal.  No wonder I have no sentimental attachment for my mother's (or her mother's, either).  Drove me to be a vegetarian...

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: It was not the right cut of meat or your mom did not cook it right.  Always a tragedy.  My sympathies.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Where's the kasha? We had slight variations in our family between the Polish and Hungarian sides, but a pot roast (brisket or not) was always served with kasha, and my mother always cooked it on the stove top in a Magnalite Dutch oven. The Hungarian side used more paprika and served with apple sauce. The Polish side served with horseradish. I sometimes cook kasha along with the brisket, with olive oil and sherry. Not authentic, but great!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: 1 cup tomato sauce and french onion soup mix.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: When I make brisket I use flanken. It is  tastier and more tender.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: No, you are not making brisket: flanken is a style of short ribs.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Would the cooking time be the same with a slow cooker?  I'm a bad cook, but this looks delicious and do-able. I'd like to get it right.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Best way to get it right is to follow the recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Really tasty and pretty simple! A good St Pat's dinner... next time, some red pepper flakes.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: There are more steps than needed to make this roast. I increased the temperature to 325 degrees, put the beef, fat side up, in the pan, seasoned it and placed the vegetables over the top and added the tomato paste and water ( about 1/2 cup or more) to the bottom of the pan. Roasted and basted it uncovered until browned, then covered and roasted for another  3 hours until was  tender. I cooled the meat with gravy in the pan, skimmed off the fat the next day , sliced the meat and reheated it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: To me brisket is a scary cut. It'll either be amazing or inedible. This was amazing. Followed the recipe as written except used beef broth instead of water. I couldn't find my bay leaf (unforgivable because I have my own bay laurel tree) so did use the thyme. Not really sure it really added anything to it.  Would definitely make again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Added celery and kale at the end. Very good.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made the recipe as written with chuck roast. Delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The family loved this.  I loved the great tip embedded in this recipe to make a little ball of dough out of butter and flour for easily thickening the sauce.  Thank you!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Cooked with farmer’s market beef chuck 3.25 lb roast.  As written except a few more carrots.  Very good-maybe best pot roast ever! Thyme works well with beef, tomato paste also fine. Served as suggested with mashed potatoes and parsley.  Fork tender in 3.5 hours. Couldn’t wait-ate it for dinner:)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The carrots are incredible. Even my daughter who normally hates cooked carrots liked them. Next time, I’ll add more - a lot more.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So good with the egg noodles and sour cream

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is similar to my grandmother's brisket, although she used no tomato paste or herbs. Just lots of garlic and paprika. She also sliced the meat after it cooked for about two hours and put it back into the gravy to cook for another hour, along with peeled, halved russet potatoes. And that's how I make it to this day.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Not great. Made this recipe exactly and by 2:30 the liquid had almost run out and started to burn around the edge of the dutch oven.

The top of the brisket that wasn't in the liquid was dried out. Find another recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Fantastic!  Made with beef chuck rather than brisket.

Couple details in recipe need correction - Step 5, add carrots thyme and water to cover, then put the meat on top.  (Since it specifies do not cover the meat.)  Also, pepper to taste only at the end.  We usually salt and pepper meat before browning.  (Recipe says salt only.)

Otherwise, and amazing result.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Instruction 5 says to cover the carrots and onions with hot water but "do not cover the meat."  How does one avoid covering the meat if one is using meat "cut into 2-inch chunks."

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Really yummy holiday meal. Mis en place is important. Make sure your blender works.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: A pot roast made from chuck is much better than brisket.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made as written and it was very delicious but carrots are very soft almost mashed at the end and there was not enough "gravy" . I'll add more vegtables next time andcook carrots separately for a side dish. Meat was excellent

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I improvised on the seasonings, using a homemade salsa heavy with cinnamon instead of simple tomato paste, and fresh basil. The sauce was delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made as written numerous times, always with chuck roast. It’s delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made the recipe as written. Simple, delicious, and the house smelled like heaven for hours. I put zero extra effort into the gravy - just blended everything with an immersion blender and added a bit of salt - and it was excellent. We will be making it again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: After cooking, I set aside the brisket and then pureed everything else in the pot for an instantly thick gravy. Pretty good!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Looking for brisket recipe made with Balsamic vina

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My lord this was delicious.  I made exactly as the recipe was written except for 2 minor things.  I added 2 extra carrots and I used my homemade bone broth instead of water. Divine. And so easy!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Your mashed potato recipe is DAIRY, and that is highly inappropriate as an accompaniment for a meat dish at a Jewish holiday dinner.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Tomato tenderizes.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My Jewish Nana covered her brisket with Lipton Onion Soup Mix and Heinz ketchup, similar to what others mention here. It’s terrific, no doubt all the sugar in the ketchup helps. 😂 But I now follow Julia Child’s Smothered Brisket recipe that uses lots of sliced onions and a can of tomatoes to produce a similar flavor profile without all the sugar and artificial ingredients. I never brown the brisket, just season and dump the onions and tomatoes on top, cover tightly and braise low and slow. It’s key to find brisket that is not overly trimmed; if you get flat cut make sure there’s still a good fat cap on it. It takes me 3.5 to 4 hours to get a fork tender result and then I leave it overnight and cut and reheat slices in the sauce the next day. I can’t imagine adding peas to this but that’s just me. I some sometimes toss in a few chunky carrots and potatoes towards the end of the braise,

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I recently made this. I used 2 lbs of Bottom Round, and cooked it whole. The only change I made was to add mushrooms. I only wish my Jewish mother and grandmothers could have tasted it. I'm going to make ot again tomorrow.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This honestly came out a bit bland. If I did it again, I'd use beef stock and red wine instead of just water when adding the liquid.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Braised Short Ribs With Peanuts and Anchovies Recipe
commentBody:
This sounded so intriguing but had few notes, or fans, here. I made it; recipe needed some help. Here are my thoughts:
• Using just savoy cabbage gives no visual appeal. Add lacinato kale as well to create more color and interest
• Peanut butter vs. peanut purée: the purée does not resemble peanut butter at all. Stick with the recipe
• You may need more water to cook meat, making dish very bland. Best advice? Use fish sauce instead of salt to season at the end. Brings disparate flavors together.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Braised Short Ribs With Peanuts and Anchovies Recipe
commentBody:
I loved Floyd Cardoz and his restaurants, especially Tabla, so I was eager to try this recipe.  It is great, and the flavors are delicious, but the recipe needs some help I think.  First, 250-275F oven is too low for a braise.  325F works better, but it depends on your oven. Check after twenty minutes, the liquid should be simmering gently. 
There is too much liquid in this recipe, especially for the peanut puree.  With this much water, it turns into a slurry, not a puree.  Use 1/2 cup.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Braised Short Ribs With Peanuts and Anchovies Recipe
commentBody: Used 4 cups of water based upon reader recommendations. Additionally, didn’t love the whole peppercorns and large pieces of ginger. We’re I to make it again, I would use less water, ground pepper and grated garlic. As it is, I strained the liquid, pulled out the ginger and peppercorns and reduced the sauce. Added peanut butter too. Liked it better that way.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Braised Short Ribs With Peanuts and Anchovies Recipe
commentBody: We thought this was delicious. Next time I might use ground pepper rather than whole peppercorns; I found biting into a whole peppercorn unpleasant. I also might cut the ginger slices in halves or quarters to distribute the ginger more evenly.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Braised Short Ribs With Peanuts and Anchovies Recipe
commentBody:
This is pretty good! Don’t see the reason to limit the cabbage cause it really just melts away- or maybe my cabbage was small. My addition was a healthy 2-3 cap fulls of rice vinegar to add some acid, which felt like it was much needed after adding the anchovies and cabbage. 
Braised in the oven at 300f for 2.5 hours. If I make it again, i’ll salt my shortribs more and let them sit out for a bit longer. Worth it, and it came together really well.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Braised Short Ribs With Peanuts and Anchovies Recipe
commentBody: Adapts very well to Instant Pot, cutting cook time to around an hour. Given that this is a real calorie bomb of a meal, I strongly suggest that after the short ribs cook, remove them from the liquid, strain it, saving the strained-out onions and peppercorns and adding them back to the meat, then remove the fat, either with a fat-separater, or by cooling in the fridge. You will lose up to a full cup of beef fat, or 2000 calories.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Braised Short Ribs With Peanuts and Anchovies Recipe
commentBody: I've now cooked this twice; once as written, and once with a number of modifications. As others have said, the recipe as written yields a very weak soup. To address this, I reduced the liquid added to a total of four cups, and used broth rather than water. For the peanut puree, rather than adding more water as the recipe calls for, I took 1.5 cups of broth out of the pot after a few hours. Finally, I removed as many peppercorns as I could prior to serving; were I to make this again, I would put the peppercorn in a cheesecloth.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Braised Short Ribs With Peanuts and Anchovies Recipe
commentBody: Would it be OK to use natural chunky peanut butter for the puree?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Braised Short Ribs With Peanuts and Anchovies Recipe
commentBody: Yep should be great!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Braised Short Ribs With Peanuts and Anchovies Recipe
commentBody: How long to bake in a 250-275F oven?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Braised Short Ribs With Peanuts and Anchovies Recipe
commentBody: See Step 3.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Braised Short Ribs With Peanuts and Anchovies Recipe
commentBody: Lovely dish!!!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Braised Short Ribs With Peanuts and Anchovies Recipe
commentBody: This was absolutely wonderful! I made no changes whatsoever. We had very high quality meat from a local butcher (grass fed meat) and this recipe let the beef shine!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Braised Short Ribs With Peanuts and Anchovies Recipe
commentBody: Any thoughts on how to adapt this for Instant Pot?  I love how it does meats

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Braised Short Ribs With Peanuts and Anchovies Recipe
commentBody: This should work (Floyd's co-writer here lol): Use the "sauté" function through the end of Step 2. For Step 3, add water just to cover the short ribs. Bring to a boil, close it up, and set the timer for 25 minutes. Let it release naturally, then pick up at Step 6, back on "sauté."

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Braised Short Ribs With Peanuts and Anchovies Recipe
commentBody:
Very disappointed with the lack of flavor despite its great ingredients. Too watery, needed a lot of salt, more cabbage. Added dry chipotle, and Aleppo Chile to pick it up. Even added more anchovy than asked for. 
This recipe is not a keeper as opposed to the contrasting one called Nihari.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Braised Short Ribs With Peanuts and Anchovies Recipe
commentBody:
Made this today. Followed the recipe.
Tasty, but a bit bland.
Would a splash of vinegar help?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Braised Short Ribs With Peanuts and Anchovies Recipe
commentBody: Very disappointing.  Had hoped for more flavor from the peanuts and anchovies, but it was very bland and very watery.  The meat was tender, but the cabbage and excessive amount of liquid were not pleasant.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Braised Short Ribs With Peanuts and Anchovies Recipe
commentBody: Not that great. I was hoping for the peanut sauce, but it was pretty bland...

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody:
While this is a good simplification of a more complex dish (which I ate growing up often and now I cook every few months).  There are a couple of key ingredients that are missing: a couple of slightly charred corn tortillas that get dissolved in the broth and two finely chopped Xoconoxtle prickle pears with out the seeds.  
I have found the Xoconoxtle in Mexican grocers both in New York and in California,  it brings acidity in a different way than that of the fresh limes.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: This recipe also works with chicken or turkey although you will want to do more of a saucy roast than a boil. I like to add 3 guajillo chiles in addition to the anchos and Pasillas

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: Absolutely delicious. All four of us were dumbstruck by the depth of flavor in this stew.  Not spicy at all, just deep earthiness and a touch of acidity from the chilis and tomatillos.  I followed the recipe exactly but decided  to add a few chunks of potatoes when I added the green beans.  The potatoes added nothing to the flavor of the dish but they were crucial to convincing my guests that this was a “stew”. I will definitely be making this again and again

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody:
Chayote squash aka Mirliton is available in southern supermarkets in the late fall and winter. It grows here almost like a weed. Inexpensive veggie. One favorite way to cook mirliton in NOLA is shrimp and mirliton soup.
You might be able to find it in Latin markets or sometimes in Indian grocery stores.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: In most parts of Mexico xoconostle is an indispensable ingredient for this dish. It’s difficult, if not impossible to find in much of the US, so I understand why it wasn’t included in the recipe. Nevertheless , in light of it’s importance to creating a true mole de olla, some mention of it might have been made in the introductory narrative.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: You can get dried xoconostle from Rancho Gordo or, in season, fresh from Mexican grocers. I used dried xoconostle in this recipe and it was excellent. Tomatillos might be good, but in my opinion, will change the flavor of the dish.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: Where can I find chayote squash? Substitutes?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: in the south it is called mirthillon. it is the same thing

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: Or choko. Substitute any squash that's not too dense and dry.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: Use Turkey meat

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: I live in Europe and fresh tomatillos are hard to come by - would it be OK to omit them? I luckily have a good stash of dried chilies, so this delicious sounding recipe is possible. Thank-you

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: Do you have jarred regular green tomatoes? If so, you could probably use one or two of those instead.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: How to you eat the corn?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: i would assume you just pick it up and eat it, like how anyone eats corn on the cob. but you could also (depending on the size of your pot and to make things easier) just cut them in half or leave them whole and then take them out and cut the kernels off before serving.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: Just reading the title of the recipe brought back to me so many memories of growing up in Mexico City. This was one of my favorite dishes that my grandmother used to make. She used to put these tiny “masa” balls which added thickness to the stew, and it was a treat to get one of those in your bowl.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: So, none of the recommended veggies (ears of corn? zucchini? tomatillos? Hah! Dream on!) are available fresh in Maine in winter. Are there any recommended substitutes, or should I wait until summer? Yes, I can get frozen green beans, and cilantro and mint, all year 'round. I've never seen a chayote here in Central Maine, and Portland is an hour's drive, and Portsmouth, NH, is two hours' drive, away. Suggestions for alternative veggies would be welcome!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: Seems central Maine lacks too many ingredients. The number of substitutions or deletions would result in a totally different dish. I recommend driving one hour to Portland.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: I substituted the zucchini with carrots and a cuke, canned tomatillos can work! I'm pretty sure I could find them in Maine when I lived there. Smoked paprika is great to add. The problem with purists is that they see substitution as a sin, I see it as this is a good starter recipe. Good luck.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: This is my 2nd time making this mole. It's a lot of cutting and step, but this mole is excellent, next time I make this (and I will) I will half the salt, it was a tad bit salty for our tastes. I have a son who lives in Brooklyn and he loves Mexican food and I can't wait to make this for him!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: Xoconostle is sold by rancho gordo. So it’s easily available online.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: We found that once you put the chili mixture into the meat, it really needs more than 30 minutes to cook. After 30, it was not tender. We cooked it another full hour before adding the veg and the sauce reduced and the meat was super tender.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: Brown beef first

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: The stew was good but lacked complex depth of flavor, which was disappointing considering all the steps and time it took to prepare it (much longer than the 2.25 hours the recipe quotes - maybe I am a slow chef). I browned the meat as my first step to render out the fat but otherwise followed the recipe as written. I would make it again but would perhaps add more chiles or try to figure out another way to punch up the flavors.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: You could add half a head of garlic to boil the beef and add 12 guajillo (chile pepper) chiles. It adds more flavour.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: The chiles should be toasted in a dry pan after removing stems and seeded.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: Step Six seems fussy and difficult.  Any ideas for a better way?  Maybe just skip the fishing out?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: It's a whole half of an onion and 3 whole garlic cloves, so it's not that difficult. Maybe you're thinking it's chopped?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody:
Ah!  I love mole de olla. It was one of the first dishes my NH mother learned to make from her Mexican mother-in-law. And she made it often and deliciously. 
I have given up most meat these days but the picture at the top of the article is literally making my stomach rumble and my mouth water. Any suggestions for for what to make it with if not beef or pork?  Had anyone ever tried making it with chicken?  Suggestions welcomed!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: It looks like mint is a substitute for epizote. But what kind of mint? Spearmint? Peppermint? I honestly can’t imagine how either would taste in a beef stew.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: Mint is decidedly NOT a substitute for epazote.  If you don't have epazote skip it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: Can this recipe be adapted to a slow cooker?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: The all-important xoconostles are called out in the Tip at the end of the recipe. Perhaps rehydrating the dehydrated product available here https://www.ranchogordo.com/products/sweet-xoconostle?variant=1711753411 would work?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: glad somebody pointed out to others that in fact the xoconostles are mentioned. perhaps it should be in the headnotes, although i find people often miss those too. i'm not sure the rancho gordo product would work because those are like candy - like a dried fruit or fruit leather vibe, with sugar added. (now i want to buy them just to eat as a snack though...)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: This dish is not good! The meat is flavorless, as is the broth. Don’t waste your time or money!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: an American simplification of a classic complex dish

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: Made this as written. I found all ingredients at the.local mercado. It was a hit with everybody. I will surely cook this one again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: Made this for our friends visiting from Mexico. It disappeared quickly!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: I am finishing this right now, having carefully done each step with ingredients purchased today. So far, tasting reveals absolutely no flavor. Very disappointed for so much work!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: I find it tastes better the next day, can you add some ground smoked paprika? Chicken broth? I put carrots into my latest cooking of this mole and I found the flavor very very good. Best of luck.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe was pretty easy and the results are delicious. The leftovers are (unsurprisingly) even better the next day. Cooked for more than the additional 30 mins after adding the chili puree as the meat wasn't tender enough yet. 
I did end up adding a couple of spoonfuls of brown sugar near the end as I think I overdid it a bit on the epazote which resulted in a slightly bitter / soapy flavor. I also added some chunks of potatoes because...I love them

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Mole de Olla (Beef Stew With Chiles) Recipe
commentBody: You can use only Guajillo chiles (6) and a pinch of cumin.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Guys everyone is freaking out about sugar and it's silly. One and a half TEAspoons of sugar is very, very little. You will not taste the sweetness, it's just going to enhance the flavor of the beets and help the flavors of everything else meld. This small amount of sugar in a relatively large recipe is not going to put you into a diabetic coma oh my god.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Plus the potatoes are a lot worse for diabetic diets than the sugar is. If you're going to complain about glycemic load the potatoes are your problem, not the sugar.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Hahaha, exactly! It’s chemistry, relax.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The reason for the addition of granulated sugar is to counteract the acidity of the tomato. It isn’t really to make the soup sweeter, but rather to balance the flavors, in this case, balancing acidity. It won’t make the soup really sweet.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Indeed, adding a bit of sugar does balance the flavour profile. I learned many years ago, from a very dear 'adopted' Jewish mother, that adding acid (in the form of sour salt) or sugar, depending on the soup brought rave reviews. I believe the practice is almost standard across many central and eastern European cuisines.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The tomato is critical for keeping the beets red. Without acidity, the beets will blanche and turn white. And since you have to use acid, you need to add the sugar to balance it out.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: As a student at the University of Michigan I always got the borscht that was served at a cafeteria called The League.  There were two cafeterias there -- one for students and one for faculty.  I always ate in the faculty one.  The soup was amazing and I spent years trying to duplicate it.  I finally found a Ukrainian  version online that I really like.   The author stated the biggest issue with borscht is whether the cook adds sugar or not.  I have had it both ways, but make mine without.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I do have a question about the cabbage.  If I'm reading the recipe correctly you add the raw cabbage towards the end and simmer for only 5 minutes.  Then you let the soup sit for 20 minutes.  I know you want the cabbage to have a little crunch but is this enough cook it so that it's not totally raw?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: It's plenty of time for it to wilt and blend in with all the ingredients.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Lose the bell pepper, add coarse ground black pepper to pot, and to taste in the bowl. Don’t skimp on the tinned fish. Sour cream on the side. I like Delba German rye with Borsch.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Black pepper is great, but why lose the red pepper?  What with all the 'hate' of bell peppers with modern cooks?  They are very nutritious.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Very interesting. As not a fish "fan" I'll sub with some other veg protein. I bet the result is just as great.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: You can leave the fish out for a vegan version. It is fantastic!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: You can also use a little ketchup as a sugar substitute which has a lot sugar =)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Seeing what's in my cupboard I'm probably going to use canned beans, a can of tomatoes (no juice) and a piece of fish.  I'll report on the results.
And, yes, I know it won't be "authentic" but it may be delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: One thing I noticed was in step 4.  It does not mention to add the chopped red bell pepper to the onion.  In step 5 you add the onion-tomato-bell pepper mixture so you need to read ahead.  You may want to edit step 4 to reflect this.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The wording says to add the bell pepper in Step 5 (which makes sense; you don't want to cook the pepper too long)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: You say modern cooks often use canned fish in tomato sauce. What do traditional cooks do? Do they use fresh fish and diced tomatoes, or something else? And what sort of fish? With the current supply chain issues, I'm not at all confident of finding the canned ones in New Zealand supermarkets.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Great recipe! Prepared according to the recipe. I served it with marble rye bread and sour cream and everyone really enjoyed it.  My son who had lived in Ukraine said it was very authentic, although he hadn't had it with fish before.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is very good. I used canned sardines and it was a hit with us and the neighbor.  It gets better the next day

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Bean cooking stage: Covered or uncovered? I did uncovered, and there's not much liquid left. I'm wondering if I need to add water to make up for evaporation.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Edited to comment above: I obviously will need to add water for the potato cooking stage. Perhaps the recipe can be modified to reflect this. My bad: should have known to cook beans covered!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe is terrific. It takes some doing. Both times I made it, I used canned beans. I want to rewrite the prep steps for that. The sugar (a tiny bit over a recipe that will feed six easy and probably more) is minuscule. The sauté and simmer reminds me of the old Julia Child ratatouille recipe with a wildly different flavor profile but just as complex and delicious. My friends and fam are raving. Slava Ukraini!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The soup was very good by itself, but absolutely fantastic with some creme fraiche and the garlic rye.  These are "musts" that should be a last step of the recipe in my opinion.  Since rye is occasionally hard to find, I sub Caesar salad style garlic croutons and that is 98% as good.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: where is the fish?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This sounds great, but I'm curious about the addition of sugar to beets(sugar beets?). This seems like a very savory soup. Will it be just a god without the sugar?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Some beets are not very sweet, even a little woody. In fact, not all beets are sugar beets!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
You can absolutely leave it out. 

Anna is exactly right. The addition of sugar is to balance out the earthiness of the beets and enhance the natural sweetness which varies across different varieties of beets.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Thank you for the politically perfect recipe for me. I'd like to make this as is for a political discussion party - but also make a vegetarian option. What modifications would you suggest if I want to leave out the fish?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Maybe miso, vegan fish sauce, or chopped mild kimchi to add a bit of funk? I am so excited to try this recipe!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Fish is very non-standard in traditional Borsch. The soup varies heavily by region throughout Ukraine, Russia, Belarus, Poland, and Lithuania. There are many vegetarian versions since Orthodox Christians give up all meat and fish for lent. Try this vegetarian version on the times website: https://cooking.nytimes.com/recipes/1019972-vegetarian-red-borscht , I can vouch it's authentic.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Hi Kate, for a vegetarian or completely vegan version all you have to do is leave out the fish. The beans in the recipe plus all the other vegetables give this variety of borsch incredible flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: well, that was interesting.  I have been making borsch for >40 years, Ukrainian style with cabbage, potatoes, onions and tomatoes added to the beets.  a dark fish will add plenty of umami

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Does it matter what kind of cabbage is used?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Feel free to experiment with different varieties of cabbage. In my family we use green cabbage but you could absolutely use red cabbage and other varieties.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My question is about the fish?  Can this recipe stand alone without the addition of the fish?  If not, what can be substituted?  I am not a fan of sardines...  Would anchovies be too strong a flavor?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Try the Sprats mentioned.  They are much tastier than sardines.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious! Definitely use 2 cans of sardines and maybe more beans ( we used canned). Oh: pumpernickel bread next time!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: A bit of a fuss, but well-worth it.  A healthy and comforting soup and the sardines are wonderful in it.  I want to try sprats next time, and maybe go from dried beans instead of canned. Also: this recipe makes PLENTY.  Looking forward to leftovers tonight.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I hate sardines and am not sure that I can get sprats in my area (and I don't know whether I like them or not). Is there some other fish that I could use (cooked or uncooked)?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Too much work for the payoff. It was good and certainly healthy but way too many steps for a 'good' soup, but not a great soup.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I wasn't sure at first because of the unfamiliar ingredients of fish, beans and peppers, but this recipe ended up being great! I am so happy to have found a tomato-based borsch - the closest yet to my grandma's recipe.
 
A few notes:

* used a whole pack of dried great northern beans - that worked
* something went wrong with my cabbage. Did others use red cabbage? How thinly did you shred it?
* the fish broke into tiny little pieces, but that was fine
* next time I will use more beets

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: less potatoes

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I added vinegar which livened it up a bit. 1-2 T to taste.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I am going to add more sardines.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: first time using a written recipe to make borsch and it was excellent. love the addition of tinned fish! made the following *very minor* modifications, only bc I've made many a borsch and these are my strong preferences. use sunflower oil for more authentic flavor. when you start piling in the veg before the final simmer, cabbage should go first as it takes the longest, then potato, then everything else, with red bell pepper last. canned kidney beans are the most perfect bean for borsch.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this with 1 can Fishwife sardines and 1 can Fishwife mackeral for a total of about 8 oz tinned fish. Roasted beet before shredding. Otherwise followed recipe. Delicious and I’m not a huge beet fan

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Wasn’t sure if I’d like it, but had most of the ingredients and wanted to give it a try since there are some many vegetables in it. Couldn’t find Sprats packed in tomato so I used Smoked Sprats packed in oil and rinsed them really well before adding. Worked perfect. Next time I’ll include more beans.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’m eager to make this, but there may be an important cultural culinary difference here. Most sour cream in the US is store-bought and not live-cultured. I’m assuming that is different in Ukraine, because most American sour cream won’t ferment a thing. You’ll just be eating 2-day hot milk (yikes).
