Stick to kefir or yogurt or something else with live lactic acid bacteria for a safe drink. This sounds DELICIOUS and I will be making it ASAP!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @allan Agreed that most US sour cream is not cultured and wouldn’t work, but many stores with a good selection of natural-foods dairy would have brands that are live-cultured, such as Nancy’s or Organic Valley.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I started making ryazhenka for myself some years ago.  Cultured sour cream, low fat or regular, works best for me as a starter--the container will say whether it is cultured. I use pasturized milk but not ultrapasturized since the culture can't grow in it. I also add a can of evaporated can to the crockpot. Cook 10 hours, wait until milk is around 115 degrees, mix sour cream with 1 cup milk from crockpot and then return to crockpot. Wrap towels around crockpot to keep it warm until it thickens.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: There may be valid reasons to avoid ultra-pasteurized dairy, but the false notion that "the culture can't grow in it" is not one of them. Lactobacilli will ferment the ultra-pasteurized lactose as well as the caramelized lactose in this recipe; I make creme fraiche from ultra-pasteurized heavy cream and a bit of cultured buttermilk regularly.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Delicious!  No added sugar like kefir and yogurt here!  I love it!
Made exactly as told.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: followed the instructions to the letter, and its perfect. Exactly how i remember it tasting tin my small hometown in Ukraine. Try this, its worth the effort.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Is it ok to use yogurt, or yogurt thinned out with milk, instead of kefir?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: It is best to substitute with sour cream, creme fraiche or a piece of sourdough rye bread to ferment the baked milk! Hope that helps.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I use a yogurt starter to make my own yogurt.  Could I use this instead of the sour cream, creme fresh, kefir?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I make yogurt once a week using a Bulgarian bacillus starter reused any number of times. 

I heat the 2%milk, my mother used whole milk from her own cows :) ( I never realized how blessed I was) to 180 degrees, cool it to 115 degrees and add the starter of four teaspoons of yogurt from a previous batch. I do know that if you let the milk heat up to or burn to 225 degrees it will caramelize. Is that it or am I missing something?

commentBody: Can you use goat milk? I have dairy goats and make a lot of yogurt and kefir. This sounds amazing!

commentBody: Why the coffee filters?  Something in the air?

I made this as described, however found that the milk curdled in the slow cooker - I still saw the whole thing through and enjoyed the flavor but ended up straining it to remove the small milk pieces that coagulated which interfered with the overall texture. Did others experience this?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: could we use lowfat kefir? i'm not worried about fat content, it's just what's already/always in my fridge. thanks!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Yes. Any kefir with live cultures -- non-fat, low-fat, or whole-milk -- will work just fine. It would be helpful for the recipe to clarify that point.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: What temperature should the target be for the low slow-cooker setting? Mine is set at 185 F and I'm wondering if I should adjust it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Think of setting it as a gas flame- lowest setting possible, over longer period of time.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’ve made this in the slow cooker before, and regular, store-bought (full fat) sour cream works just fine.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this for the first time on a whim, with high quality whole milk and kefir.  After fermentation there were some chewy, waxy bits in the mix, so I poured it through a fine mesh sieve into jars.  Fabulous!  So refreshing.  It is definitely on the tart and tangy side, which I like, but blended with some frozen blueberries would probably be amazing for those wanting some sweet.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’ve been making chebureki for years, and our family recipe is a little different than Julia's (no kefir, we add minced green onion and parsley.) But it’s worth talking about how to eat them. Inside a hot cheburek is a pool of hot broth, so it's best to keep the meat pie vertical once you take the first bite. Also, start slurping each bite after the 2nd/3rd, or else you'll end up with hot broth all over! Truly one of the world's most delicious foods. PS - uncooked tortillas will work in a pinch!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @matt I hope you mean flour tortillas! We also use frozen empanada dough

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Yum.  I lived in Zaporizhzhia in the early 2000s and this would be one of my splurges.  I can taste them now.  Was fortunate to spend a New Year's in Mariupol.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: While Chebureki are beloved in all of the former Soviet Union, it is originally a Crimean Tatar dish. My German grandma learned to make these in Central Asia after WW2 from Crimean Tatar friends, who - being Muslim - never used pork but sometimes used lamb. My grandma used to add a tablespoon of vegetable oil if she thought the meat was too lean. Ditching the pork yields better flavor and honors a marginalized culture!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’d add some dill,parsley, paprika, or anise to emphasize the Eastern European origin of this recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @GBR Anise/Caraway is more of a flavor of West Slavic cuisine, especially Czech.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Has anyone tried baking, rather than frying, these? (I hate post-frying cleanup.)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Could you do these in a deep fryer?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Absolutely!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Yes, you can.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Any suggested changes for high altitude?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Very close to meat samosas, except for the lack of certain Indian spices. My problem was always having the dough separate while being fried, thus contaminating the oil for the rest of the samosas.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Nicholas Robinson no, not like samosas outside of them being fried. Samosas  are much bigger pouches ( completely different shape) with much more filling - and about 6 more spices.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: It doesn't say anywhere in the recipe, but is it 4 oz of cooked beef and pork or raw? I'm assuming you have to cook the ground meats prior since I don't think the frying would cook the meats thoroughly.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: yes, make these at home. I have the Lithuanian recipe which asks you put a little cheese curd in the dough.  While visiting my relatives in Lithuania, we went to an open air restaurant which served about 10 or a dozen varieties, including dessert type 'cheburekai.'  (note a slightly different spelling).  The place was packed.  Also sold on the street from vending carts. : -D

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If you roll the dough out thinly enough, then the meat pie is a little larger, (about 10 inches diameter) and probably using 10 ounces total of meat would be a more filling amount of the meat mix than the 8 ounces the recipe suggests. They are delicious, and the kefir and onion give the meat a wonderful full and yummy flavor. It is very helpful to have two people working when making these, one to assemble the pies, the other to watch over them as they cook. Definitely going to make them again!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Turned out amazing! Did a few changes for convenience and taste: 
- Used "Uncooked Flour Tortillas" for the dough. A minor detail is not noticeable in the final taste test. It just so happens that both chebureki dough and the flour tortilla dough are made with the same exact dough ingredients (flour, water, and a little bit of fat). 
- Added garlic, dill, and parsley to the onion mix in the food processor.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Good recipe. It lacks a some sweetness though - which would round out the salty, sour, sweet trifecta. Substituted an extra spoon of regular soy with some agave syrup in lieu of the dark soy sauce. The rice noodles will be almost done if you wait an hour and let them soak in. So add those last in the line up. Just before the basil. Otherwise they will be too mushy. – A keeper!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If you can find Thai Black Soy you should use it - it's quite sweet. Otherwise another good sub might be a bit of Hoisin.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: the recipe is not asking for dark soy, it is asking for a "sweet thick soy sauce" like kecap manis

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Thai here, and it is more common to use spaghetti (yes!) or instant noodles for pad kee mao. Rice noodle is less preferred for this recipe :)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Sara are egg noodles ok

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Strongly recommend against prepping the noodles according to step 1; they turn into a congeal gooey mess. Rather cook them al dente according to the packet instructions, rinse under cold water, sprinkle with a little oil to prevent sticking

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Doug I wish i had read your comment before making this, and I wish NYT would fix the prep instructions. As you say, my prepped noodles turned into a congealed gooey mess upon cooking.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Some may not have Asian markets close by.  Try Trader Joe's baby bok choy, It's fresh and washed.  TJ's broccolini, mostly very good.

Like pad thai, I might put spoonfuls of crushed peanuts on this to make just a little more substantive.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’ll definitely make again.  I added mushrooms which I loved.  I added a little more soy sauce and a tablespoon of honey as I didn’t have the Thai black soy sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: made exactly as is, just tripled the sauce as suggested.  Very yummy and actually quite easy (after reading the directions 17 times)  Will make again!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: We used extra garlic, bok choy, and fresh rice noodles. Plus, we veganized this dish with vegan oyster sauce and vegan fish sauce. It was amazing and tasted restaurant-worthy.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: A nice variation if you don't happen to have oyster sauce and/or thick soy, or like me prefer the dish more "bright," is 1 tablespoon each of fish sauce and soy sauce, and 1 tsp. brown or coconut sugar, then finish the dish with a good squeeze of lime juice. Also instead of or in addition to gai lan, red or yellow bell peppers quickly sauteed are great.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Vicki b: if your cold tap water is safe to drink, then the water from the water heater is safe to drink and to use in cooking.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Definitely use hoisin if you can’t find the sweet soy sauce mentioned. Also soaking in water was key to prevent the dreaded “rice noodle clump.” I did 30-40 min in warm water and then because I didn’t think they were soft enough, drained water and soaked another 10 min in hot tap water.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was a really nice flavour combo. I added extra garlic and would add one or two more chilies next time. I’ve discovered the best way to do rice noodles in a dish like this is soak them in cold water for about an hour before adding straight to the wok, no precooking. I added these while the greens were cooking. A slash or two of water and they soften quickly but still hold up al dente. Yum

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Tripled the sauce, used hoisin as a substitute for dark soy sauce, cooked noodles according to package instructions and then put them in at the very end. Also cooked down the broccolini and king trumpet mushrooms in the garlic and chili paste with a little water before adding tofu. Finished with Thai basil. This was very good!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’ve read that hot tap water isn’t potable, maybe use hot water from another source?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Hot tap water is indeed potable, but hot water can dissolve lead if you have old lead pipes. So, either use cold tap water and heat it up, or filter your water and then heat it up.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Correct. The CDC, EPA, and even the NYT confirm. Hot water allows more contaminants to dissolve into it, worst of all lead. Even if you don’t have lead pipes, there is lead in the solder.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: What quantity is 6 gai lan or bok choy? 6 stems?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I did this but made it different because sometimes I’d rather experiment than go to the store — turned out well so I’m going to note here for myself or others if wanted. 

No oyster sauce, only one kind of soy. Subbed miso. Did rice vinegar instead of white. 

No protein, instead: peppers, carrots, cucumber, and tomato. Did the harder ones for 2-3 min then the soft ones for 2-3 more and then the noodles as directed. Mint and basil at the end. 

Tastes amazing!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Definitely double the sauce! The flavors were great but far too subtle, even the color appeared to be too light. If you can find fresh noodles, it’ll be even better. Nona Lim noodles are fantastic (from Oakland) if you have access to them.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious and nourishing. Next time I’ll make more sauce. Even without “enough” sauce, I inhaled this. Used 2 small Thai chiles. Used slightly less gai lan the full 6 bunches/stalks, but it was still very full of veggies. I used Taiwanese basil.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Delicious!  Made using tofu (cut into 1/4 cubes), broccoli (cut florets into 1/4 slices), and Italian basil...because that's what I had on hand.

Used vegan oyster sauce and vegan fish sauce (24 vegan brand fish sauce is the best on the market IMO).

If using regular broccoli, I recommend cooking it first to ensure it gets tender enough, then add the tofu, etc.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Needed twice as much sauce.  It was good, but not as good or as saucy as take out.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: To JMR: Chow Fun is the Chinese name for wide rice noodles. Maybe that will help. Good luck!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious. I doubled the sauce just to be safe but in the end didn't use much more than the original recipe. I also didn't have sweet soy so made my own with 2 parts soy to 3 parts brown sugar. Very good.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This was delicious!
I used baby Yu Choy because it was next to the bok choy in the "Good Fortune" supermarket, and I'd never used it.
I used a few teaspoons of chili crisp out of a jar instead of the peppers. I still used the minced garlic.
I could only find fettuccine-sized rice noodles.
I made it with shrimp and bay scallops. That was a little tricky because I almost overcooked the seafood while wilting the greens and stir-frying the noodles.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: You are like Jamie Oliver with chili jam.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Double the sauce if you're shooting for lunchtime-in-a-large US-city vibes.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Regarding the dark sweet soy sauce, many other recipes call for light soy sauce (1 tbs), dark soy sauce (1 tsp - its mainly for color and a little flavor), and sugar/palm sugar/honey.  This mix is basically kecap manis.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was stellar and took me right back to my travels through Thailand. Serving this without a lime wedge is criminal. Used rice vinegar instead of white (it’s what I had), and I think that was a game changer. Adding this to the rotation.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So sad that this recipe of the day, which I usually love, asks for so much basil which is so hard to come by in quantity where I live, this time of year. If it were august, I'd be golden but in early April - nada!! Sigh.......

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I loved this dish.  It was easy to prepare and only one pot to clean.  I couldn't find oyster sauce, so I just substituted with additional fish sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made it using impossible sausage meat, left the seeds in the peppers. It gave it a kick and was absolutely delicious! A keeper.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Really good recipe. Ground pork needed a bit more cook time. Next time I would probably sear it on high in 1 Tb oil first, remove to a plate, turn heat down and add remaining oil, cook garlic and chiles, then add greens and pork. Greens may need a bit more time as well, depending on variety. I used 12 oz each of noodles and pork, doubled gai lan and sauce for 4 hungry people and it was perfect. Kepis manis worked very well. Imagine dark soy would be a fine sub, though not as sweet.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The recipe is improved by slightly more sauce. I found a 1.5 recipe of the sauce provided the perfect ratio of sauce to noodles (and meat and veg).

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Added 1 tbs make syrup, and doubled sauce recipe, but it seemed like it needed more sweetness, also only had mushroom sauce not oyster sauce. Used both baked crispy tofu and shrimp for protein and 2 heads of bok choy. Eli thought it needed grated carrots.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was great but next time I would make a bit more sauce and also add egg to the dish. To sweeten the sauce, in lieu of Thai soy sauce, I used a teaspoon of palm sugar to sweeten it which is a common ingredient in Thai dishes. It’s nuttier and smoother than brown sugar. Overall a nice dish!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used 16 oz fresh rice noodles instead of dried and would reduce to 12 oz in the future. In the absence of sweet soy sauce, I used that amount of regular and added 1 tbsp loosely packed brown sugar. It was quite good!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Cut all sauces in half to reduce sodium. Make liquid volume up with vegetable broth. Double vegetable

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is sooooo delicious. Followed the recipe to the letter.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Doubled recipe, couldn’t find the right noodles or Thai black soy sauce, added green beans

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I'm not sure how much bok choy to use?  Anyone have any suggestions?  Also, my wide rice noodles did not stay intact once I added them to the wok.  Does that mean I over soaked them? They became 2 inch segments.  Can anyone advise for the next time?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I highly recommend Shrimp with Gai Lan and Thai basil  - you can even use the frozen Argentinian red shrimp from Trader Joe's and just soften them up straight out of the freezer with hot water. It's so quintessentially Thai and tastes pretty darn close to being in Bangkok. i make this all the time now - 100x better than the local restaurants.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I boil a large pan of water before I prep my ingredients.  When boiling, turn off heat, add noodles.  Stir only occasionally.  They are white, soft, and ready to stir fry once you are ready to assemble dish.  Does not take 40 min. to pre-soften that way.  This is delicious and I will make it again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Make it easier: place rice noodles in a large bowl and pour boiling water over them. Let them sit till soft but not too soft. I cut out the hot peppers because my family does not like spicy food. I added chopped peanuts -- very good!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Katie P, all the ingredients for this recipe are gluten free except for the oyster sauce and soy sauce. Most grocery stores sell gluten free soy sauce (often called tamari sauce), and several companies make a GF oyster sauce (Kikkoman, Lee Kum Kee, Wok Mei). You might have to order the GF oyster sauce online, but it’s worth having in hand to make your own GF Asian food at home.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: A family winner! I made it vegan by subbing vegan versions of fish & oyster sauces and tofu. Trippled the sauce as others suggested and marinated the tofu in it. Used double the recommended amount of noodles, using pad see ew style, for we noodle lovers. Leftovers several days later were still delicious. Will definitely make again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Next time would use only 1/4 lb pork. Liked broccolini. Would add bell pepper, tomatoes, onion. Could also try egg instead or in addition to pork.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I liked this, and didn't think it needed sweetness. I used half hoisin and half dark soy sauce for the Thai black soy sauce. I stir fried a mix of veggies and didn't add any protein. I used 2 thai chiles and it wasn't spicy at all; I put pickled jalapenos on it when I was eating and that was good. My only issue was with the rice noodles--they didn't soften, so I boiled them and they overcooked--but that isn't the recipe's fault. I'll probably try this again and be more careful with the noodles.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: To those engaging in the “don’t use hot tap water for cooking” debate: This newspaper, article link below, quotes the E.P.A. saying that “cold tap water should always be used for preparing baby formula, cooking and drinking. It also warns that boiling water does not remove lead but can actually increase its concentration.” More information is at www.epa.gov/lead https://www.nytimes.com/2008/01/29/health/29real.html?smid=nytcore-ios-share&referringSource=articleShare

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this pretty much as written, except I realized at the last minute that I needed to double the recipe, so I made it with half the pork. It was delicious and there was plenty of pork. I think the greens are essential.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious!  (Even though we did not find Thai basil leaves which I know are essential to this dish! - - - - that will have to wait until the summer.)  We used Thai wheat noodles from Trader Joe's.  We didn't have the Thai soy sauce, so we used hoisin sauce instead.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I really liked this after a few adjustments. Instead of the noodle directions, cooked a box of dried pad Thai noodles (couldn’t find wide) and decreased cooking time by a minute. I drained and poured ice water over. Added a thinly sliced green pepper and sautéed along with peppers and garlic. Chopped 6 baby bok choy as the green. I added an additional tsp of Thai soy sauce and a Tbs of oyster sauce. Caution: Thai soy sauce is more like molasses. Do not just sub reg soy for it. See others’ tips.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: #1 take away - AL DENTE noodles. Don't allow them to sit in warm water. They will be mush by the end of the cooking process.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Definitely triple the sauce, add cilantro if basil is not available. Hoisin replaces the need for sweet soy. This is delicious. Thanks fellow cooks for your suggestions and alternative substitutes.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I liked this recipe a lot! I will make it again and use a full pound of pork since that's the only quantity it's sold at my grocery store and will bump up other ingredients. I think it tasted even better as leftovers the next day after the sauce melded to the ingredients. Next time I will find red serrano peppers since there was a lot of green with the other ingredients. I also liked the suggestion to add chopped peanuts to the top. Very good!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This was fine, but did not compare to the takeout drunken noodles that I love so much. Guess I’ll have to keep ordering!

(I should note I had trouble finding the right noodles and sweet soy sauce, so used regular rice noodles and hoisin   honey. Maybe those would have made the difference!?)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was a big hit with family and friends. I doubled the recipe to feed 5 except I tripled the sauce and did not double the bok choy.  Also used 2 jalapeños so it wouldn’t be too spicy for 3 teenagers.  It was different from the drunken noodles I get in restaurants but I’ll definitely make this again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this dish with pork and broccolini previously and this time with firm tofu and baby bok choy.  Both were very good.  Having said that, even though it says in the last step "When the noodles have absorbed all the sauce ...", I think it would have been improved if there was a bit of sauce to coat everything.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: SO good. Made exactly to the instructions, except I cooked the noodles based on package instructions. Used kecap manis. If you can find Thai basil, it's worth it!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe is absolutely delicious !!! Someone recommended tripling the sauce recipe and I totally agree it really makes the recipe! I didn’t have any oyster sauce so I added an extra two tablespoons of fish sauce, and I saw some recommendations for adding hoisin which I also added and it was great ! I also added about an inch and half of ginger chopped with the peppers and garlic. This was so good ! Will definitely be making this again !

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is the worst recipe. The noodles turned into a mess of glue. The entire dish was terrible and I followed the instructions exactly. This might have been edible with regular pasta noodles.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The first time I made it, I had some of the same issues many other cooks have flagged (gummy disaster meteor of noodles) but I just made it again because I liked the flavors enough to try. This time was much better largely because I used udon instead of rice noodles. I think there must be a ton of variation in rice noodles for cooks to have had such different results and udon worked beautifully. Also don’t skip the sweet soy-cheap online and balances the salt! If you like heat, don’t seed chiles

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Must use thai basil, not regular

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If you can find fresh green peppercorns, add them in as well. They add a nice texture and burst of favor when you bit into them.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Can someone tell me the phonetic name of the noodles so that I can ask for them over the phone at an Asian grocery store? The most "exotic" noodle our local grocery store carries is Rigatoni! I drive to an Asian store where little English is spoken for a pick-up order. Thank you!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I'm answering my own question! Perhaps if I'd read the ingredient list more carefully, I might have noticed that the name of the noodles is right there - I blame age!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is legit. Make your own noodles fresh, though, if you can.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Recommend doubling the sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Substitution for oyster sauce (shellfish allergy)?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Wegman's sells a vegetarian oyster sauce that would work.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Soy sauce + worcestershire sauce (if you can eat anchovies) + fish sauce.

I don't eat shellfish either.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Since another reader recommended doubling the sauce ingredients, let me add this caveat: I doubled the sauce and the sodium level was off the charts (from soy, fish, and oyster sauces)—almost inedible. I think this is a really good dish but either use low-sodium products (I didn’t) or limit their use.  The broccolini worked well and I added torn Tuscan kale.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I failed on making rice noodles so on to plan B. Only had udon on hand and think any noodles can work. Added some hoisin to the sauce for the veg used broccoli and mushrooms and spinach at the end. Added some of the udon cooking water to get the noodles perfect and closed the dish with some firm tofu. Tofu and udon cooking water really brings a nice creamy Saucy finish to the dish. What a great recipe to add on to and make it your own. Thx!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this last night with large shrimp & gai lan. it was divine - fresh and rather less sweet than from a restaurant which is a good thing! But having lots of Thai basil is critical to creating the signature 'thai' flavor of this; how on earth could sweet basil (which I love for other reasons) work as a sub? I will say it wasn't easy to find the fresh thai basil, even in nyc - had to go to Chinatown (Bangkok Grocery on Mosco St). Where else can you get this?? Please tell me.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Nearby market had Thai Basil and Chinese Broccoli - first time I have tried that vegetable and it is excellent - no bitterness at all.  Made as directed except added some minced ginger, since our local favorite restaurant’s version has ginger.  Really good and agree that more sauce would make it more typical of Drunken Noodles found in most Thai/Fusion/Asian restaurants found in the US.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is a great recipe! It’s pretty important to use the ingredients and not sub. Chilies, thai basil and sweet soy are all necessary. I made it with ground chicken.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Agree with soaking the noodles for up to an hour, but recipe could use a bit more spice and extra chilies and/or lime zest help.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This made a medium dinner for two. It is delicious but could use a lot more  of broccoli, kale or bokchoy. I used kale and bokchoy & was glad they cooked well at the same time & temp. I bought real Thai noodles and they are great. Will need to raise the sauce quantity for added greens. It was a big hit.  Will try pad see ew next.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Great! I subbed lime juice for the vinegar and mixed in some edamame. Will definitely add to the dinner rotation.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was absolutely delicious. I'm fortunate to live near a very large Asian grocery store. While I couldn't find the black soy sauce that was mentioned I did find a thick soy glaze that worked perfectly. I used Italian basil. The store did have the Chinese broccoli and the bird's eye Chilis. I will definitely be making this again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: In my household I can’t cook with super hot chilies so I substitute jalapeño with the seeds scraped out. That gives enough heat for everyone even though I like some heat occasionally.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have lime leaves, as suggested in the introduction. But when would I add them? I assume earlier than the basil so they can flavor the sauce?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was good. I used green beans, red pepper and mushrooms, and realized later I missed the tomatoes that come with the take our version. I wondered why the author used dried noodles instead of fresh--a quick search of Thai recipe sites showed that fresh was the preference. I bought a good local brand and wow they were hard to deal with. Separating them was a huge pain (I used hot water). So maybe that's why.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I riffed on this: used random pieces of left over chicken, pork chops, and broccoli. Like others, I needed a sweet note since I didn’t have the sauce ingredient listed. I added mushrooms and didn’t put the proteins in until the dish was finished. The residual heat was enough to reheat them. I used sambal olek instead of chilis. Adding it to the rotation!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Been working my way through the Thai noodle recipes, using a Dutch oven since I don’t have a wok. Any suggestions to keep the noodles from burning onto the bottom of the pan?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I found that turning down the heat helped with that a lot.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I altered this recipe to accommodate the ingredients that were available. I used the jalapeños in the freezer that I saved from our garden. I used pan-fried tofu. I could only find pad thai noodles and they worked perfectly. I added lime leaves. Before sautéing the garlic and peppers, I sautéed a few sliced shiitake mushrooms. I made the sauce vegan by leaving out the fish and oyster sauces add substituting dulse flakes. To make up for the lack of Thai soy sauce, I used Sucanat. Delish.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was great, but I forgot the basil! I didn't have any hot chiles so substituted 1 jalepeno. I also didn't have any Thai black soy sauce so added a little balsamic vinegar (tsp) and brown sugar (tsp). Also some sesame seeds and red chile flakes for heat. So tasty.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I really enjoyed this recipe. The sauce had a lot of depth and the basil really sweetened up the dish. I couldn't find Thai soy sauce so I used 1 tsp of dark brown sugar and I think it helped. I bet honey would also work! I used pork but can't wait to try this with tofu next.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was on my radar to cook for a few weeks. One of my favorite dishes in Oakland / Berkeley. If you can find them, use the fresh wide rice noodles. Makes a world of difference. It’s such an easy dish to add some extra veggies… cook them first, put aside and then cook the noodles and garlic in coconut oil. Tonight it was cauliflower, trumpet and beech shrooms. Plus left over pork chop. After noodles are pan fried to a nice degree add the sauce and then the previously cooked veggies and meat.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Hoisin sauce

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: We made double the sauce as it seemed to little

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This one is a hit! And obviously a keeper since we’ve made it twice…first time with Chinese broccoli and second with Italian kale. They were equally good. I had no Thai soy sauce and used plain soy. No one missed it

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Anyone know the benefit of the long soak of noodles vs boiling per package to al dente and rinsing ? I’ve never seen this method. Thanks!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: 3x the sauce - watch the amount of protein and noodles (I added 16oz ground turkey and the whole bag of cooked noodles, which needed more sauce)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Cooked it, loved it.  Perfectly good as written, having said that I added 1TB chili crisp, 1TB dark Chinese vinegar, reduced the noodles by 1/3.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Sauce mixture needs brown sugar. Use at least 4 serranos fordouble recipe

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I followed some of the tips below. I used hoisin since I didn't have the dark soy -- great flavor. Used jalapeños instead of Serrano plus chopped the basil and added chopped unsalted peanuts. Delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I did this with tofu and gai lan. I used a mortar and pestle but minced both first because it can only do so much. The noodles were mushy, so I recommend erring on the side of even more undercooked before adding them to the pan. It also had almost no taste to it, even after adding all the sauce. I'd double all the spices and sauces if you are going for any real flavor. I added a bunch of soy sauce to add more flavor after the fact, but for all the effort this dish requires I was disappointed.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Molly - Did you use fresh, garlic cloves or bottled pre-minced garlic - pre-minced would really decrease flavor. The authentic Thai version is always made with pork. NYT adds tofu as an option but probably did not test it as tofu is plain and simple a flavor neutralizer - every single dish we ever make using tofu instead of animal protein requires a lot more flavoring - it is just the nature of the beast (or lack of beast! LOL) since animal proteins contain many, many delicious flavor compounds.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Sub Thai soy sauce with regular and 1 tbs honey

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used a full lb ground pork and added a red bell pepper along with 2 tsp of ginger (added with the garlic and chile). A couple of substitutions - I didn’t have Birdseye chile, so I used 2 Tbsp chile garlic paste. I didn’t have fish sauce or oyster sauce, so for the sauce I used 3 Tbsp low sodium tamari soy sauce, 1-2 Tbsp Worcestershire sauce, 1 1/2 Tbsp seasoned rice vinegar and 1-2 honey. Needed a little salt at the end. Amazing! Will make again!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was so good. I used bok choy and had to sub jalapenos and pretty much followed the recipe as is. Yum!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Use spaghetti or linguine for the noodle

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Add brown sugar to soy sauce if don’t have DARK soy sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Reduce oil to 2 T

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Is it possible to make this gluten free??

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious! I bought what I thought was Thai basil at the Asian grocery but it was not basilly at all. However, it was Delia us even with the non-basil. I used bok choy and I would definitely try other vegetables. The flavors were great.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: How many cups of greens for those of us who can't find 6 Gai lan?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Use “Healthy Boy” black sweet Thai soy sauce. It should be thick like molasses. If not, it’s the wrong one.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Great flavor. I marinated the chicken thighs in the sauce over night. I also used jalapeño peppers instead of serene as I don't like super spicy. Then pretty much followed the recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I substituted bowtie pasta for the noodles and it was also really good.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious! Sub agave for the black soy sauce, ground turkey worked great, added slice mushrooms, a few green onions, and used two serrano peppers. Heat was just right but would make extra sauce next time, no leftover and dish flavorful but definitely not saucy. Run cooked noodles under hot water and quickly drain before adding to the mixture and they noodles will separate just fine.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Loved this. Made a few changes:
- Couldn't find dark soy sauce so added a bit more soy and some brown sugar 
- Didn't have ground meat so thinly sliced chicken thighs
- Added 2 chopped tomatoes, added in with chicken & broccoli to cook down a little
- Used sambal oelek (a few tablespoons) instead of fresh chilis. Put it in with the sauce.
Came out delicious. Very flavorful.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Not my fave, but decent

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Doubled the recipe to cook for three with a little left over. Cooked exactly as directed, with dark ground turkey, and broccolini from the garden. Could've used a little more acid, definitely lime leaves/zest, or lime juice. Also would sub a little of the regular soy sauce with a touch more fish sauce to make it more "Thai". Very Americanized version, but definitely good!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Couldn’t find thick rice noodles, so I ended up using wide egg noodles but had to double up on the sauce. Also added bell pepper and onion that I cooked first and subbed egg for the meat. Turned out great!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
My sauce ratio: 1/4 cup oyster sauce. 2 tablespoon soy sauce
1 tablespoon fish sauce. 1 tablespoon distilled white vinegar. 1 tablespoon Thai black soy sauce.

Used greens from 3 heads of baby bok choy (saved bottoms for separate stirfry).

Used 1/2 lb tofu and 1/4  lb shrimp. Add shrimp with greens.
Used sweet basil but hope for thia/holy next time, if available
add 1/4 lb sprouts with 50% with greens and 50% at end with basil
Add green onions, chopped peanuts, lime wedge as garnish.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
First time I’ve ever left a comment….. I made this as directed (with bok choy as the green element and Italian basil from our garden as the basil element.). I used a combo of ground chicken and tofu for the protein.

It was just …. Lacking.  It lacked protein, veggies, flavor.

This is one that’s going in the dustbin, not the rotation.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used ground pork for protein and wish I had doubled it. I easily tripled the sauce which gave the noodles good flavor, but also should have cut back on the hoisin (which I added in lieu of black soy) and gone heavier on the spices. I stir fried broccolini, mushrooms, and peppers with red peppers separately and served noodles over bed of veggies. Good overall, lots of flavor. However, did not taste like drunken noodles per say…

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The sauce isn’t good.  There is no sugar at all and vinegar is not used in any other drunken noodle recipe I’ve seen   Had to add 2 teaspoons of sugar to make it edible.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Great recipe but double the gai lan. The six stems reduce so much that they're hardly noticeable.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: These were divine!  Even leftovers were delicious just microwaved with a sprinkle of water

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Struggle to find a good gf oyster sauce—any suggestions?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Lee Kum Kee Premium is good. Read the ingredients list. You want more oyster, less sugar/corn syrup. If sugar/corns syrup is listed first in the ingredients list, it will be more blandly sweet and less oystery.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have to agree with others - I missed the "sweetness" that I've come to expect from this dish.  Disappointing since I had gone out an purchased Thai Sweet Soy sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The sauce was too salty and dull. I added ginger, pomegranate molasses(because it is sweet and I have too much) and omitted the regular soy sauce. I love Chinese broccoli and Thai basil. I froze then crumbled and stir fried the  tofu for the protein element. We liked where I ended up, and I appreciate this recipe for the starting point, and maybe Pad Kee Mao is a specific recipe that one shouldn't change. But without making changes this recipe for me did not work.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this tonight but doubled the sauce and added more garlic and Bok choy. I added green onion as well and doubled the basil. I don’t like sweet, so I liked the sauce as is. Family loved it!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used 1 Tbsp of Chinese black vinegar in place of the white vinegar and sweet soy since I already had it. Added mushrooms. Next time I would cook the pork first and drain off some of the fat. I would probably also try dried chilis or Szechuan pepper.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
noodle packages tend to be 16 oz, so you usually have to double everything (notes below are for full package):
- quadruple the sauce recipe (or just two times if doing half of the noodle packages). add pinch of sugar and sriracha to it too.
- use ~10 baby bok choy
- add 2 T or more of fresh ginger w/ the serrano peppers and garlic
- add 1 onion, sliced before the protein
- cilantro instead of basil- save some for garnishing on top
- add a 1/2 cup of halved grape tomatoes with the cilantro

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was a very delicious and fast weeknight dinner. I live in Canada and could only find ~900g of fresh wide rice noodles in my Asian grocery store. I didn’t want to use only half and wasn’t sure what the difference was between that and 8oz of dried so I used all of them and had to double everything else to make it tasty. Tasted like it was from a Thai restaurant!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
So good with ground pork! We had last minute guests so I doubled the recipe and tossed in  some mushrooms and a thinly sliced zucchini - otherwise followed the recipe exactly. 
Double recipe served four with not a bite left over.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious! A new favorite! My only notes are to double the sauce, noodles and protein so there are leftovers.  Will make again soon.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is great. I always use tofu, vegan fish/oyster sauce, and broccolini/broccoli rabe, which is what my local grocery has. Last night I used four bird’s eye chilis and thought it could use a touch more heat, but otherwise, it’s extremely well balanced, hard to screw up, and the textures are perfect.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Hot tap water may contain basically debris, chemicals, and solids deposited over the years. The amount depends on the unit's age and especially how frequently it's drained. To avoid even a hint of off-flavor, I prefer to heat cold tap water for this sort of application.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I definitely agree with those warning against step one for the noodles, they turned into an inedible goo once added. I'll just cook them like I normally would if I ever try this again

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this mostly as written. The noodles did end up soaking about an hour and a half, but they were fine — no problem with them clumping at all. This was delicious, and I’ll definitely make it again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Tripling the sauce is a must. I used ground pork, broccolini, green beans and enoki mushrooms. Didn’t have Thai chiles so used serrano but don’t think I should have seeded them since the dish wasn’t spicy at all (I added sambal as I was eating). I liked it but was hoping it would taste like take out; it didn’t.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious. Doubled the recipe and tripled the sauce. Also added spinach and it was absolutely delicious. Can’t wait for leftovers tomorrow.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly (even used the dark thick soy sauce) and the noodles were fine but  the whole thing was just a bland salt bomb.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made 2x sauce to go with 5 twists of wide noodles, probably should’ve stuck to three twists. Also try to separate the noodles before and after cooking

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: How crucial is the basil to this dish?  I was planning on making it tonight for dinner (even got the choy sum) but don't have any basil in the house.  If it's important, I'll just make something else.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made as directed, except I used a lot more Thai sweet soy sauce.  It's a very delicious recipe.  It does not remind me of drunken noodles (which usually use more of yellow noodle), but it's easy and tasty.  If you can find packaged Gai Lan that's mostly leaf and less stem, it works great in recipe.  Double or triple the sauce and you'll have a new weeknight staple.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Really easy and delicious! Do not skimp on the basil!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I added 1 tsp each: red Thai curry paste, sesame oil and honey to the sauce. Kept the same amount of sauce. Crumbled the tofu. Added lime zest at the end with the Basil. My family thought it was fantastic. I thought it was quick and easy. Thank you!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is a delicious recipe!  Unfortunately I had to modify it a bit because some of the ingredients were not available in my grocery store. I couldn't find the wide thai noodles so I just used the thinner ones, and I substituted a teaspoon of  gochujang for the Thai soy sauce.  It provided a nice accent.  I chose tofu for my protein which soaked up the sauce (I doubled that as several commenters suggested...which gave it a lovely flavor!).

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was very good, but I would definitely up the spice next time we make it. We were able to find all the ingredients at our local Asian market too, so no subs necessary.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Apr 25 2023 used 3 Thai red peppers

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Almost all recipes written by Thai natives for this dish call for Golden Mountain Sauce, which is sold at most Asian groceries. Is that the non name-brand "Thai black soy sauce" that is called for in this recipe?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is delish. Only changes were sesame oil instead of whatever oil was recommended, whole wheat spaghetti for rice noodles because it needed to be used and hoisin instead of fish sauce. So good. Next time will add some agave and chopped cashew. Yum.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Oh my goodness, this is delicious. We substituted ground turkey for the meat and used 4 of the red bird chiles. Tip on the noodles is to not overcook (it's easy to overcook them) or over-stir (they'll break). This will be going into my regular rotation!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I liked the recipe but it did not taste like the Drunken Noodles (Pad Kee Mao) that we get in our local Thai restaurants.  Any suggestions on what could be missing?  I'm thinking that the type of dark soy might be the key, after reading the comments.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Easy fast & flavorful!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Definitely needs more sauce. Added some honey to balance out the sauce. Don't bother following the rice noodle instructions here, just follow the package instructions.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I had a 14 oz package of noodles, so doubled the sauce. I was able to find sweet black Thai soy sauce at an Asian market, so the sauce was nicely sweet. We added extra veggies (onion, carrot, red bell pepper) on the advice of my kids. I also substituted crushed red peppers for the fresh chiles, which was easier. I’m going to be more careful to keep the noodles firmer before adding them to the stir fry. We all loved it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made it probably third time and every time I’ve doubled the recipe - it’s a bit unwieldy but doable. I think it gets better each time! I soak the noodles per the recipe w good results - err on the side of under-soaking. Flavors are less sweet than takeout but I realize the Chinese dark soy I use is less sweet than Thai dark soy so I might add a bit of molasses or something next time. Sauce per recipe is more than what you need, about 1 extra tbsp (for the qty in recipe) sounds about right.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe is fantastic. I did not have sweet soy so I added a teaspoon of sugar to balance of the salt and it was great. I served with a slice of lime. Incredible. Will be making this again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Mostly posting so I can remember what I did.

With only a college student's pantry to work with, I made the sauce with 4 tbs soy sauce, 2 tbs fish sauce, and around 1/4 cup of dark brown sugar to sub for sweet soy sauce and oyster sauce. This worked pretty well. 1/4 cup sugar matched the sweetness I would expect from a restaurant but I suspect is a bit sweeter than what this recipe intends.

The thai basil is key to making this dish taste right. Can def throw in a lot of veggies tho.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I gave this one star because if you follow the instructions, you end up with broken, gelatinous rice noodles.  The picture looks like tagliatelle pasta.  Cook the tagliatelle noodles to al dente minus 2 minutes and run cold water over to prepare them to be finished in the wok.  If you use tofu, pre cook the tofu until it is golden brown and crispy.  Also, double the sauce if using tofu.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Loved this. Made with tofu - medium firm broke down a bit but that was fine for me. 

Also might blanch the gai lan in salted water next time to add flavor and also reduce cooking time in the wok.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was really delicious.  I made it with shrimp, bok choy and I added some red bell pepper for color and additional veg.  I didn't have chilies, so I added some sambal paste we had at home.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Quite good! Used the dutch oven instead of a large fry pan and really liked the results! Also used the "square rice noodles" Not noodles but square "noodles" and like the result-the noodles did not get all squished together. Agree not too sweet but you can add some if needed. Lots of fresh basil  and the protein a combo of a shrimp and 8 ounces pork.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Fantastic recipe. Have made it many times, subbing out different vegetables and proteins (if not using ground cut the protein thin so they cook quickly!). And even used a Scotch Bonnet pepper in place of serrano or red bird’s-eye once. Which added great flavor and I felt the perfect amount of heat

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This turned out great. I was skeptical that the sauce would rival my favorite restaurant, but it all came together. I made extra sauce and used leftovers to flavor a chicken and mushroom soup, perfection! Based on other comments, I used a little honey in place of the black soy sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I’ve made this quite a few times, & it’s excellent. Using a Dutch oven is my new favorite trick—especially since my apt. has an electric stove on which I can’t use a wok. Sticking is minimal, incredibly! (Except using tofu. Don’t use tofu.)

I hew closely to the recipe, except: 1) It’s best with 1 pound of fresh wide rice noodles—no soaking required, just separating. (I get these at 99 Ranch here in SoCal.) 2) I replace the Thai black soy sauce with roughly equal parts Chinese dark soy & sugar.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Loved this dish. Hot and spicy and garlicky — reminds me of hot steamy nights in Bangkok. I didn’t add Thai basil because I didn’t have any (and I don’t like Italian basil as a substitute). I’ll probably double the sauce next time. I used pad see ew noodles and put them in hot tap water for 20 minutes before adding to the stir fry. Looking forward to adding that I basil next time I can find it. Yummy!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious recipe. May add a bunch of scallions and an egg or two next time. Added a little sugar to the sauce (didn’t have the Thai soy) and it was perfect.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used a 14oz bag of Three Ladies Brand XL dried rice stick (pho) noodles. I doubled the sauce and everything else. The proportions came out just right for me.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was delicious and easy.  I could not find Thai black soy sauce so added 2 teaspoons of brown sugar instead.  I added 4 seeded Thai chiles but it wasn't hot at all (but that's because the chiles I picked happened to be mild in heat).  Would definitely make again!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made mushroom-based vegan oyster sauce. As for the dark soy sauce, who can suggest a gf option?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Came out great, and super easy to make.  Didn’t have the thick soy sauce, just doubled up on the regular soy sauce.  Also used rice stick which worked well.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
The noodles came out perfect. No sticking. No mushiness. 

I will definitely make this, or a version of this, again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Excellent and easy. Doubled the sauce and added some sugar but otherwise followed this recipe closely. Our local Thai restaurant has nothing to be worried about, but this was fun to make at home.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is one of my go-to recipes, either for just my wife and I or for company. Always draws raves. I do a summer version on the grill - grill the chicken separately while roasting the potatoes with onions and red pepper. Toss them together, add the sauce, parsley and herbs. Enjoy with a glass or two of chilled white wine on the deck. Can't be beat.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: A winner in our family! I used what I had, so made a few changes. Used Pad Thai noodles and added thinly sliced pork, broccoli, thinly sliced red pepper and bok choy. My family is not into spicy so I used 2 seeded jalapeños as my chiles and added sriracha and red pepper flakes to individual portions. No thick soy either so I subbed in hoisin as others suggested. Added zest of one lime with the basil (mostly Italian since my garden has yet to yield enough Thai basil.). Doubled the sauce for 1/2 package of noodles and it was the perfect amount. Definitely going into the rotation.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This was delicious and easy and very few dishes dirty! Definitely recommend.
Next time I would add carrots in addition to the Chinese broccoli and leave seeds in 2 of the 4 chilies. We wanted it a bit spicier.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this dish for my family, and it was super good. So many compliments! :)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was surprisingly delicious for how simple it is! The basil really brings it all together. I subbed hoisin for oyster sauce and only had italian basil. Make this!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: After reading comments, I used spaghetti and hoisin sauce instead of Thai soy sauce.  We enjoyed it though it was a bit greasy for me so I plan to cook the ground pork first and drain the fat or reduce the oil.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Just here to say that my husband and I enjoy this recipe at least every couple weeks in the summer months when my own Thai basil is plentiful. It's just superb...

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Great recipe!!! Can add quartered cremini mushrooms, and add or substitute sweet pepper strips (bell, red, yellow, etc.) for the gai lan. Sauce is fairly salty, don't need to alter it, but use judiciously. One Thai chili wasn't enough, though it's hard to gauge chili heat before cooking - can pass sambal olkek sauce at table if more spice/heat level is desired.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If possible, use gai lan bc the slightly bitter note is a nice counterpoint. Easy peasy meal, ready in < 10 min. Having some extra sauce in the fridge makes a last-minute meal effortless.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’ve used golden mountain sauce

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Great recipe! Added some minced chili and ginger in with the garlic. Also some fresh basil right at the end really added a delightful freshness to the dish! Will definitely add to the regular rotation.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: No basil.  So I used cilantro.  Substitution was acceptable but not authentic.  Cooked flat wheat pasta instead of recipe suggestion.  Cooked short of al dente.  Rinsed them & added tiny amount of unflavored sesame seed oil and then tossed gingerly.  Completed the recipe as per directions. Excellent.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: followed recipe almost completely except 1) doubled sauce 2) had to use dried basil (it is winter) 3) added a little hoisin and Mirin

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Sauce required tweaking but recipe was yummy and quick to execute

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I love this recipe but over time, have simplified.  Our local Asian market sells the fresh wide rice noodles in cellophane packages. The woman who runs the store told me to use 3 rows of noodles for 2 people, place them on a plate, cover with wet paper towels, cook in microwave 3 minutes. Let cool a bit before separating the noodles. 

Once you have completed step 6, lay noodles on top for a bit, then stir fry them with sauce.  Cook until noodles are done.  I also make extra sauce and use it all

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Also, I top the whole thing with chopped kafir lime leaves and chopped peanuts

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I've been making this for years; the recipe is on the chef's blog, Hot Thai Kitchen.  Notes on sauces: Pearl River Bridge Chinese Dark soy sauce is a good substitute if you can't find Healthy Boy black. Three Crabs fish sauce gives a more mellow umami than Squid Brand, or splurge with Red Boat. Lee Kum Kee's Premium Oyster Sauce is decent if you can't find Maekrua Brand. If you can find it, Healthy Boy Mushroom soy sauce shines in this recipe. This is fast, simple, and delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Unfortunately, this recipe lacks any instruction on how not to eat the entire thing by oneself in minutes. Wow.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Pailin has a great online site with lots of in-depth tid-bits for those of us unfamiliar with the wide array and branding of SE Asian sauces. She goes into great detail of her favorite fish sauce brands for use in particular dishes or how to chose the best palm sugar puck or how to properly brew your own tamarind. It’s been so helpful to me!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Soaking the noodles first in hot water is key. For YEARS I just boiled the noodles like Italian pasta, and they either stuck together in long strips or overcooked. This recipe has it right (other recipes I found just say "cook according to directions on package, which only helps if there actually are directions on the package).

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Mushroom soy sauce is designed to be used in these noodles. Just substitute for regular soy sauce and you'll get a restaurant-style rich flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I doubled the recipe and used broccolini and skinless chicken thigh. Cooked in a Dutch oven. When I put noodles in the pan I was afraid I made a mistake by doubling it bc the pan was brimming to the top but it turned out delicious!! I didn’t want to buy dark soy so used substitute I saw on another recipe on  NYT (for 1 tbsp dark soy, 1/2 tsp molasses and the rest w regular soy). The only change I would make next time is to use less sugar. Otherwise, impressive recipe, better than takeout

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Amazon sells a vegan fish sauce called Fysh Sauce, for those of us with a seafood allergy. It's made from five types of seaweed.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: More gai lan (it wilted about as much as spinach), cook in two batches. Doubled the egg, definitely needed it. Add red bell pepper. Generally needs more veggies! Subbed meat for tofu, seared then soaked in soy sauce then seared again. Should be cubes of tofu.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Recipe works best if you do the meat and eggs first, set aside, then do veggies, add noodles and sauce, and then add back eggs and meat at the end with last bit of sauce.  Noodles came out fine as directed here.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @haywire I would try soaking the noodles for less. I actually took the noodles out of the water when they still had a bite and had to soften them up a bit by adding water in the pan while frying. Erring on the side of soaking them a bit hard is better than oversoaking them bc you can fix them in the pan, IMO.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Couldn’t find wide rice noodles so used thin ones; agree with the other comments to add noodles when less soft because you can easily adjust with additional water while cooking. Made mine vegetarian with regular broccoli; did not have the dark soy sauce but still tasted good. Used far less sugar than recommended. I may try honey next time. Biggest issue is that I added all the sauce but still seemed on dry side. Would double sauce next time (I like it saucy!)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Subbed cubed tofu cutlet (marinated in Tamari) and 8 oz halved shiitakes and oyster mushrooms for the meat. Used about 7 stalks of Chinese broccoli.  Used vegan oyster sauce and vegan fish sauce.  Regular wide egg noodles from the grocery store.  Doubled the sauce but didn't need it.  Really excellent!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Don’t hate me for switching this up. I opted to follow the sauce recipe from Chongchitnant’s Hot Thai Kitchen website which varies a bit from this one. Used baby bok choy, shrimp and some sliced shitakes. Absolutely delish! I dare say restaurant quality. Looking forward to the leftovers.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: "Thai black soy sauce 'to taste'": Since I've never made this recipe before, I'm at a loss how much to use. Wouldn't the tasting have to happen at the very end, when the other ingredients are added?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Really good recipe, made almost exactly as directed.  The only change was to remove brocolli and egg (I used American brocolli, blanched 1 minute then shocked with cold water) from wok.  I added a little more oil then tossed the noodles and sauce by themselves to caramelized.  I finished by tossing the meat, brocolli, and egg back in, added a couple splashes of black soy, et voila!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Good quality sauce ingredients and proper cooking of the noodles are key to this great recipe. As someone mentioned, I used Pearl River Bridge Dark Soy, which also works as a lower salt, rich sub for regular soy sauce. I tested the noodles regularly during the soaking until they were flexible, but still toothy. I used broccoli raab cut into bite size lengths. Finally, add the sauce 1 Tbs at a time until you get what you like (I only used 2/3s of the sauce).

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Just made this following the method exactly and it was truly scrumptious. I used wide egg noodles, which were an excellent substitute and caramelized nicely. I also used a mix of broccolini and bok choy. Using all the sauce and sugar provided seemed to strike a great balance. Not too sweet in my opinion, just hard to stop eating.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I love pad see ew, but making this recipe made me realize how unhealthy this dish is! The sauce is shockingly sodium-intensive, for one thing. If I were to make it again I would definitely use low-sodium soy sauce since it requires so much of it. I couldn't find gluten free dark soy sauce so combined soy sauce and blackstrap molasses, following a recommendation I found online. I also used a giant bag of Chinese broccoli, and not the few sticks recommended.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Love this. Have made it a few times, most recently with bok choy, mushrooms, and a yellow onion, and ground pork. The sliced pork is so much tastier than ground though.

Pretty forgiving on the veggies so you can add whatever seems good on the day you make it. I alway add a spicy-ish pepper, usually a Fresno pepper, at the same time as the garlic.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Doubled it. Very tasty w 3 "packs" udon (soaked in boiling H20 for a few minutes), 1 head steamed broccoli florets, & tofu. Sliced block of firm tofu into haphazard thin triangular chunks, about 1-2"long, 1/8" thick. Added 1 Tbsp of soy for marinade,  froze in a single layer on a sheet pan over night.  Fried tofu in oil in a wok in 2 batches until slightly crispy.  Would fry even crisper next time.  Standard soy.  The picky child ate it, even broccoli. Will repeat regularly.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Sooo good.  I used 12 oz pork because that’s how I bought it.  This would be enough for 3 servings.  Couldn’t find the really wide rice noodles so I used narrower ones.   Will make again!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I've followed Pailin's channel for a while and she never disappoints!  She is very knowledgeable and makes recipes user friendly to western cooks. She also has great advice for sourcing ingredients as well as substitutions.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I really enjoyed this, and followed it to the letter. I would caution everyone that the white pepper was the dominant taste. For me, this was great. My partner was a little taken back by its unexpected dominance, but still cleaned her plate.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: We used 12oz of fresh rice noodles for this recipe and subbed with tofu instead and it worked out great!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: 4x veggies

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was easy and delicious! The sauce is pretty salty, so I recommend adding it little by little as the recipe says, not all at once. I would add bean sprouts and green onions next. Thank you for the great recipe!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Other brands that the late chef, Barbara Tropp, recommended are Koon Chun for dark soy sauce; Hop Sing Lung Oyster Sauce and Pearl River Brand Mushroom soy. Her wonderful cookbook, The Modern Art of Chinese Cooking, taught me how to cook Chinese food.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Lois, same! Barbara Tropp's The Modern Art of Chinese Cooking was a revelation when I was just learning to cook. That cookbook is still my go-to and the pages stained by repeated use represent a lot of happy dinner memories!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Wayyyyy too much white pepper. Took over the whole dish.  Maybe use 1/4 of what they say to do here. Needed more sugar as well.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Our family loved this meal. We doubled everything and used a 16oz package of shaved beef which crisped up nicely. Make sure you have white pepper. It comes through nicely. The kids want this again

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This may be one of the best recipes on here. In love. Arguably removes any desire to order in a restaurant or do takeout. Shockingly easy and so satisfying

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Mine came out like a big blob of salty noodles, plus I felt like I was getting heat stroke.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I could not find broccolini or Chinese broccoli, but broccoli rabe was available. I should have used more than the recipe called for. Otherwise this was good as written.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Followed the directions. Made this with thin sliced chicken breasts and broccoli. It was tasty but too dry. Also can’t figure out why the eggs were added to the mix. They certainly didn’t add anything meaningful to this dish.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Beware of adding double the sauce as some reviewers recommend! It was a sodium bomb and truly inedible.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Found this to be very flavorful and well-balanced. I used fresh egg noodles - never seem to have much luck with rice noodles.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: On making for second time, subbed oven roasted tofu seasoned with hot black bean sauce, salt and oil. Every bit as delicious as with meat.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I made this vegetarian and it was great!
Subbed tofu for beef and vegan fish sauce for the sauce. Delicious and quick!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was excellent. Followed the recipe exactly, got the exact results. Tastes better than the restaurant bc I use way less oil. I used pulled pork that I then placed under the broiler to crisp, as the protein. Sprinkled a little soy sauce over the pork as it cooled. Prep went quickly. Served with spring rolls as a starter.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I am allergic to fish (iodine allergy) and to mushrooms. Also, gluten..  I can substitute tamari for soy sauce, but any other suggestions for the sauce?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Hoisin sauce should be fish-free, despite the name - check the ingredient list - so that can sub for the oyster sauce.  It might have a tinge of five-spice flavor which would alter the flavor a bit, but I sometimes swap one for the other in a pinch.  The fish sauce is to provide funk and umami, so maybe a touch of brewer's yeast could go in, instead.  If you're not a fan (I am not) a dose of chili crisp would provide heat and flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I've only ever done this with broccolini (I can never find gai lan, even at H-Mart).
3 bunches was too many, but 2 is just right.
Also, the big wok works better than big Dutch oven, in my experience of making this a few times.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: It's frequently labeled Chinese Broccoli, even at HMart.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this last night with slightly steamed broccoli.  I also sauteed some mushrooms before beginning the dish and folded them in at the end.  Using chicken thighs rather than breast was a great idea.  Next time, I might take the egg out while I do the noodles and then add it back.  Loved the dish and my guy said "I can't stop eating this!"

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I doubled the sauce and it was still a bit dry, and I'd also love to add way more broccoli than I did this round. Also, we didn't have exactly the right noodles so I'll go to the Asian grocery store for the right noodles for this and probably soak them using them directions here instead of a hybrid version between the recipe and the box.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Nice with kernels from an ear of fresh corn when in season (step 9). At the end, add torn Thai basil, juice of 1/2 lime and a little mint on top. Cover and let set for 5 minutes. Serve with Sambal Olek. Pow! So good.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Replaced garlic with ginger (normal swap for allium allergy). Second time, deleted sugar (didn't miss it). First time used wok-read wide noodles (wheat); after an online trip to The Asian Cookshop used sen yai - so I can confirm, you can use any type of noodle - but sen yai *way* better. Took 15 mins in hot water to become bouncy; make sure to rinse before letting stand while you cook. First time used pak choi; second time, broccoli had gone over, so used beans, courgettes & carrots. Yum.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is my favorite dish in Thai restaurants so I was skeptical I could replicate  at home. I made it with tofu in place of the meat. The quick "marinade" in soy sauce added a lot of flavor to the tofu. We didn't have Thai black soy sauce so I added some brown sugar to regular soy sauce and it did the job but I'll  acquire some for next time. I happened to have a big bunch of Thai basil on hand and wilted the entire bunch at the end of cooking - this added an authentic flavor to the dish.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I added snap peas for extra veg and more or less doubled the sauce recipe. Will make again!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This has become a regular in our house! We substitute tofu. Very easy and tasty weeknight meal.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Super easy recipe but I feel like the flavor is pretty salty/one-note so I made a few changes:

1. I added a thumb of ginger with my garlic. 

2. I marinated the chicken in some lime, soy sauce, sugar, black pepper, garlic, and Sriracha for 30  minutes. 

Also, I used bok chou instead of gai lan and would recommend splitting the stalk and greens, adding the stalk after your aromatics and the greens with your noodles to minimize wilting.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have tried to make pad see ew from so many recipes and this is the first one that actually tasted like takeout!! It was soooo good. I had to use plain Jane soy sauce and frozen broccoli, but it worked out great. Will definitely make this again!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Definite winner. Followed exactly. Will make again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Adapted this recipe based on my needs: Used a crown of broccoli instead of gai lan. Skipped the chicken and doubled the egg. Added a red bell pepper that was on its way out. Threw in some wilted
basil at the very end. Thought I had noodles (I didn’t) so I used some leftover rice. End result was a very delicious Thai-inspired fried rice. Great way to clean out the fridge for a satisfying, quick dinner.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Is it me or did anyone else need to read the recipe a few times over to understand what needed to happen. I especially struggled with the sentence about the ‘hot tap water’ then in the same sentence ‘drain meat liquid.’  Great recipe but could have been written in a more clear and concise manner.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’ve made this several times, but the rice noodles always get too mushy! I don’t oversoak either. Anyone have any clues? I’ve tried all kinds of rice noodles, different widths, brands. My favorite store devotes an entire aisle to noodles.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Do you rinse them in cold water after cooking?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
A couple questions: 
Is black soy sauce the same as tamari? 
Any tips on substitutes for fish and oyster sauce to make it vegetarian?
Thanks!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Not at all, unfortunately.  There's really no substitute.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was not a favorite for my family. It was okay but it took quite a bit of preparation and the payoff wasn't as rewarding as we hoped. We did like the broccolini and would definitely recommend using more. The egg seemed to get lost in the cooking. Perhaps my technique was lacking more than the recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Loved this recipe! It came together quickly and was quite tasty. I would definitely double the sauce for next time.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with tofu?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Yes, I used tofu instead (just microwaved a package for about 3 minutes first to remove some of the liquid) and it's great.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Turn out a bit of a disaster. Trying to do this on induction is tough - used a Dutch Oven and had lots of burning - blackened bottom. Perhaps I added too much white pepper which offered a bitterness. Maybe one has to go heavy on the oil. My wife offered that Asian food is tough to cook on induction.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: UGH.  Made it exactly as written, with wide rice noodles.  The result was a sticky, gluey, inedible mess.  Next time, if there is a next time,  I’ll try it with dried Italian noodles, cooked al dente first.   P.S.:  The meat and broccolini were good over some Little Gem lettuces in a salad, though that is not the dinner we had in mind.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Noodles broke up a little but tasty

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So simple to make and so delicious! I did not use oyster sauce, but it was still incredible!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Don’t double sauce but do increase sugar. Noodles fall apart if too soft after soaking.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I soaked the noodles but they still stuck and were under cooked when in pan. They wouldn’t get soft and I kept having to add water to get them to soften. The flavor was incredible though and will try this again with less stubborn noodles.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Liked this a lot and came
Out great as written

I went heavy on the pepper. Would go light next time. 

Added mushrooms and peapods. 

Young boys liked it which surprised me. 

Doubled recipe was almost too bit for my biggest, deepest frying pan.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Has anyone made this with fresh rice noodles, and any pointers?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: noodles from kam man market, two packs of rice noodles in box on the left of first refrigerated aisle

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: five eggs, be careful of burning

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So good. Made with shrimp instead.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Bean noodles work even better with this dish and are easier to work with if you just boil them.  They absorb the sauce better and the texture is superb!  Happy accident after making this great dish for years!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Sounds like you made a version of pad woon sen, then.  Not pad see ew.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So good and so easy!  I used ramen noodles and boiled them while I mixed the sauce ingredients and added a little black vinegar and a bit of chili oil (to my dish only-I like a bit of heat but my husband doesn't). No broccoli so I used the snap peas and green onions I had on hand. This was a hit for sure!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Where do you buy the wide noodles for the pad see yew?in nyc?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Any Asian grocery store.

author: Julia Moskin
