commentBody: I did follow the recipe carefully but this is my first time working with sen yai. It didn't go well. At first, the flavor was delicious but the noodles had the texture of rubber bands. I had to keep adding more water, more soy sauce, double the recipe's sauce. By the time the noodles were done, the vegetables were mush and the flavor was no longer very good. There must have been a trick I missed.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Yummy. The noodles were cooked a d in a sealed pouch so only needed  microwaving for 2 minutes. No soaking. I didn’t have black soy sauce so took the hint to add a bit of molasses. Less sweet than the restaurant version. That tells me that there is normally a lot of sugar.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I have been making versions of this for years. This sauce certainly tastes the most authentic. My trouble lies with handling the wide noodles.
I have used fresh rice noodles as wall as the widest dried I can find and I feel like I am always doing something wrong? With the fresh noodles I have been told on any number of foodie sites to soak, steam, blanch or as the gal at the local Asian market says, "do nothing". They alwayus wind up in little pieces.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Nothing would caramelize for me but this was still a very tasty dish. I used extra veggies (carrot, bok choy, red pepper, half an onion) and would recommend that. Next time I’ll forget the protein and double the eggs, and add basil at the end.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Two things:
1) The dried noodles are hard to get right.A long soak in hot water seems important. They get more noodley in the pan if you have patience. Do NOT assume they're like Italian pasta and that you can cook them in boiling water. The wide noodles will stick to each other and you'll get four or five clumped together, completely inedible!
2) Other recipes use an enormous amount of soy sauce, medium + dark sweet, and oyster sauce. I've become more sensitive to sodium so maybe less is more.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Added mushrooms, didn't use eggs, used whole wheat egg noodles, and made a sauce with soy sauce, miso, fig butter, chia and hot water and sugar. (This sauce is delicious, but won't be smooth given the chia.) Protein was tofu in thin strips. Used bok choy and celery for the veg as that is what we had laying around. Fantastic!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If using meat or chicken,veveing the chicke will keep meat from meat sticking to the wok and avoids udon evtra oil

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: More sugar , fish sauce and oyster skip the chicken double the broccoli

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is the closest to a restaurant caliber stir fry that I've managed to make thus far. From both a flavor profile and technique it's excellent.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Very good recipe. Rice noodles: not hard. Just needs to be submerged in hot water for long enough of time until it comes soft and opaque. In restaurants they have it in warm water all day long and let it cook in a hot pan when the order comes. The sauce: should be all absorbed by the noodles and vegetables. If too dry, add hot water. It should be finished completely dry. No extra sauces! At restaurants they would add a teaspoon of chicken stock powder to the noodles when the sauce goes in.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Really enjoyed this! I doubled the recipe and thought the taste of the sauce was a little too punchy, so I'll try doing less fish sauce next time to balance the flavors more.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Sauce is good noodle stir fry base. Don’t forget garlic. Consider hot sauce, ginger, lime for extra flavor. Doubled sauce and it on roasted veggies. Doubled again for 2 containers tofu. Then would make another batch of sauce for noodles.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used two packages of noodles, half a cabbage, an onion, 4 bunches of choi sum and tripled the sauce and sugar amounts; other than that, delicious as is!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made exactly as instructed. So good. Putting this on my "yum" list.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The reason I order this dish in restaurants (I have never made it myself) was that I like the basil, which I thought was the defining flavor of Pad See Ew.  I was surprised that when I went to look at a recipe in order to give a try (and I grew the basil), it is absent.  Is this a New York version, or is the dish commonly served without it in Thailand?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Add a ½ an onion and mushrooms

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Very reminiscent of the Lin’s Garden Chow Fun from the now defunct Lin’s Garden which held court on Elizabeth and Bayard Streets in Chinatown, NYC, as everyone’s favorite late night munchie relief spot. I sliced up the soft, fresh rice noodles which are sold in folded sheets in the refrigerator case at my local Asian market and the results were stellar! Shout out to Lin’s Garden devotees!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Fantastic!  I'm not too good at doing the 'Mise en place' that this recipe requires, but it all worked out.  I had all this oyster sauce and fish sauce in the frig that I haven't used in a while...didn't have black soy sauce so just used an extra amount of regular soy sauce.  Very delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I prefer this dish with vermicelli, which I think is an unpopular opinion. I also add sambal. Great for quick dinner, but I haven't found a restaurant worthy recipe yet.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Do not use fresh rice noodles with this.  They completely disintegrate into pad see
goop.  One of my favorite recipes.  Always wanted to try with fresh rice noodles.  Learn from my mistake, Padawan.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The white pepper makes a difference!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: In Thai, this is called Pad See Ew Dam (dam meaning dark) for the dark soy sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: tasty, I think I would need to have hunted down fresh rice noodles to get the texture of my local takeout place. still I'll make it again, not just because I had to buy quadruple the amount of gai lan called for in the recipe

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @MCB I made it with fresh rice noodle and it was a killer! I was surprised how easy it was

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used fresh wide rice noodles and it worked really well! I was concerned because I saw some comments about fresh noodles not working well, I wonder if they used a different kind of noodles?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Best sauce 1tbsp tamarind concentrate,3 Tbsp fish sauce ,1tbsp sugar,2 tbsp lime juice
From Suzanne Foo Fresh Inspirations2005

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was a big hit for us! I was dubious about 1/3 cup of fish sauce, but I thought the sweet/salty/tart balance was good, especially w a squeeze of lime. I nuked the palm sugar and it mixed in fine-I didn’t want to use another pan or have the smell of evaporating fish sauce put off my kids. I added broccoli, ginger and red pepper flakes, and would experiment with frying time/order, as the end result was delicious, but a bit wet. Also, anything crunchy (ie bean sprouts) should go on top!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe needs adjusting to hit the "this is delicious!" pad thai mark. Cooked it exactly as written. Result was both a bit bland and a bit overpowering with the tamarind tang. Next time I would (1) add a dash of koji and a dash of hot chili to the sauce (2) slightly reduce the ratio of tamarind ingredient (3) remove the garlicky shrimp and tofu from the pan before cooking and tossing the noodles in the sauce (4) toss the shrimp and tofu back into the dish with the egg.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I attended a cooking class in Chiangmai.
Here’s what I was taught, my family loves my pad Thai.
For 1 to 2 servings,
1/3 cup water
2 tsp tamarind paste
1 1/2 tsp palm or brown sugar
1 tbs fish or soy sauce
1 tsp molasses 
Pinch of chili powder 
Put water in bowl add tamarind paste and rest of ingredients.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Put noodles in room temp water for one hour while prepping.  Noodles still flexible but very firm which makes for better Pad Thai.  Used the suggested Susanna Foo sauce proportions but doubled it.  Fish sauce still too salty for my taste, but otherwise came out pretty good and worthy of serving to family.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have tried out so many recipes for pad Thai, and last recipe I tried I said to myself that I was done; I would simply eat it from restaurants from now on because it’s always so disappointing. Well! This time was different! I changed the proportions slightly at times but mostly kept the same and it was delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Great tip from a recepie for Singapore style noodles: due to heat limitations of an average home stove stir fry in batches starting with eggs, followed by garlic and protein, finally the noodles and sauce. Remove each cooked ingredients to a side bowl when done and add back when noodles are nearly done in order to reheat and combine flavours.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Very, very disappointing. I have made pad Thai many times and this was really a poor recipe from the proportions through the technique. 2-3 servings? More like 4-5. My own technique involves cooking the shrimp and tofu and eggs separately from the noodles and combining just before serving. This “one wok” approach yielded a gluey, indistinct mess. 

Do not bother.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Ghastly recipe, worst ever! The one wok approach leads to rubbery shrimp and noodles burnt onto the pan (while cooking the eggs). And there's no spice.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Sweet= palm sugar
Salty = fish sauce or shrimp paste
Spicy = chilis
Herbal= cilantro
Citrus= tamarind, keffir lime leaf
Balance to taste.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Do not use hot tap water; it’s been sitting in the water heater or pipes and may contain who knows what kinds of impurities.  Warm the water on the stovetop or in the microwave.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was surprisingly bland.  I would suggest adding some red pepper flakes and lime to the sauce and making sure to use it ALL.  The recipe seems to suggest you use only half the sauce, maybe a little more, which I did.  Didn't really figure out what the problem was until later.  I added the rest of the sauce to the leftovers and am hoping it will be better tomorrow.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I did this exactly according to the recipe.  There were a few problems. (1) The shrimp are cooked in step 4b, right after the garlic and shallots.  Then they stay in the pan overcoooking until the end. By the time you're adding the bean sprouts the shrimp are chewy and dry.  (2)  The noodles are cooked, and cooked some more in steps 5 and 6.  They are sauced with a sugary sauce.  Then we move them to the side of the pan and cook four eggs till they are dry. The noodles are now burnt onto the pan

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Yuck. Made it exactly as stated, but it was not delicious. Would definitely not make this again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Don't soak the rice noodles to soften. They are way too gluey. Cook them according to the package (boil then drain). I always add noodles to these recipes at the end and don't cook them with the rest of the ingredients. I also used a bit of Thai sweet chili relish to sweeten the sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Horrid sauce IF one uses tamarind concentrate (paste).   There's no enough sugar available to make the sauce palatable and liquid.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Way too many eggs, and like another submitter, mine too was a gloppy mess. Won't do this again!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Not much flavor at all in this dish. Disappointing, especially because it takes quite a long time to prepare. Will not make again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Should give some type of sourness level suggestion for sauce, following the directions lead to super sour pad Thai. Seemed like directions were for a super high heat wok in professional kitchen. Without the super high heat, cooking entire recipe in one pot lead to overcooked shrimp, overcooked noodles, and egg gunk all over everything. May be a good recipe in theory, but poorly translated for home cooks.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have NEVER seen a recipe call for cooking with tap water from the hot side. That water is not safe for drinking/cooking! It’s the pipes and the bacteria!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Awful recipe! Not removing shrimp after cooking means they overcook. Not removing the sugar-sauced noodles while doing the eggs means that the noodles burn. Sauce needs spice, not just fish sauce and sugar and tamarind.  Did anyone who applauded this recipe really do it the way Moskin suggests????

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
David,

Did you prepare the recipe as per her instructions, or are you offering your opinion?  I'm a reader who does value the comments in the recipe before attempting, so I'd like to know.

Home Cook

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Definitely agree w David.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Doubled the recipe and 1/3 cup apple cider vinegar and 1/3 cup lime juice is a great sub for tamarind.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This is missing dried shrimp (the tiny ones). 

Also, it's worth making your own tamarind paste from a block of tamarind mash.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Best sauce 1tbsp tamarind concentrate,3 Tbsp fish sauce ,1tbsp sugar,2 tbsp lime juice

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Watcharee makes and sells fabulous sauces, including pad thai sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: We just made this. Added cilantro, used a little more shallots (about 1/2 cup total) than called for. The specialty store didn't have sweet radish, so we just used a little more sugar (maybe an extra teaspoon). We didn't have chile powder so used sweet chile sauce. It was truly the best Pad Thai I've ever eaten.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: One can sautéed tofu and egg in advance add then put them in at the end.also this is a very tofu-heavy and shrimp light recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is a dish that doesn't need eggs and 4 are too many for 2 or 3 servings. Tofu is common even when eggs are present, but absent here.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Rich is correct, you only need 1 or 2 egges.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Tamarind concentrate needs to be mixed with water first, try 1:1 ratio. Preserved radish varies with brand - taste it first, some need to be soaked in some cold water for a few minutes, then squeeze out water before adding to wok. My pad thai cooking sequence: oil/ garlic+shallots/ eggs/ shrimp/ tofu/ matchstick carrots*+preserved radish+tiny dried shrimp*/ noodles+sauce/ bean sprouts+garlic chives or scallions. *= extra ingredients I use. Chili flakes, not chili powder or add Sriracha to sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Three Crab fish sauce!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Terrific NYT feature article "Perfecting Thai Noodles but far from Thailand!" You might like to see Watcharee herself making pad thai in a 2 minute video m.youtube.com/watch?v=eXeJe5YZJjg ? Also Pailin from the same article has a longer pad thai video where she explains each ingredient, final presentation etc m.youtube.com/watch?v=efedTXK6LD8
I feel a bit at sea with cuisine I didn't grow up with, ingredients not easily found online... these videos will invite you into their kitchens!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
So, no. Just no. Pretty bland and very gloppy.

That said, if you still aspire, then 2 eggs are plenty. When it was time to cook the eggs, I emptied the wok so I could cook the eggs. Then I put everything back to heat through.

I don’t often give a low rating to a recipe from this site, but this was warranted.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Noodles gloopy. Sauce too fishy. Unfortunately this one did not work for us.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Too many of these Thai recipes have become too American, gloppy and very wet to what I have had years ago. Lacking in many of the Thai ingredients.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was so bland. The tamarind was overbearing. I was really disappointed!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Very disappointing and bland. I moved to the mid-atlantic from Los Angeles 3 years ago and have not been able to find good Thai. So I was excited to see this, alas....

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was gross. Maybe I got noodles that didn’t work as directed here? Was a slimy, mushy (yet somehow also too al dente) mess.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The flavor is good, but the recipe does not seem well designed. There are too many eggs and the noodles tend to break apart in the long cooking time needed to finish the eggs.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I am a very experienced and skillful cook. This was inedible. The noodles were simultaneously gummy on the outside and undercooked inside (I didn't dare continue to cook because the outside was disintegrating). And how can a dish that contains a full head of minced garlic plus shallot and scallion and fish sauce and tamarind and peanuts and lime be so very bland? The tofu was another miss. Even though I pressed it, it came out as lumps of plain tofu, without taking on any other real flavors.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If I do this again, I will scramble the eggs separately. There was not enough space in the wok and everything turned to mush. A Pad Thai Fail.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Having made this according to the instructions, with all the proper ingredients, I come to assure those of you who’ve read all the negative comments that this is a great recipe. You can’t leave anything out and expect it to be balanced or flavorful. A note on fish sauce, it needs to be refrigerated after opening or it gets nasty. And when it’s raw it smells pretty rotten but the rottenness turns into an irreplaceable umami when it’s cooked or combined with the right things.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This was so great 
Pad thai in restaurants is so oily and carby 
Love to make it at home with less oil
Used Worcester sauce instead of tamarind 
Less oil
Tofu noodles 20 calories per 8 ounces!
Cooked shrimp and eggs at end

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Cilantro!!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Like the best sauce from Suzanne Foo.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was a disaster for me. The flavors were close to right, but the noodles were a gelatinous mess--perhaps I soaked them for too long? Also could have used a little more heat.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Unfortunately, this recipe was a flop. Four eggs were far too many — it was an eggy, gluey mess.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this pretty much as directed and it was good enough that I made it a second time a week later. The first time I poured boiling water over my noodles, and I ended up with pretty mushy Pad Thai. The second time I used hot tap water as directed. I left the noodles in the water at least 30 minutes. They were still very al dente, but they came out perfect once they cooked with the rest of the ingredients as directed. I used all the sauce. Three eggs. Next time I'll add more spicy to the sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made tonight using the suggestion from Mel about the sauce ratios, and doubled it based on another commenter's note. We loved it. I think the key is having a NONstick large skillet; and make sure the noodles still have a slight bite on them before you put them in the pan - otherwise they'll be too soft and get sticky. Like another commenter, I've been looking for the Pad Thai recipe to rival a restaurant, and I think this one gets close! Will riff on this one a bit more...

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is not a terrible Phat Thai recipe but needs a few adjustments. I lived in Thailand for many years and consider myself an expert on Thai cooking. Most cooks add some lime juice to the sauce, and 99% of the Phat Thai in Thailand uses dried shrimp, not fresh (although you can get it with both or just fresh too). Unless you have a dedicated wok burner you can't get a wok hot enough to fry this properly, and the technique shared here will result in a gluey mess. Cook everything separately.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I changed the sauce  and it was a huge improvement:

	•	3 tbsp fish sauce
	•	1 tbsp soy sauce
	•	5 tbsp brown sugar
	•	2 tbsp rice vinegar or tamarind paste
	•	1 tbsp Sriracha (optional)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Kind of a gloppy mess. I pulled the shrimp after adding the noodles to avoid overcooking. Didn't have the right flavor until adding cilantro, roasted peanuts and lime.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The family loved this. Made the following changes: added 3 small finely shredded carrots and some broccoli. Used chicken instead of tofu and shrimp. Added a tablespoon of mayploy (because it was too tart from the tamarind), and 2 tablespoons of peanut butter because we didn’t have peanuts to top it with. Adding the peanut butter was the best decision because it cut more of the tartness. My 14month old ate 2 big bowls of this stuff.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I followed Ep’s recipe for the sauce and Ann’s recommendation to boil rather than soak the noodles which produced a perfect noodle texture. I marinated and baked the tofu (NYT Cooking recipe). Taking others comments about overcooking into account, I cooked in quick batches and put everything together for only 2-4 minutes at the end. This pad Thai was great and I will definitely make it again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Suggestions for improvement, based on my experience cooking this recipe:
1. Take the shrimp and tofu off after briefly cooking and leave it on the side until the very end.
2. I know it isn't fully traditional/in line with authentic Pad Thai, but whisking in a tablespoon (or even 2) of peanut butter majorly improved the flavor and consistency of the sauce.
3. Add both whole peanuts and if possible, crushed/almost ground peanuts (I finished up a bag of peanuts on this and the little bits from the bottom of the bag made the texture so nice).
4. Add chili flakes in both the shrimp-cooking step and to the sauce.

With the above included, this actually turned out to be restaurant-level good!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Doesn’t real Pad Thai have ground dried shrimp?  That’s what makes it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: seems like a lot more sauce than I expected from the picture

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Would appreciate recommendations on a preferred fish sauce brand.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have always (30 years) used Squid Brand Fish Sauce

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I am a big fan of Red Boat.  Two ingredients — anchovies and salt.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I second Squid. It has a funk that the others don't (in a good way).

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Best 100% shellfish- and fish-free alternative to fish sauce, anyone?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have enjoyed the Pad Thai recipe from the book "Thai Cooking: From the Siam Cuisine Restaurant" -- with the substitution of fresh shrimp for the dried shrimp in the recipe.  This book recommends using the tamarind that comes in blocks for preparing the sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Years ago we had phad thai at a restaurant that we really loved, but the sauce was browner and not sweet - my guess is very little tamarind. I've asked at restaurants since about it but never really have it served. Has anyone had experience reducing or eliminating the tamarind? what would be the sub? thanks

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Is there a substitute for the spice tamarind? I’m allergic but miss good Pad Thai. I’d be willing to try the recipe without it but I wonder if the other ingredient ratios might need to be adjusted?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: For the sauce, I did 1/4 cup rice wine vinegar, 1/4 cup soy sauce, and 2 spoonfuls of granulated sugar.  It was a total guess, but it ended up tasting like good pad thai.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: What is a good vegetarian substitute for fish sauce?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Amazon sells a vegan fish sauce called Fysh Sauce, for those of us with a seafood allergy. It's made from five types of seaweed.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I'm Thai. The best substitute I use for my vegan friends is liquid aminos.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Nice flavors but scorched the heck out of my dutch oven and the noodles (the ones that didn't burn) a bit soggy. Not a keeper for me.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this, but substituted chicken for shrimp. I felt the dish came out great, but was a bit too salty. Will need to make a change next time.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Great tip from a recepie for Singapore style noodles: due to heat limitations of an average home stove stir fry in batches starting with eggs, followed by garlic and protein, finally the noodles and sauce. Remove each cooked ingredients to a side bowl when done and add back when noodles are nearly done in order to reheat and combine flavours.

Best sauce 1tbsp tamarind concentrate,3 Tbsp fish sauce ,1tbsp sugar,2 tbsp lime juice

added broccoli

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made as written except couldn’t find Tamarind in my local so substituted a sauce I Googled that uses Worcestershire sauce, lime, brown sugar and water - family loved it. I was very happy with the results.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Good. Added 1 tsp hot chilli powder and took shrimp out of pan after initial stir fry per other recipes.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I am surprised by all of the recipes that lack the ingredient of the tiny dried shrimp. Why is the dried shrimp omitted?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Overall, I liked the recipe. I’d suggest adding the rice noodles to water that you’ve heated to a boil (hot water didn’t work). I think you need all of the sauce, and could add some red pepper flakes to get some heat. Small shrimp (a half pound) work better than the larger shrimp pictured. Reduce the eggs to 3. Portions wise, makes 4-5 generous potions.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Eh. Added some chili paste to the sauce so that had some zing. Sautéed the shrimp and tofu and removed them so they were not over cooked. But it was kind of gloopy and not as wonderful as I wanted it to be.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Used tamarind concentrate and it was too tangy. Next time I’d use half concentrate and half water. Otherwise yummy

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: After reading the comments, I made a few changes: 1. Cooked the shrimp with a little bit of the garlic/shallot first, set aside 2. Cooked the veggies (I did broccoli/red pepper and the scallions in oil; set aside 3. Added oil, the rest of the garlic/shallots for 30sec 4. Added the sauce (1TBTamarind 3Tbfish sauce, 1.5TB brown sug) for 30sec; added noodles for 30sec; added everything else. Came out really good. Not mushy, well seasoned. Added some chili paste for more heat.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this and the taste was very good. But everything was gray, so not really appetizing to look at. I guess that's because of the sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used a vegetable umami sauce by Yondu instead of fish sauce and otherwise followed the recipe as written.  I was very happy with the result and will probably make it again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Not sure why this recipe did not produce something I liked. Perhaps too much tamarind? Usually I can cook a decent version of many restaurant dishes, but I will stick to restaurants for Pad Thai - even the most mediocre ones produce something better than this. Which is strange, because it is supposed to be a simple recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’ve made this a bunch of times. So delicious, but needs more flavor. I add lots of Trader Joe’s Chili Lime seasoning. I wanted to make it tonight but didn’t have any Tamarind for the sauce. I faked it with the chili lime seasoning, brown sugar, rice vinegar, fish sauce and duck sauce (!) It totally worked. My picky family didn’t notice the difference! Now I have a use for those duck sauce packets.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My family (including my 2.5 yr old) loves the recipe. However - and maybe I'm doing something wrong - I've found that adding the shrimp to the wok first results in extremely over-cooked shrimp. Not sure the best time to add, but most of the way through the time the noodles are cooking worked well enough. (or just cook them separately.)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Absolutely do not leave out the preserved radish. It is essential for the real flavor of street food Pad Thai. The good news is: once you hunt it down at your local Asian market, it'll last you a long time. 

The brand of fish sauce you use matters a lot. There is considerable variance between the budget and high-end fish sauce, in terms of umami & salt. 

I usually add considerably more tamarind sauce. I just make up a big batch and keep excess in the fridge for when a Pad Thai craving strikes

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Best sauce 1tbsp tamarind concentrate,3 Tbsp fish sauce ,1tbsp sugar,2 tbsp lime juice

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: A recipe I have uses sriracha in the sauce.  Easy!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Well this turned into a gloppy mess.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Had to throw out the sauce it was so bad.  Whole house stunk.  Replaced the sauce with one from a different pad thai recipe (random search) and it turned out good, though.  Next time I'll try the sauce combos recommended by other reviewers.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I was taught to use dried shrimp (finely chopped) as well as fresh shrimp.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This was awful - gluey noodles, too much egg, overcooked shrimp.  I had to bribe my family to finish their plates.
Leftovers going straight in the rubbish bin.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Cook the part separately and combine at the end, this made for a goop.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The noodle package directions say to boil them for 7 minutes, then drain and stir fry. I followed this recipe with the hot water soak etc. and yes, it was indeed a gooey gloppy mess! Fortunately my husband will eat anything, including picking sprouts and shrimp out of glop!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe was not worth the effort. I followed many of the comments, and it was just meh. The tamarind adds a sour note, and I was wanting more freshness. Next time I am craving Pad Thai I will order carry out.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made a lot of edits, and used about three times the heat as we do around here, but this was good. It's another good base recipe that's easy to mess with depending on what you have in the kitchen.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I found these to be too dry. Next time, I will double (or triple!) the amount of sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I've made this a few times. It's a favorite with the family, and a double batch is typical. To mitigate the fish sauce smell I make the sauce a day or more in advance when nobody else is home and there is time to air out the kitchen. Organization is critical, as are well-fluffed noodles, as it comes together quickly once the wok is ready. I've not yet used Thai radishes; someday I'll put the effort into finding them.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is a great recipe! But the second time around I made a bunch of changes because it's pretty easy to change the vegetables and proteins, so used chicken and tofu instead of shrimp and tofu. I used a lot of garlic and added peanut sauce on the side. Love that I can make this for date night in the house on Saturday!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Everything was going more or less fine until I added the eggs. Too many! Now I’m eating a very weird Thai Carbonara. Also, shrimps overcook.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was quite bland. Big disappointment.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I did this with my children and they really put their whole heart into it!  It was delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe was such a disappointment. Sauce proportions were quite off for our tastes, too much tamarind. Also felt very one-note - some kind of chile needed maybe? Noodles were mush by the time it was done.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: It was okay but a bit too sweet for my taste. I wonder whether it's because I used brown sugar. I also used twice the quantity of shrimp that shriveled up a bit too much. Next time I'll use less sugar and put in the shrimp towards the end. I will probably also reduce the quantity of shrimp, add cubed chicken, and add pepper flakes.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe required a lot of work and it wasn't worth it as written. My suggestions , which mirror others. Do not soak noodles as suggested . Cook according to package directions "al dente" and immerse in cold water to stop cooking and then drain and set aside,. Do not cook the shrimp with the noodles. Cook them separately so they don't overcook. Slice tofu and press the tofu between layers of paper towels for about 20-30 minutes to remove residual moisture. Servings are more like 4-5 vs 2-3.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Tamarind substitute for fellow rural people in a pinch: extra brown sugar, 1/4 c worcester sauce, and 1/4 c apple cider vinergar simmered until thick.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Add cilantro, mix fish sauce w veg fish sauce. Use smaller shrimp and super firm tofu.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Very tasty dish that at first turned my 4 year old off, he didn’t like the smell fish sauce in the sauce. After a few bites he was asking us if there would be more for later. 10/10 will make again

commentBody: Way too much fish sauce, the taste was horrible.  The shrimp were overcooked and should have been set aside and reintroduced at the very end.  I followed the recipe and invested a lot in strange ingredients like palm sugar and preserved radish.  I threw the entire meal out as it tasted nothing like pad Thai, and the shrimp were too chewy.  I'd look for a different recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So I cheated a little and got the pad Thai sauce from my local Asian mart and used about 6 ozs of that . Because those sauces can be bland and cater to American palettes—  I cooked it with the shallot, garlic, palm sugar and fish sauce(1/3 cups of palm sugar and fish sauce). I don’t love the tartness of tamarind so I skipped that. Added some cilantro, more scallions, mashed peanuts and fresh squeezed lime juice and it was amazing. Also I used shrimp instead of tofu!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: That is a deranged amount of fish sauce to use. I used half as much and the noodles are still absolutely drenched.

commentBody: The sauce is not pad Thai sauce. Way too much fish sauce and tamarind. 1/3 of a cup??? 1-2 tablespoons is plenty. Same with the tamarind.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have made this 4 or 5 times and enjoyed it. This time I used cabbage rather than bean sprouts and was delighted. The amount of sauce was just right and the shrimp were great . TY to the NYTC.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I will be making this soon! Using Bobs red mill gluten free 1-1 flour and his chickpea flour. Chickpea flour adds a custard like texture, therefore those who asked,  I believe it’s essential to this recipe. It’s relatively inexpensive too. If you have dried chickpeas and a strong blender you can grind your own flour. Does anyone have thoughts if I can use extra virgin olive oil, ? I don’t have a neutral vegetable oil. Thanks Much!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Extra virgin olive oil has a low smoke point so it will smoke (a lot) at 425. Ordinary (refined) olive oil may work, but EVOO would be a problem.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: These were incredible! My family makes yorkshire pudding every year for christmas dinner and I was never able to enjoy it because I can't have dairy. This year I could eat three! Even my dad was impressed. Ended up not leaving them in the oven the whole time because they were already dry and matte on top. I'd love to know if I can substitute drippings from our roast for the vegetable oil, to make it dairy free but get some of the beef flavor back.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I thought these were delicious… but seeing as I’ve never had Yorkshire pudding before I didn’t exactly know how it was supposed to come out/taste. I think it’s supposed to be mushy inside? To my taste they seemed a little undercooked but I think it’s supposed to be a tad mushy?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I actually left my batter in the refrigerator for a few hours before I made them and they came out perfectly. I did not need to cook them the extra 10 minutes as they were already brown on top.
Super easy and delicious thank you for sharing this recipe!

I make my chickpeas using the pressure cooker. 1 cup dry chickpeas, 3 cups water, then allow the beans to remain refrigerated in the liquid for 48 hours. I cannot whip the liquid beyond a sad, foamy state. I may just try the canned stuff for this recipe and see how it turns out.

author: Julia Moskin

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was a nice treat for us on New Year's Day, and I look forward to having another one later with vegetarian chili. I omitted the last step of cooking for 10 minutes on 400, as they were already a deep golden brown and crisp on the outside after cooking for 30 minutes at 425.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: These are amazing! I whipped the aquafaba/water/vinegar mix with an immersion blender, and it became almost as frothy as egg whites. I followed the recipe exactly. As promised, the tops were crispy and the centers were custardy. I melted vegan "buttery" spread into the wells of the cooked puddings, and it was delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Is chickpea flour used just to make this gf? Anyone tried with plain wheat flour, or is it integral to the recipe working?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: That question doesn’t make sense, since the recipe uses a mix of chickpea flour and all purpose (gluten-containing) flour. A lot of recipes for things that are eaten crispy and hot will use some amount of chickpea flour blended in with other flours because it has a lot of really nice properties when mixed with other flours; it is super healthy, a good binder that adds both density and lightness, has a satisfying subtle nutty taste, and adds crispiness. Don’t substitute it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Yorkshire puddings are super nostalgic for me because my grandma used to make them whenever I visited but I haven’t seen a good vegan recipe!

This worked great! I used a mixture of vegan butter (for flavor) and canola oil (for temperature) when coating the pans. Just be careful with the salt levels if using canned chickpeas.

Will have to think about what to serve these with (maybe a rich red wine-based chickpea stew?) but in the meantime they were delicious plain with a pat of vegan butter!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this and it didn't rise then fall. Also it had a distinct garbanzo bean/sour flavor to it. So disappointing. Could be user error, again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Can the batter be chilled overnight?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The dry to wet ratio must have been off for me, because these never fully cooked and the inside remained raw.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody:
There is NOT too much sugar in this recipe.  It is not an overly heavy cake.  Professional pastry chef here - and I have to say, people who tinker with baking recipes then complain the texture or flavor are wrong are causing the issues themselves.

Sugar is a tenderizer.  Just adding/reducing it without consideration of other ingredients is not a simple thing. Adding sour cream adds an acid which may or may not ruin a batter.  Not mixing properly, not following instructions is why cakes fail.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: I just made this and it was delicious.  Grease that Bundt pan like your life depended on it and after baking, let it sit in the pan for longer than indicated before you attempt to slip it out of the pan.  The instruction to "Shake the pan to loosen" was a terrible idea and caused some of the cake to tear.   The Caramel/Applejack sauce is amazing but it does take longer than 5 minutes to turn golden brown - more like 12 minutes for me.  I will make this again!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: I made the cake as written with 2 significant changes. I swapped out the applejack with dark rum in equal quantities and I baked 12 "cakelets" using a Texas muffin pan. The rum version tastes great. I baked 6 minis for 25 minutes which was a shade too long. The 6 baked 23 minutes worked better. The amount of caramel was perfect for 3 coats for the minis (more surface area than a whole bundt). You must grease the living heck out of your pan and have patience while making the caramel. Worth it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: A winner!  Worth the trouble.  Yes, the sauce needs to cook longer.  Gorgeous texture.  I ran out of milk so had to use buttermilk, which worked just fine (maybe even better texture?).

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: Outstanding recipe....however even with a nonstick Bundt it stuck to the pan. Is Baker's Joy the solution?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: @SueAnn56 yes bakers joy

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: Yes, this does make more caramel sauce than you think you need but do NOT reduce the recipe!  After brushing the cake with sauce 3 times at the end, save the leftover caramel sauce and when you serve slices of cake on subsequent days after baking it, serve with a spoonful of sauce on the plate with the slice.  Heaven!!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: A little too much alcohol in the caramel topping. Just a splash of apple brandy is plenty. Cake is delicious and gorgeous!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: I agree that the caramel sauce should be reduced. The cake came out nice and moist and looks beautiful.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: This cake is fine, but unimpressive. I agree with others who claim that the texture is off--it is. Mine was a bit dry as well and I followed the directions exactly (using apple brandy). For the caramel coating, I did one layer right when it came out of the oven and then did another layer after the whole thing cooled, achieving the glossy finish in the photo and using the entire batch. Maybe it'll be better tomorrow, but...there are better bundts (like the Lee/Detroit poundcake) out there.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: Too dry under the sticky crust, so it crumbles & falls apart when you try to take a bite. The bottom stuck to the pan, even after following directions perfectly, & I had to pull it off with a silicone spatula & flop it on the top of the cake. Disappointing. Caramel didn't cook in recipe's time, nor mix with cream  & vanilla well.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: I made this cake with a few different tweaks. I used Frangelico instead of apple brandy in the batter and in the sauce since I saw so many "boozy" comments. I also swapped out the vanilla for maple extract in the batter and in the caramel sauce - it was perfect. I buttered, refrigerated and then floured my pan - it slid out immediately no issues. It is not as dry as people make it out, it resembles a pound cake. I thought it was very well balanced, not too sweet. Will make it for Thanksgiving!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: Made a half recipe in a 6 c. Bundt pan and baked it for 45 minutes.  Didn't make any ingredient substitutions and the cake is excellent.  Great flavor and texture.  There was a fair bit of caramel left over even after 3 applications of glaze.  Next time I might make ⅓ of the caramel sauce recipe for a half recipe of cake.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: It seems like these will be very sweet. Has anyone tried to reduce the sugar in the cake batter?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: I did, by half a coup, which was maybe too much

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: I would offer the following suggestions. It has a bit of an odd texture. It’s a little heavy but not in a moist sort of way. I think you could ponder adding a quarter cup of sour cream to it. Second, one way to use up some of the extra caramel sauce would be to poke holes in the top of the cake at random places and then when you brush the caramel sauce on it will go through the cake and make it a little tastier. Third, the caramel sauce has way too much butter. You could halve that easily.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: And yes, you can leave 1/4 cup of the sugar out and that’s fine.

recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: I'll never make this again. I followed the directions exactly and the bottom stuck to the bundt pan and pulled away from the rest of the cake. I had to pull it out with a silicon spatula and flop it onto the top of the cake to coat with caramel (which never really blended, even though I cooked it enough & added the cream, vanilla etc. properly). The previous cake (cinnamon donut type) I made came out perfectly. Disappointed.

recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: The pan used is a Nordicware Brilliance Bundt pan. A Google search will tell you where you can find it in your area.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: The cake is delicious and not heavy and the sauce is divine. The only problem I had was getting the cake out of the pan. A significant part remained in the pan. Next time I will slather the pan with butter and dust with flour instead of using baking oil spray. I am also goi to let it cool upside down in the pan a bit longer.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: great cake, great texture, but wayyyyy to much booze, and i love booze. definitely cutting back next time

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: I have this exact bundt pan and use it all the time. I always butter *and* flour it and have never had any issues getting the cake to release!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody:
I followed the recipe exactly. The caramel did take longer to darken, closer to 10 minutes. I baked this for 53 Minutes and it was a Bit dry. I would take it out earlier. I also thought the glaze was a
Bit boozy and the cake was more like a pound cake with a denser crumb. I liked it but it was a lot of work.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Applejack Butter Pecan Bundt Cake Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe to a “T”. Turned out beautifully! Quite boozy, in a good way. I generously sprayed the pan with baking spray. Easy release after cooling 10 minutes. Perfect with a bit of vanilla bean ice cream or whipped cream. It’s a gorgeous cake and texture!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Hard to take seriously a recipe that is as happy with Amaro Nonino as with Fernet-Branca. Imagine a Manhattan recipe that suggests subbing vodka if one is out of rye.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Subbed Calvados for applejack. Subbed a mix of blood orange and lemon juice for grapefruit juice. Subbed light brown sugar for Demerara. Used Myers's Original Dark for rum. A more intense version of a Paper Plane, but a rather fussy concoction compared to the original of equal parts bourbon, amaro, Aperol, and lemon juice. I might make a quart of it and save for a cocktail party, but under present conditions, that's not happening any time soon.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I think if you don’t have Nonino, I’d sub Amaro Montenegro instead. Would be closer from a flavor profile without bringing all the same complexity to the table. I agree...it’s hard to beat the simple brilliance of the Paper Plane but I’d be willing to give this a try on a chilly night!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The author’s note *does* help! Thank you!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Used 1/4 tsp brown sugar, calvados and Braulio a

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Apparently Drew did not want to give away all the goods with his recipe. Any Asheville locals seen this mixed to tell us what amaro is used?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
The original recipe uses Nonino. The recipe was adapted, as noted, to allow some flexibility for home readers who may not have Nonino on hand. I hope that helps. 
-df

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I made this and loved it. Actually, I made this three different times (over two different nights). First time, I used Nonino; second time, I used Ramazotti; third time, I used Fernet-Branca. The rest of the recipe I stayed true to. My favorite was the Nonino, but the cocktails made with Nonino and Ramazotti were nice, too. 

Amari are different from one another, true, but they all carry that characteristic bitterness that runs through amari. Not at all the same as subbing vodka for rye.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made a single by halving the recipe. It’s a lil sweet so I’ll try slightly more grapefruit or even grapefruit bitters to balance. Used lairds applejack

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: And it is not hard at all for some to take everything too seriously.  Seriously.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I met James Beard once, in Seaside, Oregon. He stopped in at the small delicatessen there. My wonderful boss, and owner, Ken, introduced us and asked if I had questions for the maestro. I asked about keeping cheese. He was so kind, so accommodating. That was 1980-81. His advice? Wrap cheese tightly, as air diminishes the flavor and texture. I thought it amusing he preferred a prepared coleslaw we served, and asked for seconds with his lunch sandwich. A lovely man.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I prepared this last night and the result was beautiful and delicious.  I will absolutely make it again.

However, anybody who can cook this well within the prescribed one hour deserves a James Beard Award.  I browned the chicken in two batches and ultimately needed 75+ minutes on the stove.  Unless you're cooking on a volcano those reduction times are optimistic.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Second the comment on the time to prepare this dish. On the other hand, once the wine is opened.,.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: brown the chicken in a pan side by side to the other ingredients. yes, 2 pans to wash, but you can get dinner on the table much quicker this way. and add a little of the chicken oil to the main saute pan. well worth the trouble to get to a delicious meal sooner.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly using mostly bone-in thighs and a couple of legs. Give yourself time because this is not a quick dish. But it was well worth it. The sauce is delicious, and with 1 tablespoon of paprika, it had a little zing to it. It is a keeper and will make a great company dish once we get back to the real world of company and entertaining.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I used to get James Beard and Burl Ives mixed up, so I was humming as I cooked.

This is a less-zingy version of Chicken Marbella. I prefer the flavor of the zingier one, but that one does have prunes, which can be risky. So I’ll keep both recipes. Thanks Burl.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is a really great dish. Made it because my hubby thought it looked good. It is good. The combo of currants and olives make this a savory but slightly sweet Moroccan style dish. The added parsley and almond gremolata is a nice touch- do not leave that out. Served it with orzo. Will make this again and again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Did Spanish Farmer’s Chicken inspire Chicken Marbella?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So... I did this with tiny modifications. 1. I added chopped garlic... 2. i was going to skip the currants entirely because I don't like them... but when I tried the sauce (which was great) I get that it needed a little sweet to give it depth- so I improvised with cranberries... it was SO lovely!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is a lovely, flavorful entree.  It is classic comfort food when served with mash potatoes and a colorful roasted vegetable.  I've made it twice (in my moms old '70s' electric skillet) and the results were clean plates and no leftovers.  And it is an easy dish for weeknights and guests.  Enjoy a glass of wine while it cooks alongside the veggies---- or use the time to bake a light lemon pudding cake for dessert.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: A superb and hearty comfort dish that is even better the next day after a night in the fridge. The cooking times in the recipe seem rather…ambitious. Don’t be afraid to go lower and slower at the end. And that lemon zest at the end really provides a nice pop as counterbalance to the substantial earthiness of the rest of the dish. Nice!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: We used apricot instead of currants or raisins because that’s what we had on hand and it was delicious

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Use a bigger pot like a 5 quart dutch oven. I made a recipe and a half and chose too small of a skillet and had several boil-overs which forced me to change pots midway through cooking.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I found this a little boring: no flavor really jumped out.  In a similar vein, but much better, is an old San Francisco Chronicle recipe for Moroccan chicken, in which the paprika, lemon & almonds all work together for a terrific flavor burst.  The James Beard recipe seems dated to me, from back when cooks were just discovering African cuisine.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I sautéed chopped garlic with some good quality anchovies (letting them melt) before adding the onions and red peppers.  Gave it added depth it was delicious

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I really liked this dish. My Dad is a bit picky but he really enjoyed it, so that says a lot!
I made rice with the dish this time, but would love to try polenta next. 
Second day taste even better.
Only recommendation I would make is I would let the chicken legs simmer a bit longer than 20 mins.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I've made this recipe twice now. Each time, it's been delicious. I usually serve it with rice cooked in bone broth. Highly recommend!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was delicious! I slightly thickened the sauce at the end which made it even better. Served with rice and a side of roasted carrots. Easy and family favorite!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this with a cut up whole chicken and it was great. Agree that it is not a quick dish, but so what? It’s not like we’re going anyplace. The leftovers were just as good the next day. Definitely worth regular rotation.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Made it tonight—it was absolutely delicious and I bet it will be even better tomorrow as leftovers. The only adjustment I’ll make next time is 1/2 the amount of olives. Also, you probably already do this, but the chicken should be drained on paper towel after it is browned. 
This is a keeper and worth the effort.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Made this last night using chicken breasts (we don't like dark meat).  Because they were skinless & boneless (so no real chicken fat to render) I chopped up & rendered 2 slices of bacon before browning the chicken.

Also, the food was still a bit too liquidy when done, so I made a slurry of 2 teaspoons cornstarch and 3 Tablespoons of water, added that, and it came out at a perfect consistency. Didn't have almonds to finish, but the lemon zest & juice were essential to balance the flavors

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe is easy but a colossal disappointment.  The most important drawback is that the cooked chicken has no flavor.  As noted by others, the times are way off.  I did not need to cook in batches and I still needed more than an hour and a half.  With the amount of liquid, reducing the sauce takes a long time.  The Times has featured much better recipes with bone in, skin on, chicken thighs that do not require browning the chicken or excess liquid.  Chicken Provencal is a clear winner.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I still find Mr Zimmern’s claim of going to Beard’s Sunday soirées suspect. Maybe it’s part of his TV hype. As a long time friend of his father he never once mentioned knowing James Beard much less his young son going there.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was a great and easy recipe to make.  Was not expecting to like it in the hot hot hot of summer.  Many thumbs up.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Honestly? This was kinda meh.

You can tell it’s a 50-year-old recipe; it has “yt discovers North African cuisine” vibes. Sorta a toned down Chicken Marbella which is a toned down Moroccan style dish.

It’s not bad, everyone I made it for liked it, but with the time it took to reduce I may as well just do a tagine next time, because this was kinda boring.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So good!  With pepper instead of garlic and currants instead of prunes and with the omission of the brown sugar, this is a lighter cousin of Chicken Marbella.  I love both, but good to have this option.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Disappointing - flavors too subtle, no depth at all.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Very boring and tasteless dish with no reason to redo. The James Beard name didn’t help improve the lack of flavor. I will not include in my list of favorites to do again. Even my wife agreed. Tasted like canned soup.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So… I had REALLY high hopes for this one… and it fell well short of my expectations! First of all, I only got 2.5 lbs of chicken and it was still WAY too much for a family of 3- can we say leftovers for a week! Then I followed the recipe exactly and added a few of the suggestions… and yeah, BLANDVILLE! Not sure how you could improve this honestly… I mean it should be good, but it just isn’t. Sorry!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Given how much goes into this, I had hoped for a more flavorful result

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: We loved this dish. We served it over rice but I would have preferred orzo. I was a bit concerned toward the end when I tasted the sauce and thought it was basically awful. I had run out of oregano so I had used Italian Herbs instead, and I was worried that was what didn’t work. Then I added a little more salt and pepper, and added the lemon, the raisins cooked through, and it all came together in a wonderful sauce of several flavors.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was lovely! I used craisins instead of currants and served over wild rice.  We devoured it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I’m surprised you can get 3-4 lbs of chicken thighs that crispy (see photo) with just 1T of olive oil. And while the idea adding currents turns me off a bit, I would totally try this dish. 

Also, someone mentioned that this had a kick because of the paprika. Paprika can be Sweet Paprika, Smoked Paprika, Hungarian (made with spicier peppers), or Hot Paprika (which can also be a blend of paprika with a bit of cayenne). The right paprika will make this dish work for you.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: 1/2/23 I made this pretty much as instructed except I don’t like green olives so I used black. 6 pounds of chicken legs and thighs. 4 cups bone broth. And I let it simmer for 2 hrs until the chicken fell off the bones so then had to pick bones and cartilage out of what became a stew. The whole olives were out of place, should have been sliced or chopped. Flavor fantastic, we ate it over quinoa. The parsley and lemon add brightness, and the nuts a crunch.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My wife was over the moon with this one.  She just reminded me today that we hadn't had it in a month.  She's at the store as I write this. Great recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Was going to make this to the tee, but then my chicken was done, and I hadn’t decided on a starch… and the sauce was still so liquidy. Pulled the chicken out. Tossed in a cup or so of uncooked Cascatelli. Thanks Dan Pashman! Prepped the chicken to crisp under the broiler, and cooked the sauce till the pasta was tender. Delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I was a little skeptical of the flavor before adding the currants and olives, but it turned out great! Sweet with a bit of a zing from the paprika; will definitely be a repeat in my house. Served over jasmine rice.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made it, but found the mix of flavors a bit off for me. The sweetnes of the raisins did not mix well with the olives for me. I really love the French and Mediterranean mix of many chicken savory dishes. But the occasional raisin didn't add to that traditional flavor....for me.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this tonight for company. I let it simmer for an hour because you can’t really overcook thighs on the bone. Was good.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Left out raisins. Subbed dill for dried oregano.
Served with fresh fennel & basil.
Chef’s kiss.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious!  Made with 4 large thighs and left the rest of the proportions the same.  Family loved it and I will def make again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Great classic dish. Sauce is rich, nice change from tomato based sauces, and not too fatty.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So tasty. Made as directed except for adding garlic in with the onion, then stirred in some fresh spinach at the end. Beautiful. Did a combo of thighs and legs.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Just added this to my recipe box because one can never have too many ways to prepare chicken, especially thighs.  I find the comparison to Chicken Marbella amusing because the thought briefly crossed my mind, with the oregano, olives and white wine. There the comparison ends.  This is nothing at all like Marbella, but sounds delicious nonetheless.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was the perfect meal as a nor’easter pummels us with rain and sleet. Sometimes multi step recipes result in a lot of time with no flavor but this was delicious and we are already talking about when to make again. Used currants and castelvetrano olives 🙌🏻

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this last night with a few modifications.  First, I agree with the comments on how much time it took, longer than the recipes stated.  Second, I didn’t have green olives, so I substituted with kalamata olives and capers.  But I think a cup is too much, so next time I’ll reduce to 1/2 cup.  I also will try this with the cranberries as suggested by another.  I served it over rice and it was delicious.  I’ll definitely add it to my rotation.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: great recipe, mostly. I opted for a wet wine as it was extremely difficult to find dry in my area.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Not sure what all the hype is about for this one. Made this for the family tonight and it was a swing and a miss. No one liked it. Ended up reheating leftover pizza instead. Way too sweet, flavors not interesting at all. One of the worst things we’ve made in a while. Typically we love NYT recipes, this one not so much.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Some in my family get paprika hiccups, so I substituted with Aleppo Pepper, which worked beautifully. Family didn’t want to wait for full reduction, so I settled for a soupy dish and added more fresh lemon juice to bring out the flavors. Still so, so good. It’s on the keeper list.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The original recipe almost certainly called for a whole chicken, cut up. The current fad for using chicken thighs robs a dish of the delight of white meat, preferred by many, and the balance nature provides in a while bird.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: We’ve made this dish twice now and the flavors are delightful! The only thing I altered was that I crisped the chicken skin under the broiler for a few minutes after cooking. It’s such a shame to take all that time to fry crispy chicken skin and then dunk it in sauce to get soggy. Other than that, it’s a perfect dish! So bright and flavorful. Can’t wait to make it for company!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Second cousin to Chicken Marbella. It was perfectly fine, but redolent of the way we cooked in the 70's. Nostalgic, but nothing special.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Absolutely loved everything about this dish. 1/2 bone in tbighs and 1/2 boneless, skinless.  Perfect mixture for us. Used golden raisins.  Made some fresh bread. Served over wild rice

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this for dinner.Amazing; wouldn't change a thing about this recipe. Perfect as is.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this on Sunday with 4 thighs and 4 legs all in one batch.  I was able to complete the dish in the time estimated.  Only change was that I used black olives and dried cranberries fir raisins because that was what I had on hand.  Rave reviews from my family.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Maybe one hour if you have a saucier and junior chef in your kitchen. Closer to 2 hours than one. Sauce didn't really reduce but was excellent nevertheless. Served with orzo. I think rice would absorb too much. No need for another side. This is a really good party dish  because it can be cooked in advance.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this last night, very good and tasty. The whole family loved it and there was nothing left! I agree with the other reviews: Overall time was closer to 1h30mn. Step 4 was closer to 15mn and the 15mn in Step 5 turned out to be almost 30. I will cook this again!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
A wonderful Sunday evening meal.  Served with Orzo and was mighty tasty.
I agree with other posts - the reduction time is longer; like 105 minutes longer.  
Will serve again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If you use thighs with skin-on, you’ll render a lot of schmaltz. The recipe doesn’t mention draining the fat, but don’t make that mistake.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I cooked the chicken in 2 separate pans as noted.  This is a very good dish.  Would make again,

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
So delicious. Change your life and makes this! 

I subbed chopped prunes for the raisins, added in 1 tb anchovy paste with the wine, and added 1 c long grain rice (rinsed) right in with the chicken broth because I am lazy and didn't want to wash two dishes. Cooked in 30 minutes at 340 and was sheer perfection.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: As others have said, a wonderful recipe, and simple too.  Bought a whole chicken and cut into wings, thighs, drumsticks and breasts.  Lacked a large skillet so used a cast iron pot to brown the chicken pieces.  Took the very good suggestion of using a separate pan to brown the onions, pepper and to add the spices and stock.  Deglazed the cast iron pot with wine and then combined all in the pot. Also want to reaffirm that this is NOT a recipe for a night when you are short on time!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This smells so delicious!!!! I added about 6 cloves of garlic with the onion and red pepper. I’m curious about using roasted red peppers next time. Yum!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Wonder if this could be adapted to the Instant Pot? It does such an amazing with braises…

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I really liked this. Used only 2lbs of chicken, but full portions of everything else.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I'm always curious about recipes that have you brown chicken and then cover it. In my experience the skin then turns unappetizing and flabby. Better to have liquid half way up the chicken pieces and simmer. They will cook just fine.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I recommend adding a dollop of yogurt to each bowl and serving with a garlicky baguette. Absolutely delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Subbed the currants for dates. Braised for long than instructed. Delicious recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Trying to empty the fridge so I subbed a few ingredients: yellow pepper instead of red; airline chicken breasts instead of thighs; and served with homemade everything cornbread instead of garlic toast. Absolutely delicious, flavorful and overall simple to make for a weeknight meal - will definitely make again!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: You absolutely do not need to add salt, beyond salting the chicken as you brown it. The olives are salty enough. I had to add potatoes to bring down the saltiness.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: It is so nice to see this recipe again. This was my go to recipe for “fancy” entertaining when I was young and poor. I’d make it a day or two ahead of time, gently reheat and then crisp the skin under the broiled or in a 425 degree oven (just for a few minutes after reheating). This just went on my calendar for a small birthday party in October.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this today. I didn’t have dry white wine so I added three cups of chicken stock. Served over orzo that was tossed with parsley and feta. So good. Feels like the flavors will meld better the next day - looking forward to the leftovers!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Second time trying it, and the first time I used about 1/2 the requested chicken. This time the jus in the pan was WAY too much. Couldn’t boil it down to get the pan almost dry (step 2), even giving it 20 minutes. I would dry the pan between batches of chicken next time, to help avoid this.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious.  Concur with 90 minutes.  Used raisins as dried currants are hard to find.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Yes, wrap cheese tightly, in parchment paper. Never in plastic wrap.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Bake chicken  20-30 minutes low oven 135-145° internal

Then  proceed with recipe

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So tasty with fresh Castelvetrano olives!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this with way less butter (1/4 the amount) and it was great. Payed attention to the proportions of the spices but just estimated on mustard, spinach, and herbs. My wife said perfect balance of tangy, sweet, and mustard spice. I doubled the recipe for leftovers and added brown rice as a side, Will definitely add to the rotation!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Some people say this takes way longer than the recipe suggests. I wouldnt go that far but you do need to be very mindful of the heat.  To low of a heat, it will take awhile for everything to reduce. Too high, it will burn. Keep a watchful eye and adjust as needed. This dish came out delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I had high hopes for this recipe with so many good reviews, but neither my husband nor I liked it all that much.  I made no changes to the recipe.  The currants were good, but the flavor of the Castelvetrano olives seemed out of place in this dish and the rest of it was just ok (meh).  Not a fan.  I'm going to pick out the olives and try to add some more zing to the leftovers.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I tried this with a broken down whole roaster instead of just thighs and it may be my new favorite way to prepare a whole chicken.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Do you add the olive brine to the recipe?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My husband made this the other night and I am having the leftovers today. It is delicious and a keeper recipe. The currants and olive indicate the farmer was Mediterranean, which is a nice twist. The only changes to the recipe were he used chicken thighs (bone in skin on) as we prefer it to breast, and he forgot to buy the red pepper so he used carrot for sweetness. He said the cooking time is accurate but he is a retired chef and is super efficient in the kitchen. It would have taken me longer

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was delicious! The combination of ingredients was interesting and different. Do not skimp on the parsley/ toasted almond gremolata! We served with orzo. Will definitely make again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: a simple reading recipe that has a ton of depth. made it as written without substitution, will be adding it to my repertoire!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Delicious recipe. 

I didn't have a bell pepper, but followed the rest of the recipe to the letter, and it turned out great!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Read other reviews so decided to zip the flavors up a bit. Used half smoked paprika and half sweet. Added 2 tablespoons of capers and a bit of its juice. Did not have sweet pepper so I added carrot. Family liked it but thought it was difficult to eat on bone (in a bowl) since the liquid is more like a stew than a sauce. Shredded chicken and put in sauce for the leftovers. Made a nice stew!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: A good enough dish, but not really worth the effort it takes to prepare it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Who doesn’t have paprika in their spice drawer?  Me as it turns out.  I swapped some cajun seasoning and this still tasted grand. I agree with some of the previous comments that some of the reduction times may take longer than indicated.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I find the dish interesting, but I am not a fan of Olives, which I find to be too much of a briny 'punch' when eating a dish. I'll try it with small Capers and see how it comes out. Thanks to those who note the time to complete the dish is likely to be over the proscribed one hour.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I thought this was just "ok".  I followed recipe exactly.  Not sure it's worth the effort.  I rough chopped the olives and raisins.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Found this rather bland and not worth the time to make again. Not sure what folks are referring to when they say sweet paprika has a zing. Perhaps it would be better with a bit of hot or smoked paprika in it for flavor. My partner and I found the chicken really boring to the extent we don't want to eat the leftovers, but 4 pounds means there is a lot left. I'm going to attempt to salvage them by pan frying some capers, garlic, and more olives.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: You might like Country Captain, a somewhat similar recipe made with curry.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This recipe reminds me of a favorite southern recipe, Country Captain. It has many of the same ingredients, including the raisins and almonds. 

Country Captain doesn't have olives. 

It does have curry which makes it delicious. The sweetness of the raisins are a nice contrast.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So good! I made this last night per the instructions except I made 1/2 the amount of chicken. I made about 3/4 of the sauce and spice, and was careful not to over salt. It was excellent over garlic toasted homemade sourdough bread.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this tonight as laid out in the recipe.  Almost didn’t use the raisens but so glad I did.  The sweetness against the olive’s brine was very pleasant. Delicious dish!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Brown the chicken. This is longer than 5 mins per side

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The recipe states "at least 5 minutes". The recipe adds "until the skin is golden and releases easily from the pan".

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I somehow missed the reducing time entirely and my thoughts while eating this on the first day (two of us at home, so meals are almost always half today half tomorrow) I thought it was rather bland. But, on the second day after noticing I was supposed to reduce it, it was utterly delightful. Will definitely make again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I happened to have a fennel bulb on hand when I cooked this so minced it and tossed it into the pan with the onions and peppers. It turned out to be a happy improvisation, giving added dimension in flavour and texture to an already sensational dish.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Great recipe. We subbed raisins for currants and skipped the toppings.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: We have friends in Alcoholics Anon and don’t consume dishes made with wine. What can I substitute for the dry white wine?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This dish was fantastic!! Definitely worth the work. I put it in the crockpot to transport and keep warm. I served it with rice and garlic toast. My boyfriend loved it!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this for the family Sunday dinner. No leftovers! It was so delicious and lots of great flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: made this and found it quite easy the sauce is fantastic i used golden raisins and jared manzanitas.  served with far east parmesan couscous it was divine. i would brown the chicken a little less as my chicken was a hair over done in my opinion. but i am a finicky chef   definitely on my rotation going forward   i will make with Israeli pearl couscous next time for a little texture but either would be good.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Easy to prepare. Fresh and light flavors with the brightness of the lemon zest. The whole family loved it and enjoyed the leftovers just as much.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Which farmers, I ask.  Certainly not American, but its absolutely delicious and worth the timer.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Easy to make and delicious to eat. What more is there?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Amazing in a slow cooker.  Not so impressive on a skillet

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious, even though I substituted red wine for white. Who runs out of white wine???

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I love vintage recipes with a story attached to them.  Because the ingredients all seem counter-intuitive (oregano, paprika, and raisins), I followed the recipe exactly, and measured everything exactly.  It turned out as it was supposed to, I hope.  The sauce was rich, flavorful, balanced.  For the last covered simmer, I added about a cup and a half of carrots sliced on the diagonal to make this a one dish meal.  A wonderful dish worthy of serving for company or a simple Sunday supper.

author: Julia Moskin
