commentBody: This was alright. It had good flavor - I followed the recipe exactly because of the unusual combination (although I substituted craisins for raisins). I think this would work really well for a dinner party but I am dreading eating the leftovers (it just wasn't good enough to eat more than once but this recipe makes SO MUCH). I also think this recipe might have turned me off chicken thighs for a while.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Don’t reduce sauce too much - lots of sauce is the star

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Instead of stirring in the oregano and paprika before the wine, I put it in after alongside the stock, raisins, and olives. The taste was much stronger that way.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this with only the slightest changes: used a broth made from corncobs  and chicken base and added some corn to the total. It was splendid, tasted very interesting and subtle and was really easy and fast to put together.  I allowed the thighs to come to room temperature before I cooked them and used the times mentioned.  I  held the dish several hours in the frig  before I rewarmed and served it. The meat was perfectly cooked.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Loved this recipe! I used deboned, skin-on chicken thighs and really browned the skin. Because my family loves crispy chicken skin, I returned the chicken to the pan skin side up and simmered uncovered without basting the skin to keep it as crispy as possible. Served this with Kay Chun’s Orzo Salad with Tomatoes and Feta - delicious! For anyone worried about the previously mentioned lengthy cook time - deboning significantly cuts down cook time and makes the dish even more enjoyable.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious and easy and I don't particularly care for lamb. Got the bone in shoulder. Used ground cumin because I was out of seed. Worked fine as a substitute. Served with couscous with lots of parsley and toasted slivered almonds.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this recipe this past weekend the first time for a dinner party, which I know broke all the rules on never cooking a new dish for guests.  Well, it turned out great and everyone loved it. Served it with orzo and roasted carrots.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody:
Two tips for this or any turkey breast roulade:
- Butterfly or pound out and salt generously 1-2 days ahead (dry brine)
- Before filling, remove the skin in one piece, then fill and roll and re-cover with the skin and tie. This way it’s all crispy skin on the outside and no soft flabby skin rolled up inside.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Genius tip on the skin! Doing it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I am going to try this, but will be adding a bunch of fresh spinach to the filling. It's what I have done in the past for veal and pork and it's just a great combo with these flavors.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Have made various turkey roulades several times & whether this recipe or others, I SO agree with cooks who have posted here about the importance of NOT rolling the skin in with the roulade but making sure the skin is kept on the outside. Otherwise you'll end up with flabby skin roasted in the final product. One of the best 'how to' videos for roulade turkey breast for technique is by J. Kenji Lopez-Alt. See steps 3 and 4. Can apply technique here. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mEofA0SvS1k

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: @dimmerswitch thank you for posting this informative video!!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody:
Two tips for this or any turkey breast roulade:
- Butterfly or pound out and salt generously 1-2 days ahead (dry brine)
- Before filling, remove the skin in one piece, then fill and roll and re-cover with the skin and tie. This way it’s all crispy skin on the outside and no soft flabby skin rolled up inside.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with porkloin?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Oops- because I'm not good at looking, I didn't see the reply- I replied to you in a post- yes, I do pork roast (not tenderloin) like this all the time with a different filling, tho. I put @ Silvia at the beginning of the post.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: It takes a village to make a turkey breast roulade. I just prepared it and have placed in the refrigerator for a couple of days (as suggested by J. Kenji Lopez-Alt at this link: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mEofA0SvS1k). Thanks Ina Garten, Julia Moskin and by J. Kenji Lopez-Alt--to get this far, I needed all of you. I highly recommend you watch the youtube video if you are going to attempt this and you are not a professional chef. Happy Thanksgiving (I hope.)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Back in Argentina, my aunts made it filled with chimichurri sauce.  I have fond memories of those flavors from my childhood

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: could mini roulades be made with chicken breasts?  I know it's thanksgiving but we do not like turkey.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Is the prosciutto necessary, or might some substitute be suggested for those who do not eat pork?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Use smoked salt in the recipe if you can find it.  I particularly love applewood smoked salt in place of bacon and other cured pork products.  You get the general flavor profile without the fat and other issues.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: This process cries out for an explanatory video. Cheers!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: PLEASE amend the recipe to indicate Diamond Kosher salt, if that is indeed what is called for here. I used 3 tsp of Morton's Kosher salt (stopped there because it just looked like a lot of salt, of which I am generally not afraid) and the turkey was too salty, though not completely inedible. If I'd known that Diamond was called for I'd have stopped at 2 tsp. I'm surprised not to find many comments about this; recipe otherwise great, do follow other tip to remove skin before rolling!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: The above note about using the skin is perfect. I also found that pounding the turkey to 1/2 thickness nearly impossible. Instead, slice off a few small fillets from the inside until you have the proper thickness. Lastly, to prevent the ends of the roll from drying out in the oven, wrap both ends in butter soaked cheesecloth and leave on until the last 20-30 minutes. Then remove the cheesecloth and let the ends brown.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Woah Lordy! What an afternoon!  Just put the boned, pounded, stuffed, rolled and tied roulade in the fridge to roast tomorrow.  Grating the butter was a surprise I had missed in reading the recipe ahead - but quite doable.  Another adventure in cooking.  My kitchen is a wreck but the roulade sure looks great!  Stay tuned.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: See my comment post-Thanksgiving above.  Good but too much salt.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: The recipe assumes that the Turkey has already been butterflied (think of butterfly wings closed then opened) to make one large piece of meat. It’s easiest if you lay the breast skin side down. Starting at the thinnest end of the breast take a sharp knife and cut horizontally in half until you almost reach the thick end and then open it up. Pound the meat, especially in the middle which is where the thick end was. Or the butcher at the meat counter can do this for you.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: We have not made this, but we bought her book “Modern Comfort Food” and it is really good. We have made several of the recipes and they were all great.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Our household has the mammal-meat allergy (Alpha-Gal), so I substituted chopped portabellas crisped up in butter and salt for the prosciutto. I was unsure about the amount of liquid the recipe called for, but added it obediently, and the result was moist, tender, and delish. Thank you.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: This was not so hard or messy as some comments imply. My one tip would be to use frozen butter and grate directly onto the onion mixture after spreading it on the turkey breast. I made the mistake of grating refrigerated butter into a bowl and then trying to spread it, but by that time it had warmed too much. This was a great twist on the thanksgiving classic! I also put an elephant ear garlic in with the turkey which was great to mix with the turkey and mashed potatoes.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: What can be used in lieu of prosciutto?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: the italians use a layer of omelette and spinach . I've done it and is fantastic.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Made this for Thanksgiving this year and it was wonderful.  I did a little online research and I added these tricks to this recipe:  I scored the inside of the breast before adding the salt, peper and then the stuffing.  I also did this on Tuesday, rolled and tied and then wrapped in plastic and then foil and let sit in the fridge until Thursday morning.  I took it out and allowed it to reach room temperature before roasting.  I was real good -- definitely will make this again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Made this for my Friendsgiving and everyone asked for the recipe. Was perfectly seasoned and so flavorful. Would recommend to anyone!!!!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Instead of prosciutto use turkey bacon!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Would mushrooms serve as alternative to the smoked meat or prunes?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Man, this is off-the-charts good.  I followed the recipe almost exactly, but used a little extra prosciutto and a 50:50 mix of olive oil and bacon fat when cooking the onion/garlic/herb mixture.   That filling is just nuts.  My only regret is that I roasted it in a larger roasting pan than necessary so the liquid cooked off too quickly.   I replenished the pan liquid a second time and still ended up with few pan juices because of evaporation.   Too much surface area in the pan.   Live and learn.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I’m so disappointed. I always read the comments and no where did anyone say BE MINDFUL OF THE SALT! DI’m actually upset with myself for not cutting it down to 1/4 of what was list. It’s almost inedible. It’s flavorful, simple to make, but the stuffing overpowers the turkey. It could be chicken or any other fowl and if I hadn’t cooked it , would call it stuffing with mystery meat. Thankfully everything else I made was lovely particularly Ina Gartens roasted carrots with oranger & balsamic glaze.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I thought there was too much salt too!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: @Marti: I completely FORGOT to put in any salt and was worried it wouldn't taste good. It was fine, the herb/onion/prosciutto filling was more than enough to flavor the meat.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Can anyone suggest a substitute for the prosciutto? I was thinking of thin slices of portabella mushrooms sautéed in butter until crisp. I have the mammal meat allergy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Hi MaryAye, I had the same question. Can't do pork. I like your idea of portabellos. I'm thinking of looking at old stuffed-chicken recipes, maybe combine the mushroom with a bit of ricotta cheese and a little egg to hold it together. Good luck!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Prosciutto slices are typically extremely thin. The flavor of portabella sounds like a delicious alternative but I would make sure they're sliced as thinly as possible.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I'd be afraid of portobellos getting watery and staining the whole thing brown. How about thinly sliced oranges? I have a regular porchetta recipe that uses those, and it's delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: With a white cut of meat like this, I would suggest brining overnight. I have used rosemary, tarragon and onion as the flavor carriers, and have had outstanding results. I also smoke the roulade at 250/275 for 4 or 5 hours on my BGE. Wonderful! Searing it before hand really adds to the flavor and tenderness, too. I'll never go back to a whole turkey with this roulade as a mainstay

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody:
Any recommendations on a substitute for prosciutto? We don’t eat pork. 

Thanks!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Baco-Bits - they're made w/ soy and taste like bacon.  Maybe sage or another similar herb.  Or, just leave it out.  We won't tell the food police.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Amal Lama - you ask about substitute for prosciutto since you don't eat pork.  You can just leave out the prosciutto and just use the herbs and butter.  For comparison, with cured pork this is analogous to chicken cordon bleu and w/out it to chicken kiev - both of those roulades.  And turkey roulade can be filled with many things...bread crumbs, dried fruit - like chopped apricots or figs - roasted red or poquillo peppers...we've made a lot of them in different ways.  Have fun!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Yes, works with chicken breasts as well. Made 5 individual remoulades and everyone at the table raved.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: De-bone the breast at home.  Pound entire breast, preferable cut into 2 to 4 roulades, to thickness of cutlets or scallops.  Then fill, roll, reattach skin if removed, and tie.  Roast roulades in Dutch oven with lid removed.  Use high-smoke-point oil to baste often while roasting.  Make sure wine bath doesn't boil dry; replenish it if need be.  Remove roulades at 150 degrees F, pour off wine bath, return roulades to Dutch oven & cover.  Let the turkey finish itself.  Reduce wine bath to a sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night. It was delicious. I had two smaller breasts so ended up making two instead of one large one. I too removed the skin first so it only covered the outside and didn't get soggy. I checked mine at 1 hour and it was overcooked but was still juicy. No need to dry brine or soak in buttermilk unless you really want to. Pan juices were also delicious. I will definitely make this again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Is there a video on this recipe?  Love to see how it’s rolled up.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Mike C: Re 'how to' video you ask about, one of the best 'how to' videos for roulade turkey breast for technique is by J. Kenji Lopez-Alt. See steps 3 and 4. Can apply technique here. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=mEofA0SvS1k I've made turkey roulades several times & whether this recipe or others, I SO agree with cooks who have posted here about the importance of NOT rolling the skin in with the roulade but making sure the skin is kept on the outside. As Lopez-Alt demonstrates.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: The biggest challenge for me was finding a butcher to prepare such a large turkey breast. It was a big hit and fun to make, and of course, delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Do you think this could be done with 1/2 turkey breast (2 1./2 lbs)?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I hope so, as that's what I've ordered! I don't see why not. Just scale down the quantities, and keep an eye on the time. Start testing the temperature after 45 minutes.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: In my experience, cooked turkey breast freezes very well.  So in December you may wish you had cooked the full recipe and frozen it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Yes! I tried it last night, halving all the ingredients. It came out very well. Because it’s much smaller, it cooks a lot faster—definitely start checking w a meat thermometer after 30 min. Depending on your oven, it will probably be ready in 45 min or so, but go by the 150F reading to prevent it from drying out. Also, I had very fresh sage from the garden, and I found it rather overwhelmed the other flavors, so go easy on the sage if yours is fresh & pungent.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: What would be a nice substitute for fennel seeds? I'm not a fan of them.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Do you like anise seeds? Same licorice taste family, if that's the problem. Otherwise, I'd leave them out and not substitute.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Try coriander seeds. I’d grind them coarsely with a mortar and pestle first.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Basil, tarragon, or just leave it out.  Fennel is the background note of Italian sausage, so if that is something you DO like, maybe just cut back to a few seeds crushed well.  Then it won't be a dominant flavor, but the dish will feel finished.  While fennel IS a primary  component to porchetta (on which this is based) there is so much flavor here already.  You could go full-on traditional turkey and use thyme and mustard instead or the fennel.  It will be lovely.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Does the roasting pan require a rack as well?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I had the same question and I just looked up this exact same Ina recipe on the Food Network site and it says it DOES require a rack.  Now I have to go out and find one before Thursday!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: to Karen - Before you go buy a rack, think if you really need one.  Do you have anything else you could use?  A couple of lengths of crumpled-up foil?  A few metal jar lids covered in foil?  Anything heat safe/food safe that you can put under this to hold it up slightly?  Or, just sit the roast in the pan - the bottom skin won't crisp up, but it will still cook.  I didn't have a roasting rack until I hit my late 40s, and we had some very nice roasts.  It helps, but it's not a deal-breaker.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Where to buy a boneless, butterflied, skin on turkey breast?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I called my local butcher and they had it!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I went to Whole Foods today and asked and they kindly did it in a few minutes.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: To Silva, below.  I use these ingredients to cover pork tenderloins, minus the onions.  I often sub strips of thin bacon for prosciutto.  Let them sit over night with the seasonings rubbed all over them in the refrigerator and then roast quickly in the oven.  So easy to largely the same effect.  In this case I don't bother with the onions, but sometimes quarter them and roast on the same sheet pan.  Great  when there's leftovers for sandwiches, too.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: My husband only likes dark meat so we do this with the thighs. Pretty great!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Where does one get a butterflied turkey breast?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I had to butterfly my own.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: had my local butcher skin and debone the breast, easy and fast and I pounded it here before making it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody:
Butterfly or pound out and salt generously 1-2 days ahead (dry brine)
- Before filling, remove the skin in one piece, then fill and roll and re-cover with the skin and tie.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Can I make this with a skinless turkey breast? I can’t find breasts with the skin on where I live. How do I keep it from drying out? Thanks for your response.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: You could roll bacon strips around it. Check out the video for Melissa Clark's one pot/one pan Thanksgiving menu this week. She does this w/her skinless turkey breast.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I am dumb. Can anybody please clarify what she means by cutting it open and how exactly to roll it?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Earlier, someone posted this video w/Kenji Lopez-Alt making a turkey roulade. Very helpful. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=--98VRFpvEQ

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody:
I just finished making this (not cooked yet, it's for tomorrow) and here's a tip: 
I watched the Kenji López-Alt video (see other comments for link) which was VERY helpful, in fact I'd say essential viewing. You have to line up the pieces of twine (or maybe this was another video)... Anyway, my twine stayed spiraled as it came off the ball of twine so I wet it, and that straightened it. It also seemed to make it easier to stay tied.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Kudos to you all who made this successfully!  I just prepped mine for tomorrow too and everything went great until the roulade part!  I separated the skin but there wasn't enough of it to really cover the meat.  I pounded it out but it was still thick and hard to roll, and there's only skin on a very small part of it.  Tying was okay, I hope it holds together!  Also hoping the cooking liquid prevents it from drying out, since it is mostly uncovered.  Fingers crossed!  Happy T-Day, y'all!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I think a whole cup of wine and cup of water is too much, in my case. My turkey breast was a small one, 4 lbs, and I'm cooking it in a fairly small dish (not a roasting pan). It is literally swimming in liquid even after one hour. So much for "serve with the pan juices". I don't quite know what to do with all that liquid.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Stunning.  One of the best recipes I've ever cooked from NYTimes Cooking.  The comments below that relate to taking the skin off and then putting it back on after the rolling step really helped, as did the comments about tying at either end before making the middle ties.  The video in the comments below gives a great hands-on visual of how to make the roll (steps 3 and 4 above).  (Disregard that it contains a different filling, the next step is cooking sous vide, etc.) Again, stunning.  Thanks!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: My $30 turkey breast from Whole Foods (!!) was skinless, so I panicked. Nonetheless, I followed the recipe (a bit challenging, so watch the video recommended by others!) and let the prepared roulade "marinate" in the fridge for 3 days. I threw in a few pads of butter, rubbed it with olive oil, roasted it, basting frequently and used a meat thermometer. The result was tastier, juicier and more delicious than I imagined it would be ... it was also pretty to look at sliced on the serving plate.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Made this for a small thanksgiving this year and it was the hit of our meal.  Also prepped and seasoned the day before and so easy to just throw in the oven. Will probably do this again for Christmas with some tweaks of my own. Thank you for the skin recs, must do that next time!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: The roulade was beautiful, although the skin did not crisp up.  I would use less salt, especially on the finished thing before putting into the oven. I would rather have pounded the meat thinner than the recommended one inch and perhaps made two smaller roulades since it's hard to keep the two flattened breasts as you make the one roll.  Tying it up is critical.   I would make this again, perhaps with bacon on the outside. The J. Kenzi Lopez-Alt YouTube video was helpful.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Great tip on removing the skin in one piece before rolling. I also put carrots, new potatoes, and sliced onions under the turkey. Worked beautifully. Lifted them out and made gravy in the pan.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: As usual Ina Garten’s recipes deliver. Follow the suggestion to remove the skin in tact. I used it to cover the seam before tying - helping the roast stay sealed & juices locked. A YouTube video for another Ina Turkey Roulade was helpful to see how to open & flatten the breast. I squared off the edges of the flattened breast to help it roll more evenly and used the scraps to make stock to supplement the pan drippings. The temp rises quickly at the end - so keep your eye on it. A huge success!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody:
Cooked for 2020 Thanksgiving for 2, with carrot puff, green bean casserole, pumpkin pie, & apple smash cocktails. 

Have the butcher butterfly the breast for you, or consult Youtube. Skip the brine, use 3tsp salt to season rather than 4.

Definitely remove the skin and use that to wrap the breast. It won't fit all the way around when you pound out the turkey, but that's OK.

To bake, make a tinfoil hat for the first 45 min, then take off for the rest. Regular cookie sheet with rack works.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I added finely diced mushrooms in with the onion mixture and cooked both down a fair amount. Very delicious add. Would be good with nuts as well, similar to how you’d prepare the meat for a Wellington. Agreed w other chefs on removing the skin, critical! If it’s not getting crispy enough just broil for a min. Did the dry salt brine a day ahead and turned out delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: We made this for our smaller Thanksgiving gathering this year and it was DELICIOUS! I brined the Turkey breast in buttermilk and salt for 48 hours (recipe from Samin Nosrat) and then prepped the roulade a few hours before cooking because that part is a little time consuming. I also removed the roulade from the fridge about an hour before roasting so that it was room temp before going in the oven.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: The best turkey breast I have ever made.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Amazing! Family was blown away at Thanksgiving. We ended up butterflying the turkey breast ourselves (which didn't go too well...) and had 3 mini roulades, but still tasted amazing. Would highly recommend to everyone!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: The 4 tsp said (Step 3) is way too much especially when one is also using prosciutto. I would reduce  by at least one half. I also followed  ASP's suggestion below. I removed skin and wrapped it around roulade tying it with string.  Excellent suggestion.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody:
Cook in smaller roasting pan so juices do not evaporate.
Check after a half hour as roulade was overdone.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I ended up making this recipe with chicken instead of turkey and it turned out great. Awesome alternative if you cannot find turkey.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Made this for Canadian thanksgiving yesterday. I did the pounding and tying 2 days before then refrigerated.  It cooked FAST!  Unfortunately it was dry, even with the butter and wine.  But i did leave out the prosciutto due to a guest’s diet.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: This comes out pretty salty.  I would recommend cutting down the salt by at least 25%.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody:
This is the second consecutive year with this preparation and it is perfection. For smaller gatherings it is elegant and very showy. Plus, it permits time for other things on the big day rather than constantly basting and checking and hoping breast and legs will cook evenly.

FOR SURE remove the skin and then wrap it around the whole roulade.

I'll be using this preparation for many years to come.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Made the roulade again for Thanksgiving this year.  It is a delicious and and elegant alternative to traditional roast turkey.  As suggested by others I wrapped the roulade with the skin after I rolled it up and followed J. Kenji Lopez-Alt’s video on how to prep the roulade.  I made the roulade the day before Thanksgiving to allow the flavors to come together before roasting it day of.  I didn’t find the prep overly difficult or time consuming.  If you haven’t tried this Ina recipe do.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Any tips on butterflying the breast? Chicken breast is relatively easy, but turkey looks kinda daunting for those of us who aren't Jacques Pepin.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Made this two and three years ago. Best turkey ever.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I needed a smaller cut for 4 people. Great tips from readers to (1) reduce salt and to (2) remove skin and wrap it around the bundle (rather than inside). For my smaller breast (3.5-4 lbs before I deboned) I followed a Skinnytaste recipe and a key tip was to remove the tenderloin and pound it for a more even roulade. I cooked this smaller version at 375 for 1 hour and it was perfection. Butter inside keeps it so moist. LOVE this and will go to it again and again for special occasions.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Very similar recipe in November/December Cook’s Illustrated, with good illustrations for assembly. I found their recipe too herbal in flavor, so will try Ina Garten’s seasoning next time.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Help!  My boneless turkey breast did not come with skin.  I don’t want to wrap it in bacon or prosciutto, any other tips to help keep it moist while cooking?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, moist perfect white meat. I thought it was a little intimidating when I first read the recipe, but now that I've done it 3x it's a snap. Totally worth it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: This was the biggest hit for Thanksgiving this year. The turkey was perfectly moist and so flavorful, and took an hour and a half.  Will be making this again!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I used this stuffing recipe for a deboned whole turkey that I made. It was 10.8 pounds and yielded 7 pounds of boneless turkey. I used Food Wishes YouTube video for deboning, excellent video and guidelines. I basted the turkey with olive oil before roasting. Roasted for 15 min at 450* and then 1.25 hr at 325 to get to 150. The pan juices were slightly charred around the edges so not suitable for pan sauce (need slightly smaller roasting pan?) All the bones yielded 5 qts of turkey broth.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Wow...This is very easy and incredibly tasty.  The combination of flavors is outstanding.  I will be making this for Thanksgiving this year.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Finally, had a chance to prepare this roulade.  The flavor is phenomenal! The fennel really pumps up the flavor of the other herbs.  A must.  Like the suggestions below such as removing the skin until the roulade is rolled, then replace.  Bypassed prosciutto and used thin sliced and chopped portobellos.  350 temp is fine but I think 145 internal temperature is better.  It will continue to cook while resting.  Tremendously flavorful recipe.  Hard to return to a whole turkey in future.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: OMG.  Forgot to add I used finely chopped bok choy.  Also, didn’t have Chablis.  Substituted Chablis and water with 2 cups chicken broth.  Great gravy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody:
Susan used a 300
degree oven instead of 350 and cooked for one and a half-two hrs.  She checked the temp of the turkey every 15 min after the first hour to make sure the turkey didn't dry out. meat temp shouldn't exceed 150

She put a rub of 1 Tbs kosher salt (on the pounded turkey breast), half tsp pepper, 1 tsp sage, 1 tsp thyme, and 2 Tbs brown sugar, on the turkey for 24 hrs before baking.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Regarding salt: am I correct that, unless otherwise noted, Ina is calling for table salt and NOT kosher salt? Thanks.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I missed it on first look but under ingredients it says "kosher salt and freshly ground black pepper"

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: The recipe calls for Kosher. Cheers!!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Andrea, since the only salt listed in the ingredients is kosher I'd assume all salt in the recipe is kosher salt.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Do you roll the skin into the roulade? or remove it?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: When you butterfly, one side will be skinless, the other half/side skin on. I would start rolling from the skinless side into the skin on. Should wind up skin outside the whole-at least that’s what it appears in the picture

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Can you cut back on the butter?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: One should never cut back on butter.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Assuming this would work with skinless breast as well?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Wondering how many this will serve?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: At the beginning of the article it says 8 to 10 servings.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Can this be made in a smaller version for 2-4 people?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Sure!  Just buy a single turkey breast.  It will probably be skinless.  Instead or wrapping it in the skin so it is moist, you can wrap it in bacon or pancetta.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: This recipe sounds delicious! Has anyone had issues with overcooking? I've not tried it yet but dryness is why I typically avoid cooking turkey breast. When cooking a whole turkey, I will often cook it breast side down. Thoughts/suggestions?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Brine it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody:
APK, SusanKR: on the skin, can you be more specific on the technique? With the exception of the salt/brine, it seems the only difference is that when the roulade cooks, the skin is separated from the meat, but wrapped back up as if it were still attached - is that right? The theory being that the skin will crisp better unattached from the meat? (I usually run a spoon under the skin to separate it from the meat when I roast a whole chicken.)
Thanks.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made a similar recipe using individual turkey thighs  and adding fennel pollen to the sautéed mixture. But I do want to try this recipe especially with the prosciutto.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I love cooking turkey breast with my sous-vide machine. The internal temp for the texture I like is 145deg F (yes, it's fully cooked). Wonder if this can recipe can be adapted for it. I would then sear the outside at the end. I would just be concerned that the stuffing ingredients might not cook correctly at this slightly lower temperature.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: The recipe says "Set the turkey breast on a cutting board and open it up". That is not enough instruction for me. Is this just one cut? Horizontally or vertically? Or more than one cut, like a spiral? Someone please help me understand this, because I'd like to make this for our small (4 people) Thanksgiving next week.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Here is a video from another Ina turkey breast roulade recipe - it has a different stuffing. This should show you how she prepares the turkey breast. I think it is similar. https://www.foodnetwork.com/recipes/ina-garten/roasted-turkey-roulade-recipe-1957892

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: This looks insanely good but my husband will not forgo the traditional whole roasted turkey - even though there are only 4 of us this year! I’m going to use these seasonings (onion, fennel seed, garlic, rosemary, etc) for our stuffing/dressing. Then we both get what we want. Happy Thanksgiving!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Needs a luscious thick gravy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Lovely recipe. Followed it closely but didn’t pound it down and it still cooked evenly. My husband is a big fan of traditional turkey breast(Ms. Garten has a great recipe for that too) and he loved this roulade. Definitely will make it again and will try the suggestion with the skin.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Can steps 1-4 be done a day before roasting, stored on a rack in the fridge?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I wonder if this would freeze well.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Dose anyone have thoughts about using turkey tenderloins with this recipe?  Would it alter the cooking time or the amount of wine/water needed?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone thought about using Samin Nosrat's buttermilk brine for this?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Yep. My breast is in the fridge in buttermilk right now. Will roll and roast tomorrow. I will let you know how it turns out.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I could only find turkey breast without skin (I'm in Rome, Italy). How do I make sure this doesn't dry out? Or how can I add texture?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: @ Caitlin, this is probably too late for you now, but you could wrap it in bacon. And since you're in Rome, wrap it in pancetta!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: perhaps you could rub it with the juice of an orange and place bacon strips on it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: An update here in case anyone reading this also has a skinless breast; I didn’t change the recipe with regards to the skin (or lack thereof) and how I was cooking it and it came out lovely. I did add more water and wine to the roasting pan, plus some pancetta which added a nice smokiness. Cooked until just done and served soon after with a nice spoonful of sauce on every slice.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: do you put the roulade in the liquid before cooking or on a rack?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I'm not sure why you have to grate the butter. You could probably just cut it into small cubes. Taking a tip from Martha Stewart, I wrapped the roulade in cheesecloth, after placing the sage leaves on top, before tying it. My deboning and butterflying technique leaves something to be desired.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Made this with turkey tenderloin (butterflied) wrapped in prosciutto. It was great, but I made the silly mistake of tying it up with string before I put the prosciutto on top. It looked so perfect, but cutting it was awkward this way.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I made this today since it was only my husband and myself and it is absolutely delicious. I did use the tip to save the skin and put it on the outside of the breast when it was already rolled and twined and it was great. the flavors were intense. I put all the vegetables that I had in the fridge that were expiring and at the end I used all the juices to make the gravy, great result there too. it was very tender and juicy, highly recommend this recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Made it as written, except I didn’t have kitchen twine. Used thread. It was much easier than expected to roll it up. The pan juices got a little burnt but enough was good to drizzle over. It was a beautiful centerpiece to our meal and we loved this alternative to a whole turkey.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Dry brinded the turkey overnight and then prepared in the morning exactly as recipe called for but did follow the suggestion to keep the skin to the outside.  It was moist and delicious.  What a hit!! I always loved watching Ina  and must admit to a bit of a crush on her.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I couldn’t find a full butterflied breast, so I ended up pounding together 4 small skinless breasts (still 3 lb total). Followed another comment and wrapped it in pancetta since it didn’t have skin. It was a bit hard to roll but turned out very well! Also did a wet brine about 12 hours before and that made it extra juicy and flavorful.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly, removed Roulade from oven when it reached 155.  Let it sit for 15 minutes, served it immediately - too dry (just like so many other Thanksgiving turkeys I’ve had).  A lot of work for such a let down.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I found the garlic was a bit too much. I’d cut back on that a bit. By far this was the best turkey we’ve had in years though. Thank you Ina!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody:
I wish I would’ve looked at the notes before I started for that skin tip! The turkey breast we ordered was actually 3 breasts, so I butterflied them and made one roulade with two rolled together side-by-side and one smaller one. 

I wasn’t getting that lovely browning from the photo so I let mine cook past the 150 degrees and it was a bit dry, so I should’ve watched out for that. Overall a pretty good recipe but thought it could use more flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I was surprised by how simple this was to make (though I was well-prepared, for once).  The day before I prepared the garlic herb filling and stored it in a small bowl in the fridge.  I also salted the front /back of the butterflied turkey the day before, and patted it dry an hour before stuffing.  For some reason the butcher left me with a small bit of skin, but this was not a problem as I could center it on the top of the roulade and it still browned nicely.  A huge hit!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Boned a whole turkey and made two smaller roulades. Worked out fine. Really nice alternative to plain turkey. Made with Ina’s do ahead gravy base and just added turkey juice to finish. Keep the turkey fat from the pan to cook veggies for turkey pot pie for added turkey flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Used dried sage and bacon fat instead of oil. Omitted Prosciutto. Worked out beautifully.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Fantastic. Delicious. Easy. I used chicken breasts instead of turkey.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: You don't need the prosciutto (or the butter) if you do as SeriousEats recommends and sous vide it.  The skin crisps up beautifully after and it's perfectly moist inside.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Used chicken for time #3, very good. Note to NOT out salt inside the chicken/turkey, prosciutto salty enough.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I'll be preparing with wild turkey breast, and the skin has been removed. Do I still brush with the oil/S&P? Do I cover with foil/paper to keep it from drying out?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I've done this with a filling of breadcrumbs, cream cheese, sour cream, thyme, and coriander, ending with a dusting of coriander on top. The idea came from John McPhee's essay "Brigade de Cuisine," a must-read for anyone who loves cooking. It's a portrait originally published in The New Yorker of a chef who ran a restaurant in the sticks and avoided publicity. The prose is as delicious as the food he describes. Read it!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody:
I have made this twice-once as the original recipe and again with a filling of cooked chopped spinach, swiss chard, onions and golden raisins. Delicious both times. After deboning and “opening up “ the turkey breast, I brine it for 2 hours before stuffing, rolling and tying it.
My preference is a wet brine of kosher salt, brown sugar, peppercorns and bay leaves.
easy and delicious-crispy skin and tender, flavorful,meat. btw-I have also done this with a whole chicken that I deboned-harder to do.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Good but not brilliant.  Even by taking the advice in the comments/tips to remove turkey skin (so as to not be wrapped inside and turn flabby, instead putting skin on outside of entire roulade), the skin didn’t add much value.   We found the prosciutto was nice and salty, but flabby texture on inside.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I don’t use prosciutto or onion.  I make a paste of thyme, sage, rosemary, garlic, fennel seed, salt, pepper, olive oil and lots of orange zest.  Smells and tastes great.  On a sheet pan I roast brussels sprouts, fennel bulb, carrots and onion and serve that on the side. Easy low carb Thanksgiving dinner.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Stuff from the cut side don’t cover the tenderloin and roll from the tenderloin side see Boston University video. Use carrots and celery two whole turkey up or you done with these things are not

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: If you’re doing a dry brine w salt a day or two before, should you cut back on the salt when preparing the roulade? Or proceed with the recipe as usual?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I made this for Christmas dinner tonight and it was wonderful. I put it together yesterday and just popped it in the oven today, which was perfect. I’d ordered a whole turkey breast with the skin from our butcher, but realized I’d ended up with two skinless halves once I got it home. I managed to keep them together with an excess of twine, and I wrapped the whole thing in bacon to make up for the skin. It was delicious! Will make again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: This was a delicious main for Christmas for a family that avoids red meat. Followed the recipe except added some cooked kale to the stuffing and dry-brined it as one reader suggested. It appears my thermometer is broken, but even a little overcooked it was delicious and tender. A keeper.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: My son is a turkey hunter so I made this last year with a wild turkey breast but had no skin. It was a tad dry so I think I'm going to try it again this year but wrap it with the prosciutto instead. I'm also going to make it ahead of time (as suggested in that fine video from J. Kenji Lopez-Alt) and see if the flavors permeate the meat a little more. Overall it's a really great way to have turkey and pairs well with the traditional Thanksgiving sides.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: 6 garlic cloves is WAY more than two tablespoons minced?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. I used 1.5 lbs turkey breast for two (with leftovers). Agree with making sure the skin part of the breast is on the outside. Also, the wine/ pan juices were great to serve on the sliced chicken.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this twice now and it’s really the only way I’ll ever eat a roasted turkey breast! I don’t cook mine with the skin on bc I want it cooked and served with the spices and sage, but you can cover in foil so it doesn’t get too dry. Start checking the temperature at 45-60 mins. I think the time shown is for a much less enthusiast oven! Definitely don’t skip the butter or prosciutto.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody:
Made this exactly according to the recipe, except I followed the tip from alot of folks : removed skin before rolling it up , then placed skin as far around the turkey breast as it would go before tying it up. 
I had also brined the turkey breast for a day. 
It was utterly delicious and will definitely do this again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Novice cook here! Are you supposed to lay the rolled breast in the liquid? How deep should the liquid be? I saw another recipe that used a roasting rack over a tray so that the skin would be crispy all around.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I've made this twice now; once exactly as written; most recently w/a turkey half-breast (2 lb). Halved all ingredients; went easy with salt. Left out fennel seed this time. Turned out great both times, moist and flavorful. Started w/half the liquid, ended up needing all of it. Total cook time (on convection roast) was about 50 min.  Pan juices are fabulous and would make a great gravy with a nice browned roux and a bit of stock.  Looking forward to sandwiches!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: This was moist and delicious and a hit. Couldn't bring myself to include all the salt listed for fear I'd be oversalting. It was fine with just 1/2 of what's recommended.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: This was fantastic and it impressed my guests. I didn't plan on making this for Thanksgiving initially, so my turkey breast could have been a better cut. Still! Working with the cut I had, this was one of the best turkey dishes I've had. I tented some foil over the breast while it baked, and it was so juicy. I would definitely make this again. And with the thyme, it had that distinct Thanksgiving seasoning, which was prefect because I forgot to make the stuffing.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: Can this be prepared and refrigerated a day in advance?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I made this last year. it came so good that it's worth doing it again. My guests said it was restaurant quality.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody:
Two tips for this or any turkey breast roulade:
- Butterfly and pound out to equal thinness, and salt generously 1-2 days ahead (dry brine)
- Before filling, remove the skin in one piece, then fill and roll and re-cover with the skin and tie. This way it’s all crispy skin on the outside and no soft flabby skin rolled up inside.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: I didn’t salt the inside.  The prosciutto, salted butter, salting the outside and the pan just is plenty of sodium.  Not to mention all the sides!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Turkey Breast Roulade With Garlic and Rosemary Recipe
commentBody: This looked great but tasted just alright. I've made similar recipes in the past that had brighter and more complex flavors in the stuffing. In this recipe, most of the flavor came from the prosciutto. It wasn't bad overall but I've had better.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I may be wrong, but this dough seems to be a pate a choux - so, if you don't have a food processor, try adding the eggs by hand with a wooden spatula or with an electric hand mixer. If by hand, vigorously mix in the eggs one at a time, waiting until each is fully absorbed into the batter before adding the next. When you scoop up a bit of the dough and let it drop back into the bowl, it should create a "v" shape off the edge of the spatula. Adding cheese after this point should be fine :)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: @Jio P Thanks. Not everyone owns a food processor!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: @Jio P Thank you for your personal note.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Can these be made without piping? Perhaps using a standard sized cookie dough scooper? And, what’s the best way to freeze these? Before or after baking? Thanks!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: The recipe notes you can just use 2 spoons instead--that's how I make gougeres!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
Hi Andrea, I often make gluten-free puffs in my French home and freeze them after baking and cooling.  To bring them back to life, I just place them in a warm oven for about 5 minutes.
On the shaping, I use a very round and well oiled coffee scoop (smaller in size than an ice cream scoop),  and then remove the batter inserting a small spoon along the edge.  My gluten-free batter is stiff enough that I can do that, but I have no idea if the dough in this recipe would handle the same.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I guess you could just spoon them...

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Is there any technique that works if one does not have a large food processor?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: You can add the eggs, one at a time, with a wooden spoon, but  first you need to cool the dough down a little, stirring it vigorously, otherwise the eggs will cook! You might also want to beat the eggs before incorporating them.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I beat them in my mixer but do let the mixture cool a bit before--usually, just 30 seconds to a minute of letting it mix with the paddle attachment is enough. Add eggs 1 at a time...

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: It is a pate a choux dough, and I've never used the food processor to add eggs and cheese.  A whisk and elbow grease work fine for adding the eggs, one at a time, until each is fully incorporated, and the cheese.  Less equipment to clean up, too.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!  And I made these only using a wooden spoon— I forgot my mother’s kitchen lacked some useful appliances.  I also spooned them out, so I probably made them bigger than needed.  So, don’t be intimidated by lack of equipment.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: These are essentially gougères with a critical difference. In the classic French recipe, the flour is added to the melted butter & cooked for at least 2 minutes to cook the flour, then the milk is added slowly. I made this using Ina's method. Next time I will use the classic method as these were on the heavy side; use a much lower oven temp as they browned before cooking properly, use a Kitchen AId as the dough was too heavy - the FP blade repeatedly lifted up & I won't bother with the egg wash.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
Home cooks with a small recipe like this do not have to waste time and dishes use a  piping bag. 
Just use a spoon  or small scoop. They will puff up fine and you won't notice they are not perfectly symmetrical . They might even look homemade which is more appetizing.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Old recipes suggest two teaspoons to form the balls of dough, much as you would for drop cookies. Works fine. You can also make a circle of the balls and bake it as a free-form ring. This is a classic gougeres and quite forgiving. It's good warm or room temperature with a salad for a light meal, once the typical way to serve gougeres in Burgundy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: One can pipe batter from a heavy-duty freezer bag by cutting off a lower corner.  Start with a small cut and fine tune its output, and don't overpush the dough to avoid tearing the cut.  Best wishes,

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: If you like this try searching for pao de queijo recipes ;)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
We, in Equador, make the same using yuca flour and a cheese that is similar to feta, it's a treat. We call it pan de yuca or yuca bread.

Next time I will try using pecorino.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Classic recipe also in Silver Palate cookbook - remember that?  We’ve made this for years as a gougere or puffs.  All variations work- adding bacon, mixing cheeses, etc.  Halving recipe works fine too (half cup milk, half cup flour, half a stick butter and two eggs). Our family “go to” specialty.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: @Berni have the Silver Palate Cookbook!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: For step three, I would use an electric mixer, fitted with a paddle. I do thIs for Dorie Greenspan’s gougeres. Also I rarely pipe the dough so it’s probably worth a try. I will make these, they sound delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: p.s.  my bullet proof recipe which I've made countless times is nearly identical to this one except for I use water instead of milk along with a pinch each of salt, nutmeg and cayenne.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: It is actually a French specialty from Burgandy called "Gougeres", you usually prepare it with water instead of milk

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Help! Novice cook here. My batter was too runny, and would not form into peaks or balls. It seemed right until I added the eggs and cheese in the food processor. Any advice before I try again?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: hughejd:  Welcome, novice cook, to the wonderful world of home cooking!  It is an addictively fun activity that has kept me sane over decades and especially now. First, this dish is a gougere.  Re your question about too runny batter...did you use 4 eggs in the mix as specified?  That may be too many - based on other gougere I've made.  If you try this again, start with 3 eggs pulsed in 1 at a time to see how that is b/4 adding 4th.  Also search this using gougere for other recipes to compare.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Anyone tried to successfully cut this recipe in half using 2 or 3 eggs?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: If you were going gluten free, what flour would you recommend?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Tough one as gluten gives this recipe the stretch. I would opt for rice or cassava based flours. King Arthur is a great source of all things baking and they may already have a recommendation.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I used Cup4Cup which is my go to.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I served these as an appetizer for thanksgiving. I made them a few days ahead and froze them on sheet pans in batches (uncooked) and stored in a bag. On the day of I simply thawed on a sheet pan and popped them in the oven, they came out perfectly and were a big bit

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: In general, you don't need to pipe gougères. They may look slightly less smooth, but that's nice, like a rustic french bread vs. a smooth loaf. You can make them smaller or larger, just test one by opening it to see if it's done -- i.e. not moist, but seems like it would have been moist 2 seconds earlier. ;)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Cook per bon appetit Carla Lalli instructions. Preheat to 450, off for 10 minutes then 350 for ~20 min. Make sure dough is soft enough to make peaks.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Turned out beautifully. I agree that oven temp could be reduced so they don't brown too much before cooking all the way through.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I'm confused about the volume/weight corollary with the cheese. I do not know what "ground" parmesan or pecorino means, but I finely grated the cheese (used a Microplane zester), and quickly had much more volume than suggested before reaching 70 grams. I settled on about 25 grams of each - and had about 1.5 cups of cheese. The results were fantastic.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
Make these now bc everyone will think you’re a superstar! 

Made the recipe AS IS, using a wooden spoon with spectacular results: 
- the dough get stiff but a wooden spoon is strong enough to mix it well. 
-add eggs one at a time. The dough will seem slippery and slimy. KEEP MIXING vigorously, bc it will come together again. Then, add the next egg. 
-they will look disappointing for the first 15 minutes of baking, but in those last 5 minutes, they rise beautifully!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: On the shaping, I use a very round and well oiled coffee scoop (smaller in size than an ice cream scoop),  and then remove the batter inserting a small spoon along the edge//Bake from frozen for about 20 mins in a 375F oven until they are golden brown.//For step three, I would use an electric mixer, fitted with a paddle. I do thIs for Dorie Greenspan’s gougeres. Also I rarely pipe the dough so it’s probably worth a try. // I made this using Ina's method. Next time I will use the classic method a

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
I’ve never used a food processor for gougeres; I’ve always beaten the eggs in one by one. I also freeze them unbaked, so that they are easy and to pop into the oven for a last minute special treat. 
I am quite loyal to Heidi Swanson’s recipe (which suggests and encourages liquid and cheese variations), but will report back after doing a side by side comparison!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: These are fun! I misread the recipe and made mine very small. They were really cute. Very tasty. They freeze and reheat well.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
I followed the recipe and the food processor bit and piping bag were a fail. Food processor left the pâte à choux runny. The resealable plastic bag for piping worked but was so wasteful! I’ll stick to elbow grease and double spoon method! 


The rest is fine.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I don't know what I did wrong, I tried to follow the recipe as is, but mine came out flattened, not as balls at all, any suggestions?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Make an air fryer. Dollops of dough on parchment and air fry at 350° for five minutes then turn them over and fry for another two minutes. No need for an egg wash.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: This is how I learned to make pate a choux in cooking school, from a French pastry chef. The flour gets cooked in the pan after you've added it to the milk, and before adding the eggs. I use a stand mixer with a paddle attachment, and add the eggs one at a time. You could also lightly beat the eggs and add them a little at a time. You want the flour mixture to absorb the eggs and if you add them all at once, all that liquid will slosh around, making it harder for the ball of flour to absorb it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I use a small cookie scoop dipped in cold water, it's time saving and works perfectly.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I have made gougeres many times with great success. This was a disaster. Thin batter, too salty, oven too hot. I used a scale to measure ingredients. Perhaps something failed in translation from the original recipe? Never again. Gougeres are so much easier to make too.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Cooking time is 13

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight using a GF flour - Cup for Cup. I was worried they would be gluey or just weird. Not so! They were amazing! This was a great recipe! Will definitely make again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Didn’t use the food processor and still turned out great!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Great as is. Must be eaten within an hour or two of coming out of oven.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: First time making this - went exactly as recipe written - dough more forgiving than expected. End product: sublime! Def will repeat.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: This recipe has been around much longer than food processors!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: See note about freezing dough balls and cooking frozen.  Scoop with cookie scoop instead of piping.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Bake from frozen 20 mins at 375

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I have gone into my cheese drawer and added leftover cheeses from past parties to this dish with good results.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made these with both the cuisinart/ piping bag and also using electric beater and spooning them out.  They come out beautifully both ways.  I let them cool completely before freezing them in a ziplock.  When I serve, I put them straight from freezer into 375 oven for ten minutes and they are a huge hit! (I’ve also experimented with different cheeses - really versatile!)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I just tried to make this but the "dough" was much too watery to use. What did I do wrong? I carefully measured/weighed all ingredients, followed the recipe exactly. But got thick soup not dough.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: @Richard This has happened to me twice. No idea what caused it, though

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Can I completely make this and just pop them in the oven when my guests arrive?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I cut this recipe in half using 2 eggs in the dough and a third for the wash.  Followed the directions exactly and the puffs came out perfect!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Delicious with or without cheese! Works well with Kitchen Aid - add eggs one at a time until incorporated. Baked in the morning, so I made a few without cheese or egg wash for a quick breakfast - peppery and light. The cheesy crusty ones would go well with a nice stiff drink. I probably still prefer the no-cheese version though.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: This worked perfectly. I was nervous about opening the oven during the baking and having them deflate so I baked one sheet after the other. The advantage was we had a fresh batch coming out of the oven just as we finished devouring the first batch.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
Looks and Tastes like a puff  pancake.     Wish I would have known ... pouring it on cast iron much
Easier...for a tired dad.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: To the novice cook whose dough was too runny, 2 possibilities to check - first it is really important to cook the dough for the 2 min in the saucepan after adding the flour, second the recipe calls for large eggs if you used extra large it would have added more liquid (I learned this the hard way making almond cookies :). I’ve made a lot of gougers and I freeze after baking, wonderful to have on hand.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
Don’t need quite as much egg,  Whisk eggs before mixing in.  Fine to do all by hand.

15 mins, baking enough.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: These are fantastic and so easy! I made these nearly verbatim from the recipe. My only changes were to use all pecorino and I halved the recipe. We ate a few fresh out of the oven (to die for!) and froze the rest (tossed into a Ziploc bag). We’ve resurrected a few in the toaster oven by baking at 350 degrees/10 minutes (our Breville toaster oven’s max baking temp is 350) and they were still excellent - fluffy and not gummy. If fresh from the oven was a “10”, reheated they are a 9.5!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
Ina does it again! 
These are easy and feel very sophisticated. You'll want to make the whole recipe, and no need to dirty a piping bag -- I use two spoons to shape my puffs; it makes them a little bigger than pictured but I like the feel of that. 
These remind me of Brazilian pao de queijo.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Fabulous. I was a little intimidated having never made anything like this before. I still need to work on my piping technique so the balls are more consistent, but overall it was a huge success. Everyone positively raved about them. I reheated them from frozen as described and they were delicious, but I know they were even more amazing when I tasted a couple fresh from the oven the first time. If it’s at all possible to serve them fresh, it’s worth the effort.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: To mix these by hand I used Julia Moskin,s recipe for Cheddar Cheese puffs as an additional guide. I followed the Cacio e Pepe recipe through the middle of step 3, then let the mixture cool for 5 minutes before adding the eggs one by one, stirring vigorously each time: I then mixed in the cheeses. I used two teaspoons to drop the dough onto the baking sheets, before applying the egg wash and extra cheese, and then baking. The finished puffs were as beautiful as they were delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
