commentBody: Two teaspoons worked better than putting in bag. Way faster and less messing.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Everything old is new again. I went back to my 1966 Julia Child recipe: Petits Chou au Fromage. Definitely use 4 eggs per cup of flour.  Her recipes notes:  the puffs will not rise as high because of the large amount of cheese. Love all the input on recipes. They are very helpful!!!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Can you make these with lactose free milk?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Are these solid after baking, or do they have a hollow inside?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: made a similar French  recipe using Gruyere in dough plus sprinkled on top minus the egg wash, sometimes formed as individual little buns or in a bread ring which puffs when done. Freezes well. Made in a bowl, spoon mixed. Baked on parchment paper.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Mine came out so runny and absolutely no puff! I followed the recipe exa!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Gougeres + game night! We made these as the centerpiece of a cheese board with salumi, sardine pate and a bubbly pet nat. Made exactly as directed, next time I might add a bit more pepper in the dough and flaky salt to top. I mixed in my stand mixture and used my piping bag, which is always fun to work with.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Try filling these with a thick béchamel enriched with grated pecorino and plenty of cracked black pepper

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: No need for a food processor; mixing by hand was a breeze.  Very easy recipe for novice bakers like me.  Very pretty results - tastes good, but a bit bland.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: In response to halving the recipe, only 4 eggs go into the choux. The fifth is for the glaze. Easy to misread. I wonder if this is the cause of a comment about the choux being too loose?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: after food processor, dough was still VERY runny. ended up mixing in extra cup of flour by hand to make more "dough-ey"

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I have never made something like this before so I was a little nervous but I followed the recipe as written and they were perfect. My family of 5 demolished them in a few minutes. Definitely recommend as a snack or appetizer.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Easy and quick, but have everything out and ready before you start. Super addictive and delicious. Really much better warm.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: These were inCREDibly delicious. Made exactly as per recipe. 4 eggs in batter - 5th egg for egg wash. Batter was definitely not runny. Don’t forget final sprinkle of cheese on top. They needed the full baking time to puff up with air and properly brown to retain their structure when served. Can’t wait to make again!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Didn't use a food processor, just a wooden spoon and some effort. Over piped so the puffs were big, but very tasty.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made gougeres for years with great success and decided to try this. Unfortunately, it was a disaster and after eating a couple, the batch had to be thrown out. I substituted bread flour (didn’t have regular flour on hand) and used all Parmesan (didn’t have any pecorino or gruyere. I usually do half gruyere and half parmesan. The batter was thin and could not be piped and the puffs did not rise. Some were not even hollow, all were ‘greasy’ and heavy. My technique for mixing is a wooden spoon

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!  Followed recipe exactly and piped out of a freezer ziplock.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I made these and they were delicious. However, the recipe said I was suppose to get 40-50 and only got about 18! I scooped them instead of a pastry bag, so maybe I made them too big…. Once baked they were about the size of a half dollar. Anyway, pleasantly surprised at how light and airy they were on the inside.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Can you say savory æbleskiver?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
i squished a clove of garlic and tiny bit of cayenne into the milk mixture.
Adds another element to this tasty recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe as written with the exception of using a stand mixer instead of a food processor (mine is tiny!). I also used the spoon method for plopping them on the sheet. They’re so delicious and simple for how good they are. I like them with tomato soup and they’d definitely be a potluck hit.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
Just made these for friendsgiving and followed the recipe exactly. My batter came together just fine and I followed the instructions exactly using the four eggs and a food processor. 

I tried to use the large ziplock bag to pipe these, it didn't work well so I just rolled them into little balls instead. The result is magnificent! They look like little buns and taste like cheesy bready fluffs of heaven. This one is a keeper!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: very nice, quite light.  Could use a bit more cheese and a pinch of cayenne.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Is the 70 grams for the cheese before grounding or after?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Food processor is not needed. Just put the dough in a bowl after cooking and added the eggs one at a time with a whisk until incorporated. By the third egg it was too difficult to use the whisk because all the dough got stuck inside. So I used a wooden spoon at that pint. When I make these again I’ll use a hand mixer

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly and they came out perfect.  Brought these to a friend's house and served them at room temperature.  They were a big hit.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
You can probably use a hand mixer instead of food processor if necessary - dough will just be pretty thick and you might need to finish by hand. You don't have to use a pastry bag - you could use spoons or your fingers - just need to make little blobs.

Do not over-mix.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Just made these yesterday and they were pretty quick and easy following the recipe instructions as written. I found it essential to measure the cheese by weight rather than volume! The full 1cup of both cheese combined only came out to 75grams by weight instead 140grams! Piped through a gallon freezer bag and the process was pretty painless, the results quite tasty.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Made it essentially as written, but used all pecorino instead of the parmesan and pecorino combo because it what I had on hand. Absolutely perfect and very flavorful!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I just wanted to mention that I was able to prepare these one hour ahead of time, and they sat at room temperature and baked up perfectly.  I Also did not use a food processor. I just whipped them up with a wooden spoon in a bowl and it was just fine. A little freshly cracked pepper on top was nice! I will definitely make this recipe again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: This worked well for me as written. They puff up very nicely and the combo of parmesan and pecorino was perfect. They also freeze well for months. Just bake, cool and freeze. Then reheat frozen at 350 for 5 minutes or so.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Second time trying this recipe and it turned out much better because I got a better piping bag. Sometimes it's not the baker but the baker's tool that's the problem. The one I used today was the Ateco plastic coated (linen?) bag much sturdier than the limp plastic one I used before.  I tried to follow the size as in the recipe and some of the puffs were a little big.  got only 30.  All said, delicious puffs and loved the two types of cheeses. Perfect!  Definitely a keeper.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed- turned out beautifully but not really round. I used spoons instead of piping. Probably my fault for not shaping more into balls, but it was quite sticky. Still delicious and a really lovely texture and color. I used a food processor which made it really easy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: These were OK. My dough/batter was quite thin so I ended up with something more like flats with smooth tops than puffs with crinkly tops. Taste was good though. I used large eggs and next time I'll try three instead of four. Weighing ingredients was important - 70gr cheese was closer to 1C than 1/2C. Made for a nice cheesy flavor anyway. Might make them again because of the flavor rather than the looks.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Don’t sprinkle salt on top of the puffs. They are salty enough from the cheese.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed and it was a big holiday party hit! I don’t have a standing mixer so just used a wooden spoon throughout and two spoons rather than piping and they turned out wonderful. Only change I may make next time is more pepper.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: We used a small ice cream scoop which worked well. A piping bag is too much work.  If ill-shapen  the wet spoon use was great.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I have been stalking this recipe for weeks and finally took the plunge. They were really tasty! Followed recipe as written only mixed by hand because that's how I learned to make choux pastry in middle school home economics class, and used a cookie scoop. Very easy. Hubs really liked them. I plan to bring these to our Thanksgiving potluck at work.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: To post Thanksgiving-type recipes one day before Thanksgiving is ridiculous and useless. The same goes for the habit of doing the same for Christmas and Easter.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I doubled the size of each puff, adjusting baking time, and use them as little puff sandwich appetizers, with the barest hint of good Dijon and torn pieces of prosciutto set inside

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Followed as directed. Had fun with a ziplock bag until it blew a hole and squirted onto my chest and then fell to the floor, where the dogs enthusiastically cleaned it up. :)  But out of the oven they flattened out immediately and therefore weren't light and fluffy. What could I do to improve results? And additional egg white? Less cheese?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: @Barb About the bag…that has totally happened to me too! As for the flattening, try turning the oven down and let them cook a bit longer. Also allow them to cool in the warm oven. This firms them up. The hardest part is not just gobbling them while they’re piping hot!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed, except used a hand mixer instead of food processor. Came out perfectly: very light puffs.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Terrific little puffs for an appetizer! We loved these gougeres and so did our guests. You don't need a food processor, however. You can easily mix everything by hand. You also don't need to pipe them; you can use a spoon to portion them out on the parchment. They're easy to then shape into domed rounds with your fingers (the dough is not sticky). I make these a day or two ahead, let them cool, freeze them in a ziploc bag, and reheat when the guests arrive so they're toasty warm when served.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: In response to users who find this recipe a bit too wet, my experience is that 4 eggs is too much to get the pate a choux to a consistency that it puffs up nicely.  I looked back a the Julia Child recipe in Mastering the Art of French Cooking and it uses only three eggs for 1 cup of flour along with 6 tablespoons of butter and 1 cup of water rather than milk.  Add the eggs and cheese in the food processor.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Followed the directions to a T, and just didn't find them very flavorful or interesting.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: They are delicious but I'm not sure why they are called Cacio e Pepe cheese puffs as they are just cheese puffs seasoned with salt and pepper.  Might try them next time with way more pepper actually incorporated into dough

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: A cookie scoop is SO  much easier. Occasionally dip in ware between puffs.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
Bake from frozen 14 min at 350.
Eggs one at a time, try mixer instead of food processor. 
Allow to cool just a minute before adding eggs.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Puffs did not puff up as round and nice-looking as the NTY photo.  Perhaps warming up eggs more and preheating sheet pan would have helped.  Some did not think this was cheesy enough.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Does anyone know if this dough be made a few hours ahead and then baked as guests arrive? I want to make this for a dinner party but trying to time things out so they’re hot and fresh…

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Excellent and very versatile. I boiled baby potatoes in the water before adding coconut milk etc. it was absolutely delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Any cheese substitutes recommended or possible?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: People use all sorts of cheeses for cheese puffs. Gruyere is usually used  in the classic gougere. You do want a sharper/firmer/drier cheese, I think--something that grates nicely.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: JD:  You ask about cheese substitutes.  Do you mean a different kind of cheese than the parm and pecorino called for here?  If so, gruyere is the most classic cheese for gougere and that is what this recipe is.  If you search for gougere on this site you'll find other recipes for ideas for cheeses and other elements to add if you want to experiment. I've also had great gougere that were made using blue cheese.  If you mean subbing something that is not cheese, I don't think cheeseless works.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I’d also add a tsp of freshly ground pepper to this recipe, something to give it a bite.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: In addition to the pepper called for in the recipe?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Does anyone have a Gluten Free recipe for cheese puffs?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: you can reliably substitute King Arthur gluten free flour if you can find it. https://shop.kingarthurbaking.com/items/gluten-free-measure-for-measure-flour-3-lb?utm_source=Google&utm_medium=CPC&utm_campaign=Shopping&utm_keyword=Gluten-Free+Measure+for+Measure+Flour+-+3+lb&gclid=Cj0KCQiAqdP9BRDVARIsAGSZ8AlkOXwx8KpzKQjW4_-zNaaW4DgsFygcmUZpTudikZodsB83WA_b7HUaAhcGEALw_wcB

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this recipe with a gluten free flour? Advice on which type to try?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: what adjustments would you make if you were making these at altitude?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I wonder if this can be made with a non-dairy milk?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Flour Bouqet:  You ask if this recipe could be made with non dairy milk.  This is a gougere and while I have not tried non-dairy milk in any gougere recipes - including this one - some gougere are made using water not milk and I have made those with great result.  Search this site  for gougere and you'll find several recipes to compare to this one and I think some of the others may use water.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I made this with almond milk and they turned out fabulously!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I made them with coconut milk and they were delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: What if you split the flour with cornmeal? A variation on the hush puppy?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: A couple of you have mentioned "gluten free" cheese puffs.  I would very much like the recipe.  This recipe sounds delicious and I would like to make for Thanksgiving but my mother is gluten intolerant.  Also can only eat aged cheeses, so parmesan works well.  Can someone recommend either a different gluten free recipe with cheese and chive or modifications to this one?  :)  Thank you

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I believe they are referring to Brazilian Cheese Puffs (Pao de Queijo) which is made with tapioca flour instead. I have not made the recipe on NYT cooking but generally, they are quite good!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried refrigerating the dough so you could just make that ahead?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: For the people who missed the note on a pate a choux (beating in one egg at a time), I added two teaspoons of flour and put my dough in the fridge for a few minutes to make this salvageable. I also tried a batch in a mini muffin pan. Likely better to follow the real directions, but at least you won't end up with cacio e pepe cookies.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: What in the world did I do wrong?? Totally flat runny “dough”

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: These turned out amazing, just as the recipie is, no alterations. You can use two spoons to scoop the dough and make a ball and then wet your fingers to shape them. I have a small food processor and split the recipie in half still made about 20 puffs

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I use cassava flour (gluten free)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I tried to make these and the dough was a runny mess! Any tips on what I did wrong? Maybe not cooking flour mixture long enough? I also used kitchen aid mixer with paddle attachment.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: It's not the kitchen aid ... I use one every time I make gougeres.  My guess is that you didn't cook the flour-milk mixture long enough.  The purpose of beating it in the pan over low heat is to dry it out somewhat.  By the time you are finished, the mixture should be coming together and you should see some dry streaks on the bottom of the pan. Only then do you add it to the mixing bowl and beat in the eggs (which I do one at a time).

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: This was a pretty good recipe.  I won’t win culinary awards for excellence for appearance, but the puffs were soft and very tasty.  The family like them, so I’ll keep it in our recipe box!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
Time consuming w/out the “wow” payoff.
Adding cream to berry mixtures dulls the beautiful fresh bite of tangy berries.  It’s dense heavy texture overall was not appealing.  Next time, I’ll mix all berries together, add a bit of sugar & cornstarch & splash of lemon & pile it into a dbl crusted pie.  
Crust directions way complicated.  Mom’s mazola, flour & ice water is always an easy winner.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Think you meant this for another recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: If you don't have a piping bag you can use a ziploc bag. Cut off one corner and fill with the dough then squeeze out from the cut corner. Adjust the cut as needed depending on the size you want for the final "blob" of dough.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Add more pepper. Make them taller. Tasty but not worth it

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Love the freeze ahead suggestion!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Egg wash is a must. Use Maldon as finishing salt.  425 is way too hot. 400 on convection setting kept a slightly gooey center.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Subbed self-rising flour for AP and gruyere for romano (preference against romano). These substitutions were improvements in a big way, the crumb came out so airy!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Surprisingly bland.  Prefer to up both the salt (I know... Crazy...right?!) and pepper.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Penzeys pepper, Costco parmesan. 30 piped puffs on my oversized baking sheet. Delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Would there be a way to make this gluten free using rice or almond flour? Don't really have the money to buy a different type of flour right now.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I used almond flour, a couple of tablespoons tapioca starch and 1/8 teaspoon Xantam gum, it worked very well.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Very similar to Brazilian Pão de queijo

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly and my dough was runny, got flat results. What could I have done wrong??

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I often see recipes like this one that instruct to beat with a wooden spoon    Why a wooden spoon and will I get the same results if I don’t use a wooden spoon?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Don’t need quite as much egg.  15 mins of baking enough.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
Does anybody have any idea what KASinYVR meant by "Scoop the drought"?

Mystified here.....!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: KASinYVR meant scoop the "dough". Spell-check thought "drought" was a better option, but then, spell-check can't cook nearly as well as it can spell.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Thanks, Dan!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Is there any reason I couldn’t substitute cheddar for the cheeses? (It’s the only lactose-free cheese I can find, and we have some lactose intolerant family!)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Question: if I bake them first and then freeze, do I defrost them first before warming in the oven, or pop them in the oven frozen?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Made 1/2 batch, through step 3, and froze. Made 20 puffs, formed with 2 spoons. Will report on finished product after baking.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: My first attempt was very successful.  I have a baked batch in the freezer, ready for my next dinner party.  Question is, when reheating, do you put them in the oven frozen or defrost first?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Put them frozen in the oven. No need to thaw.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: These were too salty.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I grated the cheese into a bowl and measured 70 grams for each after grating. They came out perfect.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I used 3 eggs instead of 4 and they still came out flat?!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I have made and loved these on many occasions - today I was deciding between these and far more time-intensive yam croquettes; went with a combo (not better than the original but yummy in their own right): replace pepper w/harissa, replace cheese w/1/4 cup Gruyère + 3/4 cup of well-cooked + pureed (skins on is fine) sweet potatoes (I roasted in lemon-infused olive oil so a squeeze of lemon juice may be well-placed as a substitute). Highly recommend.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Will this work with 1 or 2% fat milk?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Not super flavorful - next time I’ll increase the pepper and get older cheeses.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Personally, I would add a little more cracked black pepper, but they were delicious with our steak and salad!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: This was an easy recipe to put together.  BUT using a piping bag is really hard to do. I used a 5/8 tip and I had a plastic one and boy was it a mess; all over my hands, the counter, the floor.  It was a culinary crime season.  I think I need a different type of piping bag or just more practice.  In any case, about 24 puffs came out looking ok, some bigger than others, brown on top and really delicious.  Soo, I'll try again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: My dough was more of a batter....I weighed everything...not sure where I went sideways.  Any suggestions are welcome!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Tasted wonderful but…the dough was so runny it was more cheese crackers than cheese puffs. Followed recipe exactly. Any ideas why this happened?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone used gluten free flour in a recipe like this? How does the technique differ?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Can I make these ahead of time to take to a party several hours later, or are these intended to me baked and served immediately?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Yes. Look at the end of step 7

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
These are a favorite, often requested by my family. 
QUESTION:   Can these be formed, then frozen and later baked?  When I reheated the frozen, baked puffs, they deflated.  They tasted good, but didn't look as pretty.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: how much salt & pepper

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Flipping divine! They were a big hit! I followed the recipe exactly. Next time, I might pipe the dough.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Perhaps I did something wrong but the puffs did not puffed! This is a gougère, something I have done successfully many times before. So not sure why mine did not rise. I reviewed the recipe and I had followed it properly. The dough was very soft. I even weighed the cheeses to be sure I had the correct amount to my dismay, when I weighed my 1/2 cup of the cheeses it did not weigh near the weight equivalent on the recipe so I decided to weigh the cheeses anyway and added more to the correct weight

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: For those who have made these gluten free, do you sub out tapioca like a brazilian cheese bread or use a gf all-purpose flour?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Doubling the recipe did not work for me.  I cooked the base much longer than the recipe directed, but it was too runny and wet.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I made these and they were delicious, but I used the proportions for Melissa Clark’s Gougeres; used water instead of milk. So less butter, no milk and still an indulgent treat.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: These were a huge success!  I prepared them in advance as suggested, and got them out of the freezer as guests were arriving.  Within minutes I was serving these delicious hot puffs to everyone, and receiving lots of compliments.  I thought they were the perfect finger food--not greasy or messy like some finger food can be.  Later in the month, I made a second batch, and only used half of them for a smaller holiday grouping, so now I have some tucked away in the freezer to complement a soup...

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: This was so good.. makes so many good for dinner party / picnic!!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: is it pecarino ramano cheese?  and is there a good sub for that?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
Has anyone doubled the recipe? Does it work ? 
Thank you

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I froze the dough in 1 1/2 in silicone dome molds, then baked for 25 minutes. I heated the oven to 375, then turned down to 350, when I put them in the oven. This creates perfectly round puffs

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: These are so delicious, easy to make and perfect for sharing

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I finally got around to making these today and followed the recipe right up to the instruction to make a piping bag. Instead, I used a small cookie scoop. I should also note that I cut the salt by half, since I used Morton’s kosher salt and lightly salted them after applying the egg wash and pecorino. I was flabbergasted by how light the little gems were. I had to hurry and freeze them before I ate them all!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
First effort was a disaster - choux was too wet, resolved that by adding a drier choux, and then they all burned (convection oven at 425 for 12 minutes. Next batch was a success, but I went back to a water-based choux and added eggs 1 at a time after removing it from the heat. Then baked at 375 for 20 minutes, turning sheets at 10 minutes.
I don’t have an oven thermometer, so maybe mine is hot - still, proceed w caution and keeping a close eye on the bake.
Finished puffs are delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I have a question about the cheese measurement. Half a cup of shredded cheese is nowhere near 70 grams. Pre-shredded weight please?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Pre-shredded weight is the same as shredded weight.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I cut everything in half and did everything by the instructions and the dough was way too runny to pipe.  Something is off here.  I used a scale and measured everything in grams except the 2 eggs.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: They have been posting and adding holiday recipes for weeks now.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Super fast to make and simple.  Such a tasty, and fun to eat warm up.  Make them a little on the big side and they come out better in my opinion.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I made these and followed the recipe exactly. They tasted delicious and looked great right out of the oven, but flattened out almost immediately. Where did I go wrong?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: The teaspoon of pepper in this recipe isn't enough for the "pepe" title. I'll probably triple it next time.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Just pate a choux dough.  Don't need a cuisinart.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
I was nervous about trying this new (new to me) recipe, but I read all of the helpful comments and took them to heart and I had a huge SUCCESS!
I am thoroughly enjoying all the recipes that I now have access to.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: SO easy & delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: The batter came out very watery for me, after reading other comments I believe it's because 4 eggs is too many. They taste good, but didn't really puff up at all. More like... Cheese cookies?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Can I use a gluten-free flour successfully with this recipe?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Cool the dough before dropping them on the pan. It’s less sticky

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Once again, NYT Cooking fails to indicate in the ingredients' list that something needs to be held back for another step... in this case, the eggs.  I even compared this recipe with Ina's origina before making.  We all know Ina uses extra large eggs, so her 4 to this recipe's 5 large ones made perfect sense to me, until the dough came out looser than I'd expected.  $30's worth of really good cheese later, I have Cacio e Pepe Cheese wafers.  What a waste of time and effort.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: See my note above and add a little more flour and chill, makes for firmer dough piping

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Isn't 1/2 cup of butter 1/2 a stick?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: 1/2 cup butter is one stick.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Bake them off? Off of what?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Delicious but this is $50 dollars worth the delicious cheese!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Hmmm...... I'd like to know where you buy your cheese.  140 grams of cheese isn't even a third of a pound.  I usually pay less than $25/pound for parmesan, and less than $20 for pecorino (when it's not on sale).  Which, by my math, is less than $8 for the cheese.  Am I missing something?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I made these as directed.  While tasty, I found them to be too salty.  Next time I will use Diamond Crystal, instead of the Morton's I used this time.  I also would not use the final sprinkle of salt.  Pecorino provides plenty of salt.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: My first attempt was more like cake batter and not really puffs, too flat. Added a little flour and chilled in the fridge for a bit and that worked well.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone made a version using oil instead of butter? If so, please advise.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
Horrible recipe.  Combining measured, by weight, ingredients give a liquid obviously too thin.  No comments cover this, except use fewer eggs. This advice omits the "Throw it out and start over." step.  
To achieve the "V" shape when testing the mix, what steps might be taken to get to that point?
I am clearly not cut out to be a cook if this recipe works, which the comments indicate it does.
I'd rather blame the recipe!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe carefully, and it was a resounding flop.  The dough was very soft and runny and would not make a "hershey kiss" like mound at all.  It drooped into flat discs, like mini flat breads.  And this was after adding additional flour in a desperate attempt to thicken it.  Where did I go wrong?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: What does “bake them off” mean?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: These were a huge hit at thanksgiving although i employed no make ahead techniques. What i did do was omit the food processor because i was in someone else’s kitchen (trust me it was necessary). I mixed in the eggs and cheese by hand, then dropped them by spoonful onto a cookie sheet. They bake up beautifully and were promptly demolished. Next time i would change up the cheese with a Swiss and other cheese combos. Also these would beautifully accept cheddar and scallions. Play!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: These need a LOT of pepper to approximate cacio e pepe; ended up at a tablespoon of corns.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: We made these for thanksgiving 2025. Easy lovely & delicious

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I made these in the stand mixer like I would do any gougères. Came out great

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I made these for a Thanksgiving day appetizer. I froze them a few days ahead and reheated them. They were excellent, and super fun to make!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I made this in early afternoon for a party. Left dough at room temp for a few hours and then transported to site and assembled there using two spoons. It was easy and a big hit!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I have to agree that there is something wrong with this recipe.  I followed it precisely after having read many of the comments.  Grated my own cheese (I thought maybe that the problem some people where having was due to buying grated cheese). Cooled the batter which made it thick enough to form the nice little mounds. As soon as they hit the oven they melted. I have cheese flats not cheese puffs.  Tasted good.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I read the comments and then followed this recipe to a T.  I have cheese flats.  Grated my own cheese and cooled the dough prior to using. Mounds formed fine but melted in the oven to flats that are only slightly puffy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
Isn’t this like the Brazilian thing pão de queijo? 
Haha
But I tested this recipe and it is way better than the original pão de queijo

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: The pate a choux came together perfectly, cooked it for two minutes more,  added eggs one at a time beating in well. I ended up with batter not dough. Will try again. Was it the gluten-free flour?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: Sift the flour. For puffier cheese puffs. Use less cheese.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody:
In the past I have made paté a choux for eclairs without a problem. Twice with this recipe I end up with batter not dough. I transferred the hot dough into a bowl before adding the eggs and vigorously beat in each egg as required. I am quite puzzled as it seems that the liquid of four eggs is just too much.
Any tips?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cacio e Pepe Cheese Puffs Recipe
commentBody: I tried this, following the directions precisely, and the dough came out quite thin and did not puff up at all in the oven. I wound up with little cacio e pepe pancakes. They were edible but not quite what I expected. What could’ve gone wrong?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I'd be inclined to try this with half as much butter.  If you serve 4 people this recipe, each person is eating half a stick of butter, and this is just a side dish!  Yukon Golds are already pretty buttery.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: A baked potato, quartered and sprinkled with salt, pepper, and brushed with butter and lemon juice would be just as good, IMHO.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is in Ina's new cookbook, Modern Comfort Food. Made it this week exactly as instructed and it was creamy and delicious. I wouldn't recommend cutting down the butter.  Ina indicates that butter has a tendency to get bitter so if you want to make in advance, I would recommend prepping it completely without the lemon.  When you reheat it, cook it over low heat, add more milk if needed and then whisk in the lemon.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Agreed on not cutting down on the butter. This is not mashed potatoes. It's a different dish. The butter is most of the point.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Greeks have been eating lemon potatoes for centuries.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: lol, I am french originally and my mum taught me to steam potatoes, use a little of the steam water and then plenty, plenty of butter.  The potatoes when steamed really retain more flavor...  ;)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Try this: cook sliced potatoes in salted water, don‘t drain completely after cooking - this water serves as the „basic“ fluid for the purée. Then add condensed milk (10% fat, the „normal“, NOT the sweetened one), about 40-80 grams to taste, and add a few teaspoons of butter. Whisk it with an electrical hand mixer right in the cooking pot - that way and by using some of the potato flavored cooking water you keep as much potato-taste as possible.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I think you mean EVAPORATED milk - at least in US, all condensed milk is sweetened.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I've never put milk in mashed potatoes. I drain all but "enough" water and mash with a stick of butter.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Same here. It’s a shame to waste that cooking liquid and I always forget to save it as stock. Remove potatoes from cooking water, mash, and add butter and cooking water until you have the consistency you want. It won’t need additional salt. 
Btw - I like the idea of the lemon zest but I’d never use two sticks of butter!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This isn't (American) mashed potatoes. This is pureed potatoes aka pommes puree, a French dish. Different, and absolutely heavenly.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I grew up with something similar to this (it was my dad's favorite) with the following exceptions: Butter was replaced with olive oil and a good bit of lemon juice was whipped in the puree. I can not be certain of milk in the puree but just that, not certain.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I had to change this slightly because we have dairy free folks.  So, I used olive oil, and goat cheese (1/2 a log), and roasted a head of garlic and added that.  These were absolutely delicious because of the lemon.  Brilliant.  Some day I will be able to try it out with full dairy.  Love this.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I wish to clarify your comment about needing to be "Dairy Free".  Goat Milk & it's products belong in the "Dairy" family.  Goat milk is made of  much smaller molecules than Cow milk, thus it is tolerated much better.  My homemade soft goat cheese was much smoother/creamer than store brands of cream cheese because of this factor.   Find yourself some real & farm fresh goat milk, esp. that of the Nigerian Dwarf Breed, sip the milk and make your own homemade cheese.  Oh so good!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I was tempted by the lemon idea, but that much butter and salt is a restaurant trick, neither creative or healthy in a home kitchen!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
That is a crazy amount of butter.
For holidays? Go for it!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is one of the most unnecessarily complicated written recipes for one of the easiest food items. Easy: peel and boil potatoes, drain water, mash potatoes with a hand masher in the pot, add milk or cream enough to make it as liquid as you like, add a good amount of butter and/or olive oil, salt, pepper, lemon zest, mash again, done. Takes 2 minutes after boiling, it’s fast food.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have used a similar recipe in the past but it also includes a nice amount of garlic together with the lemon. Delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made it to take as one of the potato disched at our family Thanksgiving.  It was a big hit and there were (sadly) no leftovers

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: OK - these are silly instructions.  Peel (or not - I never do) the potatoes.  Just melt the butter in with the milk.  Then process the whole thing in a food processor with the lemon zest, salt & pepper.  Creamy, tasty, lemony, done.  Nuff said.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used half the butter but it was still amazing. Whip the potatoes with a handheld mixer with whisk attachments and the texture is light and airy and wonderful. The lemon zest makes this *chef’s kiss* spectacular.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Ina, you did it again.  I love these. I did not have a rice mill, so I asked my son to mash the boiled potatoes with a potato masher. Followed the recipe but added chopped chives for color.  Everyone loved it (including my two kids who usually do not like potatoes)!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: so... regular old mashed potatoes with 2 tablespoons of lemon zest?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delish with fish and NYT Cooking recipe for Alison Roman's Leafy Herb Salad.  Perfect meal.  Whisking wasn't a practical option, as the potatoes with cold butter were too thick without liquid first. I should watch a video on how Ina does it. However, I toned my arm muscles making sure those pots got whipped!  Turned out great.  Loved the flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I have absolutely nothing against butter, and use it as one of many different variety of fats in my cooking, but honestly 2 sticks of butter with anything would make that thing delicious.

Re the gummy potatoes, you let them cook too long. They should be piping hot when you mash/rice them.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: For presentation, Add chopped chives on top with a pat of butter.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I reduced the butter to 1 1/2 sticks and used Lactaid instead of regular milk. I only needed about 1/2 cup to get the consistency my guests wanted. The lemon was an amazing and delightful addition! Who knew? Not I.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I halved the recipe and found it delicious. Really creamy and worked really well with a chicken dish with pan sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I'm tempted to try this with sweet potato!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Would a hand blender work to puree the potatoes?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Wonderful recipe but adapted. Used yukon golds& left skin on. Used less salt, extra virgin olive oil. Will make again

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is a very nice and easy recipe. I used a hand masher and the texture was still fine. I second the recommendation to not even bother with milk and just use the reserved water that the potatoes were cooked in. Had a great consistency and cuts down on additional calories.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Used more lemon, multi color Yukon golds with skins and mixer. Delicious

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: These potatoes are to die for!  Don’t skimp on the butter, heat the milk in a liquid measuring cup in the microwave to avoid washing another pan, dry the riced pasta by stirring it over low heat before beginning to add the cold butter, use a wooden spoon to beat the potatoes as you add the butter and then, a little at a time, the milk—-honestly, this dish is all I needed for our Thanksgiving dinner.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: could you use oat milk if you're lactose intolerant

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Yes. Or lactose free milk.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: How in advance can you make these?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Has anyone tried this recipe with non cow milk?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I'm from Wisconsin.  We don't have any non cows.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Yes, use much less butter and why add 2 tablespoons of salt when cooking the potatoes?  Too much!  Otherwise, excellent!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The reason for this much salt in the cooking water is the same reason you do it for pasta. I didn’t get it either until I tried it, and It makes a HUGE difference (it doesn’t make it super salty). I’m sure there is some scientific reason why it works but I’m not sure what that is. So, Ina is saying that the water is should be like sea water, and it flavors the potatoes from the beginning. You just need to watch the salt you add later

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Doubled it. Worked perfectly.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Very good. Potatoes got very gluey. May have been because they were cooked ahead ahead and kept war. Did not use all the butter.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I found this to be way too salty, almost inedible. I should have tested it before I added the two teaspoons salt. I also found the consistency to be a little gluey.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was delicious and was even toddler approved! Beware the milk and do add it in smaller increments per the instructions to get the consistency you want. I dumped in 1/2 cup and it was even a bit more than I probably preferred. Would definitely keep the butter quantity next time but go slower with the milk.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Divided into 4 servings, approximately 600 calories loaded with saturated fat.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Sounds like a special occasion dish

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Lemon in regular mashed potatoes is yummy, also. I do this to accent any Greek grilled foods. Really lemon anypotatoes….

But here, how does one avoid the whole over-mixed potato paste that happens? Does it not end up in this same texture?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: 2 sticks of butter - I'll pass.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Yellow potatoes are common in Peru, often flavored with lemon. Peruvian Causa a la Limeña is a gorgeous creation of tuna salad iced with lemon mashed yellow potatoes. One memorable Causa we had in Lima in 1989, during the Sendero Luminoso troubles when American diners were offered astonishing creations in every restaurant that remained open. Tuna salad iced with lemon potatoes, black olive slice for an eye, red and green pepper slices outlining gills, and scallion frills the tail. Heavenly!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Yum!  Am not a huge mashed potatoes fan, but these were the bomb

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I followed the recipe but it came out too salty

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I pretty sure I won't be finding rainbow carrots at the Piggly Wiggly down the street.  Will regular carrots do?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: They are at my local Safeway in the organic section

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Yes and Happy Thanksgiving!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Try them. Just make sure they are not old and dried out and that the quartered pieces are of similar thickness.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody:
Can these be made in advance and reheated gently on serving day?
Best way to reheat?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I’ll be making this with a mix of carrots and parsnips for Thanksgiving.  Just what I was looking for.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: This is the second time we’ve made this recipe. It did take about 30 minutes instead of 10 minutes in the broiler. We also added about 2 tablespoons of dark brown sugar with the orange juice, zest and balsamic vinegar. This is now our go to carrot recipe!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Does anyone know if these can be made ahead???

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I cooked this for thanksgiving. The flavors were wonderful. Next time, I'll heat up the sheet pan before adding the carrots to help promote charring. I had to leave the carrots under the broiler for quite a while before I got a char and the result was very soft carrots.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Uh... what’s a Piggly Wiggly? Obviously regular orange carrots will do if you cannot find readily available rainbow carrots. I love how that tripped more people up than aged, syrupy balsamic.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: @easy & delicious but... Piggly Wiggly is a grocery store in the south. Often times in smaller towns. It is a Southern staple.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Trader Joes rarely co-occur with Piggly Wigglys. Moreover, if you live near a Piggly Wiggly (as I do), it may be a very long drive to a grocery that carries rainbow carrots.  But lovely locally-grown, all orange carrots are readily available, so that’s what I’ll be using in this recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody:
My Piggly Wiggly in the middle of nowhere DOES carry Rainbow carrots. Ha!
The purple ones are sweetest.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I used lemon juice and zest instead. It was still a delicious item on our table!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Was good, everyone liked. I found TJ’s rainbow carrots without much taste. Used instead regular organic orange ones, they were sweeter, and parsnips. Will make again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: @Jane I find that regular carrots are tastier than rainbow. But as said earlier, fresh is important, not dried out.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I made the the other day and it was delicious.  I am on a restricted diet and can't have grain, dairy or sugar.  This is a perfect dish for me.  Thanks!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Just a bit to orangey, but otherwise delicious! We’ll make them with balsamic only next time, or heed the recommendations of other commenters to use a different type of citrus.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Will definitely make this and adding parsnips is genius.  I may just turn oven up high and roast, allowing flavors to really mellow.  If high enough, with their sugar content, carrots will char.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Cold climate carrots will be sweetest due to the plant producing more sugars to protect itself from low temperatures than carrots grown in a warm climate.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: @Steve Hmm, that’s an interesting factoid

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Do not line the baking sheet with Parchment! Or if you do, make these at a fire station

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Made this with parsnips, carrots, and beets. All were soooooo good! And very quick to make compared to everything else I was cooking. Using a variety of veggies addes a nice variety of colors since the store only had orange carrots. If you make with beets beware, they are more juicy and will color your other veggies if mixed.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Outstanding! I didn't have access to fresh thyme soI used a Penzey's herb blend with shallots, peppercorns and dill but otherwise made as described. If anything these were even better the next day cold!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Made this for Easter dinner for 18 people today. Used 2.5 lbs regular carrots, and 1 lb parsnips. Cooked on two sheet trays on convection roast 425 degrees, and when I switched the trays (on top and bottom shelves) at 10 minutes, the top sheet was already getting charred. After another five minutes they were both charred and delicious! Next time I will add more parsnips.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: So many cooks liked the parsnip option, so I may try that combo sometime, but I believe the rainbow carrots alone are perfect in flavor and appearance. Purple, orange, yellow and white each has its subtly different flavor, and it's hard to believe their glamorous colors were born underground. On a serving platter or individual plate, they make a sophisticated presentation with a minimum of ingredients, time, and effort.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I'm making this with baby cut carrots because it is hard to find multicolor carrots where I live (a rural area).  Instead of drizzling oil on my carrots, I will spray them with my Misto, much easier and less fat. Otherwise, I will follow the recipe as written.  Sounds perfect for Thanksgiving for one, along with roasted potatoes and perhaps tofu with a sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Should I par cook the carrots before roasting them?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Please don’t.  They will char and brown much better if you don’t.  Additionally, a bit of firmness (but not rawness) in the center of the carrot is preferable.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I guess the correct answer is - it depends on the width of the carrots and how long you will be roasting them.  You asked about roasting not broiling: I always parboil the carrots that I include with my roast chicken because I like using whole small baby carrots and larger chunks of bigger carrots. However if you slice the carrots as Ina describes, then I think Lura Palmer's answer for broiled carrots makes sense.  Just don't look at the picture above!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Made these with dried thyme and regular organic locally-grown carrots. Turned out lovely, but increased the time under the broiler with 5 minutes. Lovely, and will make it again (especially as my two toddlers ate them happily).

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Skipped the zest and my oven was monopolized so I did em on a pan on the grill on high, more like 20 mins. Delicious

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Lovely recipe! To make it extra special, I like serving it with some creme fraiche. The sweet and tangy go perfectly together.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed and it was really good, albeit a tad orangey. Next time maybe we’ll try lemon, or go a little softer on the orange.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. Used regular carrots and a balsamic glaze rather than vinegar. Left them in the oven while I finished the rest of dinner up to help the soften up a bit more.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Trader Joe’s has them.  Most supermarkets do, too.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Rainbow carrots are very pretty, usually cost more than orange carrots, and taste exactly the same.  Use what ya got.  I find organically grown carrots always taste the best.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Interesting debate about orange vs rainbow carrots.... I am sorry for those who lack a convenient Trader Joe's, as it is truly a great place to shop.  Piggly Wiggly is a supermarket chain in the Southern US, for those who are puzzled by it.  I'm making this on Thanksgiving eve with rainbow carrots and sliced brussels sprouts, and I may throw in some cubed sweet potato..... veggie side and obligatory T-giving yams in one dish.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: These took about 30 minutes (not 10) under the broiler set to high. I also made a balsamic reduction the day before. This recipe really took the humble carrot to new heights.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Amazing and simple. My new go-to carrot recipe. I always wondered how restaurants achieve this kind of result with the humble carrot.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: These were amazing and a bit hit at the Thanksgiving table. I changed nothing on the recipe, except that I kept part of the greens and they looked prettier. Tasted delicious.I also did not need to cut the carrots as I was able to get thin organic carrots!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: This was the star of our small Thanksgiving dinner, even with dried thyme and supermarket balsamic.  Note that charring carrots under the broiler sent the smoke alarms into overdrive all over the house.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Why would you discard the aromatics? I added shallots to the roast and happily ate it all. Used dried thyme instead of fresh and a combo of lemons/limes - was incredibly delicious. Added coriander powder to the creme fraiche.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I didn’t have orange so I substituted grapefruit. It worked out well. It’s a delicious recipe

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Really tasty!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: My new go to recipe for carrots.  So simple and quick.  Cooked them to go with a Cajun Meatloaf while that cooled.  Ten minutes or less...yum!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Regular carrots work! I love making this on the grill

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!   I used maple flavored balsamic and gave also made with an 18 year aged balsamic.  If you can get the maple it’s the bomb!  Definitely making again. I used a blood orange olive oil so skipped the fresh orange squeeze. Easy side for entertaining

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody:
Still looking for a response as to whether anyone has made these ahead of time and gently reheated…or how reheated

Thanks!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I was wondering this as well, but as I read the last step of the recipe, it mentions you can serve it warm or room temp. So maybe char carrots, make the vinagrette/sauce, bring separately, then toss everything together right before dinner? I think that's what i'll do since I have to bring a side dish and won't be able to use their ovens.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Mine charred in five minutes.  I prepared I’m advance and reheated for the meal.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: How did you reheat and for how long?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: It did take about 30 minutes instead of 10 minutes in the broiler. We also added about 2 tablespoons of dark brown sugar with the orange juice, zest and balsamic vinegar. This is now our go to carrot recipe!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody:
Reading this recipe today gave me an idea for roasting some acorn squash...
the orange and balsamic wil make a nice touch.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Finally got around to making these on Christmas Eve for my ham buffet.  Finished them with a sprinkle of Za'atar seasoning (the one I use has a lot of thyme). They were absolutely the best.  I tripped the recipe and they were gone in a flash.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Love this recipe - have made it many times. Don’t need orange/orange juice either. Love it with Meyer lemon to bring tang without all of the sweetness.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Tasty but too moist to char. Will try reducing oil substantially next time.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: This works well with any root vegetable, parsley root, parsnips carrots, even red beets. Will make it again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: No grating required but I'm grateful for a carrot recipe where timing comes out just as specified. I followed directions for slicing and timing and was rewarded with perfectly cooked carrots in 10 minutes, making this an ideal way to show off a platter of gorgeous and toothsome root vegetables.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I love love love this. Grab some carrots from the farmers market and roast those babies up. I paired it the the cod with miso butter panko crumbs from on here. Beautiful weeknight dinner

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Should the orange juice and vinegar be whisked together before drizzling over the carrots? or separately?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: The carrots are going to be tossed to coat after the zest, juice, and vinegar are added, so that’s when the mixing will happen. Just drizzle evenly, no need to whisk liquids beforehand,

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I love the recipe section of the NYT but wonder why every recipe requiring salt seems to specify kosher salt.  Is there much of a difference between that and ordinary salt please?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Ordinary salt is about twice as "salty," so it really does make a difference.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Regular carrots will be fine, but check for the rainbow carrots - many places have them (perhaps even a Piggly Wiggly).

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: none of my curbside grocery online groceries have rainbow carrots or long slender carrots.  but i tried with quartered carrots (i think baby carrots would work.)  luckily, the frost has not killed the thyme back yet, so that is available.  i think these changes worked fine and allow for a piggly wiggly compatible dish.  (the balsamic is the ordinary kind.)  tasty.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: But do try carrots that come with the tops on, trimming off the tops, leaving a little nub of green. For some reason, carrots with the tops on are less likely to have woody centers

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: When to add the balsamic?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Where it says “orange juice and vinegar” in step 4

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I’m still hoping for a response to the question about whether par boiling the carrots first would be beneficial.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: why would you want to take that extra step? speed?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: No, I don't recommend, it will change the texture of the carrots.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I have an abundance of carrots due to a grocery mix-up. Can these be frozen?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Can these carrots be frozen once broiled but before drizzled with juice/vinegar?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Frozen cooked carrots usually thaw to a mushy texture. Not sure you'd want that for this recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Made this nite. Delish. Paired it with baby frozen peas. Took it all up a notch.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I agree with Lucy. A mixture of carrots and parsnips is divine. Colors will be comparable to rainbow carrots. Plus, parsnips become so much sweeter and deeply flavorful when roasted and charred a bit.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I’d give it an A. Like the method of the broiler. Didn’t have an orange available but I as really good despite.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Could pomegranate syrup be used instead of "syrupy" balsamic? Or is it best to use balsamic that is not syrupy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: If you use pomegranate syrup skip the orange juice

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I don't have easy access to fresh thyme but do have fresh sage, tarragon, rosemary, and dill. Would be interested to see what others think might work best

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: add parsnips?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Could these be made a day in advance?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: So, do you peel the carrots first?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I did not; I scrubbed each carrot with a vegetable brush, which effectively removed dirt and the tough peel.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Not a fan. The cooking time was too long and the carrots were too soft. Also, they smoked up the kitchen.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I just cooked this along with steak and my chard dish and it was fantastic!  So incredibly sweet, that took me by surprise and balanced well with the other food.  I cooked it ahead of the other two dishes and it was totally fine serving it up room temperature.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody:
Going to try them this week......What about "charring" them on the BQ?
After blanching them?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Don’t peel and be sure to watch them! They can burn easily. I served at thanksgiving and made them ahead, simply microwaved them on medium-low heat to take the chill off. Very popular and so easy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Mine took much longer to get charred than 10 minutes...but I used regular carrots.  I don't know, maybe they are thicker?  Either way, they taste great!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I too found that the carrots took much longer to cook. Closer to 35 minutes and still no charring. They were very tasty and festive. I was surprised and pleased that they did not need to be peeled, just scrubbed. I would probably use the same ingredients and roast them instead.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Yum! Carrots are a big staple veg in my kitchen, so finding a new treatment that is this good is great.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Use with parsnips too

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Worked out deliciously, using food brush for olive oil & fresh thyme.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, and beautiful, if a bit too salty- I will add half the kosher salt initially, and then to taste when serving.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: These carrots were a hit at Thanksgiving! I accidentally doubled the orange zest. It made that flavor more powerful, but not overwhelmingly so.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Super easy, pretty and delicious. Trader Joe's sells packages of carrots perfect for this recipe. I just slice them long-way down the middle if they are a bit too thick.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: This is one of my yearly thanksgiving staples now. Takes longer than the recipe says. Really easy to do right before serving dinner. I would not make ahead :) have made with both rainbow carrots and regular and good both ways but better with rainbow.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I make every year since 2020. A perfect dish and adds beautiful color.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I didn't have fresh thyme so I used 1/2 tsp of dried. It worked great.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Air Fryer 15 min

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I often microwave my carrots for a few minutes, then roast or broil to char. I also will often sprinkle with a little smoked paprika.  I always finish with chopped parsley.....so yummy! Great side with poultry or pork.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: These are delicious!!!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I very much appreciated the burst of fresh, citrusy flavor in the heart of a Midwestern winter! A simple but effective recipe, and a good variation on oven-cooked carrots.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Delicious along with Veg Forward roasted asparagus with white beans and pesto, poached chicken breast with aioli

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly as is with regular full sized carrots. Delicious for Easter dinner, will make again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: a really easy way to do something other than boil carrots!!!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
