commentBody: Great recipe but the key is finding the freshest carrots, preferably from your local farm. The flavor of fresh far outweighs rainbow colors if they've been stored for a while.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: So good!!!! Kids loved it. Probably new favorite roast carrot recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I don’t care how much of this recipe I make for my wife and myself, there are never any leftovers! This is absolutely delicious. I make it exactly as written, although I do squeeze the entire orange onto the carrots. Absolute keeper.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: I believe I have handed this recipe out to at least 20 people, and they have passed it on to their friends and family. Just fantastic, people who say they don’t like carrots love this recipe.:)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Yummy and so easy! The carrots go from al dente to very done rather quickly. So pay attention. And they continue to cook a little more, even when taken off the sheet pan. Skinny carrot ends may have to be removed earlier.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Charred Carrots With Orange and Balsamic Recipe
commentBody: Last year or the year before, this was insanely good, maybe the best thing on the table. This year, it was really good, but it was outshined by other sides. Maybe there weren't enough carrots and too much oil. I followed the recipe to the letter. Compared to other sides, this was pretty fast & easy.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: These are great and well worth the effort... but I had quite a lot of oozing of filling while I was shaping them and challenges getting edges to stick. Some tips: After you put circles in the fridge, give them a quick extra roll because they shrink when cold and bigger = more filling. Less filling rather than more; 1/4 cup was too much for me. Use index finger for very light egg wash around edges. Keep a good amount of space for fork crimp. And don’t worry about oozing... all ends well!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made these with freshly picked blackberries. Pastry is fun to make, super flaky & tasty! I doubled the recipe & froze half of the pies (before the egg wash/baking). For the 2nd batch, I cooked the filling on the stove until it started to thicken, & added a bit of cinnamon. More filling/pie! Cinnamon added nice warmth. Also sprinkled coarse sugar & cinnamon on top before baking. I had enough pastry left over to make 2 8” pie shells, & also extra filling (to add when eating!).

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Wow, these turned out better than I could even imagine! I can confirm that these totally work with a store bought crust if you don’t have time to make it from scratch. I used fresh blackberries, blueberries, and raspberries, and while the combination tasted incredible, the raspberries caused a ton of leakage due to their moisture content so I’ll probably skip those next time. I also added Turbinado sugar to the tops for some extra sweetness and crunch. Seriously delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: To avoid syrup and oozing: Prepare sugar and cornstarch mixture, whisk thoroughly to combine and reserve. To make up : Place 1/4 cup of berries on each round and sprinkle 1 Tablespoon of sugar mixture on top.Shape per recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was a HUGE hit with my picky partner who often complains that pies and tarts are either too sweet or too acidic for his taste -- this hit the sweet spot!  I made the pies using just blueberries and it was delicious!  One only needs 1 egg for the wash, and I'd say more like 1 1/2 cups of fruit, not 2.  Pastry is super flaky!  I got 10 pies out of the dough.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this with fresh peaches, using Sam Sifton’s peach pie recipe for the filling (cut in half). I agree that you only need about 1 1/2 c. of filling. The crust came together perfectly—flaky layers of deliciousness. They are beautiful when baked!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: No need to prepare these on a separate baking sheet.  I filled the rounds on the parchment-covered sheet, refrigerated it and baked it all on the same sheet.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I use Genevieve Ko’s all butter pie crust for these because it’s less work (there’s no smearing with the heel of your hand). I make it in my food processor. Comes out great every time. A real hit with my crowd.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @DVB great advice, made Genevieve’s recipe with this filling, so easy to make (used a mixer) and rolls out perfectly. I made 8 pies and had enough dough and filling left for a small galette (6” after folding the edges) that I prepped first, put in the freezer for 10 minutes while filling the hand pies. When those went to the fridge for an hour, I baked the galette for 25 minutes and we scarfed it down in about 4.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Let me preface this by saying I bake a LOT of pies. This recipe was a big fail. 

The instructions call for WAY too much filling, and if any of the liquid reaches the edges during assembly you'll never be able to seal them and the pastry will turn to goo. In the end, I had a sheet pan covered in filling. I wish I'd just baked a berry pie with lattice crust. 

To those of you for whom this recipe worked well, big kudos.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Would store bought puff pastry work?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Absolutely, Beth!
I have used the filling with puff pastry as turnovers, and when I’ve run out of the flaky pie dough, I’ve also used store bought pie dough.
An 1/8 tsp of cinnamon and a 1/4 tsp of rose water makes a fine addition to the pie filling as well.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made these with strawberries which, lesson learned, make things mess due to high water content. They were difficult to seal and leeched a lot of filling. The pastry also ended up being very thick, but they were still delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This crust turns out well, light and flakey and tasty. I sprinkled a bit of coarse sugar on the top of mine too, I was a nice finishing touch. 1/4 of berries was a little too much to fit in the circle.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Pretty good. A lot of work but special. As others have said, the 1/4 cup of filling instruction is comical. I could only fit about 1 tablespoon of filling in each pie.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I took a lazy weeknight shortcut and used a food processor instead of cutting the butter into the dry ingredients for the crust. I also happened to have nothing but overripe strawberries on hand for making the filling. The result was a light, fluffy, biscuit-like crust with a sweet-tart middle. I essentially wound up with delicious strawberry shortcake pockets! I knew I wasn’t using the right technique to achieve the flakey crust promised, but the result was still lovely.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Another fail here. The berries (extremely fresh and sweet) completely liquified after being sliced thin and put in the sugar/cornstarch mixture. No way to get filling in the 5 inch circle. So, I ended up putting the discs on top of one another to make a pie. Filling still oozed out and made the pie impossible to seal. How is this rated so high?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @DrT same thing happened to me

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @DrT. How do you put 1/4 cup filling in a 5” circle? And, leave 1” around the edges? I can’t be this stupid. I am so frustrated when recipes actually don’t work.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Love this recipe and have made it many times!! I make them a bit bigger and w two pieces of dough each. This time I added a cream cheese filling w 8oz cream cheese, 2 tablespoons of sugar, one egg yolk, and little hit of vanilla and salt. Wow

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Recipe calls for way too much filling, the pie circles recommended are too small. 

A useless hand pie recipe with derivative filling recipe (doesn’t even tell you to mix cornstarch and sugar first so you end up with clumps). If the size and filling amount are wrong this is less helpful than just taking a guess - your intuition is probably better…

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Baked 20 minutes at 425 try 400

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So I love it I’m gonna make it for Thanksgiving Desert

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Anyone think I can sub the berries for apples and not have a problem with baking time?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
If you mean substitute apples for berries* ( = use apples rather than berries), then I suggest that you par-cook the diced apples in a saucepan over a low heat.

*One can't "sub" the original for the proposed replacement -- that's backwards. When in doubt, ask about using X rather than Y.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Has anyone tried these using frozen blueberries? Picked fresh by me then frozen.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Anyone tried cherries??

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I got about 10 hand pies, and only needed the 1 egg as suggested by another review. But - as much as others were delighted it wasn't overly sweet - I was equally disappointed it wasn't sweet (or tastier?) enough. I'm trying to tease out the issue. Might have been my store-bought blueberries (though 1/2 C was frozen wild blueberries) For us, the concept and dough were amazing. It was the filling that fell a little flat. Something I may enjoy solving actually!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Thanks to everyone for all the comments. I found them quite useful. I made the pastry as stated, which turned out great. I cooked my blueberry filling but used my preferred instant tapioca as thickener. 1-1/4 Tablespoons was perfect, once cooled. You can’t really put more than a Tablespoon of filling in the pastry without it becoming messy. It’s still enough and baked with no oozing. Got great reviews!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Has anyone made these and frozen them BEFORE baking? If so, do you defrost before baking or not? at what oven temperature. These would make a school lunch treat! thanks!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Hello has anyone made these ahead and stored them? I’m making a large quantity (about 60) for a party and would like to ale some ahead but I don’t want to compromise quality! Any advice appreciated.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If cooking in an air fryer, set the temperature to 360 and air fry for ten minutes.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made these hand pies with blueberries and they are perfect! Nice little treat that fits in the palm of your hand!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious crust and worth the effort.  Used wild blueberries that I picked and froze last summer.  Mixed the berries with the lemon juice, and in a different bowl mixed the cornstarch with the sugar.  Followed a commenter’s suggestion to load the berry part onto the crust first, and then add the sugar mix on that before closing the hand pie. Resulted in minimal spillage.  Excellent recipe.  Definite crowd-pleaser. Thank you!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If you can, you can make a fortune PIE if you managed to NOT put the berry filling. Write a fortune note on a paper, put it into a pie, then bake the pie. And don't worry about the paper.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If you dared, try this. Make a fortune note. Then make a very thick hand pie with no filling. Slice it in half, then put the note in the pie. Stick the other half to the first half. After you bake it, give it to someone. After they see the note, they will read it and do something that the note said. Have fun!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I made these with leftover cut apple pie filling I make on the stove top. 
I am making them again, today with apples, cherries and peaches. Separate finger pies in the dumpling maker I use for pot stickers.
Cannot wait to hear the raves at the New Year's Party tomorrow!
Keep you posted!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Have made these three times using various berries and even cherries in—cheating here—pre-made pie crust and the most impressive—frozen puff pastry.  Raves all around! I do a wash and sprinkle sugar outside the way I trim my pies and you cannot miss with this recipe!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made these for my little grandson yesterday.  What a hit!  He wouldn’t give his father a bite!  These are fun to make and really charming; my only changes: vanilla for the lemon and flour for cornstarch because I had neither.  Looking forward to experimenting with apples this Fall.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The four star rating was about right. These were hard to make!!! Made them for my office party and   they were a hit. Very doughy but easy to serve and cute.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I've been making my pie dough like this for decades.  The technique for "smearing" the dough I learned from Julia Child.  (On mise le frisage.). As another shortcut, you can make 2-3 batches of pastry crumbs and keep in the freezer, then weigh out what you need for a single or double crust.  I whir my butter and flour up in the food processor, but always mix the dough by hand.  Of course you can do it all by hand like Julia describes.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Do you mean "On met le fraisage?"

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is my new go to pie crust recipe, & especially helpful when running short on time! Used my food processor for mixing, & did the smearing by hand. Was only able to refrigerate it for about 30 minutes.  Rolled out easier than any recipe ever.  So easy to make I worried it wouldn't be flaky.  Rest assured, it came out beautifully:  flaky, a bit puffy, and even without par baking, the bottom crust was fully baked and not soggy. Ample amount of dough too!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I haven been looking for pie crust recipe that is flaky and light. Look no further. I used this recipe for my French onion tart and for my Apple pie and Pumpkin pie tarts. My French husband loved the crust...and the pies. Easy to freeze and bake too.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Beautifully clear and detailed directions for someone who is working on their pie crust skills. This crust came out great.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: View Julia Child's French Tarts Apple Style and How to Make a Basic Pie Dough escoffier school videos for fraisage technique.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Ann Grant mentions "smearing" dough, courtesy of Julia Child. For decades I did that when making pain de mie, as Julia suggests.
Lately I've been making the dough in a powerful Kitchen Aid. I take it to the point where the dough looks satiny, and fine as it is. Then I add clumps of butter ( about half tablespoons ) as the machine is on. That's my machine way of "smearing".
I incorporate the butter, not quite completely, it still will go through 3 risings. I wonder if that would work here.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: i have never understood why one would smear the mixture on the counter as it seems counterintuitive to me. that is, until i read this recipe which says to layer the clumps of dough on top of each other. i think as long as you could it cold you would get nice flakiness out of this.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is a truncated version of puff pastry.  If you have ever made puff pastry, you know that you layer, flatten, chill, roll, layer, flatten, chill, etc.  The layering is what makes the flakes.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This has been my favorite pie crust of late! I find it so easy to roll out and weighing the ingredients equals perfection every time. I do most of it in the food processor and stop when it just barely comes together, then I pat into a ball, divide by weight and continue. I was a vodka in the crust fanatic, but this is even easier and flakier!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So fun to make, so good to eat. This may be my new go-to! Excellent for hand pies.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Gorgeous pie crust. Thank you so much

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this for apple hand pies and it was a perfect, flaky dough. I did add some vodka to my ice-water and used a food processor with great results!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this and it had beautiful layers and was easy to work with. However, it came out pretty tough. I was careful not to overwork it and even let the dough relax in the fridge a second time. I’m not sure if it was the lower butter to flour ratio compared to other recipes like Melissa Clark’s or user error. Either way I think I’m going to keep shopping for my go to crust.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Be sure to pick a cool day to try this one. I made the mistake of using it on a very hot day, and the butter had completely melted into the flour after just one pass on the "heeling" brisage. As a result, the crust had no flaky layering at all.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Back in the day is my absolute favorite bakery. Thank you for sharing!! (If you are ever in Savannah, go early and try to get their cardamom bun with a turmeric OJ - life changing!!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Beautifully clear, well done instructions for someone working on their pie crust skills. The crust looked and tasted great

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My pie dough usually comes out tough or bland, this dough is fantastic. I used it to make the Berry Hand Pies (next time I'll cook the filling first) but it really shone in making curried pork meat pies. The baking soda gives a little lift to the layers that is quite scrumptious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Perfect crust with visible flaking after baking.  Amazing to be rid of soggy bottoms!  Any crust decorations, like pastry leaves, will puff up beautifully.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have stopped using plastic wrap altogether - put discs on a flat platter (could use two plates if no platter large enough) covered with a large bees wax cover. It works fine.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Can this be made without sugar?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Best pastry crust ever!!!!! This recipe succeeds in all aspects, especially for this untalented home pastry chef. Made exactly per recipe. Will be my one and only go-to henceforth. BTW I made fresh blueberry hand pies with this crust, and they were to die for.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Can the butter be frozen and grated rather than cubed?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Looking for your instructions for using store bought pie crust.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: First time making this, turned out delightful! Used for a pot pie and 15-20 min pre-bake helped keep it from getting soggy on the bottom; the top stayed crisp amd flaky, too!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious. The flakiest, most perfect pie crust. Made it exactly as written. I used to make fresh blueberry hand pies and they were extraordinary. I had no idea what I was doing with the smearing but it seemed to have worked: so tender and so flaky. We rolled out the scraps, covered in cinnamon sugar and then rolled those up tightly and baked with a little egg wash: couldn’t stop eating them.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I thought I had overworked this dough for my hand pies, but everything came out tender.  I only refrigerated for a few hours and I made it by hand, because it’s so relaxing to smush the butter in the flour.  I love this recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Wonderful flake, not "biscuty" at all and had folks wondering when I was going to quit my day job and open the bakery.  That good but, very needy of refrigeration.  I would recommend keeping any portion not actively being worked at ~34" F.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have tried to make this  dough two times now and mine always comes out a melted mess by the time I smear the dough.  I even start out with using frozen butter so I do not know what I am doing wrong.  I do not understand how you keep the butter from warming up when you are using the heel of your hand to smear the dough and making it lay flat. Is there a video that shows the technique and how to do this correctly?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Propah chowdah uses potatoes cut "thick/thin" into wedges, so the thin parts dissolve and the thick parts retain texture.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
The best fish chowders are made with haddock, not cod.

You can also make a wonderful fish soup using the ingredients above, adding chopped carrots and celery (and other fav veggies) simmered prior to adding haddock, and omitting the cream.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I agree, use haddock. And do your heart a favor by using a piece of salt pork instead of butter and bacon. Try out the pork and use the fat to saute the onions. Use Whole milk plus a can of evaporated milk. That is traditional, although in the Maritimes they start with bacon instead of salt pork. Better the second day as the potatoes yield their starch and the salt pork flavors the milk. Serve with fresh ground black pepper and snipped parsley. This is the Maine way, no thickening flour.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Why 2 pans?   I fry the bacon in the soup pan, remove it and enough bacon fat to allow me to add a little butter for taste, then fry the onions and potato in the same pan and proceed with the recipe

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The dashi broth was a revelation - changed the way I'll make fish chowder in the future. Delicious recipe. I used light cream and shrimp and pollock instead of scallops and cod, otherwise made the recipe as written.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have bonito flakes but don't know the ratio with water to make good broth.  Suggestions?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Here it is (scroll down to the bottom of the page): "The recipe for the perfect dashi" http://www.ninben.jp/katsuo/ 30-gram bonito flakes per 1-liter water.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was such a light and delicious chowder. Dare I say healthy? We are watching our $$$ and our waistlines, so used whole milk, frozen cod and frozen shrimp, and used vegetable broth. Skipped the bacon. Delicious and absolute yes-will-make-it-again-recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Agree with Nina Pearlmutter.  As a native of Maine,  haddock is the fish of choice.  It is wild caught more abundant and far tastier than cod.  Cod is highly over rated!  Can still have an Asian bent, but please , haddock!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Haddock. Not Cod. Haddock.

Scallops are a nice touch as is a half pint of fresh picked Maine crabmeat. Use 'em both if possible.

Tabasco to taste. Not peppercorns. 

Serve with Crown Pilot Crackers if you can find 'em.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Cod. Not haddock. Cod.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’m from the Midwest, and one of my dad’s favorite places was Portland. Eventide was a must visit. I had the chance to go with him a few years ago. He was pretty sick, but even so wanted to visit one last time. Made it exactly as written, used instant dashi from Chaganju brand that I ordered on Amazon. I think my dad would have approved.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: There’s no way you can bring one yellow onion and a pound of diced potatoes to any where near golden in 5 minutes…all these times are a bit off.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was delicious! Eventide is a favorite when we visit Maine and it's nice to be able to cook this since we won't be traveling this summer. Made it with the Dashi and loved the flavor. Added fresh corn and extra bacon!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Where did you get fresh corn in May?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Made this today and it was delicious! Use the largest Dutch oven or other pot that you have.  A few changes thanks to helpful commenters: Cooked the bacon in the pot and used 2 tbsp. of bacon fat to sub. for the butter; used 1.5 qts. of fish broth rather than 2 qts. (perfect!); added 3 cans of minced clams for more seafood goodness. The dashi is indeed a game changer. I'm going to look for more recipes using it as an ingredient. 
Thanks, Ms. Moskin & helpful commenters!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I've lived in Maine, slurped up many tasty fish chowders. My 2 guests and I rated this one "meh" at best. Spent $50+ on ingredients at Whole Foods (the price of scallops!) and followed the recipe. Ended up with a huge pot of thin and relatively flavorless soup, "chunky goodness" was scant, no one asked for seconds. The fishy Hondashi broth was not a plus for us.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I agree the dashi is a revelation.   I used cod and scallops and it was truly delicious.   Proper seasoning with salt snd pepper is important  in this recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I found two quarts of stock was one quart too many. There was too much liquid. I agree that frying the bacon in the soup pan and leaving some of the bacon fat for the onions and potatoes is easier and more flavorful..

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious with chopped fennel bulb and stems.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Disappointing recipe. Made it as written because Eventide is supposed to be so fabulous. Really bland and uninteresting, even with a freshly made and very flavorful fish stock. Added a little smoked paprika and a little cayenne to wake it up, which helped.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was delicious! I had to make substitutions due to being stuck in a snow storm. I used leeks instead of onions, and omitted potatoes because I ran out. Flounder and shrimp were used for fish. Homemade dashi (bonito and kombu) gave the chowder smokiness that was similar to using bacon fat. I did make the bacon as well but I don't think it really needs it. I will definitely make again!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made a version of this for my family in Nova Scotia.  Used tips from others and adapted for their less adventurous tastebuds. Cooked the bacon then browned potatoes and onions in the bacon grease.  Substituted haddock and hake for the seafood.  Family prefers a creamy chowder so I only used 2 cups of fish broth, 1/2 cup of whole milk, and a can of evaporated milk.  Cut the potatoes thick and thin. Everyone wanted seconds. Will cook again soon, and try as written back in California.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made stock with the shells from a dozen Maine lobsters we steamed for Thanksgiving.   Will the lobster stock work in this recipe?   I have used the stock to make shrimp, okra and andouille gumbo per Paul Prudhomme's recipe which was fantastic.   I don't have any dashi on hand.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Yes; emphatically YES! Lobster, shrimp, and crab shells make for great stock simmered in water with a little vermouth or white wine, onions, carrots, celery, and thyme.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used lobster stock I had made weeks ago and frozen, and was looking for something to use it in. I also replaced scallops with clams for a mix of fish/clam chowder. The broth and dish came out perfectly for flavor. The next time I will use the fat from the salt pork to sauté the potatoes and onion, and will try to get the broth thicker with the potatoes. One of my first times making a chowder and this recipe provides a really good base to experiment with.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I added a touch of saffron. Just because.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I almost never use recipes but stayed pretty close on this one. As at least one other reviewer stated, fewer pans! Toasted the peppercorns (WOW!!! game changer!!!) then the lardons into the onions etc. I do love me a homemade dashi and this was indeed a “revelation” in a chowder, as others have said. I got out my Japanese seven spice togarashi but didn’t even use it: the toasted ground peppercorns, lovely dashi and utter simplicity of this meal was delicious. Inspired my first review here!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Absolutely lovely recipe from Eventide. I did not deviate from the recipe. I made my own dashi, which was very easy and I highly recommend doing. I enjoyed the Japanese influence greatly. The broth tasted almost light, yet unmistakably complex compared to a typical American chowder. I think cutting the potatoes so they don't dissolve lends to the delightfully clean feel of the broth, and seems intentional, as this is a marriage of Japanese and American soup styles.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If you fry up the bacon in the soup pot after sizzling the peppercorns, drain off the fat and remove the crispy pieces, that's one less pot to wash at the end!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
No need for scallops!
Omg. This is the best soup

It’s so comforting. Omg. 
You can find Dashi on Amazon. 
One teaspoon per cup of water… there’s your broth. 
Go easy on the heat. Protect the cream from breaking. 
Trader Joe’s Ciabatta loaf toasted with butter and

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
For everyone complaining that proper fish chowder uses haddock, not cod (among other outrages), note that this recipe is called “Eventide Fish Chowder,” not “Traditional Fish Chowder.”

I’m a proud Mainer and I love traditional chowder. I also love Eventide’s chowder. There’s room in this world for both.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Absolutely agree. This is intended to be a fusion dish, not a "proper American chowda". And it delivers well.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Garnish w salt pork!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made as directed except substituted two leeks for one onion. It was a superb summer chowder - light and flavorful.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: NEVAH use bacon - always use salt pork, get it crisp before even beginning the rest of the recipe, set it  aside, use as garnish atop each wonderful bowl od chowder.  While Haddock is usually the preferred fish, cod works well - in fact, any fish will do.  This is my standard Christmas Eve starter - one of the seven of the feast in my Italian household.  Of course, it should be known, that Italians do not usually make chowders such as this one - they usually make a fish soup with tomato base.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Really uninteresting version of fish chowder, I've always wanted to try Eventide so was excited to see the recipe, but I've had and made much, much better chowder.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this last night with halibut, and the addition of carrot, celery and fresh white corn, it was great.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Rendered fat from thick-sliced bacon; sauteed veg in that and omitted butter (one pan). Added leek and minced red pepper to veg. Loved the textural difference between cod and scallop (haddock not available on west coast).

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Fabulous and relatively simple chowder. Especially loved the textural difference using both cod and scallops. I started with cooking the bacon, then sauteed the veg in the bacon fat instead of using butter. Added a leek (leeks make everything better) and a minced red bell pepper for color.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I swapped the cream for whole milk with a Tbsp. of corn starch, used clam juice instead of fish stock, and then decided to throw in the chopped clams along with the cod and scallops. It was truly delish! I love a flexible recipe like this.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made homemade dashi - a gamechanger for how to make fish-like broth when you don't have any. Will try with haddock next time. Can't make this too often - it's rich.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this tonight. Used leeks instead of onions and added 1/2 red pepper. Halibut, as my grocer was out of cod. Delicious. A keeper. Also, I cooked bacon first, as another cook mentioned. Used that fat with a little butter.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is really good and quite easy to make, even on a workday.  For no other reason than I haven't used it enough and it will expire in a few weeks, I used ghee instead of butter.  My fish "stock" is Better Than Bouillon fish base.  I used what I had and my potatoes were a mixture of golden baby potatoes and fingerlings. No big difference, I'm sure. I was too lazy to crumble my nori too much, but it wasn't a big deal.  Scallions instead of chives because I needed to use them.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I got the jar of hondashi. How much do you add to the water to make the broth?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: 1 tsp to 2 cups of water.  Heat until it simmers in microwave.  That's it!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is the best fish chowder I have ever made.  I substituted tempeh bacon and cooked it with the onion and potato which gave it a nice smoky flavor.  The nori in this was great and didn't need to be cut all that small.  Amazing recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Don’t miss the early step of salting the cod and allowing it to sit and permeate the fish while you prepare the rest of the ingredients.  I missed this step and while the broth was well seasoned and delicious, each bite of cod was bland and flavorless. Used sweet potatoes instead of Yukon’s as that is what I had on hand. It was a delicious substitute.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
It's all about the dashi!
I've made this several times.   First time, used a local Maine (Rosemont Bakery) fish stock and the result was just O.K.  BUT, using dashi transformed the dish.  It was a revelation.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: /found this delicious and light. I think more of a stew or soup--not thick chowder. I prefer it "thin" so perfect for me, but others might be disappointed

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is thin broth as written.  While delicious, it wasn’t totally satisfying. I will use less even less stock next time.  I added corn and I that was my favorite part in the mix with everything else. I followed the “propah chowdah ” suggestion on the cut of the potatoes and discovered that I am from the west coast and prefer nouvel chowdah.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Absolutely delicious!  Hondashi stock adds elegant depth.  Do toast the peppercorns and do render the bacon in the same soup pot you are using to saute your vegetables.  I increased the cod and potato and decreased the broth and the result was rich, chunky and satisfying; we topped it with salmon furikake.  If this propelled you to buy  Hondashi powder from your Japanese grocery, you'll also find a lovely NYT recipe for Niku Udon (Japanese Beef Noodle Soup) which uses Hondashi powder.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Superb, especially if you take the trouble to use the Japanese ingredients.  (Dashi combines very well with bacon, thank you.)  This could be adapted for any kind of mild fish or shellfish.  On the other hand, some are losing patience with those zealots who constantly criticize a recipe for not being something else.  "Propah" indeed.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is by far the best-tasting favorite fish chowder and my favorite fish chowder recipe. There are two things I love about it - the toasted and ground peppercorns (don't skip this part) and the use of dashi instead of fish stock. I can never get fish stock/fish trimmings/fish bones when need it. That dahsi is easy and makes it tastes like the sea.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So many commenters don't seem to understand that this is not a recipe for classic chowdah, Maine or elsewhere.  It is a recipe for Eventide's chowder.  As written, it is very delicious.  Why are so many people so unwilling to try something new?  I grew up in Rhode Island, so I've eaten many gallons of chowdah.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Oddly enough on the west coast we have cod, but I have never seen haddock.  According to Wikipedia haddock is a true cod, so maybe it is a better cod than that from the Pacific?  

Oddly enough I have dashi powder in my spice cabinet that I can add to veggie stock.  Apparently due to Costco I have almost everything needed to make this recipe (even scallops) in my house other than heavy cream.  Now this is where we get creative. (which I can do, I am the only fish eater in the house!).

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I think haddock has a short fishing season and is harder to find than cod.   I buy seapack frozen haddock fillets at Whole Foods in Mississippi whenever its available so I always have some in my freezer.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I added a splash of fancy soy sauce at the table. This was great for summer - really light!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I have been making fish chowder all spring using 
haddock which is delicious Making it with dashi is a great idea and I will do it now on

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Small dice of potato
separate solids from broth when cooling

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have a daughter who can’t eat dairy. What’s the best non dairy milk or cream to use here?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was REALLY good. I didn’t have bacon, so omitted it, but didn’t miss it, although it’s always a welcome addition and is no doubt delicious in this. I found that the broth to chunks ratio was a bit high, but I’m not complaining because I’m going to repurpose the broth for ramen so really, win/win!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: To make stock I used Better than Bouillon (clam base)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Wow! Dashi is a miracle ingredient! Followed all cooking times but added a dash of red pepper flakes and carrot, celery, peas, and corn in place of half of the potatoes...and then negated the health benefits of that by doubling the cream. Worth it! I've never made any sort of chowder before, but my fiance said it was restaurant quality!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I thought this was delicious.  I am lactose sensitive and used 2% lactose free milk instead of heavy cream and my whole family loved it.  The Dashi was a game changer!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I added 3 steamed clans per serving....

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Did this and loved! With the following substitutions: doubled the recipe, mix  of cod and flounder, chicken stock instead of dashi, 3 lbs potatoes, 3 c heavy cream, 1 package bacon. Did not use peppercorns. Used the seaweed snacks, and they were unnecessary. Added 5 c. rice and spinach as always to fill it out a bit more.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Used Kitchen Basics Seafood Stock. Lovely flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I highly recommend visiting an Asian market - there is a high volume of seafood going through our H Mart and the cod and scallops were the best quality and lowest cost I have ever experienced - at least 1/2 of what I would pay at grocery.  I found a seafood seasoning that can be used to create the stock. Super easy recipe - a 15 minute cook and light prep. The stock was mildly flavored so the seafood was the highlight, which is what you experience at Eventide in Portland, ME.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Don’t forget to take the little muscle flaps off the scallops!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My husband and I really liked this recipe!  We followed the recipe exactly, but made a 1/2 recipe.  Seemed to translate well!  I was very careful adding salt during the cooking process, as my husband is very sensitive to salty food.  The end result was perfect!  Thank you NYTC!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Next time, skip the pricey scallops. Use Cod/Mahi Mahi/Shrimp.
Suggest a classic Fumet (Fish Stock) instead of Dashi. Don't toss the Shrimp shells. Use them in the Fish Stock. A can of Snow's Chopped Clams also adds flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This sounds interesting, but I restrict seafood 'chowders' to recipes which were made aboard ships prior to refrigeration. There was no source of milk or cream. Just hard crackers and water.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Do you use an electric stove? A gas stove?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Is there a substitute for bacon for pescatarians?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made it with Sea Bass and Scallops, it was fantastic. Thanks!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Double the cream, add red pepper flakes and clams

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Agree with John: use less stock. I will make with 6 cups instead of 8 next time- it is way too watery. Not to mention, I have way too much (can you freeze?) I used vegetable broth and dashi (granules are very easy). Also, I agree you need a Dutch oven - mine was full to the brim, and I like his idea of using the bacon fat in place of butter. The bacon on top definitely added texture and flavor. Glad I did the extra step. My only addition was corn, which I will continue to add.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This recipe relies on realllly good knife work. Having been to Eventide, their potato’s are perfectly square and bite sized.
I just made this and I liked it but I wish I cut the fish/scallops smaller and paid more attention to the potato cuts. I also added some red pepper flakes to spice it up:)
Also just a psa this is a very thin chowder!! Don’t go in expecting it to thicken up if that’s how you prefer chowder!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Wow, amazing and easy! I made this on my boat in the Caribbean with barracuda and shrimp because that is what I had. Instead of dashi I used my home made lobster broth, 1 shallot and 1 small onion. I love the flavors and how quickly this recipe came together. We have been to Eventide for their brown butter lobster roll but now that I know they have this chowder I’ll have to try it next time we are in Maine! Definitely a keeper recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I agree with the meh. And simmered scallops? I should have known better! Added shrimp and clams..they were the high point.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: After adding everything except the cod, I found this soup to be watery and lackluster, needing more than just salt and pepper.  In rescue mode I sliced some small shiitake mushrooms and sauteed them in the bacon fat and seasoned them with tamari, Then I steamed and added some fresh baby spinach.  My soup made absolutely no pretense of being Eventide’s but the additions rescued a bland soup that was headed for the compost and turned it into a balanced flavorful one dish meal.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I find this recipe an excellent base but less flavorful than I prefer. For me, the addition of confit garlic, smoked paprika, and Old Bay really elevate the dish. To make dairy free, I use olive oil to cook with and cashew cream instead of heavy cream. I enjoy serving it with toasted herbed panko and a drizzle of good olive oil.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Good solid chowder; followed the recipe using Hondashi and previously frozen cod and halibut instead of the scallops.  Would like it to have some more brightness; maybe I'll add some acidity next time.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe is excellent! Hondashi is OK but I would say that Shiro Dashi is better for this dish: https://a.co/d/eTFUQuD

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I make this with fish and shrimp (makes it "fancy") and make shrimp broth from the shells by boiling them for 5 minutes and straining.  Will add instant dashi for a double fish broth hit.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Dashi is pretty easy to make, and you can make extra and store it in the fridge. Every Asian market will have Bonito flakes and dried kelp. Get online and follow a real easy recipe. It takes about 10 minutes. It is a Japanese version of American chicken stock.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
As a New England girl, I’ve made Clam Chowder many times. This is my first time making Fish Chowder and I’m very happy that I gave this recipe a try. I made it as written except for using all cod because that’s what I had on hand. 
It was delicious and dusting the soup with nori is an extraordinary idea.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Combined 2 cans of Bar Harbor fish stock w/2 bottles of BH clam juice. Added  3 1/2 tspn Hondashi powder to the broth, heated gently. Rendered bacon in the pot, removed, added a knob of butter to the bacon fat, added/sweated onion, 4 minced cloves of garlic, 2 sliced celery ribs and 1 1/4 pound of cubed mini yellow and red potatoes, s&p. In w/the stock to cook potatoes; then milk/cream, frozen corn, 3 lb of cod cheeks (available locally; an amazingly sweet, tender bite) to braise. Mighty good!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So delicious!!  I make it over and over, again.  This time, due to larder conditions, with patty pan squash and sweet potatoes in lieu of white potatoes (cooked half the time).   Once again, it’s stupendous!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This chowder is delicious!! I have made it many times. "Hondashi" is OK, but I like Ninben's "Shiro Dashi" much better: https://a.co/d/j1qNN37 It's made from natural ingredients, but is highly concentrated. So, use it little by little.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Throw goldfish in for kids to go fishing!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Here fishy fishy…  I really enjoyed making this! It was super easy. But for those of us whose pantries are limited, I didn’t have Dashi on hand. To add fish flavor and richness, I added about four flat anchovy fillets and melted them into the butter. I also did carrots, celery, and fennel with the potatoes and onion, and deglazed the pan with a splash of sherry before adding the stock and bacon. I threw in a little frozen corn just because I love corn in chowder!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made as instructed except I only had 1/2 pound of scallops so I threw in some shrimp. I reduced the liquid to make it thicker

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I added 1/4 cup of marsala -- enough to enhance the flavor but not enough to overwhelm the dish.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Amazing flavor!!! I did not use scallops but used Pacific cod (20oz) and also added 8 oz chopped clams. I make dashi from scratch frequently so already had kombu and bonito on hand. (Making dashi is so easy, I highly recommend making it from scratch with kombu and bonito.) I opted to use bacon and reduced the stock to 1.5 qts based on other reviews. This chowder was so flavorful! My new favorite chowder.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Love banana bread in almost all its forms and have baked many over the years. My fav recipe is very similar to this one but with one substantial difference: It uses 1/3 cup of sugar instead of 1 cup and 1/3 cup of oil instead of a 1/2. The result is a plenty sweet enough, moist bread that tastes like banana and cinnamon instead of sugar.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Thank you!  I used your measurements (actually a tad less sugar), and the bread was moist and plenty sweet.  Horrors of horrors, I omitted the sugar topping and sprinkled the top with a little cinnamon and shaved bittersweet chocolate. I agree with other commenters who nixed the chocolate chips.

My crust did brown, so I turned the oven down at around 45 minutes.  (My oven runs hot.)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Because I bake banana bread fairly often, I buy one or two more bananas than I expect to use. A few days later, I take the last seriously ripe, bananas, and pop them into the freezer, skin and all. If I want to make banana bread later this afternoon, I take 3 frozen bananas out of the freezer and let them thaw on the counter. Three hours later, I cut the stem off each thawed banana and squeeze it directly into the batter, as if it were a tube of toothpaste.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: She's in the oven right now and I also added one cup of chocolate chips because WHY NOT??

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I feel the same way....WHY NOT!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Toss the chocolate chips in with the flour before adding wet ingredients. This helps them "stay afloat" in the batter, instead of sinking. A full cup of any add-in is an awful lot, and chocolate chips are the one add in that I am concerned about because I think it affects Integrity of of the cake, but that leaves more chocolate chips for you!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Go completely nuts and add a cup of roughly chopped walnuts or pecans. A hefty teaspoon of vanilla wouldn't hurt either.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Seems like replacing the water with rum or bourbon would be a great way to introduce a little more flavor into the recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Soak some raisins or dried cranberries in that rum or bourbon for 30 minutes or so before you bake. Use orange juice if you don't want to use alcohol.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Steve what does that do

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: We didn’t have honey so we substituted maple syrup. The bread was great!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Really delicious! 

After seeing all of the comments about the cinnamon topping, I replaced it with a version of Deb Pearlman of Smitten Kitchen's streusel topping. Worked really well:

1/2 c. flour
1 tb. granulated sugar
3 tb. brown sugar
1/4 tsp. cinnamon
Pinch of nutmeg
Pinch of salt
3 tb. melted butter

Stir all together until slightly lumpy, sprinkled on top before baking (pressed in slightly).

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: No bananas so used one cup of pumpkin puree. Wonderfully moist and great flavor. Loved the Demerara sugar and cinnamon on top-such an easy topping instead of a streusel topping. In my oven loaf was done at 50 minutes so it is good to keep an eye on it. Highly recommend.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Kathy would mango work?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Love banana bread in almost all its forms and have baked many over the years. My fav recipe is very similar to this one but with one substantial difference: It uses 1/3 cup of sugar instead of 1 cup and 1/3 cup of oil instead of a 1/2. The result is a plenty sweet enough, moist bread that tastes like banana and cinnamon instead of sugar.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Drop blobs of nutella on top and gently push them down into the batter before sprinkling on the topping.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Just a note for someone who thought wax paper could not go in the over.  Not true.  Years ago, [40's-50's] parchment paper was not readily available, especially for home bakers.  Wax paper was the standard for lining pans.  In loaf pans, it can extend a bit on the long sides to make removing the loaf easier.  If the oven temperature is really high, the paper may brown a bit on the exposed edges, but it is still safe and works great.  Use parchment paper instead if you are concerned.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Add chopped nuts, teaspoon  of vanilla.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I added chopped walnuts ... bang on!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this gluten free using Bob's Red Mill gluten free 1 to 1 flour. The banana bread came out very tasty!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is the best banana bread I have ever eaten, much less made. I’ve made it twice, exactly as written, and am excited to try some of the variations suggested here. For others at high altitude (I’m at 9400’), I haven’t needed to change the amount of any of the ingredients, but it takes forever to bake. Close to 90 minutes, and I loosely covered the top with foil and increased the oven temp by 15 degrees for the last 15 minutes.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Following other suggestions, I reduced the sugar by half and subbed maple syrup for the honey. It’s very fluffy banana bread, and the topping is delicious, it pretty delicate.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: also works as muffins

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have a question about this recipe - why does it offer wax paper as an option for lining the baking pan if wax paper is not heat resistant and isn't supposed to go in the oven?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: 1/3 c sugar, 1/3 c oil, 1tsp vanilla, nuts

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I love bananas. I love cinnamon. I love banana bread. I love this recipe. The end.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Now ya did it! Got to make this now!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Per Laura's helpful notes, made this with 1/3 cup sugar and 1/3 cup olive oil and added 1 cup of toasted chopped pecans.  Really, really good.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Very easy to prepare with the kids.  I’ve starting to eat bananas more and am left with a few miscreants towards the end of the week so decided to try my hand at some banana bread.  The pandemic really makes you rethink your free time right?  Only so much to binge watch after all.  Only add was vanilla I just don’t understand recipes without it—it’s my jam!  If you are a newbie don’t be freaked out by the batter...it’s thin but turns out great and the topping does add a lovely crunch!  Enjoy

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Not a fan of banana bread but had 3 very ripe ones so tried this. Only change was to make 12 muffins instead, baked for 27 minutes. Loved these, light and fluffy, the crunch topping is really good and the perfect amount for 12 muffins.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I initially made this recipe exactly as directed, but I prefer a more banana-forward recipe when making (or rather, when eating) banana bread. The topping is excellent, so I add it to the banana bread recipe that is otherwise my favorite. I also love this recipe with a can of pumpkin puree instead of bananas. Add 1 tsp vanilla extract.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I reduced sugar to half cup, added tsp vanilla and baked in 8 x 8 (lined) pan 40-45 minutes. Also cut all the topping ingredients in half and it was plenty to add a crunch. Perfect as a snack cake.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This banana bread is so good! I was running low on bananas so I did half bananas and half apple sauce. Still turned out amazing.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Used 3 mini loaf pans (5 1/2" x 3" x 2 1/4" high) and turned out perfect! Filled the pans 2/3 full and baked at 350 for 35-40 min. Needed a little less of the topping mixture for the mini loaves.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is an incredibly delicious banana bread - it’s been out of the oven for 30 minutes and half of it is already gone. As another commenter suggested, I used only 1/3 cup of sugar and 1/3 cup of oil and it was perfect. Also, swapped honey for maple syrup.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Used an 8.5 x 4.5 x 2.5 pyrex and wound up leaving it to bake for about 65 minutes total at sustained 350 degrees.   I took it out, let it cool a bit, and then popped it back in the oven while they both cooled down, and this seemed to work okay!  Very moist but not soggy, and cooked throughout.   Love how the flavor of the honey took on molassessy notes as it carmelized.    Will definitely make again, though will try it with less sugar as another reviewer suggested.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Can't have my banana bread without pecans added to the batter. I also add my favorite spice nutmeg along with the cinnamon. I look forward to the addition of honey.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Nuts are an add-in. Use them or skip them. It won't matter to the Integrity of the cake.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Absolutely right about adding chocolate chips. Cut sugar in half. You won’t miss it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made as written, this is the best banana bread recipe out there

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: i am going to be adding in some raspberries or strawberries, just to give it a little zing, and I'm also going to use 2/3 cup sugar instead of a full cup.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Be careful with strawberries. They have a lot of moisture, and can throw the recipe out of kilter, in a way that other berries do not.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I substituted coconut oil (in place of butter) and coconut sugar (in place of the brown sugar) and it turned out great. Really easy recipe - with chocolate chips of course

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: There's no butter in the recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Anyone try this substituting GF flour? (my husband has celiac).

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Very tasty! I halved the sugar in the batter and was sweet enough. I added butter and flour to the topping and mixed in chopped walnuts so I have a nice crumbled topping. That also seemed to keep it very moist on top. I don’t know that the cinnamon really shined through but as far as banana bread goes, I would definitely make this again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Amazing GF version accomplished! Used 1/2 cup each Bob’s 1-to-1, oat flour, and sweet rice flour. Also reduced batter sugar to 1/2 cup and topping sugar to 1 tbs granulated and 2 tbs dark brown sugar- swirled it into the top. Added 1/2 chocolate chips but would be fabulous without. Longer baking time of about 70 minutes. Did not last the day with 2 adults and 2 kids.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this as noted except didn’t have veg oil so used refined coconut oil (which doesn’t taste like coconut). It’s yummy, but I think the topping should have some butter or oil mixed in to make more of a crumb as the sugar doesn’t entirely stay put when slicing. I will add more cinnamon to the cake next time because I didn’t find there to be enough cinnamon flavor. I did 3/4 c sugar and 1/4 c honey.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I followed another reader's advice and only used 1/3 of a cup of sugar (instead of 1 cup) and 1/3 of a cup of oil (instead of 1/2 cup).  I also added 1/4 cup of yogurt and the recipe came out beautifully.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Good, not as good as I'd hoped. Sugar on top didn't really get crunchy, just crumbly; reduced sugar to 3/4 c. due to comments about sweetness. Used Ceylon cinnamon and it just doesn't taste cinnamon-y at all - used prescribed amounts, maybe I should've used more? Added walnuts; checked doneness at 50 minutes, baked for 1 hour and it isn't as moist as I would like - more cake-y. I might try this again with more banana, more Ceylon cinnamon, maple syrup.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Great recipe, we only used two bananas first time we made this which left a bit of the banana flavor out so next time we make it we will definitely use three.  Outside of that this is a great recipe loved by the fam that we will definitely make again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Upped the bananas to about 1 1/4 or 1 1/2 cups and it came out really nicely—anything less and the flavor would have been lost out to the cinnamon. Loved this and will be making it again!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I reduced the white sugar to 1/2 cup and used about 1/2 of the topping and found it sweet enough. I also swapped out 1/2 c of the flour for whole wheat flour. Delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this GF and vegan by substituting 2T ground flaxseeds for eggs, 1 3/4 C GF AP flour for the flour (ImagineThat GF), and 1 C vegan chocolate chips, b/c yes, of course - amazing!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this as is and it was really good. My family said how much they liked the crunchy topping, and I did too! The only thing that didn't work quite right is that it needed to bake for about an hour and 15 minutes, and that was even with bumping up my oven to a higher temperature since I am at 5,000 feet. I'll definitely make again!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Way way to much sugar. My teeth are hurting!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I don't get the too sweet comments (but admit to having a sweet tooth).  Nevertheless, I listened to the comments here and reduced it to 3/4 cup and thought it could have been sweeter.  Also,  I could barely taste the bananas. I think it needs at least three large (about 1 1/2 cups).  The cinnamon is a nice change of pace, but I have an old fashioned banana cake with streusel topping that is far superior to this IMHO.  I may try that topping on this bread.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Agreed re: bananas, could use more flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Makes 12 muffins as well, baked for 35 minutes

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is the first bananna bread recipe I've used where the bread turned out how I was wanting it to.  Not difficult to make, good texture and it tastes great.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Just made this delicious Banana Bread! I eat pretty low sugar these days and didn’t even add the “1 cup sugar” and it tastes perfect to me. I still used 1/4cup honey and it was sufficient, plus the cinnamon sugar topping (way more than enough sugar). I layered sliced bananas about halfway thru the batter and topped with a whole banana halved lengthwise on top, then sprinkled all the cinnamon crunch on top of that. Will definitely make this again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My favorite quarantine banana bread! I also reduce sugar to 1/2c and oil to 1/3c. After my first bake, I decided the topping isn't worth the effort or crumbs. Delicious without it!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is the most decadent banana bread I've ever made. Even I said "wow, that's a lot of cinnamon sugar" when pouring on the topping. Would I make it again? YES! But this isn't one of those banana breads where you can try to pretend even just a little bit that it's good for you. But my goodness, it's tasty. I added in some walnuts, which were a nice addition.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious with wonderful banana flavor! Followed recipe with the following changes: used 3 large bananas, added a teaspoon vanilla, increased cinnamon in batter to 2 tsp, used 1/4 cup whole wheat flour with 1 1/4 cups AP flour to equal the 1.5 cups flour called for in the recipe. For the topping I made only half the amount. I liked the idea of adding another flavor instead of water (whiskey, coffee, kahlua, orange juice?) Will try that next time. Moist flavorful bread, crunchy topping. 5 stars.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Used molasses instead of honey, allspice instead of cinnamon and doubled it,  added half cup toasted chopped walnuts and half cup fresh cranberries (should have halved them), eliminated topping:  delicious!  Sliced and keep in freezer for breakfast treats.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: press topping into bread

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Following the notes: Three bananas, 1 t vanilla, 1/2 cup chopped toasted walnuts, 1/2 C sugar, 1/3 C oil. Baked in a wax paper sling without incident. Next time I'll try the streusel topping suggested here.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I've made three loaves.  Fail-proof!  So delicious.  I was hesitant since the recipe called for oil instead of butter, and although I LOVE honey, I customarily don't like cooking baked goods with honey.  The texture of this recipe is so pleasing.  The sugar topping can definitely be reduced, but don't omit it, the crunch of the topping is a very nice element. I've never had consistently reliable banana bread... until now.  This recipe is a keeper

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this following someone else's suggestion to reduce sugar & oil to 1/3 cup each. I used two bananas. It was absolutely beautiful that way, moist and perfect, and undoubtably much lighter than the original version. I also used the cinammon nutmeg topping suggested by another comment and it was spot on, giving it some added sweetness without being cloying. Chocolate chips would also be good. It took only 40 mins in my oven (3,000ft elevation). It got rave reviews.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made the same adjustments as other readers - 1/3c of oil (used olive oil) and 1/3c sugar. I added 1tsp of vanilla and a bit of ginger - came out great! Super light and not overly sweet. So good!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So delicious. Mine took about 70 minutes to finish. But everyone liked it and it’s a lil spin on my normal banana bread. Will be making this again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this in muffin form.  25 minutes at 350 worked well.  It made 12 large muffins.  The topping made the middles sink.  They aren’t very pretty, but they are delicious!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Add some vanilla reduce the sugar by 1/3 and use a struessel topping.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Swap the location of the tipping—putting it in the center of the loaf to make it more like a coffee cake. Less mess and less likely to burn.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
replaced:
1/4 cup of warm water with greek yogurt
1/2 cup of veggie oil with butter

added:
1/2 tsp of cardamom

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: As muffins, bake 30 minutes. I halve the sugar and omit topping. Replace cinnamon with garam masala, doubled to 2 tsp, for very slightly spicy flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: No ringing endorsements from the family. I was a little embarrassed by this recipe. Very little banana flavor. Window was extraordinarily wet and took an additional 15 minutes to bake. End result was not dry but tasted more of cinnamon then anything else. Balance of flavors was just not there.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made 2 loafs. Neither had the topping, and both halved the sugar and lessened the oil. Added cardamom to both and increased the cinnamon to account for the topping. Also used buttermilk in place of water. I added matcha powder, chocolate chips & pistachio nuts to the second loaf. really good (but I guess its not really the recipe then?) But tasted like a spiced banana cake. Easy to make and I will do it again!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Substituted King Arthur GF (cup for Cup) and added an extra egg to combat dryness associated with GF baking- delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
As others have done, I reduced the sugar by 1/3-1/2 and otherwise kept it true to the recipe. People love it- I love it. Cinnamon sugar makes it into a comfort food. Made it as muffins recently. Cooked for 25 minutes and lasted about 6!
Thanks Melissa.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So so good! Made as instructed, with only the addition of vanilla. Because I am kinda addicted to adding vanilla to all baking…

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was divine and so easy to make. I served it at a coffee gathering and everyone wanted the recipe. I, too, cut the sugar in half.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So so good. Added walnuts and baked off as 12 muffins. I wouldn’t change anything else.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Ok so I did some changes to this recipe but it was phenomenal. 

I added maple syrup instead of honey, rum instead of water, added one tsp of vanilla extract, added 1/2 cup of walnuts, and used browned butter instead of oil.

Best banana bread I have ever made. It was so good.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Did anyone else need to bake this for a lot longer than one hour?  My tester was literally wet with batter!!!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: 1/2 c sugar

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Tried it with 1/2 cup sugar and it was perfect.  May try next time with 3 bananas, just to see the difference, but it was great with two.  Got rave reviews at home.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: 10/13/23. - The Mark Bittman banana bread with unsweetened coconut is better

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Can also be waffled!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious recipe. I love that it has oil instead of butter. I followed Jody’s suggestion and used 1/3 cup of olive oil, 1/3 cup of sugar, also added 1 cup of chopped, toasted pecans. It was delicious, great for bringing to a gathering.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is my go to recipe and is perfect as written. I did recently make it with bread flour and subbed brown sugar for the honey and it was maybe the best batch yet. Flexible and delicious, does well with add ins like nuts and additional spices too.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Nice and easy recipe. As always I made it all in one bowl, starting with the bananas. I can’t be washing 3 bowls. I halved the sugar and it was still plenty sweet. One cup of sugar would surely cause hyperactivity! I also topped it with walnuts mixed with a little melted butter, brown sugar and cinnamon. You can almost never overdo cinnamon in my opinion.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Too sweet. I cut the sugar in half and used brown rather than white. I also cut the oil in half. I added coconut and raisins and baked it at 375. Fabulous.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Next time I think I'll only use 1/3 cup of sugar instead of 1. I also replaced 1/2 cup of flour with 1/2 cup of almond flour for added protein and fiber - as well as 1/2 cup of greek yogurt and only used 6 tablespoons of vegetable oil instead of 1/2 cup. The greek yogurt made it very moist! plus the added protein!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’ve been making this recipe for many years, first published in Sunset magazine. Bakesale Betty’s is an institution here in Oakland, and you’ll never find a better banana bread recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: How would one adjust ingredients in this recipe to use sourdough discard in place of flour and liquid?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I would add 120 grams of discard, skip the water, and reduce the flour to 130 grams. I would add the discard to the egg-oil mixture with the bananas.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I don’t know how that would work-no yeast in the recipe and only 1teaspoon of baking soda as leavening. 
Check King Arthur flour site for using sour dough discard in baking.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Seems like a lot of sweetener in the batter. Thoughts from those who have already baked?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: We baked this with 1/2 cup sugar instead of 1 full cup and it was delicious and still sweet. I would even consider reducing it to 1/3 sugar.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I'm unclear why you butter the loaf pan if lining with paper?  Can someone explain?   Thanks!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: the paper forms a sling, so the short ends of the pan are not covered with paper.  when baking like this, i only butter the ends of the pan, but it can't hurt to butter the whole pan, in case some batter leaks under the paper, for example, and sticks or burns...

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Would coconut oil work for the vegetable oil?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: it should work, but coconut oil is high in saturated fats. you're better off, IMO, with a neutral vegetable oil like canola or avocado.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made Banana Bread for the first time this weekend;it worked.  I substituted Chia Seeds for one egg, 4-5 mashed old banana's, 1 tsp pure vanilla, 1/2 cup agave, 1/2 teaspoon baking powder and 2 tsp baking soda, 1/2 cup canola, 2 Tb Cinnamon, 1 tsp nutmeg, 2 Tbs cashew milk, raisins soaked in unsweetened cranberry juice and minced in processor, cup stewed old pears; and 2 regular cups flour. Nut snack mix. Mix the wet 3- 5 min;add the dry and mix, stir in nut mix. Bake 45 min 350. Two loaves

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This break was amazing; definitely the best banana bread I've ever had or made.  But I thought the topping was overly-sweet-- I had so much sugar on top, half of it fell off, and it was STILL too much.  I'd rather just top it off with barely sweetened whipped cream, or, as we had tonite, some delicious vanilla ice cream!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made with a GF flour baking substitute and it was perfect!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Started at 45 minutes at 320

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Loved it! It’s more of a coffee cake than a bread, but very tasty. Would add walnuts next time.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Because I had it in my pantry and the theme seems to be WHY NOT.....I substituted granulated monk fruit sugar for the white sugar and it turned out surprisingly well! It was not too sweet and did not have an "off" flavor at all.  I did, however still use the brown sugar in the topping....but if I did it again, I think I would omit. One thing, I only had two bananas and definitely wish I had a third. All in all, a win.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: We enjoyed it and I would make it again. Second time around I would use 1/2 c of sugar at most as we thought it overly sweet, almost a spice cake with bananas.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Very nice banana bread, but not the best I've ever made.  Took about an hour and twenty minutes to bake (but that might have been my oven).  The bread is moist and comforting, and the cinnamon sugar crust delicious -- but overall a bit too sweet, probably due to the honey and the chocolate chips I added.  I would make again but would cut the sugar and skip the chocolate chips.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: It’s OK but not amazing. Doesn’t need as much sugar as is called for, could cut in half and be OK.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this today and it was delicious! The topping makes a huge difference. I only had 2 brown bananas, so I added 1/4 cup of yogurt. Also added 1/2 teaspoon of ground ginger for more flavor. Loved it!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If anyone has a recipe for banana bread that’s got a soft crumb and a crunchy top crust, please let me know. Thanks.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Um... this one!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Could anyone tell me if I could substitute fresh mashed sweet (drained) pineapple for the banana?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Maybe,if you pureed the pineapple, otherwise it might not be enough moisture?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is truly great. I also added just a handful of chocolate and cut the sugar just a bit. A true delight and a great use of the rapidly ripening bananas. Thank you!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Awesome recipe- house smelled so good could hardly wait for it to cool!  Replacing all other banana bread recipes with this one!  A winner.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Followed directions and it was the best banana bread I’ve ever made. Moist and with a caramelized taste. Highly recommend.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Chocolate chips negate the subtle unique flavor of any banana bread and this one for sure

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is a wonderful banana bread/coffee cake recipe. I will probably reduce the sugar to 1/2 c next time. On someone else’s suggestion, I reduced oil to 1/3 c. I also didn’t read instructions well enough and just added everything to the flour mixture without mixing separately first. It turned out just fine, so I saved an extra bowl.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Why show a picture in which the bread is burned? Banana bread made with white flour does not look like this when baked at the correct temperature for an appropriate time.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was a lovely cake and baked up just like the photo. However, I didn’t get as much flavor from the banana coming through as much as I did the cinnamon. Regardless it was great.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My granddaughter has made this twice.. it was delicious! She used honey and agave.. she cooks healthy. I’m not a banana bread lover but the cinnamon crunch was wonderful!! Even I like it!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: And if your only available bananas aren't ripe: mash them on a large plate and leave under a warm light for about an hour. They will brown up but the sugars are developing so don't worry about the way it looks. Mix  it up midway through.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was so delightful! I highly recommend it. Next time I’m adding chocolate chips.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I use 1 tsp ground cardamom along with cinnamon  and 1/4 cup raisins instead of honey, use 1/3 cup sugar. Comes out delicious

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Definitely a cake. I used a wildflower honey with a strong dark taste, which came through in the finished product, making it more complex and burnt-caramelly, in the best way. Glad I used only 1/2c sugar in the recipe; it was still plenty sweet. The crunchy topping made it darn near addictive. Next time I’ll try to get more topping along the edges of the pan. The center collapsed into itself under the weight of the melted topping, making it hard to assess doneness, but it was great anyway.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious, moist and the cinnamon topping may have been the best part.  A keeper!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Followed the recipe and it was the best version of banana bread I've ever had. Next time I may ad some cocoa powder to add another flavor profile...or not. The crust made it special and would work with other quick sweet breads.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this today - easy and turned out perfect! Winner ;)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: 5/2020 Had scant 1 cup of banana, added vanilla, chopped pecans and handful of mini choc chips.  Not sure the crust adds much and it's messy.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Can I make this recipe as muffins instead of bread?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was so easy to mix up! I didn’t change anything called for (well, actually a tiny bit of cloves!), but I did add pépites and I’m excited to have their little salty crunch in this sweet bread.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Perfectly delicious. Two modifications: I used mostly Whole wheat flour, and I used 1/4 c butter and 1/4 olive oil.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I did not like this at all. Too dry, lackluster texture and the topping added very little.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Ooo-eee, this is so good! You may think as you're sprinkling the topping on: "Is this too much sugar?" No, it is not.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Definitely a cake! Nice texture and sweet crunchy top. It took over an hour - I may have added more water than called for. Add some very lightly sweetened whip cream and it would be a nice dessert.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have not tried this. I have a wonderful recipe for banana bread that my daughter and son-in-law rave about. I use whole wheat flour only for that one. Will this one do well with whole wheat flour?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Used 1/2 c white sugar, still plenty sweet. Added raisins soaked in bourbon as suggested in another comment, delish. Made in mini bread pans, ready in 30 min.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Very, very sweet. Also, did not bake through even though I kept it in the oven for more than an hour. It tasted good, but I probably would not make this again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I liked the fact that I could cut out a stick of butter from my usual banana bread mixture. The only negative was the topping - it splattered all over the bottom of my oven when I moved pan off the rack and all over the plate where I sliced it. Could probably leave topping off or push it into the batter.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Way too much sugar. Otherwise a standard banana bread with cinnamon

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I reduced the sugar to 1/2 cup, on a friend’s recommendation. Definitely sweet enough!! The top is delicious but very messy. Also, the crust loses some of its crispness upon storing so best to bake and eat most of it the same day. I wish they had included better instructions for maintaining crispness in storing.

author: Julia Moskin
