commentBody: I followed the recipe as written but added a handful of dark chocolate chips.  It was a huge hit.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I found this recipe to be much too sweet, without the cinnamon topping which added even more sweetness.  It's more cake than bread, though it is moist.  I prefer a less sweet version.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this and cut the sugar in 1/2 as had been suggested.   It was fab and perfectly sweet... have passed this recipe on to m y friends who are raving... my new favorite banana bread recipe

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I cut out 25% of the sugar and oil and added 3/4 cup chocolate chips.  I skipped the sugar topping.  I'll make it next time.  Ate two slices while it is still cooling.  New favorite recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Took the advice of others...cut sugar to 1/3 cup and oil to 1/3 cup. Added 1 teaspoon vanilla (cause I'm a sucker for anything with that vanilla bourbon-y taste). Honestly? I'd say skip the topping. About 5 minutes out of the oven and it was crispy and coming apart from the rest of the loaf. Ended up taking almost all of it off. Not worth it IMO. Tastes good though!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this with only 1/3 cup sugar, 2 T. honey and rum instead of warm water.  That gave it plenty of sweetness.  The rum is essential.  It was absolutely delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made it today, and added about 2/3 cup of chocolate chips. They seemed to sink to the bottom, but it was still good. I had good results with the crunchy, sugary top. I thought it made the recipe - different from other banana breads.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I cut sugar by 50%; soaked golden raisins in amaretto, and used that liquid instead of water, as per a few suggestions. I used dark muscovado sugar  instead of light brown for the topping, no granulated, just cinnamon. It needed over an hour, maybe 15 min, but no signs of burning, topping excellent, maybe better because of less sugar in the bread itself.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious, but I had a weird issue: doubled recipe to make two loaves, same batter, same topping, same oven, same timing -- but I used one dark metal loaf pan and one glass pyrex loaf pan, same sizes, and reversed their oven positions midway through the bake.  The  topping on the pyrex loaf came out as a nice crunchy lid on the loaf, but in the metal pan the topping sank to the bottom of the loaf and became a wet streak of spice.  But they were both swooned over by everyone who tried them.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Definitely too sweet, even with reduced sugar to 1/3 cup as several commenters suggested, and I was low on honey. I can’t imagine it with the amount as written in the recipe. If I make it again, I’ll reduce the sugar further.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Incredibly delicious as prescribed by Julia. A drug for the palate. : )

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I can totally understand why people wait in line for this incredible “ banana bread”.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I just pulled my second loaf out of the oven--the first from yesterday is gone. Reduced sugar to 2/3 c., then to 1/2 c. today. The honey and sugar topping add more than enough sweetness to compensate. What I love is the uniform moistness, rich flavor, and crunchy topping. I usually struggle with banana bread drying around the edges before the center has fully cooked. Not a problem here. Thank you for sharing!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Way too sweet; this one won’t be revisited

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
When I read the water addition, I thought “Gee that’s
odd...”. The bread was ok..not lucious, not dry, just ok.  I did enjoy the topping.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is amazing banana bread - one of the best recipes I've tried! A few notes: I cut the sugar to 1/2 cup and it's plenty sweet. Honestly, you could probably reduce it to 1/3 cup and you wouldn't miss it. (But don't skip the topping) I made these into muffins - baked at the same temp for 25 minutes, and they were perfect.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I loved this banana bread. It is very different from the banana bread I am accustomed to, but I think I like this better. I will definitely make it again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I also found this too sweet.  Not worth the effort

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I am making this for the second time in 5 days per my husband's request - says the best one yet! I added one cup of chopped walnuts. I baked for 60 mins at 350 degrees.  I used agave syrup that I got in Vietnam instead of honey. Bread was moist and delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is the best banana bread. So easy to make. I doubled the recipe and made in 2 loaf pans. Everyone loved it. Even my son, who hates bananas, actually ate and enjoyed it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I cooked this exactly according bro the recipe because I’m not very experienced in baking breads and cakes. I read al the notes and they sounded interesting. My bread turned out great but next time I want to experiment. HAS ANYONE ACTUALLY SUBSTITUTED BOURBON FOR THE WARM WATER AND ADDRD WALNUTS?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Yes I did! My measurements below:
210g flour
1 tsp cinnamon + 1 tsp baking soda + 1/2 tsp kosher salt
200g sugar
2 eggs
120mL canola oil + 60mL honey
~340g bananas mashed (3-4 bananas)
60 mL of bourbon whiskey
75g toasted pecans
Baked for 1 hour. Turned out great - the alcohol seemed to help dial down the sweetness because I didn't find this to be too sweet at all.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’d like to mention that any quick bread batter can also be made into pancakes. Banana cinnamon pancakes? Yes, please!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe is very forgiving. Due to COVID I didn't have all the ingredients exactly as listed. I used brown sugar in place of some of the white sugar. Skipped the demerara sugar on the topping. Used only 1 banana instead of 2. Used evoo in place of veg oil. It was perfect!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Banana Bread Pudding modification 

To existing batter recipe:

Add an additional 3/4 cup warm water (so you have 1 cup total), and resulting batter is soup-like 

Cut or tear apart 2 brioche hamburger buns (stale preferred) and  place evenly in an 8x8 baking pan, then pour batter bread.

Optional: Double sugar topping recipe to sprinkle on top since you have 2x the surface area

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Wonderful recipe!!! Used less sugar (1/2 cup)instead of 1 cup. Other than that it was spot on.  Can't wait to make it again

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this with coconut oil (not warmed) instead of veggie oil and only 1/2 cup of sugar

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this - delicious. The topping didn't stick to the bread. When you cut a slice the topping fell right off. You can layer it on the slice but is there a solution to this??

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is so good we are lucky it is not outlawed as addictive. I agree that slightly less oil might make it even better.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Just wondering - I want to make this for relatives on the east and west coast . Can it be doubled?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Reduced sugar from 1 cup to 3/4 cup
Added raisins  and walnuts.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So I just made the Banana Bread and followed the directions however the topping was cracked like brittle and looking separated from the top.  I need some Help!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Me, too, exactly.  I'm thinking cover it toward the end of baking?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I found pressing the topping down into batter a tiny bit helped; didn't fall off and still got crunch!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This really is very good banana bread.   The streusel or whatever you call it makes the difference.

This recipe also makes exactly a dozen muffins.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Replaced ¼ cup of warm water with coffee, cut sugar in half, accidentally used ½ cup of whole wheat flour instead of all white flour and added cocoa power to topping.  Delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Less sugar in bread. Make topping a crumble ?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I made this exactly according to the recipe a couple of weeks ago. It was fantastic. I read on notes that someone suggested replacing bourbon instead of water and adding toasted pecans. 
I cooked it again and used BIRD DOG BOURBON instead of water and added chopped walnuts. IT WAS FANTASTIC ! Much better than the original which I loved. TRY IT YOU WON’T BE SORRY.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is DIVINE. I started checking for done-ness at 45 minutes and it took the whole hour. This recipe was so easy to whip up and I’ll definitely be making it again!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used 1/3 cup sugar in the batter and it was plenty sweet. Only needed about half of the topping too. Subbed canola for grapeseed. Was done at 50m so perhaps check a little earlier.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: We loved this. I did add chocolate chips because we love those even more and it was excellent.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Absolutely Delicious. Will add a pinch more cinnamon the next time.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
We baked this with 1/2 cup sugar instead of 1 full cup and it was delicious and still sweet. I would even consider reducing it to 1/3 sugar.
32 This is helpful

Seems like replacing the water with rum or bourbon would be a great way to introduce a little more flavor into the recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Used half of the sugar and it still came out pretty sweet.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Reduced oil to 1/3 cup and added 1 tbsp butter to topping.
Bananas when mashed came to a little over 1 cup.
Might try adding a little baking soda in addition to the baking powder to the recipe to bring out banana flavor next time? 
Pressed topping a little into batter.
Moist and delicious.  Topping did not fall off.
It has been requested that I only make this banana bread from now on
which is something because I make a lot of banana bread.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Read the comments and took some liberties as suggested by the other commenters. I cut the sugar by half, added some raisins that I had soaked in rum for half an hour, some toasted walnuts, and just a few dark chocolate chunks. I also took out half of the warm water and replaced it with the rum that I was using to soak the raisins. I also used a streusel topping that another commenter so graciously left a recipe for. It was so delicious! I might not cut out as much sugar next time, though.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: It tastes good, but I agree with other users, definitely don’t need to use as much sugar as the recipe calls for. It tastes much more like honey cake than banana bread as is. I added blueberries that were a little past their prime and they were an okay addition.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Others have noted that the topping does not stick to the bread. I recommend wrapping it and letting it sit for a day before eating. It becomes more moist, the flavors deepen and the topping stays put. I've made this several times and I love it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used dark brown sugar and only put half of it on and it stuck fine and was definitely enough crunchy cinnamon sweetness.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If I wanted to use a 4-compartment mini loaf pan, would it be the same cook time?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Thanks to Laura's comment, I halved the sugar, but kept everything else the same, as we love it moist. My family raved over this banana bread!! It's so rich and delicious, especially with the cinnamon sugar topping. I used dark brown sugar because that's what I had and it gave it a stronger - but no less yummy - flavour. Will definitely be making this on the regular!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious! I increased flour to 2 cups (as 1.5 cu. left my bread uncooked in middle). Added a pinch of cardamom, some chocolate chips and chopped nuts. Also have sub. zucchini for bananas (didn't add the water in this case) and olive oil (when I didn't have veg. oil).

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This banana loaf was delicious......I added some vanilla and a bunch of chopped pecans.  Next time I will also add chocolate chips.  Awesome!!!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious!!! I added a bunch of chopped pecans and vanilla.  Next time I will add more cinnamon.  I would like to add chocolate chips too! Yum!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Add 1 cup mini choc chips (or chopped Giradelli choc chips?)

Fold into batter after it is mixed and ready

Add 1/3 cup cocoa powder 

Reduce flour by 1/4 cup (to 1 1 /4 cup, less 34 grams)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: As others have noted, I reduced the sugar in the batter and it still tasted great, but I found the topping to be unnecessary. Too much sugar, it overpowered the cinnamon, and is just a dry sugar bomb on top of the bread that breaks and makes a mess when you slice it. An okay recipe but I'm going to try some other banana breads as well.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Topping amounts can easuly be cut in half. It is simply too much sugar.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Anyone ever try booze instead of the warm water? Seems like it might be uplifting.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was a delightful discovery.  Cinnamon adds a ton of depth and flavor.  I increased the mashed bananas a bit but otherwise made as written.  Would like to repeat with the less-sugar suggestions from other bakers.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I added 1/2 cup of walnuts and reduced the sugar to 125 g. Great result! Still sweet but not overly so, the banana really shined through!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Cut sugar to 1/3 C, used agave syrup for the honey, added 1cup walnuts, and substituted and doubled garam masala for the cinnamon.  Great!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Oh, amateur hour not reading the notes first. This took twice the time to bake than directed for me. It's delicious, but a total sugar bomb. It could probably be just as good if not better with 1/3 the amount of sugar.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Instead of 1/4 cup water, use 1/4 cup coffee.
1/2 cup sugar instead of 1 cup.
Add chocolate chips!
Everybody raved.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I'll try nutmeg for xmas taste.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Can I use a slightly smaller pan that is 8 1/2 x 4 1/2 instead of 9x5.   What do I need to do differently?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: shouldn't have to do anything differently.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe is excellent but I highly recommend you remove 'wax paper' from the recipe instructions, since wax paper burns when in the oven. I learned the hard way and just barely saved my banana bread! Otherwise, delicious and would definitely make again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used 1/3 cup sugar as others suggested.  The ingredients for the topping is way too much.  I don’t think it’s necessary.  Because of the low temperature, it took the full 75 minutes for my bread to cook.  Otherwise an excellent, moist bread.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I don't know what went wrong! The entire middle of the bread was completely unbaked after an hour in the oven.  It looked like all the honey went straight to the bottom as it was super sticky. The top part seemed to be unscathed and what was actually baked tasted delicious! It doesn't seem that others have reported this issue so perhaps I will give it another go..

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: sounds to me like you have an issue with your oven.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was a very sweet banana bread. I loved the cinnamon flavor, but next time, I will add less of the sugar mixture on top. The top was a bit too sugary for me, but otherwise was delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Too sweet for me. Would add less sugar. Also the too was burning and inside still not cooked. Used toaster oven. Still yummy
Tho.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: 160g sugar

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious! I replaced the water with honey whiskey and added pecans.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So good but super sugary. I love the topping but I might use half next time.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is a delicious banana bread! We’ve have trouble with getting other recipes to cook through; I cooked this one for 65 min and it could have gone in for 5 min more but it’s fine. I will try it with less sugar and oil next time, and perhaps a pinch of cardamom.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was excellent. I added a 1/4 cup coconut-milk yogurt, subbed a 1/4 of olive oil in for the fat, a teaspoon of ginger, a bit of cracked black pepper and used 6 tablespoons of Just Egg to keep it clean(ish). This one’s a mainstay.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was insanely sweet. I almost threw it out, would definitely add way less sugar especially if you're using honey, too!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I see lots of people needed to cook it longer, for me it was done in 45 minutes.  Wondering if anyone else had the topping crack and fall off when they eat it?  Absolutely delicious but I want every bite of that topping.  How to get it to stay on?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I love this recipe! That said I changed a couple of things and it turned out perfectly. Firstly, I wanted it to be a little less sweet so I put in 1/2 a cup of sugar. Secondly, I have been experimenting with other types of flower (gluten free in particular but really for added nutritional value for my kiddos). So, I replaced one cup of flower with one cup of buckwheat flower. I was shy so I left in 1/2 cup of white flower a d it turned out so so scrumptious. Next I’ll try using all buckwheat

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This bread was awesome. I subbed browned butter for oil and added vanilla nits and doubled the cinnamon. I also added a banana and cut a 1/4 of the sugar. It’s fun to busk! Can’t wait to make more.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Used 2/3 c sugar. Added 1 c walnuts. Delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Really great! Made with GF flour, coconut oil and used 1/4 cup of sugar. Made less topping too.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this with 1/2 cup sugar...came out great!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Have used this recipe many many times, my go-to for banana bread.  Sometimes I omit the topping, sometimes I add chocolate chips.  It’s all good!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Incredibly easy and quick to bake, but the payoff is nothing special... just a nice banana bread.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious. Very different taste than the usual banana breads I’ve made, maybe it was the rum I added instead of water. Recommended. I’ll make this again with add ins.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This is definitely more cake than bread, so the title is a misnomer. The instructions referring to it as cake are more accurate. 
The topping could have been a bit better as more of a crumble but this tasted very good as is.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Make it vegan. Enjoy cruelty free cooking and eating!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Can you make this without the sugar????

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I love how easy this is. The recommendations for add ins, variations here are great. I’ll try some of these next time (& there will be many next times!)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used 3/4c gluten free all purpose flour and 3/4 cup buckwheat flour; cut the sugar down to 1/3 cup and used the streusel topping recommended in the comments. These are perfectly sweet and moist and rose nicely, but overall I think could be more interesting...next time I’ll add berries or nuts or both. :)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Just chiming in to agree with 1/3 cup sugar, 1/3 cup oil, and no granulated sugar for the topping. So insanely good, and that's without chocolate chips as suggested! I'll try that another time. Also, mine only took about 45-50 minutes to be cooked through so keep an eye on it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: A little less sugar in the recipe itself. Increase leavening.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The best part about making this recipe is that my house smelled heavenly. The "bread" itself is very dense and - as others have noted - the topping just falls off. I wonder if something (butter?) was missing from the ingredients? The taste is ok - just ok - and that's after I was very generous with the cinnamon, and added allspice and nutmeg, too. I followed the advice to reduce the sugar and am glad I did. This recipe requires a lot of tweaking, don't think I'll make it again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Yes, reduced the sugar.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Added a cup of toasted walnuts plus 1/2 tsp of cardomom and baked approx 65-70 mins.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Use rum instead of water
Add 1 c of nuts

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I followed others’ advice to use 1/3 c sugar, and I also used coconut oil rather than vegetable oil. Amazing!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I was underwhelmed by this recipe. Although the banana taste did come through and I added vanilla essence, the result was bland. I’d probably go with yogurt or milk instead of water.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Made 2 loaves at the same time. Neither had the topping, and both took advantage of other comments and halved the sugar and lessened the oil.  (I also increased the cinnamon to make up for the lack of topping). Also added some cardamom. 
The 2nd loaf I added a little matcha tea powder, chocolate chips and a smattering of pistachio nuts and topped that one with cardamom cookie crumbs. Both were really really good.  A bit like a banana spice cake. Good flavor, easy to make. Will def do it again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Wish I had read the comments before I made this recipe. My misgivings about the sugar amount (wow) and the oil would have led me to make some reductions. I used more sugar in this one recipe than we use in a month. My habit is to follow a new recipe as instructed the first time and then adjust to our taste. Before I try another recipe from this source I’ll read the comments first! And I will not be putting this much sugar and oil into anything.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: On first taste I thought it was too dense, too wet, and too sweet. That was six bakes ago. It’s no surprise this bread-cake sells out. It’s fantastic. People now request it from me. I didn’t like using Demerara as much as light brown. Confess… I add chopped up chocolate chips, carefully so they don’t sink to the bottom (made that mistake the first time). Does take a bit longer to bake than you may expect.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I like this recipe but the cake was dry. Perhaps add a few tablespoons of sour cream? Or use cake flour? I also cut the sugar in half because I don't like overly sweet banana bread.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The cinnamon sugar topping on this banana bread is bomb but I don’t love the banana bread itself. I followed the suggestions of some other peeps and used 1/3 cup sugar and 1/3 cup oil. Found the loaf moist and sweet with this ratio but the flavor was underwhelming.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I also made this as written except ~1/3 c/75g sugar and with the sweet topping it's perfect! So delicious, highly recommend.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Probably the best banana bread I’ve ever made. I took everyone’s advice and lowered the sugar to 1/3 c. Hard to believe a recipe that calls for a full cup would be delicious and sweet with 1/3. (Also, there’s honey and sugar crumble on top— what’s with all the sugar!) I also used whole wheat flour instead of all purpose, and added a little extra banana to make up for any moisture lost on this change. Perfect.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: great recipe, will definitely make this again. recommend adding in 1/2 cup or more of chocolate chips. Baked for 50 minutes and it seemed done with toothpick test but probably could have used 3-5 additional minutes in the oven.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used 1/2 cup of sugar as others have suggested and it was plenty sweet (ripe bananas are very sweet on their own). I only had one egg, so substituted 1/4 cup unsweetened applesauce   1/4 teaspoon baking powder for the second egg. Instead of a loaf, I made 12 muffins. Gave away half and kept the rest. Delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this recipe based on other's suggestions to reduce the sugar, which is definitely the right call. The banana bread recipe is good, but I wish it had a stronger banana taste. I'd probably recommend increasing to 3-4 bananas vs the 2 that I used. Also, the cinnamon sugar topping just didn't stick for me as it was too crumbly. It needs some type of binding agent to stick to the rest of the bread. Overall good, but not my favorite banana bread recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Tossed in some walnuts & used dark rum instead of water.   Used agave syrup by mistake (oops) instead of honey.   Great!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Used 1/2 c whole wheat flour and 1 c all purpose
Used 1/3c very light olive oil
Used 1/3c granulated sugar
Added walnuts and 1 tsp vanilla

Used topping made of 1T sugar, 2T light brown sugar and 1t cinnamon

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So good.  So very good.  Finally a recipe that doesn't result in leaden bread!  So very good.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Very good but I read many comments first. Changes: Reduced sugar to 1/2c  and agree probably could reduce to 1/3. I cannot imagine using a full cup of sugar in this. I used 1/3 cup of olive oil, likely bump to 1/2 next time, 1/4 cup of gold rum in place of water, added 1 teaspoon almond extract, 1/4 c maple syrup instead of honey, and stirred in 1/2 c dark chocolate chunks at the end. Topped with cinnamon sugar.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was wonderful! I did small loaves, one as written and one with about a cup of shredded carrot mixed in. They were both incredible, but we preferred the one with carrot. The crunchy topping was a revelation. I think I'm going to do it for lots of loaves and muffins in future. The sling is a must.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Have been using this recipe for years as 
my go-to banana bread recipe, know it by heart.  The fried chicken sandwich with slaw at Bakesale Betty's is to die for, BTW).

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Just a note- Do not use wax paper in the oven- wax paper is not interchangeable with parchment paper.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
The "cinnamon crunch" is a misnomer. Nothing about that topping develops a crunch, as baking at 350 is not hot enough to melt the sugars. You end up with granulated white & brown sugar on top that mostly just falls off and makes a mess.  
Also agree 100% with reducing the sugar by half and the oil a bit.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Glad they said check after 50 minutes, mine was completely done. The fragrance!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Anyone try this with skins on?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I don't like my banana bread super sweet or oily, so i used 1/3 cup buttermilk, 2 T walnut oil and 1/3 C sugar and used only 1/4 C demerara sugar on the top. Plus sprinkled whole pecans (to get toasted but easily removed for nut haters) on the top before putting on the sugar. I kept the honey at 1/4 C and it was plenty sweet. Also subbed half the flour with whole wheat and added vanilla.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made it as written and loved it. It stayed moist and fresh on the counter for the several days it took us to eat it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The crunchy sugar topping was a nice added texture! Definitely worth it to use some turbinado sugar. I chose to forego the nuts and chocolate that I would usually mix in so it was easier to share with friends with dietary restrictions.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made on June 11 2022.  Used 1/3 oil, 1/3 white sugar and scant 1/4 cup honey.  Otherwise, followed recipe.  Needed 1 hour of baking time.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used parchment paper and did NOT butter the pan. The loaf lifted out just fine with topping intact and no need to deal with a greasy pan. I, too, followed Laura by using 1/3 cup sugar and 1/2 cup oil. I might try maple syrup next time as “D” did.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Can you make these as muffins?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Replaced oil with yogurt. Cut back on sugar (as per others suggestions). Also added chopped nuts (and some chocolate chips). As I didn’t do the topping (didn’t need it really) I doubled the cinnamon in the batter and added cardamom. Incredible. Best banana bread I made. Great banana taste and not too sweet. Will go in regular rotation.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Another vote for using lots less sugar (1/3 c), even though my bananas were only just ripe (and thus, less sweet). Used brown sugar for the topping and that made it sweet without being candy-sweet. Also used a mix of whole wheat flour, almond flour, and regular flour.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is hands-down the best banana bread I've ever had! It was so good that after making one loaf and then devouring it with the help of my family, I made another loaf the same day. It is moist and just the right amount of sweet. The cinnamon crunch really adds.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Nice. I cut back on sugar to 1/2c , used part maple sugar and mixed evoked and butter 
Also mixed a little sugar with pecans and cinnamon for topping instead

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Less sugar, more chocolate chips/nuts (if you want it to be dairy)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Kind of meh to me. The banana flavor was overwhelmed by honey and so dark.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Has anyone frozen it and if so does it hold up well?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was a hit! On my first try, the bananas were lost. This time, before adding the bananas, I mashed them and lightly salted them, then let them sit for a little bit. Perfect.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Doubled the recipe and divided the batter among eight small foil loaf pans that were lined with parchment paper for easy removal. They were done cooking in about 55 minutes. They turned out good enough to wrap with wax paper and tuck into small gift bags as treats for friends. Although it uses quite a bit of banana, the banana taste is not that prominent. This creates a fine-grained, moist, dark brown cake with a fragile sugar crust on the top of the loaf.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Easy and delicious. Great with coffee. Followed the recipe exactly and it baked up fluffy and had great texture. Don't over mix!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used the strudel topping note from Kyle- perfect!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: When I called this a banana bread, my husband corrected me insisting it is a cinnamon cake. He may be right. It is tasty.  However, I followed one of the earlier notes and used bourbon in place of the water and the alcohol did not cook out in the baking. If I make this again, l will use water or buttermilk, definitely not bourbon!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Hm. Maybe I didn't use enough brownies. Found these cloying sweet but surprisingly bland.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Best banana bread I've had or made. Just amazing flavor, moist, love the crunchy topping. Definitely is better to give it a day in the refrigerator before eating but it's still really good after cooling. Confession, hate when people review something that isn't the original recipe so I'm calling shame on me. Added 1tbls of dark rum, 1/4tsp of vanilla and about 1/4-1/3 cup dark chocolate chips. Forgive me Ms. Moskin. Beauty of banana bread.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious. We couldn’t wait for it to be completely cooled to try it. Used white whole wheat flour, added 1/2 cup chopped walnuts.  Took 65 min and was slightly burnt in some edges, will need to cover w foil part of the time in next batch. Very sweet, will try using less sugar.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made two loaves.  First one followed the recipe exactly, then added 1 tsp vanilla & still felt it needed something more.  I didn’t have more than 3 ripe bananas left, so added the vanilla plus a half teaspoon more of cinnamon and pecans to the batter of the second loaf and it was fantastic!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Excellent recipe , I did it almost exactly the way it was written but I reduced the sugar to 1/2 cup and it was sweet enough for me. So moist and delicious

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’m so full right now because I can’t stop eating this. My mods: WW flour, quarter the sugar and honey, add 1/2 cup chopped walnuts

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I added a large spoonful of almond butter, along with some of the almond oil. Then put pepitas and walnuts inside the batter, pecans on top with the cinnamon crunch. All of the adds elevated the flavor, and the pecans on the top added extra crunch and overall aesthic look.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Added Rum instead of water as suggested, warmed it up a little first - can confirm it worked! I also used a streusel topping using allspice instead of nutmeg and it turned out great. Thanks NYTimes cooking commenters :)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Happy I ready to cut the sugar and oil ammount. Loved it, best banana bread and I’m picky! I cut medallion bananas and put three lines of three bananas on top. I forfeited the brown sugar entirely.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Disagree about this being too sweet. It’s a cake, not a bread. It’s so good! No changes.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Split oil by half, and half butter
Add almond butter or pistachio butter, 3 tbl spoon
Add in pecan, pumpkin seed, or walnut

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I usually bake banana bread about once a year. I’ve baked this four times in the past month. Definitely my new go-to! Yummy and so simple to pull together. I’ve tried doing more of a streusel topping as some commenters have mentioned (adding flour and butter to the sugar topping) and do probably prefer it. But great as written, too.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used coconut oil since I try to avoid cooking with seed oils and it turned out perfect. I also used a butter knife to swirl some of the topping into the bread, which created lovely caramelized ribbons.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Cut the white sugar and honey back a bit due to running short (94 g sugar and a scant 1/8 c honey), and upped the banana (probably 1 1/4 c) but it was a hit regardless of the alterations. It was breakfast, snacks, and dessert until it was gone.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’ve started making these with any extra bananas I have and they are a huge hit at work. Incredibly moist, not too sweet, and the yummy crunchy topping is the best part. This recipe makes me glad when I “accidentally” buy too many bananas. Hands down, the best banana bread I’ve ever made (or eaten).

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this banana bread according to the recipe and with the subtractions suggested by the notes from Laura.  1/3 cup of sugar and 1/3 cup of oil.  A cup of sugar would be way too sweet for me considering the topping has sugar in it and the bananas add a natural sweetness to the batter.  I also changed the type of flour to 3/4 white unbleached and 3/4 spelt flour.  It probably didn't rise as much as the original recipe but the spelt adds some fiber.  Turned out very well and plenty sweet.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I made this to the recipe except I reduced the sugar to 1/2 cup and did not add the honey.
I also substituted half spelt flour to make it a little healthier.  It didn't rise as much which was expected since spelt flour is somewhat heavier than white flour.  I think 1 cup of sugar is too much since the recipe already has a topping that has sugar as well. 
The bread turned out well flavored and moist.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
My husband loves banana bread. He loves walnuts in it, but I’m not a fan, so I opted for this cinnamon crunch recipe to add the extra texture. 

Instead of adding the 1/2 cup of vegetable oil, I did a stick of unsalted butter and 1/4 cup of canola oil. It was perfect! 

This recipe is a little on the sweeter side, but I love how easy it is to adjust to your liking.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious! I looked at the other notes and decreased the granulated sugar by half but could have decreased further. I also added a splash of vanilla as others suggested.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Making this for the 3rd time in 2 weeks! Awesome bread. Reduced the sugar in 2nd batch to 1/2 cup. My bananas had been frozen and were pretty wet so skipped the water. Added chopped pecans.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So yummy! The crust on top is fantastic and adds some excitement to the recipe. We added some chocolate chunks and loved it. I think this will be my new go-to banana bread recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is the best banana bread I have ever made. Highly recommend.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have made this probably 10 times, it is my go to banana bread recipe now. I use a little more banana and make sure it stays a little chunky for more banana flavor and use slightly less ripe bananas. I have also halved the sugar every time and it is plenty sweet. Sometimes I up the cinnamon and add other spices. Also mix wheat flour with the white and add walnuts. I am trying it with molasses this time rather than honey.  I skip the topping but that is just my personal preference.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made a topping that turned out crunchy, covered the loaf well, and didn’t fall off: 1/8 C sugar in the raw, 1/2 tsp salt, 1 Tbs flour, 1 Tbs white sugar, 2 Tbs butter, 1 tsp cinnamon.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Usually, I am not a fan of banana bread. However, baking eliminates the stress of life in my being! This recipe is amazing! The crunchy top made it fabulous!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Lovely recipe though I did tweak it a bit. First, make sure you measure your banana mash because I’ve lost track of what is truly a medium banana and it took me four bananas to get 1 cup. I also cut the sugar down to 140 grams, omitted the cinnamon sugar topping, and added 1 teaspoon vanilla, 1/4 teaspoon garam masala, and 80 grams chopped walnuts. Yum!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Next time bake for 48 minutes

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Very good. I was worried that it’d be too sweet, but it’s not. Easy too. Will make again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is much sweeter than I’d like and will def reduce the sugar if I make it again. Very cinnamon forward and the banana seems like it’s there just for moisture: I detected little banana flavor under all the cinnamon. Tender texture is nice though…

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Have made this several times (with reduced sugar & oil) and it’s always really great. One issue is that the top of the cake cracks open. Any ideas on what I’m doing wrong?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Terrible. I was suspicious as I was making it. 

The amount of sugar/honey was double my usual Mark Bittman recipe, and the batter was way too wet. I added vanilla and walnuts, which was a good call because this tasted dull even with those additions. 

The biggest issue is that this was so wet that it didn’t cook through. It has the texture sort of bread pudding. The honey flavor is non existent. Not even good enough to put on the work snack table.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This DID NOT WORK for me at all. It is terrible. Way too wet and too sweet. Just yucky.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I reduced sugar by half, substituted coffee for water, and added a TLBS of cocoa powder to the topping. Love this banana bread. It's light and not too sweet the way I make it!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Perfect.  Entirely perfect!!
I added a bunch of walnut pieces and it was great. 

Perhaps a tad less sugar in the bread as there is so much in the topping.  Maybe I'll leave the time if next time. 

I will DEFINITELY be making this again

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I had great banana flavor, and the cinnamon crunch on top was a nice twist. That said, it turned out really oily. Next time I’ll cut the oil in half. Definitely making again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’m a frequent banana bread baker, and this is my new go-to recipe. It’s the best banana bread I’ve ever made and all my colleagues agree. The brown sugar topping is just *chef’s kiss*

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was great! On the sweeter side, but a real crowd pleaser, and mostly pantry ingredients.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Reduce sugar

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Easy, subbed maple syrup for honey.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: It really doesn't need the topping. Otherwise really good.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Followed others' advice and reduced sugar by half. Did 50/50 water+brandy and last dregs of vanilla extract. Also ended up using only 1/2 topping, which was plenty to create a nice, subtle crackly crust. 

One more thing: I don't own a loaf pan, so used an 8x8 pyrex since it's about the same volume. Baked for same amount of time. Came out great. Excellent springy tender texture.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I've made this at least 3 times to bring to dinner parties and it's always a huge hit! Not a crump left. I wouldn't change a thing. This recipe is perfect exactly as it is.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I added raisins and chopped walnuts, used 1/3 cup each oil and sugar, and a darker hone (Whole Foods amber).  It was delicious.  Baked for a group dinner - 8 of us - nice size slice for everyone and no leftovers.  I don't love banana bread in general, but I will be making this one again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Have made this many many many times, is my go-to banana bread recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I really dislike banana bread, and try very hard to never have over ripe bananas. But here I was with 3. Found this recipe, and !!!!! This is the first banana bread I have enjoyed eating. The cinnamon pairs well with the banana, adding a depth of flavor that is excellent. I now have a banana bread recipe that I like, and I’ll be letting more bananas become over ripe in the future!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: i don’t even like banana bread and i ate this like i was gonna die tomorrow. did a crumb topping on top and it was perfect.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: i made this recipe but have been trying to cut back on sugar so i used truvia brown sugar substitute and did 50/50 truvia and regular granulated sugar and its SOO GOOD. everyone in my family was impressed.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: popped off and added about a 1/3 cup of light sour cream for tang and it was gorg! definitely recommend adding it into the wet ingredient stage

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Definitely substitute dark rum for the water! I used 2 cups White Lily flour and 3/4 cups sugar and got the perfect-for-me sweetness and texture. 55 mins in my oven.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe is easy and the end result is great. I do modify it and skip the crumble. Instead, I make a glaze with powdered sugar and lemon that is on the thicker side (about one cup of the sugar for 1/8 cup lemon juice). The sweet tartness goes great with the spongy and not too sweet cake.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: At 1 hour this was still VERY wet. I added about 10 minutes, which resulted in it being cooked through, but almost burnt on the edges. The cake itself is delicious, but the topping is just ok. Honestly, could have used about half the topping and it would have been more than enough. As is, a lot of it just falls off. Overall a very good recipe; next time I will do about 65 minutes and hopefully that will result in it being cooked through without the outside getting tough.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this as written but used olive oil because that’s what I had and the result was a very tender and moist cake. I used Demerara sugar for the topping but found this sugar topping to make the whole thing overly sweet. I wish the cinnamon came through more too.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Can I substitute butter for oil?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Has anyone had to cook it for a lot longer than suggested?  Mine is not burning on top (thankfully!) and yet, after 90 minutes, still not done.  I changed the temp to 325 after an hour, and now changed to convection at 325.  Fingers crossed!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This turned out heavy and oily. The word lifeless, comes to mind. Disappointing.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: MIL gave us bananas she couldn't eat so made this today after bananas got nice and spotted. Really good.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: dropped my (baked) banana bread in the oven twice. there’s sugar everywhere now

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Surprisingly enough, this baked through in 50 minutes at Convection Bake 350°. But I used a little less liquid: no honey. I reduced the sugar to a half cup, a mixture of brown and white. Following another reader’s suggestion, I subbed out the quarter cup of water with Cognac. If I use cognac next time, I’ll make sure it’s just an eighth of a cup! Also, I used one cup white flour, and a half cup rye flour. The cognac and the rye flour give the bread a bit of depth. This is a personal taste, clearly; I might add cloves next time too. The crumb top came out just fine. I was really pleased with the recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: O.M.G. This was the richest, dirtiest dish, my husband barely spoke whilst eating it other to say ‘oh my word’ repeatedly. I used Barbacoa spice blend on sirloin steak instead of cayenne and left to marinate for a couple of hours. It was seriously good. A few days later and my husband it still randomly saying ‘mmm, ..beer cheese’ out of the blue.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: What is the brand of barbacoa spice you used?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: lol i love this post

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I love this post too. And this really was one of the best things I've eaten in a long time. One small note: it doesn't matter whether you cube the cream cheese or not, you're just melting it. Just toss it in the pot on low heat. And I used a cheddar that was spiked with habanero to take the heat up a notch.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Proportions for the fries, onions, cilantro and lime juice are omitted from the ingredients.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Thank you so much for flagging, WherestheBeef. We've corrected that issue. All of the missing ingredients are now reflected under "For serving."

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
grill the steak whole--not cubed--and cube/shred after. 
use ancho chillis for the powder.
add some minced cilantro root to the marinade.
don't use cream cheese--use queso fresco.

much better!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: How about you give the people the original, Lolita's carne asada fries from San Diego... Where it was originated..

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I read the preparation for the carne asada; as a Hispanic man, I made my own version.I've also made plenty of cheese sauces before. This recipe was the worst I've ever tried. It was grainy and very unappetizing. I went back to making a béchamel, then added to cheese sauce with milk. Much better. It's  also easier to add some Veletta. It was good idea to use french fries instead of chips.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I accidentally burned the french fries making this, so warmed up some corn tortillas and made carne asada & beer cheese tacos instead...mega yum.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: When a food writer defines carne asada as "the toppings for a plate of beef nachos," you should move on to other reading as quickly as possible.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: lCJ remove the cream cheese from its foil wrap, rewrap in plastic wrap, place in the freezer for 15-20 minutes.  you wont get clear edges on your cubes, but the idea is small uniformly-sized chunks of cream cheese.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious. Those that are pointing out the lack of fidelity with original carne asada fries should have read the accompanying article which clearly identified this as a pub’s take. Added some cubed avocado to the toppings, since, well, what isn’t better with that? We are big fans of Trader Joe’s frozen fries which have great potato flavor and can be deep-fried nice and crispy. Fried some shishito peppers with sprinkled cumin for the side. Fun Saturday night splurge!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Some of the comments-wow. People seem to feel the need to haughtily tell us How It Should Be Done. Loosen up! This is like a Mexican-inspired poutine! Delicious! Health food? Nah. So what? Eat what you like. This tastes great and will be in my winter rotation.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Carne Asada fries are not made with beer cheese. This recipe is like someone took inspiration from movie theater nachos with that horrible liquid cheese.

Go to San Diego and eat the real thing then revise this recipe. (or just call it something else because this is not Carne Asada Fries)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: When I saw this recipe, I thought it sounded like a Chicago invention; something with a Mexican flavor combined with Wisconsin beer cheese. It may not be authentic, but it is festive and very tasty.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Marie D As someone from San Diego, it’s made clearly in the head note where this version originated. Most recipes are variations

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was fun to prepare and delicious to eat!  Our plated food looked just like the picture and was absolutely scrumptious.  My only "add" was a little more beer to the cheese sauce as it cooked to make it saucier.  Additionally, servings were perfect for 4 adults.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I don’t know, but I made it exactly as directed and the cheese sauce came out super grainy and without the cheesy flavor I’d hoped for for cheese fries. Next time, I’ll do what Mark S. suggests and go back to a tried and true bechamel   Velveeta.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I would marry this dish

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The fries aren’t in the ingredient list. Perhaps this is an oversight?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Down in the For Serving category, 1-1/2# of fries is called for

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Proportions for the fries, onions, cilantro and lime juice are omitted from the ingredients.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: They are listed under “ FOR SERVING” after the Beer Cheese recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I haven’t made this yet, but I bet in a pinch (or a fit of laziness) you could use Trader Joe’s Beer Cheese. It’s pretty good! Yes, I’m that lazy.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this for the whole family. Normally our middle daughter is fussy and anything that looks like she won't eat it doesn't get tried. This however went down like a house on fire. Sauce is amazing. Serves with some garlic bread. Children would have eaten this twice over if there were any left

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Finished dish is amazing!  A few things I’ll do differently next time really have to do with the timing. Prep the garnish before starting on the beer cheese and start warming the cast iron skillet while finishing the beer cheese.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Ugh. Sauce was grainy and inedible. I wound up sending my husband to a Mexican restaurant for queso. Too much chili powder; it's all you can taste in the meat.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: We live five minutes from the Piper Inn, which is about as Dive Bar as they come, but yes, this is the signature dish. Nice to know what goes into it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Beer cheese suspended, turned into a stringy cheese lump. Added the other half of the block of cream cheese (another 4 oz) and more beer, which freed it up to a smoother, thinner consistency. Everything else was followed as written, was pretty delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: How do you cube cream cheese? Is it something different in the US, from the thing you can spread on bread?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Well, it comes in a big cube, or rectangle. I would stick it in the freezer for 15 minutes or so then get after it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Trying to figure out how 4 oz Cream cheeze, 2 c shredded Cheddar, 1/3 c beer reduces down to 1 1/4 cup, even allowing for air in shredded.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Well, this was delicious. My son and I gobbled it up. Followed exactly except for cubing they cream cheese. In the words of Tamzin’s husband...”mmm beer cheese”!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Why is my beer cheese so thick? Certainly too think to "drizzle" over the fries, especially after sitting off heat while cooking the meat. I followed the recipe exactly. Still tastes delicious though.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Hi, same here, I just added a little more beer-- it was perfect!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If you use packaged shredded cheese, it contains corn starch, which will thicken the sauce.  I made a comically playdough-like cheese sauce once and learned my lesson.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Delicious! I was alone for Christmas this year, due to the COVID.  This  sounded like it would be a special treat, and indeed it was, though of course the quantity was a bit much for one person. 

The only change I made was to cook the carne asada in a slow cooker, for 8 hours, whole rather than cut into cubes. 

I also omitted the onion, cilantro leaves and lime juice, as the cheese sauce provided plenty of flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Although this marinade leaves a lot to be desired if you’re striving for authentic carne asada flavoring, it does help somewhat to marinate this the day prior.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: MAKE THE FRIES FIRST!!!!  The recipe totally neglects to mention cooking the fries.   Otherwise very tasty.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Meh. I agree with another commenter the cheese sauce was horrible, definitely grainy and not smooth. Definitely should’ve went with my gut and start with a roux base. The flank definitely is better seared off whole too, and the marinade itself needs more flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This has become a treat we enjoy in front of big football games. Fun, festive and definitely naughty. Especially when you substitute tater tots for fries. My boys love it!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Guilty pleasure.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was delicious! The skirt steak was tender and the cheese sauce seasoned was seasoned very nicely. This is on my go to list.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Really nice beer cheese sauce; no idea what the complaints about grittiness are on about unless they used a pre-shredded brand that uses cellulose. Don't worry about cubing the cream cheese, just mash and whisk it around as it melts. I might add some fresh chopped cherry tomatoes next time for some extra acid with the lime and a little more color.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
We really enjoyed this meal but made a couple of minor substitutions.  We decided to make our own French Fries by cutting and seasoning russet potatoes.  We used garlic powder, paprika, salt and pepper to season the fries before baking them for 25 minutes at 425F and flipping mid way through.

The only other substitution was using a non-diary cream cheese because that's all we had but it turned out pretty good.  The serving size for the butter cheese is huge so it might be good to do half 1st.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: exited the premises with enthusiasm.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is so good, holy moly. Only change I made was to marinate the steak for longer and I subbed some quick pickled shallots for onion. The beer sauce came out beautifully.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: One uses flour tortillas for burritos. Use corn tortillas for enchiladas. Mozzarella is for Italian food. Use Monterey Jack. Otherwise a comforting Americanized kind of casserole dish kids enjoy.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Corn tortillas should briefly be submerged in a pan of  warm enchilada sauce before stuffing and rolling.  Otherwise, they don't cook completely and soften. Instead, they may just dry out.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: OMG, the recipe reminds me of my mum's Tuna ala King from the 1950's- canned tuna and condensed cream of mushroom soup on Wonder Bread toast! What's next, Texas canned fruit cocktail suspended inside jello topped with Reddi-Whip (aerosolized cream).  Who knew it stuck to ceilings as well as your ribs?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe needs some help.  3-1/2 cups of the sauce left, to pour over the top? How about mixing half of the sauce with the chicken and green chiles to make a proper filling.  Taste for seasoning (I would also use two or three 4 oz. cans of green chiles); they add so much flavor.  I would also use cheddar or colby-jack cheese, rather than mozzarella.   And finally, I would just up the sour cream, and ditch the cream of chicken soup.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Can corn tortillas be used instead of flour?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: They *should* be used instead of flour tortillas. Otherwise it's a baked burrito, not an enchilada.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Not good. Way too much dairy and way too little flavor. The sauce texture was off-putting. Needs a little color beyond the diced tomatoes. Also, who uses flour tortillas in enchiladas?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I second the report that the sauce has a "strange congealed gelantinous quality" and wish I'd read that helpful review before I made this. There are many good versions of Creamy Chicken Enchiladas online; this isn't one of them. I hesitated to add the Cream of Chicken soup that's called for, but thought that if NYT Cooking had published the recipe that it must be a worthwhile, if strange "hack." Despite my upgrades (poached organic chicken and Monty Jack instead of mozzarella) it was unpleasant.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is am Americanized version of enchiladas pasteur. Much better with Monterey jack cheese, chicken stock and sour cream or crema, rather than cream of chicken soup.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: There is nothing traditionally Mexican about this as written — it’s Americanized Tex Mex-ish — and the recipe is Less cooking than assembling.  But I will confess I’m making it for the third time during the pandemic. I agree that it’s comforting. I freshened it up by making my own roasted tomatillo salsa verde, roasted my own chicken, used “soft” corn tortillas (no soaking required) and cut the amount of sauce by 1/3.  The kids loved it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Yes, I have made these with corn tortillas and like them better.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
- use corn tortillas
- dip tortillas in sauce first
- add chopped onions

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Bland for a hispanic but good if you're feeding a lot of people. Seriously is bad if you like flavor but everyone went "its ok". I ended up putting cilantro and white onion, tomato and sour cream, avocado and salsa on top.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Not a fan. I thought this was disgusting. Texture and flavor. Sam Siftons recipe is superior.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Awful beyond words. Way too much sauce and you have to change gears to fix that.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Blah. Make it all from scratch and use corn tortillas!!!! Cilantro is a must!  Stay away from cream of chicken soup!!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Love the comments how embarrassing the ingredients are while I enjoy all its gooey cheesiness deliciousness on a cold Midwestern winter evening. I made this pretty much to the letter and would not change much. I didn’t get all concerned about exact proportions to sauce to chicken mixture in the tortillas. Just layout the tortillas and eyeball it. I used flour and they were perfect. Broil at the end for perfect crustiness. Add store bought pico de Gallo to add tomatoes and cilantro.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I tried this.  Adding the chicken soup and sour cream to the green enchilada sauce dulled or muted many of the flavors I like in green sauces.  The mozzarella cheese adds a melted stringiness but not a lot of flavor.  Verdict; I prefer a straight green sauce with plenty of clean, bright zip and a cheese with more flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Oh heart be still, a recipe with Cr of Chk soup.  This may send the natives over the top.  As for the recipe, I have made it with both chicken and pork and both are adequate, nothing great.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I strongly suggest forgoing the canned soup.  Chopped onions are a good addition. For more spice, use a red sauce, such as El Pato's Hot Tomato Sauce...and swap the mozzarella for pepperjack.  This also works well with using a good Greek yogurt in place of the sour cream.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Folks, this is closer to hot dish in terms of excitement than it is to a Mexican enchilada. Don’t expect anything but bland.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: True enchiladas are never made with flour tortillas. Which is why they are almost always a good bet for celiacs. Made with white corn tortillas and excellent (if 1000 calories is your thing)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Enchiladas use corn, not flour, tortillas.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So many negative comments on this recipe, yet it is four out of five stars, so most people who've made it must like it. I have made this twice (and will probably make it this weekend) and my family and I have loved it both times. It might not be authentic, but it is wonderful cheesy, mushy comfort food. Of course, I am a Midwesterner by birth, so I always enjoy something with a cream-of-soup base.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is like a faster food version of enchiladas suizas, which as it is are somewhat fast food. It does indeed sound like a 60s canned soup recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Fed a big group of D1 college athletes after a competition- and they loved it. Maybe not haute cuisine, but super fun easy recipe. Some Cholula options on the side add a nice zing for those who want it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: It looks like we’re in the minority, but my husband and I LOVED this dish. Of course, don’t go into it expecting a truly authentic Mexican dish, but accept it as it is: a quality comfort food. We thought the flavors were excellent for the amount of effort required (which was not very much). It’ll be on our rotation for an easy weeknight dinner!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe is definitely reminiscent of the 1970’s, when every kitchen had an electric can opener on the counter! It’s not gourmet but I’ve made it twice during Covid and all are very happy! Comfort food for sure. Double the green chiles (Hatch are best), use medium enchilada sauce not mild, and use 1/2 mozzarella and 1/2 sharp cheddar. Serve with Knorr Mexican Rice, as Joanna recommends. Do not feel guilty at how easy this dinner is!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I love this recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this as a nod to my mother-in-law who had more than one tasty dish in her repertoire that started with a can of soup. Made as is, this recipe is okay at best with both the texture and blandness issues noted by others. Family of 4 couldn’t/wouldn’t finish it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: THE best sour cream chicken enchiladas out there.  It's requested on a regular basis. The only addition to this is I put in a can of Rotel in the creamy mixture just to kick it up a notch on the spice.  Added black olives to the top before cooking.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I needed to cook this quite a bit longer than the recipe stated. It didn't seem fully cooked and was still very saucy. Added another 20 minutes and then about 10 minutes on 425 to brown it.  I also had a lot of the sour cream sauce left over. Could reduce the sauce portion by a third in my opinion

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Decided to read the comments while buying ingredients for the recipie, panicked a little then chose to substitute tomatillo salsa, medium  heat for the mild enchilada sauce; crema for the sour cream and as suggested chicken broth for the cream of chicken soup and it is wonderful.  The salsa worked really well to add flavor and zing to the meal.  I also used low carb flour tortillas and they were perfect.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is the almost exact recipe a friend shared with me when I arrived in Guatemala over 50 years ago. We use the extra sour cream instead of the chicken soup and skip the tomatos on top. The corn tortillas should be the thin variety, not the handmade thicker ones.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Nothing authentic about this, but since we're living in the time of COVID-19, throwing together a dish that basically involves dumping cans of prepared "Mexican" and Cream of Chicken condensed soup together is strangely comforting. Jazzed it up with much needed cayenne. Garnishes made the difference.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Boring and flavorless!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this recipe but I should have known better because I almost never use canned ingredients (except tomatoes) in my cooking. I did not use the cream of chicken soup, but did use the enchilada sauce. Both of us had intestinal problems after eating. This recipe goes away from my files now.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The description says salsa verde helps to make it special, but then the ingredients do not include salsa verde. Green enchilada sauce is not the same — does not include tomatillos. I’m winging it and it will be good but come on!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I followed the recipe exactly, they were really delicious, I thought I made enough to have extra but my guests proved me wrong

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So good!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I'm sure I'm not the only one who thinks a different cheese would improve this. I used shredded cheddar, but I think anything would be an improvement over mozzarella.
I also made a mistake and added the diced chiles to the sauce, then added more when I rolled the enchiladas. Liked it that way. It's all good.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Ok everyone, using this recipe as a base I made it for the first time to rave reviews.  I followed several of the suggestions and tweaked again.  I did mix up the cheese:
 - included a slice of Jack/Pepper cheese & a cheddar/Jack mix to tortilla
_ used 2 cans of Rotel Tomatoes one to the chicken mixture and another drained on top of the casserole
- doubled the chilis 
This is a family keeper in the rotation from now on!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Since this seems to be something that belongs in the I Hate to CookBook from the 60s I took a few more short cuts.  Instead of trying to make enchiladas I layered this like lasagna with corn tortillas.  Much faster.  I substituted mexican blend shredded cheese for the mozz.  My chicken was ground so some texture was sacrificed.  I used some canned diced tomatoes drained since I happened to be out of fresh tomatoes.  I added serranos for spice.  Not fit for a prince but my 18 year old liked it

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Use fresh chopped jalapeños instead of canned pickled peppers. I like lots.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The recipe is delicious. I made it as written. My family raved about it. It is in our meal rotation now

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I have never seen many recipes for enchiladas, but never one that called for bagged mozzarella, condensed cream of chicken soup, and flour tortillas.  The flavor was awful, and the sodium hit from the canned soup and the supermarket chicken was downright dangerous.

Many NYT recipes can be a bit over the top and precious, but this was a dive down to no flavor, gummy texture and leathery tortillas.  Did anyone test it?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Sorry but enchiladas should NEVER be made with flour tortillas! They turn to glue. Corn is the only way to go!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I read the reviews while this was in the oven and I got nervous. Whisk the sauce well, swap cheese.  The final product wasn't terrible, I added green onion, cilantro, shredded lettuce, and a dust of cayenne pepper on the top. It didn't blow my mind... but I didn't expect it too. Obviously authentic methods will yield better results (corn tortillas, no condensed soup) but as a mom of two littles, this was an easy to assemble casserole. Doctor it up to your liking.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is a good weeknight dinner although I tweaked it a bit: Substitute corn tortillas for flour and pepper jack/cheddar for mozzarella. Ditch the cream of chicken soup. Sauce: 1 28-oz can mild green enchilada sauce, a cup of sour cream, and a few dashes of hot sauce (I used Cholula). Top with diced tomato, cilantro, & finely chopped red onion. I happened to have some mango salsa and it was great on the side.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I agree with the other comments about this being really gelatinous. I wanted to like this, but it tasted a little too aspic casserole for me. Think it would be better without the cream of chicken soup.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: At the risk of being politically incorrect, these enchiladas are a version of what is known in our house as "gringo enchiladas"    We first had them many years ago made with rabbit meat from a neighbor who raised rabbits.  We use green enchilada sauce, more sour cream, more diced canned chiles and ditched the chicken soup years ago.  We also use whatever tortillas we have around, whatever cheese is around (I'm partial to Swiss)  Cilantro and lime is  on the side.  Everyone likes it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Without apology I say that we liked it....condensed cream of chicken soup and all.  The only changes I made were to 1)mix cheese, chicken, chiles and some sauce together to make the filling process more streamlined; 2) used an industrial "Mexican four cheese blend" instead of the mozz.  Is it the same recipe.....yeah, pretty much.  It falls into a category of recipe I call "lazy food"  And there's a place for that in everyone's repertoire!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The other enchiladas I have made before were more like this with a saucy inside combo but my spouse noticed this was "just chicken" pretty much and he definitely preferred it that way, said he never liked the other as much as it "wasn't enough chicken."

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I would use a similar sub for mozz.  I mean, who uses mozzarella in Mexican dishes.  I think that perhaps it is the creamy sub for fresca.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
She did not use green enchilada sauce.  Added melted butter to the sauce to make it smooth and silky.  She used extra sharp cheddar instead of mozzarella which definitely added more flavor.  Also added lots of garlic, onion and cheese to the filling.  Corn tortillas instead of flour, but she briefly oven-fried them with a coating of oil to make them pliable and slightly chewy before filling them and adding them to the pan. 

I did try this recipe, but preferred my mother's.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Weirdly gelatinous and no flavor, deeply upsetting to eat.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Not terrible, but too bland and way too much sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Wish I’d read the reviews …

The ingredients seemed weird for a NYT endorsed receipe. 

End product not great, but not terrible. OK at best. 
For the time it takes to make the filling and roll the tortillas, you’re better off just ordering takeout or making a legit enchilada.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Bland and not deserving of a spot on NYT.  You need to add a lot more seasoning and cut out all the sour cream and canned soup and basically just go with a completely different recipe. You’d be better off buying frozen enchiladas at the grocery store; honestly.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Very bland. Does not taste like a mexican dish at all. Would be better with spices mixed together in the chicken mixture. Do not recommend.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I was skeptical, but hey, sometimes I’m wrong. But not this time. This time, canned soup and mozzarella did not alchemize into anything resembling Mexican, or even authentic Tex-mex. Maybe with a small budget and a good contractor Joanna could help me renovate this mess of gelatinous carnage into something resembling enchiladas.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
If you half the recipe (or opt for smaller pan), I would still recommend using the standard 3 and 1/2 or 4 
 cups of shredded chicken for a meatier dish.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: No, no, no. I see the Texan influence in the canned enchilada sauce and cream of chicken soup. I would never cook that way. But the real travesty is terminology. This is NOT an enchilada dish. Enchiladas are never made with flour tortillas and mozzarella cheese. Enchiladas are made with corn tortillas, or they are NOT enchiladas. This recipe is like a "weeknight spaghetti" dish made with orzo instead of spaghetti! I live in New Mexico, where we know about enchiladas.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Very good recipe that makes quite a bit. Opted for red enchilada sauce, just a personal preference. No mozzarella cheese either. If you have Harris Teeter around you, they just started carrying Carolina Reaper shredded cheese. Now that adds some kick to it. Did mix about 1/2 cup of sauce into chicken mixture which really makes a big difference in the final product.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I ended up pressure cooking the chicken in the enchilada sauce and the can of cream of chicken. When I removed the chicken to shred/add the chiles, I then put the sour cream in the sauce. This worked out very well, and was a lot cheaper than buying pre shredded rotisserie chicken. 
We liked the recipe ok overall, but I think less sauce might be good, as well as potentially using corn tortillas.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Doh! I forgot to buy the tortillas! So last minute I made chicken nachos with tortilla chips. Layered as much as possible. Dinner was saved! Pretty, pretty, pretty good!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I skipped the sour cream in the sauce and used lightly fried corn tortillas. My teenagers loved them!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was delicious and easy!  Used salsa verde instead of green enchilada sauce which gave it a good spicy flavor.  Also used colby/jack instead of mozzarella, half of the condensed soup, half of the sour cream and doubled the green chiles.  Add more verde salsa if the sauce is too thick and not covering the tortillas completely.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is pretty good on its own, but is so much better when you add a little more acidity. I made it as directed and added salsa verde on top, which helped cut through all the creaminess. Doesn’t reheat well at all though.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: A soupy and tasteless mess!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Hopes were high but this is so bland.  I took notes from other cooks and left 1 c of sauce out, added an extra 4 oz of green chilis, jalapeños and cilantro on top of the tomatoes and it was still boring.  My grown boys said it was not a do over and they eat everything.  Corn tortillas would give it a better flavor but unfortunately I won’t try this recipe again to find out.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: These reviews are pretty harsh, ultimately very delightful.  Swap the mozzarella for Mexican cheese and add an extra can of green chiles for perfection

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I'm from New Mexico, and we like stacked enchiladas with real green chile and corn tortillas and none of the sour cream or canned enchilada sauce nonsense. Mozzarella? Get out of here! But I love this. I've made a few tweaks, like stacking the flour tortillas to make it like an enchilada casserole or lasagna and replacing half the canned enchilada sauce with (canned/bottled) salsa verde. It's still great comfort food. The cheese melts perfectly, and browns for great texture under the broiler.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Would this be freezable?  Freeze before baking, adding cilantro and tomato after baking?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: These were great!  My first ever Mexican meal.  My husband loved it!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Is there any recipe for replacing that canload of condensed cream of chicken soup? They don't sell it in Europe, no doubt for good reason, but it rules out a lot of American recipes for pot pie, tuna casserole and now this.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Yes, very easy - make a veloute using chicken stock. It's a better option than canned soup and you can control sodium levels (canned soup has a high sodium content). To veloute, I add a touch of dry sherry, cayenne, and fresh ground nutmeg. Of course, these ingredients could be added to this recipe also. I use a veloute as the base for pot pie, turkey tetrazzini and numerous other recipes.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: We tried this last night and while the flavor was great we found that the recipe made way too much sauce. We will probably try 1/2-2/3 the amount if we make it again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: It was just ok- tasty and very easy but nothing stellar or something I would make for guests. Next time I will use about 1/4 at least less sour cream and spicy verde sauce. Plenty creamy without the full 8 oz of sour cream, and I wanted to taste the verde more.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If canned products are not available, please check out the other enchiladas recipes available on the website. They are pretty simple: a red sauce with roasted tomatoes/garlic/onion or tomatillo/garlic/onion (to which you can add creme fraiche, crema, or sour cream). Good (preferred) substitutes for mozzarella are mixtures of Colby/jack or a package of Mexican style cheese, which is a blend of four kinds of cheese. Where I live (Colorado) fresh food is in full supply but not canned.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Although it has been decades since I used a can of soup in a recipe, this turned out much better than I feared. I poached chicken breasts until they were just done, so they were tender and delicious. The sour cream, cilantro, and fresh tomatoes contributed flavor and color. The main problem was the flour tortillas which became a soggy mess. I think there are techniques to avoid this. Nonetheless, my quarantining family gobbled everything up!

author: Julia Moskin
