commentBody: I thought this was pretty good.  I didn't have a rotisserie chicken so used chicken thighs, which I rubbed with oregano, garlic powder, onion powder, cumin, salt and pepper, then cooked in the pressure cooker.  I also used salsa verde instead of the green enchilada sauce.  The salsa added a nice tang to counteract the creaminess of the dish.  I will make it again, but next time I'll try corn tortillas.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I tried this recipe today. It was an exercise in nostalgia. When I went to Grad school in Austin the co-op I was staying at served this for dinner. I have always remembered it fondly. I didn’t have any cooked chicken so I marinated some chicken breasts in lemon juice, cumin, garlic, and chilies and then grilled them. I also added freshly roasted poblanos to the sauce. And because I like it really spicy I tripled the chilies. It was delicious. Comfort food at is tastiest.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Actually recipes with Sour Cream don’t freeze well.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Thoughts on subbing yogurt for sour cream?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Used this as a guide for what was on hand. Used 3 c cooked spicy chicken strips, chopped; added a can of black beans and chopped black Kalamata olives, skipped the green chilies. Subbed in cheddar cheese, filled whole wheat tortillas. Used 1 10 oz jar of salsa Verde with a couple each chopped green and ripe tomatoes; cream of mushroom soup in the sauce.  Served with Spanish rice. Very good.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I substituted pepper Jack and a Mexican grated blend of cheeses for the mozzarella, and while the flavor was good, the consistency of the sauce and texture of the enchiladas was off-putting.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Sounds easy & tasty.  But definitely would use corn tortillas & Monterrey Jack cheese.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I subbed plain yogurt for both the sour cream and the cream of chicken soup. Corn tortillas rather than flour. Was pleased with the results. No, it’s not authentic; but it makes a ton and our family enjoyed it. I’ll probably make it again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Perfectly ok, sort of like a '50s B-horror movie (Earth vs. the White Enchiladas of Doom).  Use pickled jalepenos and a fairly spicy salsa verde and it's tasty, sort of like drive-in french fries.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: To the chicken and Chile's,  I added Penzy's chili 9000, cayenne, Chipotle and sracchi sauce. I also swapped out the chicken soup for chicken stock, and I used a three cheese Mexican blend. It came out awesome!.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This was a phenomenal dinner that blew the minds of everyone in my household last night! We were all still talking about it this morning. :) 
I did make some adjustments:
I added 2 T of fresh ground coriander seed, 2 T of dried oregano, 2 T of red pepper flakes, 1/2 a red onion, roughly chopped, and s&p to taste, and mixed these in with the chicken and chiles. 
I also subbed corn tortillas, warmed in a foil wrapper until ready to use.
Really fantastic!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: We thought this was OK, certainly better than most commenters indicated. And there are lots of recipes available similar to this that call for flour, not corn, tortillas, which we prefer anyway. One confusing thing is that writeup says the cream of chicken soup is mixed with the chicken meat, but that isn't what the directions say, so one or the other is incorrect.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: this recipe was okay. Great for someone who wants a very mild mellow flavor profile. I think next time I will swap out the cream of chicken soup for a little less than a brick of cream cheese. I think this will add to the creaminess without the gelatinous texture of the canned soup. plus green chilies go great with the cream cheese. I will also amp up the spiciness to my taste.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Second time making this. I made it as a layered enchilada casserole this time. I added diced onions, fresh spinach and fire roasted tomatoes to the chicken mixture. I also used green tabasco to kick up the sauce. And Monterary Jack cheese instead of mozz. Much better this time!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is Tex-Mex with a big emphasis on the Tex.  We liked this and it isn’t too hard, but next time I’ll use corn tortillas.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Actually, this recipe was perfect for running to a family who had an illness. Based on the largely unfounded criticisms, I did mix 5 oz of leftover mild green enchilada sauce directly into the chicken to pump up the flavor. I also halved the cream of chicken soup buy probably wouldn't next time. Ended up using both mozzarella and a pre-shredded Mexican-style blend. Easy to put together and tasted great....doable, unfussy, comfort food.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this recipe with some minor tweaks. I added one can of the enchilada sauce to the chicken and green chile mixture, I used monterey jack cheese, instead of mozzarella, and the sauce mixture was 1 can of enchilada sauce, half a cup of sour cream, and half a can of cream of chicken soup.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I really liked this recipe; reminded me a lot of growing up in Texas in the 1980s. A few suggestions I would make that made this taste for authentic (for me): use corn tortillas, before rolling up each enchilada, add a bit of chili powder for more heat and flavor; rather than canned chilis inside the tortillas, I sprinkle pickled sliced jalapeños on top (Mrs Renfro’s are good or make your own). I also use a tomatillo salsa for the base that’s at least medium spicy to add some more heat.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: A good prep ahead family meal. This dish is common in my area of TX. A dish you can prep in the morning when it’s cooler to heat up at dinner time is a winner to me. Leftovers microwave well, it’s practical and ingredients are easy to find.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Maybe stick to pizza on staging night

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This casserole is really good, better than the harsh helpful comments make it out to be. Kept eating a bite or two all day. Made a few necessary changes. Halved recipe. Still enjoyed four or more servings. Used full can Cream of Chicken soup, no guilt. Probably added extra sour cream. Used large flour tortillas. Grocer here doesn't have rotisserie chicken. Used whole package pre-cooked chicken fajita meat. Shredded mozzarella melted into sauce. Benefits from lime juice, cilantro and tomato.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: How is it possible to make enchiladas so gross? We agreed these were the worst enchiladas we've ever had. Should have read the comments first.  Whoever said "strange gelatinous" really was spot on.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: An overall underwhelming meal even after tweaking the recipe by using homemade chicken filling that actually had seasoning/ veggies added and opted not to use the cream of chicken soup. Also very visually unappealing when served. Only positive note is that adding the sour cream to the enchilada sauce did give it some body and would do that again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I’ve been looking for a weeknight enchilada recipe and did like this! I used the cream of chicken, but may half it next time.

I switched to corn tortillas and mixed 1/4 cup of salsa verde with the chicken along with a quarter of the sauce and used Monterey Jack cheese.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I live in Texas and its 50/50 whether a Tex-Mex restaurant makes Enchiladas Suizas with flour or corn tortillas.  Suizas referencing Switzerland for the dairy in the dish.  It's a combination of shredded chicken, mild green chilis, sour cream, and a Mexican "melting cheese" (yes, that's what it says on the package in grocery stores) or monterey jack. Sometimes you see a vegetarian filling of mushrooms and spinach. I wouldn't be surprised if condensed soup is in there- Queso is made from Velveeta

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This gets a bit creamy - slightly less soup helps.  But this is an amazingly comforting, filling, savory recipe that I crave regularly. Even better with high-quality organic ingredients. I err on the side of cooking until the top is bubbly/crusty. Yum!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe is good! Honestly. Just needs a few tweaks that some have mentioned. First, substitute chicken stock for the cream of chicken soup. Next, season your chicken/chilies mix with some coriander, oregano, cayenne and salt and pepper. And add some chopped red onion. The mozzarella is fine! And so are flour tortillas! For your topping, simply dice a tomato and toss with some chopped cilantro, juice of one lime, and some salt. Viola. Quick, easy dinner everyone will love.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
First, for the doubters—yes, Enchiladas Suizas is actually a real dish. It’s especially popular in north Texas. It’s definitely Tex-Mex, which is, in turn, a uniquely blended culture and cuisine, not just Americanized food.

This recipe, however, won’t get you the classic dish. This is more like a version of King Ranch Casserole. 

Use crema and a Mexican cheese such as asadero. And no cream soup. Don’t “improve” the recipe by making Enchiladas Verdes instead. There are great recipes out there!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I love enchiladas suizas and definitely recommend people make those. This might also be good, but one's missing out if they don't try suizas sometime. Bonus points if they are still almost too spicy to eat despite the cheese and crema!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was OK, not great. I would add more spices to the enchilada sauce, including cumin and garlic. And the sauce was also a bit dull. Must be better recipes out there.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Inauthentically delicious.  My husband absolutely loved this comforting casserole.  I think next time I’ll substitute extra sour cream for the cream of chicken, but it was really tasty, if only vaguely Mexican.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used one can Cr of Mushroom soup, 1 can water, red salsa (didn't have salsa verde)-was fine and added a kick.  Used combo of shredded gouda and muenster cheese instead of mozzarella.  Topped with more shredded cheese, scallion greens, and parsley after 20 minutes, and used broiler for last 3 minutes-nice  crusty and bubbly dish. A good option for leftover roasted chicken.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Yup. Agree with the below comments. Corn tortillas are for enchiladas. I learned to cook in a Mexican restaurant, so I can attest to this. To do it the traditional way, you fry the tortillas quickly in lard beforehand. For me, I have updated and use coconut oil. I haven't tried it, but it does sound bland. Add some cumin, chilli powder or paprika, and some corriander to make it less bland please. Also, how about cotija or queso fresco instead of moz? This is the whitest recipe I've read recenlty

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Make sure to use flour tortillas & I recommend Sharp or regular Cheddar or Colby Cheese. Mozzarella just doesn't give it that oomph! I found a great low carb, low calorie tortilla by Ole! Check it out.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: How could this recipe have 4 stars? I haven't seen a single positive review (not surprisingly!!!)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My changes:  1.  made my own green enchilada sauce, used 16 ounces.  2. Used 4 cups of home rotisserie chicken, including skin.  3. 2 cans of drained green chilis and mixed with the pulled chicken and 1 cup of mozzarella cheese for the filling (also used about 1/3 cup of pan dripping from rotisserie chicken).  4. Used 8 flour tortilla - 10 was too many.  5. Did NOT just dump all the sauce on top.  Only use about 1/2, froze remainder.  6.  Cooked at 350 for 20 min and 375 degrees for 30 min.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this today.  Pretty bland ingredients.  Trying it anyway.  And, what the heck is "2 T" of cheese?  Anyway.  It will probably be edible.  Cheers!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was great for our family.  We have 3 "older" adults, none of whom can eat spicy food but like a little bit of heat if careful.   I have a big garden and freeze salsa peppers and used about half of one rather than buying diced chilis. I also had some leftover mozzarella pearls from a lunch where no guests ate them, so that's what I used inside the enchiladas. On top I didn't have much of the mozzarella left, and I just used some (gasp) Velveeta queso cheese. Family wants this again! Keeper.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Mozzarella cheese?  Recipes from
Molly Yeh and Joanna Grimes?  Is there a marketing agreement?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I think they are using mozzarella as a substitute for queso fresco.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Sub our green salsa for hatch green chili and American shredded cheese and you have a New Mexican classic.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Highly recommend the frontera green enchilada sauce, and add extra! Delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe is incomplete because there ends up being zero texture.  We added onion, green onion, red bell pepper, Mexican Cheese Blend, and tortilla chips on top.  It’s still very rich, the flavor has potential but it’s very unbalanced.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Subbed 4-cheese blend for Mozzarella. They came out great. Did a pan of flour and pan of corn.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I've eaten Mexican food for 50 years in California. Mozzarella cheese on enchiladas is like making New York cheesecake with Velveeta. Jack cheese is usual, or store-bought shredded Mexican 3 cheese mixes are good. I like to make a filling combining shredded cooked chicken with sauteed spinach, onion and tomatillos. And you need to soften tortillas before rolling them or they split. You can fry them lightly but I prefer briefly steaming them in the microwave under a plastic dome cover.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this for my family ( kids 9&11) they LOVED it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My mother used to make something like this for the many buffet parties my parents had: crepes stuffed with shredded chicken breast mixed with a small amount of bechamel and chopped, canned jalapenos, the rolled crepes laid out in a baking dish covered in more bechamel and "swiss" cheese. Prepared ahead of the opera or wherever they were going, baked when the guests arrived at midnight. Very old-fashioned, fifties (or sixties), but every bite was eaten. And I liked it, too.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I thought I would enjoy this, a throwback recipe similar to stuff I ate as a child. But the flour tortillas make it gummy and the mozzarella seems just wrong in the recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Perhaps you should consider doing a skillet prep job on your tortillas in hot olive oil until they've been browned a bit on both sides? Then roll them with ingredients, etc.. Mozzarella: Oaxaca Cheese is very similar to Mozzarella and my mouth likes both. I've spent serious time in Oaxaca FWIW, my fave larger Mexican city.  Sin of all sins, I buy Walmart Mozzarella.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was a valuable learning experience; read the other cooks’ notes before making a recipe. I had questions about using flour tortillas and mozzarella cheese canned soup, among others, but who am I to quarrel with Joanna Gaines. Made the recipe as written and now wish I had modified it as suggested by other cooks.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used the recipe as a very loose guide since for me the Magnolia Table cookbook wasn’t a hit. I cut up corn tortillas to avoid the breakage when rolling and subbed browned chorizo for the chicken to add flavor. I layered a small amount of sauce, tortillas, chorizo/chilies, sauce then cheese (cheddar jack mix). Repeat layer. It’s was a super easy meal to get on the table. Everyone enjoyed it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Tip of the day: Krogers seems to be the only store that carries the Mission brand extra large & thin corn tortillas which are not that large but great a product. We never see them elsewhere. No local Tortilleria here bouts.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Ha! I think i’ve made this about 10 times now, and this is the first time I’m looking at the comments. Sad that so many dislike it! I use an organic version of cream of chicken soup, and broil it at the end. Tons of lime, cilantro, tomato, and hot salsa once it’s out. Kids love it and it’s so easy to make large portions for a crowd.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is SO EASY and kids love it. It benefits greatly from lots of fresh add-ons at the dinner table for more adventurous palates, especially raw onions, hot sauce and sliced jalapeños. I just made this for friends with kids, and the 7-year-old said “Wow! You made the BEST THING!” So don’t sneer too hard, gourmets…this is weeknight family-friendly comfort food par excellence.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe is good but I did make some modifications because I thought there was too much sauce and it was too thick.  The first modification I made was omitting the chicken soup altogether then I used 3/4 cup of sour cream instead of the full 8 ounces.  I also added 1 can of Rotel mild chopped tomatoes with peppers to the sauce for added texture and flavor.  The mozzarella is great because it’s not a cheese you would think would work with enchiladas but it does.  A great recipe!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is easy, delicious, comfort food. Bake this a day ahead. The chilis and green sauce need the time to develop. In fact, reheating on the third day, you will really appreciate the flavors in the thick, creamy sauce which is absorbed by the flour tortillas. Thanks, Joanna. We are not all snobs.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
My son makes exciting, authentic green chicken enchiladas that are incomparably delicious!
BUT I have a friend who has made this recipe since the 70’s and it is yummy. I’m always delighted when I learn it is on her menu. She knows it’s not the “real thing,” but everyone loves it. 
I am a Texan raised on Tex-Mex in Texas and SoCal, and taught at least one authentic dish by a friend’s mom who spoke no English, but I am sadly appalled by some of the rude, snarky comments posted here.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: no flavor, almost too creamy and goopy sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was pretty gross all around. Love heavy food, love cream, and love enchiladas - hated this. Swing and a miss.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Agree with other commenters—this is a good base for a recipe but is way too bland. Double the green chiles, make your own cream of chicken with bouillon, milk, and herbs, and use corn tortillas instead of flour. This updated version has become a weeknight staple for our family.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: For sure more than one can of green chiles mixed with chicken, at least two is needed for flavor. Way too much sauce to put on after layering the bottom and not necessary. I found it to be bland, and too creamy. Something is missing.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I totally agree with using corn tortillas for enchiladas, unless you are like me, allergic to corn.  So, I'm grateful for this. HOWEVER, I love corn tortillas, they are one of the best foods in the world.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Only one change- use Monterey Jack cheese instead of or half and half with mozzarella.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Do not use flour tortillas unless you really like mush!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This is a Midwest PTA potluck version of enchiladas.

1. Enchiladas *never* use flour tortillas - which will cook into wallpaper paste. Use corn.
2. It involves a can of cream soup instead of proper sauce. Use green chili or tomatillo sauce. 
3. Mozzarella is for Italian dishes, enchiladas use Monterrey Jack or even yellow cheeses. 

If you want to make this, put it over crushed corn tortilla chips and you’ll get a rough version of King Ranch Casserole. But no mozzarella, please.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is a family favorite. I’ve learned that dishes dismissed by commenters as “bland” are often the ones my kids will actually eat. I do enjoy Monterey Jack more than mozzarella on this, and I don’t rush the cooking time—I always broil for 5-10 minutes at the end to get the top browned and crispy. It needs some textural contrast or else it is too saucy and soggy. But I’ve made this probably 20 times and buy cream of chicken soup cans solely for this recipe!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Love this recipe, but I do use a couple extra cans of chilis in it and use mont jack and cheddar. I do like a bit of mozzarella for topping. A weeknight favorite here..

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I hated the flour tortillas in this! Mushy, gooey texture, though the filling was yummy. Lesson learned: next time I make enchiladas, I will use corn tortillas.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Don't use the soup or sour cream - use 1 bricks of cream cheese. Delish!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Just fantastic... Do your corn tortillas and change up everything in the recipe and then come on here and call it terrible...

Such a weeknight treat..

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is a basic comfort food recipe, not an authentic enchilada recipe. It was exactly what I was searching for in the moment.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I've made sour cream chicken enchiladas for years, always with corn tortillas. I admit, when I made Joanna's sauce with salsa verde, sour cream, and cream of chicken soup, I loved the taste. I could imagine it with diced rotisserie chicken as a dip at Christmas. But things went downhill from there. I just find flour tortillas incredibly gummy. Gobs of Mozz don't help. I'm going back to corn tortillas with Cheddar or Mex cheese. And maybe some of her sauce. Maybe even on tostadas.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I think I have made this about 10 times (?). The big alterations that have been made is to actually divide the full mixture into 20 fresh tortillas, over two pans. 
In each pan I do 1/2 c. of the enchilada sauce to start, and then set aside about 1 c. for each pan to pour over top. The rest of the mixture is mixed with the chicken, along with an extra can of diced chiles for a bit of kick.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Followed the helpful notes that suggested corn tortillas (dipped in sauce), upgraded the cheese, added a bit of sherry, nutmeg and cumin. Eliminated the soup in favor of straight up sour cream; no regrets!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! We baked the bacon so didn’t have the fat and substituted with EVO. We thought the taste would have been overpowering with both fat and the bacon.  Browning the hash browns ahead of time would be an improvement. Lastly, topped the dish with panko. Will most definitely make this dish again!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: I made this with fresh potatoes, cubed and sautéed after cooking the bacon.  I also added more chopped kale, which gave the dish more body and flavor.  So delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Can this be frozen after cooking?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Could just be me, but I found the proportions to be off and the cook time way off. Egg all over the bottom of the oven, smoke alarm going off and still not cooked after over an hour.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: I was excited to make this, but disappointed with the results. It was dry and needed more salt (and I hardly ever salt my food!) I’ll probably try this again, and tweak the recipe a little bit. As another commenter noted, it may help to lightly brown the hash browns beforehand, probably in evoo, and add a teaspoon of salt at that time. Also, I would increase the amount of cheese in the egg mixture (maybe by 1/2 cup) because the frozen potatoes seemed to absorb a lot of the cheese’s moisture.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: I substituted spinach for kale, grated cold day old baked potatoes and cooked them in the bacon fat before baking into the casserole. It was fantastic! Thanks!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: I added more bacon and more kale, and will do so again - you can double the greens if you like. This recipe lends itself to modifying for your tastes - I went heavier on the garlic and used a smoked Gruyère and got wonderful flavor. I used 9 whole eggs and 3 egg whites (the yolks were for a key lime pie), and texture was light & not dry. Made this for Father’s Day - both my husband & father raved about it. Definitely will go back to as a base recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: I hate kale, but loooooove cabbage. Could this be used as a substitute?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: It would change the taste and texture a bit, but I'd definitely give cabbage a go with this.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Rich, have you tried Swiss chard? I don't care for kale and usually replace it with chard. It grows great in my Oregon garden (zone 8b), so I incorporate it into as many recipes as possible.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious, but the cook time definitely felt off. Needed about another 20-30 minutes. It’s a perfect holiday recipe, though!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: I sustituted thinly sliced Brussel sprouts for the kale and added a dollop of sour cream for richness. Yum!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe!  Great for a crowd or to have leftovers for weekday breakfast.  Quite a bit of prep work, but worth it.  I used my potato ricer to squeeze out the water from the potatoes.  Will continue to make this.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: In addition to pre-cooking the hash browns, and/or making them from potatoes, I suggest halving the number of eggs to 6, and doubling the vegetable,whether kale, broccoli, or other greens. I make a version of this in a simple bread crust, another variation to try. As written, this recipe is pretty bland and my family found ourselves thinking we'd rather prepare and have short-order hash browns, bacon, and eggs than baked together like this.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: I loved this. I sautéed cubes potatoes as suggested by others. I love kale and frittatas so this was perfect. I ate it for breakfast all week.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: This was fabulous!  Made it for ladies’ brunch and everyone asked for the recipe.  After reading other’s comments, I did a few things.  Tried to brown the hash browns but all they did was dry out a bit— probably a good thing.  Used spinach because I prefer it to kale.  Added more cheese, but not 1/2 cup—maybe 1/3 more.  Also added halved cherry tomatoes which did add moisture.  Sprinkled Spanish paprika over the cheese. The middle wasn’t quite done so had to put that back in 375oven for 10 mins.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious! Fed a crowd so did 1.5x the recipe. And baked in large casserole dish. Added 1tsp red pepper flakes with the garlic and kale. Increased salt with two large pinches added to kale and 1.5tsp added to egg mixture plus lots of cracked pepper. Prepared night before and baked the next morning. Also, did not defrost and pat dry the potatoes based on comments that this could be dry. Ours was very moist and delicious! Highly recommend.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: The 1st time I made this it was OK. Second time, I cubed potatoes and sautéed them for a while in the bacon fat. Subbed spinach for kale. I did the bacon   potatoes day before the save time Easter morning. It was a hit! Soft and flavorful.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: I have extra poblanos - bought to many for a posole.  I think they will be great in this!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: What can i substitute the hash browns with? Any ideas? Can i do something else with fresh potatoes maybe? Thanks,

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Or riced cauliflower?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Can someone explain how 3 cups of kale be equivalent to 3 ounces?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Boil your own potatoes. Cool in refrigerator until potatoes are pretty cold or make ahead the day before and leave in refrigerator until ready to make the dish then  and grate with a cheese grater.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Hi- Anyone try this with stale bread? Please let me know and thanks!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Made for Xmas breakfast, turned out very good.   Did kale, bacon, potatoes mixture day prior.  Didn't use Gruyere, but might have made it a little cheesier.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Even better when one replaces the kale with frozen broccoli from last summer’s garden.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Made for Christmas Day 2020

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Can you make the batter ahead of time?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Can this be made ahead and frozen?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Easy and 3/4 family members approved. Forgiving recipe; I used cubed hash brown potatoes and tossed them in the skillet after cooking the bacon (rather than thawing and patting dry as recipe suggests) - could definitely exchange bacon for sausage or use other cheeses or veggies as well.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: I followed the advice to brown the hash browns and it definitely made a difference. I used a cheddar Gruyère and also added sauce from chipotle peppers in adobo jar for some smoke/spice.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: I added a cup of fresh corn, halved a handful of grape tomatoes, and added a small can of green chiles to the onion and kale saute. I loved this and will definitely make again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Enjoyed at Epiphany potluck at church tonight. Was made with sausage instead, according to the bringer. But so delicious! As they were kind enough to tell me the recipe source, I’ll try making it myself now!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: This was great. I used 8 oz. of smoked gruyere and it added to the smoky bacon. Top with plenty of hot sauce, but I'll make this again often.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Seriously bland. As previous commenters noted, needs more salt, browning potatoes would have helped, and in reheating, a little cayenne goes a long way.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: I made this for Easter - it was very tasty and fed lots of people, and I could make it the day before. Given the notes, instead of store bought hash browns I diced and sautéed/browned 2 potatoes, then proceeded with the rest of the recipe. I don’t think it added much more time. Added in a bit more bacon and less cheese. It was tasty and leftovers were great.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Used about a teaspoon of ground Szechuan peppercorn in the eggs mix before cooking. Delicious

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Meh. Pretty bland, even after doubling the salt and pepper and adding a bit of cayenne pepper. Might be better with stronger cheese? Next time I'll just make some eggs, bacon, and hashbrowns separately.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Next time will pre-cook the hash browns to improve the consistency in the final dish.  We added a diced red bell pepper and a whole diced onion (instead of half). We also used EVO instead of bacon fat, but probably could have gone either way. Easy & fast. Will make again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly by the recipe and found it bland and uninteresting. Nevertheless, I would make it again, perhaps doubling the amount of bacon and adding salt and pepper. I think the previous suggestions to brown the hashbrowns might help, too. If it weren't for the fact that cheese doesn't agree with my digestive tract, I might also add more gruyere.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody:
For the potato component I put some russets in the microwave on the potato timer and took them out about 3/4 the way through. I roughly chopped them. I had a 10 oz bag of Trader Joe’s lacinato kale, used all of it. Did not have mozzarella or Gruyère so I used only a sharp white cheddar. 3 Tablespoons Dijon. 
Tasty. Nothing to write home about though. 
I guess it’s a nice way to get some Veg. And some bacon grease too. :)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Giant meh. If i made it again, which I won't, I would use less cheese and less bacon. Made exactly as written, but used spinach because I had a ton on hand. Maybe kale would have made it less leaden, but regardless, it needs more veg, less fatty stuff, and some spice or herbs.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: I'm going to try poblanos! I'll roast or broil them first - the house will smell good.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Snowed in and looking for comfort food - Loved the kale, I’d chop up the stems and sauté with the onion next time. Thought the bacon added very little. I’ll probably look for another recipe with sausage and bread, and add kale to it!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: This is the second time I’ve made this…the first was planned and perfect. Today’s version is brought to you by the broccoli farmers of America. It was last minute. We did not have mozzarella and used 10oz of Gruyère instead. We used broccoli florets because we had no kale…and we used shallots (bougie onions) with a yellow onyawn. This is to die for. Well, Jesus did anyway…on this Easter Sunday. Have you accepted cheeses into your life? Praise! Buon Apetito!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Kale and Bacon Hash Brown Casserole Recipe
commentBody: Meh. This was bland, even though I doubled the mustard and quadrupled the garlic. I think the shredded potato was detrimental and I would rather have an egg bake without them.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I have been making this recipe since I found it in the 1992 Southern Living as Frosted Peanut Butter Brownies.
Absolutely the best recipe!  Only difference is the original recipe calls for a 12 oz jar of chunky peanut butter.  
It’s a personal preference as both are good.  Just don’t overcook the bottom layer.  These brownies can be made a week ahead and stored in the refrigerator.  Best eaten at room temperature.  They also freeze beautifully.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My mother always added a blob of marshmallow fluff to her chocolate icing and fudge, and now I do.  It does something to hugely improve the texture, and if you go easy on the powdered sugar, is not too sweet.  I usually eschew weird processed additions, but this one does help a lot.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Do you think marshmallow fluff would work in this recipe instead of marshmallows? We are vegetarians and don't eat gelatin.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Looked through the 16 comments for useful tips from people who actually made the recipie (isnt that what the notes are supposed to be for?), found two from people that made the recipie.  Still no flour at pandemic-streiken grocerystores here, but found brownie mix.  Made the brownie section according to the mix, then made the peanut butter layer and forståing as directed in the recipie.  Marshmallows did fluff up the frosting nicely.  Peanut butter layer easy and salty-good.  Wonderful!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The photo looks amazing but the marshmallows are giving me pause. Can anyone say more about the taste and texture of the frosting? Thanks!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Jess, it looks like the marshmallows act like a shortcut of a meringue base and create a frosting to hold its shape.  I'm game to try the recipe but will cut back on sugar in the brownies.  2 cups seems like a lot when there's just 1 1/2 cups of flour.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Liz, I see that, but the recipe directions say you melt the marshmallows and mix in the other icing ingredients. ? It looks like the photo and the recipe diverge. I hope someone makes these and reports back!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This tastes great! I did cut down on the sugar and definitely used the marshmallows - Jess sometimes we just have to throw our preconceived marshmellow worries out the kitchen door! A big hit with the family-

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: By how much did you cut back on the sugar?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: These were  -- not to put too fine a point on it -- bad. I had two tiny squares after they were done and immediately felt sick. The underlying brownies were dry, crumbly and tasteless. The peanut butter and not-so-chocolatey frosting were terribly rich. I'm sad I wasted the ingredients.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Regarding marshmallow... I add it to my basic buttercream frosting.  We have a family recipe that uses it as a whoopie pie filling, and I pirated it to use on cakes and, especially, cupcakes.  It gives a wonderful texture.  Being a New Englander, it has to be Marshmallow Fluff.  I think the marshmallows in this would add a similar texture boost.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Made these brownies last night. The balance between the layers are nice.  The actual brownies are not super sweet with the 2 cups of sugar but the frosting is very sweet with the 4 cups. Cooked very quickly, 25 minutes was way too long. These brownies have a different chewy, since they are refrigerated. If you’re looking for a fudge-like texture, these are not for you. 
Just one piece a day for these brownies though! Calories are probably off the charts!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe was disappointing- cloyingly sweet and the texture of the brownie was dismaying.  If you want a perfect brownie I’d stick with Mark Bittman’s recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The brownie party of this dessert is awful.  To cut the sugar, I sprinkled flaky sea salt on both the Brunei’s and the chocolate coating to help with the sugar high.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This looks interesting. Would vegan marshmallows be ok for the frosting? My grandkids are vegetarian and I don't use marshmallows with gelatin.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Brenda, my health food store sells a vegan fluff (it’s divine), I don’t remember the name, see if you can find that.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used vegan marshmallows last night! They worked great!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Do NOT use jumbo marshmallows! I, always the fool, used ten jumbos (disregarding the recommended gram measurement) and ended up with some sort of weird, sticky ganache. The brownies were still good, but they looked nothing like the photo.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I admit I don't have strong feelings about brownies, but I think a lot of the complaints about both the texture of the brownie ("too fudgy") and the sweetness of the frosting are resolved by serving the brownies at room temp. Even refrigerating them congeals the butter in the brownie, making them fudgy, neutralizes the peanut butter flavor & gooeyness, and crystallizes the sugar in the frosting, making it painfully sweet. Try them at room temp.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: You’re right, the flavor and texture are better at room temp. The slightly weird crunchy-chewy bottom layer turned back into a normal squishy brownie. The only trouble is, the peanut butter layer melted and the top layer slid. But if you care more about flavor than aesthetics, room temp is the way to go.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Jess if you’ve ever made Rice Krispie treats, the concept is the same.  Melt the marshmallow and butter, add the other icing ingredients and whisk away. Try it! Will not be difficult and will be tasty, no matter what. Let us know how it worked for you.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I would like to make this but there is so much sugar in all the components. It just seems like it would be much to sweet . Has anybody made these and cut down some sugar ? Or substituted some of it with Swerve? Just curious.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: To me, it's the sweetness (the flavor) that is the problem, not so much the actual sugar. If I were you, I would cut back on the sugar as others have done, rather than add something else that tastes sweet.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: These are… okay. A lot of work and very messy. Don’t use Trader Joe’s brand marshmallows as they don’t melt properly for the icing.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: OMG: 3 sticks of butter AND a jar of peanut butter - sorry, most of us DON'T have personal trainers

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So don't make   Simple, wasn't it?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I assume the peanut butter used here is the more processed stuff, like Skippy or JIF? Any reason one couldn’t use the natural PB?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Try it, and see if it works.  It might, and it might not, depending on the brand.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I'm just going to use regular store-bought frosting for this.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I just finished the frosting and it’s delicious! Freezing now and excited to eat later

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Only had three cups of confectioners sugar on hand, and it worked just fine. Plenty sweet enough! I also used a combo of smooth and chinch PB, as I prefer the latter for texture.

Yummy and decadent, just like a PB cup.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Following the directions, mine turned out well (I had to bake the brownies for 28 min.), but I found them to be the kind of dessert that is SO sweet, you just get the blast of sugar and don’t notice the flavor. I cut down the   sugar in the frosting to three cups, but then realized my peanut butter had sugar in it. If I tried these again, I’d use PB without sugar and probably make less of the super sweet icing to rebalance the flavors. Overall,these are super rich and one pan should last a while

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
For half batch (8 x 8 pan)
1 stick butter 
2/3 C cocoa powder
1 C sugar
3/4 C flour
1/4 t salt
2 eggs 
1 t vanilla

peanut butter layer 1 C p bu

Frosting
4 t butter
4 oz bittersweet chocolate (melted with butter)
2 C confectioners sugar
whipping cream to spreadable consistency

Don't overbake

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have to agree with Laura these were bad. They were tasteless and sugary. The photo looked amazing but the taste and textures were not what I expected and not in a good way. If you like fudge from candy stores in tourist towns you might like this recipe. I wouldn’t describe it at all like a brownie. I am also sad that I wasted the time and ingredients on these.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: These brownies tasted great, just like a Reece’s peanut butter cup! I only used half of the sugar for the chocolate top layer, because 4 cups seemed like way to much (on top of the entire jar of peanut butter and two cups of sugar for the brownies). I also used a 9x9 pan and found that these brownies were thick - if you have a 9x13 pan use this as instructed, that way your brownies and layers are proportioned better!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I should have read the comments before making this.  The frosting is much too sweet for me, I would cut the sugar in half if I made this again.  The combination of the layers works very well.  I would call this recipe "peanut butter cup bars" and leave the word brownie out of it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I was so excited to make these for Mother’s Day but I wish I had picked a different recipe. I agree with another note that the brownies themselves don’t have much taste. The frosting is WAY too sweet. I felt sick after a nibble. Maybe I’ll try again with less sugar and a different kind of PB. I used Teddie, all natural creamy. Happy Mother’s Day, everyone!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The photo of these brownies make them look a lot better than they taste. I agree with other reviewers that the brownie part had no flavor and as a whole tasted like sugar. I will not be making these again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this for Mother’s Day exactly as written. Love the concept but agree with others that the frosting is way, WAY too sweet. Wish I had omitted it all together and drizzled some melted dark chocolate or chocolate ganache on top.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: These were absolutely delicious. I cooked the brownies for 20 minutes exactly and they came out perfect. You DO NOT want to overcook them. It was surprisingly easy to put all the layers together. The peanut butter never gets very solid so if the brownies are not well chilled it kind of squishes out the sides when you cut them into squares.  Also, I think 4 cups of confectioner sugar in the frosting is a bit ridiculous. I’ll probably look for a different chocolate frosting recipe next time.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Horrible. Stay away from this recipe. I'm really surprised that it was featured in the times. The brownie layer was dry and not chocolatey enough, the straight peanut butter layer was too dense - the texture didn't really jibe with the other two layers, and the frosting was just bizarre. It called for a ridiculous amount of confectioners sugar and was way too sweet. I only added half of what it called for and it was still almost too thick to spread. I'm sorry that I wasted the ingredients.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I added a bit of flakey sea salt on top, as the brownies by themselves are incredibly rich. It bumped these up to the next level.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I specifically made this recipe to use up leftover vegan marshmallows, and it was a bit of a disaster. They don't melt as smoothly as regular marshmallows and the frosting ended up gritty and unusable. If I were to attempt this again, I'd try melting the marshmallows in the microwave first, as recommended by the packaging. I used boxed brownie mix and spread peanut butter on top, so at least that was delicious! But if using brownie mix in a 9x9 pan, reduce the amount of peanut butter to 8-12 oz.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
The brownie layer reminds me of Thomas Keller’s recipe which also uses unsweetened cocoa. But like the Keller recipe, I added one cup of chopped bittersweet chocolate to the brownie layer which really improved the recipe. 
      I also used a French cocoa (Michel Cluizel) which I buy online: that made both the brownie and glaze more intense.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Tip, make sure to sift or wish the powdered sugar. I opened a box and directly added that to the butter/cocoa for the icing. I wisk-ed things together best I could but my icing had little lumps in it. 


These are amazing (the icing tastes like fudge!)   keep well in the freezer. If you’re like me and can’t stare at sweets all day without coming an amount of an unsupervised child.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: It's very sweet with everything- very filling! might reduce the amount of sugar in the brownie by a tablespoon or two.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The brownie part of this leaves much to be desired. It is unlike any good brownie I’ve had. It’s basically a fudge layer on the bottom which is far too rich given the frosting on top. Something more cake like would have been more balanced. The flavor of the brownie was shallow, too.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Best dessert I've ever made.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Wow, hard to believe but I agree with Lauren Paul’s assessment that these are quite bad. Mine turned out “great” (no issues following the recipe) but the brownie layer is flavorless and has a strange crumb, and the frosting layer is very sweet and lacks a chocolate flavor; despite a nice brown color, it just tastes like sugar.

If you do decide to make these I would advise checking the brownies at 15 min, and I would also advise shaving 100g of sugar off of both the brownie and frosting layers.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I've been making these brownies for 15 or 20 years; I got the recipe from a neighbors cookbook of southern recipes.  I wish I could remember the name! 

The only difference is the amount of peanut butter - 1/2 cup rather than a full jar.  They are delicious with a scoop of vanilla ice cream.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: A huge hit.  We followed others suggestions and changed the frosting to 2 cups confectioners sugar and 1/6 cup of milk

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
To echo another review, these are just bad. The brownies are dry as stated, the peanut butter layer is well, just plain peanut butter and the top layer is overwhelmingly sweet. I added sea salt to calm the sweetness and it didn’t help much. I couldn’t eat a whole one. This recipe needs work. A better brownie (with nuts for texture too) a better peanut butter layer (mousse maybe) and a toned down chocolate topping to offset all the sugar. 
I love chocolate & peanut butter so disappointed

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: These brownies are a hit in our family - and we don’t particularly love peanut butter or marshmallows! Flavors go well together. I did half the marshmallows and half the confectioners sugar for the frosting and was pleased with the outcome. I’m sure it tastes great with the full amount but I doubt I could eat more than a couple bites.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I love chocolate and peanut butter. But this recipe was cloyingly sweet, tasted mostly just the peanut butter element, and the icing left a waxy mouth feel. A lot of steps/layering for a messy, disappointing finish. I’d rather use a high quality box mix. I won’t make these again. Live and learn.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Spray 13x9 pan with Baker's Secret. In a bowl, combine 1C of peanut butter...crunchy is best...with one egg and 1C sugar into a paste.  Spread the paste evenly in pan, top with brownie batter.  Bake 3-5 minutes longer than the box calls for.  Cool and top with anything or, nothing!  I like caramel, melted or, frosting.  Awesome tried n' true recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Good but sweet. Small square will do
In reading other comments I was wary about the texture with straight pb so I blended 1/2 cup powdered sugar with the pb until it was fluffy and cut back to 3 cups sugar in the chocolate frosting.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this for my husband’s birthday a few months ago and it was a big hit, though I could have used more time to chill it. The brownies are extremely rich and a little goes a long way. The only change I made is that I used about 1/3 the amount of confectioners sugar called for in the icing - mostly bc we didn’t have enough but I also didn’t want it to be too sweet. That may have affected the texture & explained why the icing didn’t set as well, but the taste was perfect.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I cut on sugar. 200g for the brownies, 150g for the frosting as it was already sweet enough. That way I could actually taste cacao :)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Ever since the pandemic, these have been in my baking library — good for parties, great to make ahead, always a crowd pleaser.

I add 1/4 cup less sugar to the brownies and don’t use heavy sweetened peanut butter. I don’t add salt and I use salted butter — a keeper!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This brownie base is excellent for ice cream sundae w cocoa (chocolate) sauce- 1/2 cup CP 1 cup sugar 1/2. cup water; heat to simmer over med heat until thick smooth and slow  bubbling for 10   minutes then add 1/2 to 1 teaspoon of vanilla and pinch of salt.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: After reading other comments I only used half the sugar and it is still quite sweet. So keep that in mind while baking

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this once and I don’t think marshmallows are necessary in the icing. I have not tried the version with marshmallows but it tastes fine without them and It was already very sweet with the powdered sugar.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: We only had 2 9x9 pans, not a 9x13 pan, so we made 1.5x the recipe and filled both pans. We were not sad. I definitely recommend keeping them refrigerated whenever they are not being consumed, which probably won't be long.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I am a type 1 diabetic and am making this. However with 6 cups of sugar (plus a cup in the peanut butter) i calculate i will need to inject nearly a half vial of insulin, several thousands units based on carb ratios, about 5 full syringes for a couple bars. That is more than an entire week of insulin for two brownies. I have never taken that much insulin at once in 30 years i have had the condition. Has anyone tried drasticaly reducing the sugar? This is a serious question. Peanut butter and chicolate are my faves.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My question is whether you can taste the marshmallow or and which texture it adds. I really don't like marshmallow flavor or that stiff, hollow texture of marshmallow chicks. Anyone tried these yet? Anyone have an alternative in mind?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: You can't taste the marshmallow.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Are there any suggestions on how to create the frosting without marshmallows (trying to avoid a run to the store for one ingredient!)?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Anytime I make brownies I put a glob of PB on mine and then some ice cream. Isn't this just basically the same thing with some extra chocolate frosting?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Someone asked about if you can use vegan marshmallows - we did since I am a vegetarian. We used Dandies brand (had to go to several different places before I found them in a health foods store), used 12 marshmallows instead of 10, did 2/3 cup of milk instead of 1/3, and only 2 cups of powdered sugar.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Bake 10-12 min, FYI

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I really enjoyed these brownies.  However, my not so fond of peanut butter husband wasn’t impressed.  Next time I may substitute peanut butter with caramel?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I substituted Cup to Cup gluten free flour.
the brownies were very sweet and fudge like.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: As expected, these were very sweet, but then again, I have a sweet tooth.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I cooked this exactly according to the recipe.  Absolute perfection.  

The marshmallows elevated the texture of the creamy chocolate topping.
We loved it served from frozen in delicate slivers with ice cream.  A fabulous dessert, but there is no way on earth I could eat a whole square in one go.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I made those for a friend'ds quarantine birthday party, and they were an amazing success. My main concern was that they were going to be too sweet, but it was just an illusion!

My only minus point would be that I wished that the brownies part was more chocolaty and maybe a tad bit less sweet. Maybe next time I'd cut off on sugar and flour a bit for the next recipe. 

Will definitely make those again!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Yum I made and shared with my coworkers at the hospital. Everyone loved, I did think the brownie was a little dry, but planning to make again!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I was really pleasantly surprised with this recipe! As other reviewers have noted, it is quite sweet and rich, so I cut the brownies into small squares. The brownie itself was great -- dense and moist. The marshmallows gave the frosting a great texture. I'd reduce the sugar in the frosting a bit; I used 3 cups of powdered sugar instead of 4, but might go down to 2 cups next time.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I did not enjoy the brownies. 
It was probably something I did wrong. They weren’t as fudgey as I like. The icing was too sweet for me. I think I’ll stick to classic brownies without icing. I did enjoy the peanut butter

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this last night. Excellent, although very sweet! Probably would cut into 24-32 bars. Anything more will create diabetes on a plate.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: These brownies are RICH!! A little bite goes a very, very long way. I cut these into 2x2 bites and froze them. They taste great frozen and thawed.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: For me these were basically just sugary fudge. My kid is allergic to egg so I replaced them with flax egg which might be to blame. I had two bites and felt sick with the amount of sugar. But the kid (for obvious reasons) loves them. If I tried again I’d also reduce the amount of sugar.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I was very satisfied with these. I did, however, in a moment of weakness, use a box of brownie mix. I don’t regret that decision. I also used a natural peanut butter with reduced sugar, and that also was a good decision. I recommend cutting these into very small squares. They are *almost* too much. I’ll probably make these again!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So due to all the negative reviews, I tweaked this recipe a bit and they came out delicious, particularly good the second day. I added extra vanilla to the batter and a half a cup less sugar. Used natural peanut butter. For the saccharin sweet frosting, I halved the butter, added a handful of extra marshmallows(maybe 6 or 7?) Squeezed half a lemon, a few shakes of Himalayan salt, and a splash of milk and 2.5 cups of frosting instead of the 4. They were perfection.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Hands down the best brownie recipe I have ever tried (and there have been MANY!)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: After reading the other reviews, I decided to make these using Mark Bittman’s brownie recipe as the bottom layer instead and WOW, I was not disappointed. These are a chocolate and peanut butter lover’s dream, but so sweet and fudgy that a little goes a long way. For anyone wondering about the tastiness of the frosting with those marshmallows tossed in... don’t hesitate. It was so good that I was scooping the leftovers out of the pan with my fingers, moaning a little every time I got a taste.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Brownie recipe was good. Topping was too sweet for my family's taste. We thought it would have been just fine to stop at the peanut butter layer.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I almost didn’t make these bc of negative reviews, but they were delicious! I did cut back on sugar in the frosting—you can start w 2 cups and add more to your taste. Also, watch the time on the brownie base—they’ll still look a little wet in the middle when you pull them out, but they are done.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe is certainly a lightening rod! I’m with the cooks who found these brownies delicious - and I don’t typically love super sweet sweets. I used peanut butter made from only peanuts and salt (i.e., no sugar added), which likely helped. Otherwise I made the recipe as directed.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’d been eyeing this recipe for awhile. I too was skeptical of the marshmallows but they make for a smooth yummy frosting. Very rich and best eaten with a fork.yum!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: i made this yesterday and for the frosting, accidentally only had 2 cups of sugar instead of 4. taste-tested and it was great w/o the remaining 2 cups. will continue making the frosting with 2 instead of 4! fantastic recipe otherwise. everyone loved them!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I was worried about the negative reviews in the comments, but figured the 4  star rating carried more weight. Glad I did! These were delicious and a decadent treat that I’m glad to have made for Christmas dessert. I do agree with commenters, however, that there’s no reason to use 4c of confectioners sugar for the frosting. I cut it in half to 2c and the frosting didn’t suffer at all!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Very nice. Needed to freeze more than 30 mins before slicing. Cut the sugar by a quarter in both layers and it was still extremely sweet. Would cut further.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: These are okay but nothing special. I wouldn’t make them again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Hello!
I made this exactly as the recipe stated. Notes from my house:
1 - the brownies needed to cook a bit longer, ended up about 27 - 30 minutes
2 - I would suggest sifting the powdered sugar first. The frosting was a bit lumpy even after stirring for quite some time.

They are really good, super rich. I would maybe even sprinkle some flaky sea salt on half the pan - for people who like that.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I was hesitant to make these because they looked to rich. Wrong. They are not overly rich. The base is a perfect compliment to the peanut butter and fudge icing. Don’t be afraid of the marshmallows. They simply make the fudge icing a little less rich. I made several different items for a bake sale and these were the biggest hit. I made them a week later for my family. It’s now a new favorite and on the Christmas cookie rotation. Simply put, they are better than Reese’s Peanut Butter Cups!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Brownie itself was really disappointing

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The brownie layer and frosting layer are amazing!  I used the Whole Foods organic peanut butter and it was too salty and too soft.  Next time I would use an old school one like Jiff or Skippy for their consistency and slightly sweeter taste.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I cooked another dessert to do a bit of a dessert showdown against another brownie recipe. Out of 6 people, five voted for these brownies. They are really good. A few things I did differently. First off the layer of brownie is somewhat thin, so I only cooked it for about 15 to 18 minutes. The recipe says 20 to 25, but I think that would make for a dry brownie. I also added one cup of chocolate chips to the brownie mixture. I used coconut flour because of gluten sensitivity. The frosting is good!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So while my guests loved these, I think there is something inherently wrong with the recipe. They are far more fudge bars than brownies. This is because of the repeated freezing steps. The butter solidifies in the brownie, and you never get a chewy gooey consistency again. So it’s a harder, fudgier cake like treat. Which is fine, since both peanut butter and marshmallow frosting is gooey, but the name and picture are misleading. I personally came here looking for brownies. Not hard fudge.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Brownies themselves not special but topping was fun

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Although delicious, this brownie resembles a piece of fudge more than a baked good. If one were to indulge in a slice as large as the pieces pictured, prepare yourself for an intense sugar rush!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
These brownies are absolutely delicious!  They are dense, moist and flavorful!  
I made them according to the recipe, but added 1/2 cup of cocoa, instead of 1/3.  I don't care for marshmallow frosting, so made a simple buttercream frosting of 3/4 cup butter, 1/3 cup cocoa, a couple drops of vanilla extract, 4-5 cups of confectioners sugar and a couple of tablespoons of half and half.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I made these today and they are wonderful! Next time I will half everything because it's too much for a family of 3. I am wondering how long it would be good for if frozen. 
Excellent recipe, great textured crunch, with the snappy chocolate top! 
Would be great for a big gathering!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The brownie base is delicious and the combination of chocolate and pb is undeniable. But! The icing layer is far too sweet. Keep the brownie as is but half the pb and the chocolate icing layer. I’ll make this again but will definitely half the topping layers.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Good recipe. The frosting is extremely sweet on its own but works in balance with the other layers. Both the top and bottom layers could be more chocolaty. As written they are like the milk chocolate in candy bars: very sweet and only mildly chocolaty. There’s no reason not to deepen the flavor by adding a little more cocoa, to either layer or both.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I would add that my 1-lb. bag on confectioners sugar, 454 grams, fell slightly short of the amount called for, but it was more than plenty.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: man. I had a bad feeling. with 4 eggs it’s just really thick and dry. not at all the kind of gooey fudgey brownie I am into. Would not recommend this recipe. If you do make it, maybe do 12 mins instead of 25. Way overcooked and pretty inedible at 20/25 for me.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: These are definitely decedent and very sweet, as many others have noted. But delicious and a hit with chocolate/peanut butter lovers. I browned the butter for the brownies which added a lovely nuttiness and depth of flavor. And I only ended up baking the brownies for 20-21 minutes. “Regular” sized marshmallows measured out to the correct weight.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made these last night. INCREDIBLE. Best brownie I have ever made. Love that there is no additional sugar in peanut butter. All hail marshmallow amendment! I am OBSESSED.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Love the topping, it's my new go-to for brownies! I honestly found the peanut butter to be a bit too rich. I scraped some off and ate the brownies with the fudge topping and found that better. I want to experiment with maybe adding just a bit of peanut butter to the fudge topping to get that flavour without the added richness.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: These brownies were not good. Bummed I wasted the ingredients. I was excited, too, because peanut butter and chocolate are my husbands favorite combination. Oh, well. I will keep looking and try somethings else.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe is amazing. I make it as is and the brownies get devoured every time. Highly recommend!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: They are tasty but way way way too rich. I can honestly only eat a very tiny slice. Probably not worth the effort, or cut the whole recipe in half and just make a very small batch.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Insanely sweet. Treat these more like fudge than brownies. The marshmallow chocolate frosting is very one note and cloying, I would recommend melting in some dark chocolate with the marshmallows and butter, and adding up to a tsp of kosher salt. I used Trader Joe’s “no stir” chunky peanut butter and it was perfect. The brownie itself was fudgier than I thought it would be. Would make a good brownie bite, if they were cubed and rolled in granulated or powdered sugar.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I hate to join the chorus here, but I agree that the brownie base was pretty terrible. I might try again with a basic box mix.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The brownies are not too sweet and are a nice foil for the sweeter toppings. I used half of the confectionary sugar and still found the chocolate topping to be cloying. I ended up drizzling the chocolate on top of the peanut butter and found that to be a good solution. Cutting the sweetness with a cream cheese or omitting the marshmallows would potentially be other options to explore if I made this again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Not a fan. There are better recipes out there. This recipe sounds similar to my favorite peanut butter brownie from a local bakery, but it didn't come together well. When the brownies are allowed to warm to room temperature, the peanut butter layer is too soft to hold the frosting, and it ends up being a mess. Perhaps the PB needs to have some sugar and butter in it to help firm it up. The brownie is like a dense cake - I am ok with that texture, but it didn't have much going on flavor wise.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: These were ok. I won't make them again. The tip someone else gave about not overcooking the brownie layer is important. The freezer dries out that layer and if it's too done to begin with it becomes much too dry. They are much better at room temp than cold. But the taste is mostly very sweet and very peanut buttery without much chocolate flavor. If I did make again, I'd cut the peanut butter in half and leave the brownie layer very moist. I'd probably skip the second freeze step too.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The photo enticed me, but as previously mentioned, this recipe was a letdown. The brownie was fudge-like--or would be considered fudge-like with a higher cocoa content. Due to limited confectioner's sugar, I used half the quantity listed. This meant that my bars were not quite as sweet as everyone else's, and the frosting consistency wasn't quite right. My frosting was more of a glaze. Should I have whipped it in mixer instead of hand whisking? Flavors and layer consistency were disappointing.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Waaaaaay too sweet. I even cut some of the sugar in half and it was intolerable. I took them in to work and they did not get eaten.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Followed the directions exactly and they were a total hit. My kids gave them a 10/10.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Don’t listen to any of the haters. It’s a gooey, delicious, mess and you deserve it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Everyone who tries these loves them! One friend even called me the next day to see if I had any left over that she could have. :D Chewy brownie layer with a thick, creamy frosting yumminess. Very sweet, though, so I cut them in pretty small pieces. They freeze well too. Just freeze in a single layer and then stacked with parchment in between.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this with almond flour and didn't have any marshmallows, so I used the NYT chocolate buttercream recipe. I cut the sugar in the brownies in half and made a half-recipe in an 8x8 pan. I still used the amount of cocoa powder called for in the whole recipe--1/3 cup. They were delicious! Everyone loved them.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: We had high hopes for this recipe being chocolate peanut butter combo lovers here, but we were sorely disappointed. So disappointed that we threw out the whole batch after each having one bc they weren’t even remotely worth the calories in our opinion. The brownie was a spongey texture that wasn’t sweet enough, and the frosting was way overly sweet. Somehow these two things did not balance each other out. A miss for us.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Loved the peanut butter and fudgey topping layer, but the brownies weren’t quite chocolatey enough. I would make again but use a different cakey brownie for the bottom layer. I do think it needs to be a cakey-type brownie rather than a fudgey brownie (which I otherwise prefer) for this particular recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: These were amazing! Even with me screwing up the frosting (I added a 1/2 cup of milk instead of 1/3. Really need to start wearing my readers when I am reading a recipe!). After reading the comments I pre-sliced my portion so half would be room temp and didn't find it changed the sweetness, actually preferred them cold.  After taking one bite my husband declared this is a keeper.  I will definitely be making these again!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I love these but I’m going to try them with a thinner layer of peanut butter.
I use my own brownie recipe

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Can I make this without icing?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The “brownie” collapsed into a fudge-y block and the peanut butter was too runny (I did drain the extra oil). The amount of sugar was overwhelming even with cutting it by 25-30%. So disappointing

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: On step 3 I find a better consistency and it easier to just add all the ingredients in at once.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious!  I used low sodium chicken broth instead of water in steps 2 and 4.
To keep the chicken skin crispy, I used a large cast iron skillet for all the cooking, added 3/4 cup of broth instead of 1/2 cup water in step 2, made sure that the skin was above the liquid, and cooked the chicken in a 325 oven, UNCOVERED in step 3. After this, I removed the chicken, added the potatoes and more broth, then laid the chicken atop the potatoes, and put it back in the oven, again uncovered, for step 4.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: @Gary Came here to ask who actually has a Dutch oven large enough to hold six thighs in a single layer plus baby potatoes submerged in liquid that doesn’t submerge the thighs?!? I think the answer is what you suggest (using a normal sized Dutch oven)!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: @Andyb Staub 5.75 oval iron coq au vin. It was my one pan 6 months caretaking a remote property - used everything from frying eggs to stews, soups, and even Bittman’s no-knead bread.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: The recipe seems odd to me - you go to all the trouble of browning it and then submerge that lovely crisp skin in the liquid. Doesn’t it get soggy?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: My question exactly. SO many recipes for braised chicken have you brown the chicken then cover. But in my experience that makes the skin go all rubbery. And then you look at the photo and it looks all crispy. Food styling?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: In lieu of pine nuts, pumpkin or sunflower seeds also work (for those with tree nut allergies or a more limited budget)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent. Followed advice here: added the potatoes in step 3, check. I got tender potatoes and not-overdone chicken. Also, pulled off the chicken skin and crisped it in a pan to serve. Overall this recipe seems pretty adaptable. Added carrots to the aromatics; they were tasty. I Step-3-cooked it in a 325° oven for an hour instead of on the stove top, perfect. I used lacinato kale but any sturdy green would be equally good. Most important: The Teens Ate It. Will make again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Just made this. I didn’t see any reason to cook the thighs and then add potatoes later. Too much time. Put in the potatoes at step three. Then used spinach. Husband liked it. It was an easy weekday meal.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I did this, too -- added the potatoes with the chicken. Twenty minutes later both were done, threw in a bunch and a half of sliced kale leaves. Should be ready any minute!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This works if you brown the chicken skin down first and remove from pan. Then cook the vegies a bit. Using an oven proof pan, place the chicken thighs on top of the vegies and pour in just enough broth and wine to cover the vegies. Oven braise uncovered at 325 for 1 hour and 15 minutes. The chicken skins remain crisp, the chicken and potatoes are tender. Add the spinach on the stove top after removing the cooked chicken. I would also toast the pine nuts use as a garnish

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This dish features a unique combo of flavors... loved the vinegar, cloves, and saffron. I removed the chicken before adding the potatoes and stirred the whole thing, then rested the chicken on top. It worked nicely. Delish!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I like this recipe a lot. Like the others, I put the potatoes in at step 3. I also used chicken broth instead of water and cut down majorly on the oil. I also did not add any butter to the recipe at the end (I really do not think it needs it.) I also used swiss chard instead of kale - it's what I could find. Overall, good recipe - super warm and lovely to eat on a cool day.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This was terrific!  I followed the recipe as written, except for substituting baby spinach for the kale, and eliminating the butter at the end (sauce was already rich and thick).  I used a wide, large, round dutch oven which allowed for the browned skin to mostly remain above the liquid.  In Step 4, I added enough water to allow the potatoes to cook, but not cover them.  At the end, I continued cooking uncovered for an additional 10-15 minutes to thicken the sauce and soften the potatoes.  Yum!!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
