commentBody: This does not work for all the reasons mentioned already. Wasted expensive ingredients such as saffron, pine nuts, and white wine on a dish that was way too acidic, had undercooked potatoes and overcooked chicken with soggy skin.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody:
This was a little frustrating. It tasted great, but with the lid covered, the amount of wine and water - half cup and then to cover the potatoes - it was like whole chicken and potato chunks sitting in soup. This made the pine nuts just floating there pretty pointless. Really they should have you take the lid off at step 3 to cook down the liquid. Also, I agree with others, there is no need to it the  potatoes in separately at step 4. 

The taste was great, but sooo much thin liquid.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: BAC. I’m not a fan of kale, either. I might try arugula or spinach. I’m leaning towards the arugula.  I think it has sturdier leaves, more like the kale.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Excellent! Took the chicken out in Step 4 and turned up the heat, as I couldn't picture the potatoes cooking in 20 mins at low temp, Used chicken broth instead of water - not sure how much, eyeballed it. The pine nuts seemed to dissolve into the dish by the end. The cloves and large no. of bay leaves were brilliant, would not have thought to do that. Added fresh corn off the cob with about 3 mins left in cooking. (It's August, trying to use it almost daily!)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: the potatoes are nowhere near cooked, even after double the time recommended.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: They need to cook at a higher temp than the very low chicken simmer. And be mostly covered with liquid. And the fresher the potatoes the shorter the cooking time.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I’m not sure if we didn’t use the right baby potatoes (ours were kind of waxy) but this didn’t have the greatest flavor for the time it took. Seemed like a little too much vinegar, the pine nuts lost their flavor in the process and the broth was too thin. We won’t cook this again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Seems very similar to kale, sausage potato soup, minus the cream. A nice addition to kale, because it can be so tough, is to massage salt into the kale before adding it to the pot. Salting the kale before adding it to the soup,  makes the kale less fibrosis and easier to chew, and thus, a more enjoyable meal.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Hi Gary.  So I tried your modification of using the oven vs. stove top for steps 3 & 4.  Not sure if I missed something but the potatoes we're no where near done after 20 minutes.  I put the lid on and back to the oven for a bit, anxious to see how this works out :D

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Odd instructions: the potatoes didn’t cook through at all, so I had to turn up the heat for an extra 15 minutes. That didn’t do it either so I threw it in the oven at 375 F for 10 minutes. Additionally, the suggestion to add water to almost cover the potatoes left the recipe extremely soupy - the gorgeous photograph depicted in the recipe is not at all what this recipe ended up looking like.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe just doesn’t work! 
We trust the NYT app for most of our meals, with the certainty that by following a recipe, we will always succeed at making great tasting food. 
This dish was a complete failure, despite following the recipe: potatoes were undercooked, chicken was soggy, and the flavors felt completely random in the end. Don’t waste expensive chicken and saffron for this recipe! There are many better chicken thighs options on the app.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I too found this recipe odd...and not worth the time or worthy of the ingredients. Everything cooked properly, but it was very bland & watery. This was the first NYT Cooking recipe that was a disappointment.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This is a fab recipe AS WRITTEN. I don't get the crispy skin fetish. You brown for flavor, not always for crisp skin. Cook per instuctions, you end up with a lovely subtle sauce/broth, anchored by cloves & bay, undertone of safron, somewhat sweet, somewhat tart, with perfect thighs and skin that melts in your mouth. Delicious the first day; same two days later. No need for stock. Onions may take longer. Push potatoes into the liquid, then time them to taste. Absolutely Spanish! And fab!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This was so delicious and we could not stop soaking up the broth with a baguette. I did swap out the pine nuts for sunflower seeds, spinach for kale, and I threw in a cinnamon stick (but I don't think it added much).  I did not have saffron.  Finished in a 350 oven for an hour and a half at step 3, adding in the potatoes and some carrot chunks final 40 minutes.  Everyone loved this! Unusual combination of spices and flavors.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I learned to cook from my mom and use different methods. Like doing an egg wash with some Progresso bread crumbs. They are amazing when the coating of bread crumbs are mushy and flavorful.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: WAY too much lquid. The Skins got soaked.  The potatoes took 3x the time suggested.  It seems like no one tried this first.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Again, I ask, why the kale?  It's a noxious weed that is bitter and lends nothing to any recipe.  I made this recipe without the weed, adding some spinach.  You might do the same.  Sam:  Please stop trying to shove kale down our throats.  Recipe gets two stars because the recipe developer wants us to use kale.  STOP.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Tastes fine but nothing like the photo.  The cooking liquid is still there.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody:
Since I gave this recipe 1 star; a first in my NYT use; I guess I should explain myself.  I was excited to try it since it has so many things I like to eat and cook; chicken thighs, celery, garlic, cloves, cinnamon and potatoes.  IT WAS BLAND, BLAND, BLAND.  And I added extra of all the things I like; extra celery, extra cloves AND cinnamon.  AND IT WAS STILL BLAND.  Also, it was really soupy.

For the first time in forever, I'm not looking forward to the leftovers.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Would another green besides kale also work? Sorry, not a kale fan here. Otherwise it sounds delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: You could try chard, collards, or spinach as a substitute.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: whenever i see a recipe calling for kale, i substitute swiss chard. i don't see why you couldn't in this one.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Chard

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: The flavors sound wonderful, but how come you cook the chicken until it's done and then cook it 25 minutes LONGER with the potatoes and greens?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody:
A very nice technique for cooking chicken! Tender, and just removed skins as they do get soggy (I think the browning functions to flavour the dish?).
Had to simmer for a while to evaporate some liquid off, as there was quite a bit, even when not adding a lot with potatoes.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This was amazing! I added a few carrots about halfway through since I just had them around, and used spinach instead of kale. It worked like a charm. Totally adding this to the rotation, and looking at playing with other seasoning options. Loved what's called for, but the technique has so much flexibility.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night and the potatoes were completely raw after 20 minutes of cooking. Also, adding water to the sauce really diluted the flavor. The chicken was delicious and fall off the bone. Next time I would par-cook the potatoes before adding, and add just a small amount of chicken stock.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Gary’s idea of putting in oven and putting in potatoes early worked great.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Typical British, somewhat bland and heavy on the grease. Cooked it exactly as specified, wasn’t bad, wasn’t great. It’s basically braised chicken, may be more boiled in a stock with potatoes and greens (which were good). Not my favorite, thankfully we all have different tastes, it makes the world more interesting.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Added a can of white beans to help plump it up, and wish there’d been more kale (double?) but otherwise a tasty and easy to make dish.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Added carrots, otherwise followed recipe to a T. Surprisingly flavorless - not bad, but far from delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This one didn’t work out well for us. As others said, it was a bit bland even with all the vinegar and spices. It had great potential, but in the end it seemed that roasting these ingredients might have made a more successful, flavorful dish, rather than this method. Great ingredients, so-so execution.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Reading the comments, I too put the potatoes in when the chicken went back in. Stuck with  the recipe and left it on the stovetop at lowest heat, which produced delicious moist chicken but the liquid needed some thickening and I didn't have time to boil it down.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Made this as written except I didn’t have the wine. I used chicken broth.  It was absolutely delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This recipe was such a delight.  The broth was divine, made me feel like I was in SOHO in a magical night in London.  The saffron made it.  I didn’t have pine nuts, so omitted those.  I used only one yellow onion and a shallot.  I also didn’t have whole cloves, but the cinnamon worked.  Don’t skip the saffron!  Bravo!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I used red wine because didn’t have any white. Don’t do that, I guess. Also be careful with salt — the broth seemed like it needed more salt so I added a bunch little did I know the potatoes had absorbed all of the salt and it made for an extremely salty experience. I’m not sure about this one. Will try again with saffron and the correct color wine and report back.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I just cooked this, actually it's still cooking and I am a bit confused. Between steps 3 and 4 there is no mention of raising the heat. So after opening the pot to add potatoes I lost the simmer I had going. Put the lid on thinking it would return and 20 mins later pulled the lid no simmer potatoes hard. Just turned the heat up to medium and put them back on for another 10 mins. I must have done something wrong but I can't see it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Despite my problems last night the recipe came out great! Totally delicious I would make it again but as suggested in the comments I would cook the potatoes with the chicken next time.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe, but following other notes, I put the potatoes in the third step.  At the end I removed the chicken and stuck it under the broiler as it has lost some of its crispness. The sauce was very good. Happy to use garlic, lacinato kale, and parsley from the garden.  It cooked much faster than the recipe stated.  All in all about 45 minutes.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Really delicious! Was concerned it would be too greasy, I did remove some of the oil/chicken fat after browning the thighs. With the wine and water and chicken juices it thinned it all out. Added the potatoes after 30 minutes, cooked perfectly after another 20 minutes, felt if I added them in stage 3 as suggested in other posts they'd be mush. Had no pine nuts, didn't miss them. Did use the saffron.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Odd recipe - good sauce but an excess of liquid so at the end I had to resort to cornstarch to thicken it so the chicken wasn't awash on the plate. Will try Gary's method next time.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I’ll reiterate what some have mentioned: undercooked potatoes, soggy skin that was formerly crisp, sliced not diced onions? Chicken was “fine”, but had to chuck the potatoes and kale mess. I regret using my pine nuts and white wine on this dish.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This recipe simply doesn’t taste good. I wish I understood it before I made it. In the end, it makes no sense to put pine nuts with potatoes (mush on mush), cloves, vinegar, bay leaves, and saffron (subtle spices cancelled by acidity). Top it off with too much onions, huge chunks of garlic, and a ton of celery (?!). The flavors and consistency were bad. Also the timing and cooking directions are wrong, making it impossible to succeed. This looks better on instagram than it tasted on our plates!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: To me, this recipe was lacking in flavor;. I have a Patricia Wells recipe for chicken in tarragon vinegar I much prefer. FYI, I followed suggestions for cooking uncovered in the oven, with the chicken atop the potatoes and onions to preserve the crispy skin, using chicken broth instead of water, etc. I doubled the amount of pine nuts. The cook on the chicken was perfect, but the potatoes were slightly underdone, even though they cooked for an hour at 325 F. Sauce was not flavorful enough.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Pleasant but not very interesting. I agree with others here that there should be a lot less liquid, and that the chicken should be cooked so that the skin remains crispy. I’d boost the spicing next time, too.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody:
Way too much liquid, and with cover on and low temp, no reduction so you end up with soggy soupy stew, and yes, pine nuts are lost. I love the acidity but this was better the next day drained and served as a salad over lettuce. 
Adjustments- cook potatoes separately.  Avoid the water part.  Add potatoes once cooked and if necessary add wine or broth as needed.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Could not agree with this more. Soupy, soggy mess with some decent bite in the acidity of the Sherry but an otherwise really low return on long cook time dish.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This was a delicious surprise. I used ingredients mostly as listed, substituting chicken stock for water. Modified cooking as per others: in Dutch oven, added potatoes in step 3 and then placed, uncovered, in 325 degree oven for 65 mins. Chicken skin stayed above liquid and remained crisp, potatoes cooked perfectly, pine nuts retained texture. Sauce was thin but tasty (I never added more liquid and chose not to add butter). Chicken dried out a bit on reheating for leftovers.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Surely the “cool kids” of London after waiting “hours to score a garden table” would be deeply disappointed by this meal were it prepared as instructed here. Without taking emergency measures similar to those described in nearly every comment below the recipe leads to a sodden, soupy disaster. Add a Step 6 to this recipe: once you realize dinner’s in jeopardy, throw the whole thing onto a sheet pan and cook at 385 for 45 minutes.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly according to recipe and it was delicious. The cloves and saffron add great depth. I'd pour out all but 2 TBSP of the oil after Step 1, as the combo of 3 TBSP of oil and the fat rendered from the chicken makes for a lot of fat.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody:
Blandly terrible; flavors weak but unbalanced.

Made this exactly as directed, except for a few of the oft-mentioned tweaks (potatoes in earlier, broiled chicken after dish was cooked); had to microwave potatoes even after cooking dish for an extra 30 minutes.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I like this recipe a lot. Have made it twice. The second time I decided to speed it up and cook the chicken and potatoes on medium heat with a nice boil. I didn’t notice any flyover loss and it shaved about 30 min off the overall duration.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I'm not sure what I missed with this one, but it took SO long for the potatoes to cook, and left behind an enormous amount of broth. The potatoes were essentially boiled potatoes - which is fine, but I'm not sure what the benefit is of adding them to the Dutch oven. The nice crisp chicken skin was made soggy by the braise. All in all, this was not worth all the time and effort.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Jan’s note is spot on. Use oven proof frying pan. Once chicken thighs’ skins are crisp remove from frying pan. Add all of the vegetables including the small potatoes. Cut larger one's in half. I added baby carrots cut in half length wise adding them to the vegetable blend. Add the wine, the sherry vinegar and the chicken broth. Let it simmer for 10-12 minutes on medium-low heat. Then place chicken thighs on top and finish in the oven in an oven proof frying pan for 60-70 minutes @ 325 degrees.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I followed the directions exactly, but after 20 minutes the potatoes were barely cooked. I think it would make a lot more sense to add them with the chicken, or pre-boil them.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Wonder if I did something wrong- after I added the potatoes and water it took FOREVER for it to cook down. Must’ve slipped up on something

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Mushy pine nuts inspire despair.  “Mush on mush” captures it exactly.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I don’t understand how there can be this many ingredients, and it can taste this bland and boring. Will not be making again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This was kind of bland and agree that the potatoes need to go in much sooner; they were undercooked when made as written.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: If the chicken thighs are already browned nicely, cooking them a total of another 70 minutes seems like a very lot.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: IMHO, I don't think olive oil should be exposed to high heat.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This recipe is great, if you enjoy eating bland chicken with soggy skin. Really reminiscent of chicken you’d get at a hotel conference banquet.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: What if I seared or broiled the chicken AFTER the braising to crisp it up? Working the problem of an otherwise fantastic dish. I’ve rarely had vegetables flavor each other like this.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I made this using whole chicken leg and thigh. It was excellent. I did add the potatoes in at the end of step 2 placing the chicken on top of everything, and used homemade chicken bone broth instead of water. At 50 minutes I added the kale and 2 handfuls of halved shiitake mushrooms. It needed 1 hour and 15 minutes to cook. Deeply browned the skin in step one, and it maintained a great texture through the cooking, and seared in flavor and moisture in the meat. I recommend!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. I followed suggestions to cook uncover in a 325 degree oven for step 3. Then take chicken out add potatoes nestle chicken on top then back in the oven uncovered. I only used a total of 1 cup of chicken broth, (rather than water.) It allowed the chicken to remain crisp and the sauce and potatoes were tender and tasty. Pine nuts remained toothsome. A definite keeper.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Yes, recipes are road maps, one can choose alternatives, but if you have a map to go to NYC and you end up in Chigaco, you've made up a different map entirely. This is a lovely recipe, with subtle, complex results. Maybe not for everyone, but no recipe is. Skin as prepared here is valued in many cuisines, both for texture and for taste. As for cooking times, cook for the result you want and forget about the clock. Step-by-step this recipe works. Don't worry: you won't overcook the thighs.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody:
I used skinless, boneless thighs and browned first, removing from the pan. After sauteeing the vegetables and adding the wine, spices and pine nuts, the chicken went back in again for a bit to soak up the wine and then I pulled it back out to cook the potatoes at a higher heat. When the potatoes were closer to done, I tucked the chicken back in deep and turned it down to low to steam a bit. I also added Cabernet instead of white wine for more flavor and chicken stock instead of the water. I love kale so I didn’t sub there. Multi-colored potatoes make this dish even prettier. 
It all cooked to perfection and we really enjoyed the flavor. The whole cloves are important to this dish, I think.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: In what universe do baby potatoes, halved, cook to done - at the lowest possible heat - in 20 minutes? The answer is - not this one. The chicken is well overdone - the potatoes, well underdone. I've got some good soup stock, some sloppy chicken, and quite a few baby potatoes that need another 5 minutes in a steamer. And a question - has anyone else had my results?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Brian, Bingo!!  Exactly my experience.  Very disappointing.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: How does one measure 3.oz of kale?...asking for a friend.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I have a kitchen scale and actually weighed it.  It was exactly the bunch of Kale that I bought (with the stems removed).  So a medium bunch?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Would spinach work in lieu of Kale?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I despise my electric stove and do not trust its “lowest possible heat.”  Can someone recommend an oven time/temperature equivalent?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Nina: yes it does get soggy!  I’m doing it now and it’s interesting but I’m pulling the chicken out and broiling a little to get that crisp back...

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: in recipes like this you brown the chicken skin to get the rendered fat and fond to cook the other ingredients in. so once the dish is done, you could broil the chicken pieces to try to re-crisp the skin, or, you can remove the skin and just crisp the skin alone (so as not to overcook the chicken) and add it back to the dish as a garnish when serving. because yeah, i agree - crispy chicken skin is SO good! :)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Used Statlers because I had them. I put the kale in with the potatoes since 5 mins of steaming seemed too short for such a hearty green. Also added 1/2 cup chicken stock instead of topping up with water at potato cooking phase.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Very disappointed in the finished dish.  Not flavourful... Didn’t have pine nuts - used roasted pumpkin seeds. Maybe used low sodium chicken stock rather than plain water.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I haven’t made this yet but I will because I am a sucker for all of the ingredients, especially Sherry vinegar. Sherry vinegar can do no wrong. However, I definitely will not cook the thighs and then the potatoes but will in fact do this in a large cast-iron pan in the oven, with the potatoes, greens, garlic nestled in between. Then no soggy will occur with no broiler necessary to avoid it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Be sure to taste for salt before you add the potatoes, as they will need to absorb the salt. I diced my onion, as I don't enjoy the stringiness of sliced in a braised dish. A cast-iron pot will probably provide better caramelization than I achieved in my non-stick, as well. The recommended seasoning seemed a bit bland - or maybe my spices were too old.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I have not made this yet, but maybe someone can explain something that I have seen in other recipes in addition to this one: step 3 says "heat until cooked through." Then step 4 says "cook another 20 minutes."  Does that make sense to anyone?  Wouldn't doing this make the chicken hopelessly overcooked?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Onions of what size! We need details!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: The amount of onion you use won't really affect the overall recipe, so do whatever feels right to you. They will almost melt away by the time you're done, so don't be afraid to use 2 of whatever size you have.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Better yet would be rapini (broccolini).

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I recommend this recipe, although various steps took significantly longer than described.  It took 15 minutes to achieve golden brown skin; took 40 minutes (not 20) for the potatoes to be cooked.  I would also like to know whether/the extent to which the dish is meant to be served with the broth.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I made this two nights ago forgot I was supposed to buy thighs and purchased chicken legs. When I was preparing the meal I reached into my freezer to grab some parsley-I had written parsley on the bag but when I started snipping, I realized it was cilantro! I picked out quite a bit, got the parsley and added it.  The end result was delicious, the tang of the cilantro was subtle and delightful. Next time a little of both.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This was a real disappointment.  Very greasy, not very flavorful or complex.  And certainly not worth the time and trouble.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I made this in a pressure cooker and thought it was very good. I’ll make it again. Only changes:  eliminated the additional water (there’s enough moisture in the recipe to bring up to pressure without it) and toasted the pine nuts before adding.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This was an easy and very tasty recipe. I followed the advice of others and added potatoes at step three. Didn’t have while cloves, so used a 1/4 tsp of ground cloves  and a pinch of saffron. The end result was amazing.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Made this per recipe (added broth instead of extra water for potatoes), and it’s my partners favorite thing I’ve ever cooked. Amazing recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: We made the recipe almost as listed but we still have silverbeet growing in the garden so used that instead of the kale--the chicken and potatoes were perfectly cooked; great flavor with the cloves and saffron but found the result quite fatty so next time we'd use less oil; not add the butter.  We didn't find that the parsley was needed as parsley can be a bit bitter which might have detracted from the other fabulous flavors.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: In the first step, the chicken pieces began bleeding - too much for comfort. I've made chicken like this before but never experienced this. Is this a result of the method of cooking (similar to frying)?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Some variations....  chicken stock, cut up fingerling potatoes, red wine vinegar, rose wine & spinach.  For those concerned about crisp skin, run the chicken under the broiler while the spinach cooks down (covered) in the last 5 minutes.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Flavor of the gravy is delicious; a little different in a good way from the pine nuts.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Used rice vinegar to substitute for the sherry, which was fine, and slivered almonds that held their shape and a little crunch. Didn't add butter at the end. Would definitely add the potatoes earlier - chicken got over cooked.  The warm spices and vinegar were an different flavour profile for us, which I found  more compelling when it cooled a little, developing quite a fruity taste. I'm going to try the round 2 leftovers with halved pitted prunes added to balance the acidity.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Can this be done in a slow cooker?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This was really good! The allspice gives it a subtle but interesting flavor, the dish was even better the next day with leftovers. Note: I delete the kale, no substitution, eliminated step 4 - added the potatoes in step2 and let it cook on the stovetop while the oven preheated.  Then followed the comment idea to bake it instead of stovetop cooking. Covered, 1 hr. Delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: chicken broth, other greens, sunflower or other seeds, add potatoes in step 3

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody:
I made these adaptations:
Used a cast iron skillet. 
Added broth instead of water
Put in oven at 325 for 40 minutes
Took chicken out of pan after that and put baby potatoes underneath. Add broth if liquid was diminished (1/2 cup?) Put chicken back on top and roasted for 20 more minutes. 
Put kale in on stovetop to wilt

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I thought it lacked some acidity in the end. Added a little white wine vinegar to each bowl as we served and that was great!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Edit: add potatoes before reading chicken, leave top off so skin remains crisp

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I made this almost as directed but only had (or needed) three chicken thighs. I have a 6 1/2 qt Dutch oven and six would not have fit. I did brown the chicken on both sides, and cut the potatoes in thirds as they were small red potatoes, not fingerlings. There was a lot of sauce, almost like soup, but delicious. Even my picky 8-yr-old granddaughter ate the chicken and potatoes. I’ll make this again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Made last nite with what I had. One purple, one russet potato. Sauce divine. Had no celery or kale. No matter.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Huge fan! Next time would omit the pine nuts and substitute cinnamon for the clove.  The amount of liquid is important to adhere to.  The end result was a perfect sauce to accompany the small potatoes and thighs.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Try it with chard instead of kale.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I would like to use a different vinegar, something other than sherry vinegar. Any suggestions?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Use a brazier, use broth instead of water. Make sure that the skin is above the liquid, and roast the chicken in a 325 oven, UNCOVERED in step 3. Remove the chicken, add the potatoes and more broth, then lay the chicken atop the potatoes, and put it back in the oven, again uncovered, for step 4.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Didn’t find this one to be a winner. The flavor profile wasn’t great. The sheet pan chicken with feta and dill is much better.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: The chicken was very tender and juicy, cooked following suggestions from others: bake in oven, uncovered, on top of potatoes. I could have taken a little more time browning the chicken but it had a nice golden color, was very tasty. My family found the pine nuts to be superfluous not adding much to the dish. I browned the chicken (I used breasts, legs and thighs), in my cast iron pan, then after softening the celery, onions and garlic and adding wine and vinegar, I placed everything in 9x13 pan

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: We added more of everything except water; cooked browned chicken with skin above water for about 30 minutes; removed chicken and put in both potatoes and kale and a touch of chicken stock, then put the chicken back on top, skin up; after 30 minutes, removed lid and cooked for 10 more; finished with 5 minutes under the broiler. Nice blend of subtle flavors, but bordering on bland, especially for all that work. Didn’t taste the pine nuts; felt it needed Something Else to give it zing...

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Whoa! What a change of flavors for this Midwestern cook! As cooked with mods below, it was delightful and a welcomed change to our usual chicken rotation. I added the potatoes at step 3 and added chicken broth in lieu of water as some have recommended.  I used a few dashes of homemade red wine vinegar in step 2. Cheers!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Delicious flavor. The cloves add much to the taste and aroma.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody:
Made without cloves. Didn’t have any. 2/23/2021
Delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Really tasty. I followed the instructions to brown in a cast iron skillet and roast in the oven with chicken broth in a Dutch oven. I used fennel and leaned on the saffron, with a 1/4 teaspoon of allspice.  I kept the liquid to a minimum—you can always add more. I finished the dish on the stove top. I omitted the pine nuts. The flavors really melded.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Anyone try this in an InstaPot yet?  Recommendations for cooking times, etc?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I should have read the notes before I cooked this. I had to double the time for the potatoes and then when that didn't work, I remove the chicken and added chicken broth and cooked without the chicken for 15 minutes more...turned the heat up a bit too. I think step 4 needs more clarification about the temp to cook the potatoes...in hindsight, seems like it should have been raised.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I used chicken broth and this was so DELICIOUS!! I also put the chicken on top of the potatoes as an earlier commenter suggested. The flavor is really something-- a huge hit here. Everything else I did exactly as in the recipe. I think this will be a keeper.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Thank you Zach, well put. I too put it all together, then did what you suggested. My potatoes went into the oven, and the chicken into a cast iron skillet to brown. Nice flavor , just a few tweaks. Makes me want to visit London and enjoy the real deal.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Okay. I didn’t take the advice in the recipe and added way too much broth which made the skin a slimy consistency. However, I removed the broth when reheating the leftovers in the iron skillet and that was pretty good. Overall, I would recommend cooking the chicken separately.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Following the very useful notes, I substituted broth for water and decreased the quantity. I cooked the potatoes and kale separately before putting them under the chicken parts and cooked them all uncovered in the oven.  I should have reduced the cooking time a bit.  I toasted the pine nuts and added them at the e d with the butter and parsley.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Yummy but not mind blowing. Followed Gary’s suggestions an d got a lovely crispy skin. Added potatoes about 30 mins into first bout in the oven, kept them in around 35 mins total and boiled them on the stove without the chicken for about 5 mins after the pot came out of the oven; they were still a tad raw. Definitely a weekend recipe- 2 hours including prep!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious but my potatoes took at least an hour to cook so next time, following the advice to cook the dish in the oven, I will add the potatoes first, for half an hour and then add the chicken for another 30 minutes. I might cut down on the sherry vinegar by 1 tbsp so the flavor of the saffron comes through better.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I’ve been ‘traveling’ the world through NY Times recipes and hands down this is the best dish I’ve made so far - not counting the most popular chocolate chip cookie recipe. The flavors are subtle yet interesting, incredibly complimentary. This felt satisfying, delicious, healthy, comforting, and a way to take something that may have tasted pretentious and make it like a home cooked meal with all the crazy friends I have in different parts of the world. Who knew, from just a chicken dinner.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Tasty - but potatoes weren't really cooked in twenty minutes.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Followed the recipe as directed and it was kind of bland.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: An easy one pot meal but the flavour was meh. Needs more something. Would define use more kale if I make it again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!  As others recommended, I added the potatoes at step 3.  The combination of spices along with the pine nuts was outstanding.  Chopped up baby kale at the end.  Very nice weeknight meal.  Will definitely make again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Was just ok.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I made it just the way "Gary" (most helpful comment) advised, and it was perfect.  Don't worry about having too much broth--it's delicious.  The saffron is key, but we grow our own (as well as potatoes), so that's easy for me to say.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I loved this! I did make two adjustments that others suggested: 1) I added the potatoes at step 3 and 2) I put it uncovered in the oven at 325° for 1 hour 10 minutes then returning the stove to add the kale and butter. Maybe it was my Dutch oven, but the liquid allowed for covering the veggies while placing the chicken such that the crisp skin was just above the surface of the braising liquid. This yielded the silkiest most savory amazing sauce. The flavors infused the potatoes and the chicken.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody:
Fabulous. And SO easy. Definitely keeping this in mind when I need a super easy and impressive dinner party dish. Just be sure to serve with hearty country sourdough bread. The braising liquid is very very tasty. Great for sopping up with some bread.

I too followed the advice of others and put my potatoes in at step 3, didn’t add more liquid. I put the dish in a 325 oven for 1 hour and everything was perfect. I did remove the chicken and stuck them under the broiler while I mixed in the kale.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I always follow recipe as written to begin with and then adjust. I used a lidded sauté pan, which made adjusting the amount of liquid to avoid covering the crispy skin easier. The chicken, braising liquid and greens (I like spinach) are fantastic. My issue is the potatoes. I found braised potatoes bland and difficult to time their cooking. My solution was to roast them. I thought the crispy potatoes worked well with the crispy skinned chicken.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This tasted nice, but felt too thin. Loved the heavy use of bay and cloves; may try again but would definitely add potatoes/chicken at same time, and not cook covered.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody:
Next time roast the pine nuts and add towards the end.
chicken stock not water was good idea
spinach also good idea
carrots with potatoes good idea
need to make it a little thicker of a sauce
maybe add peas
good for a cold night

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Used skinless thighs and some white meat tenderloins. Cloves AND cinnamon. Skipped the pine nuts. Delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: A winning dish! Made a few small adjustments -- added anchoives with the onions and celery, omitted the cloves/cinnamon, added olives, and used spinach for my kale adverse family. The sauce was gorgeous and was happily soaked up with some fresh bread. This is a wonderfully adaptable recipe! Will be making it on the regular.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Chicken was cooked through after 20-25 minutes, 45 minutes would have way overcooked it. I removed the chicken & added potatoes after 25 minutes. Placed the chicken back on top and then continued to cook as directed from the “add potatoes” step.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Followed all the steps to the letter except that I left out the kale (did not substitute). I used cinnamon in lieu of clove (as per the recipe) and it came out very nicely. Flavors were very nice and my dinner guests loved it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: big hit! soggy skin bc I browned it and then put in fridge, don't do that and make sure it's crispy!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This was the perfect meal, on a very cold Sunday evening. I didn't have much celery so added 1 parsnip and 1 carrot, very good. I had no saffron, I would like to try with that, but used a variety of otters spices, and it was delicious. It's easy to make and the house smells so good, while it simmers.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Interesting. But I was researching figs ...

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I actually have all the ingredients including a pouch of raw pine nuts from the grocery store. Do i toast them before making the dish?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Chicken thighs is one of the most underrated food choices. They are flavorful and can be cooked as easily as marinating them in a bowl real quick with some Wishbone dressing. They are also amazing cold. So I always cook up 8-12 at a time and eat them the next day. Lately they are smaller in size as the chicken farms are rushing them to market.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Never cook with Olive Oil on HIGH heat.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody:
Would “impossible//beyond 
Vegan “ chicken “ 
Work?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Potatoes just not getting done in time.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I followed the recipe as written because I had never cooked it before, except I used chard instead of kale and forgot to add the butter. I cooked it in a large 1960’s vintage Guardian Service aluminum pot with a glass dome cover. Perfect! Big hit at my house.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: The recipe seems to overcook the chicken - has anyone tried putting the chicken and potatoes at the same time?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Yes, too much liquid even though I erred on the less side.  Potatoes were a underdone.  If we're to start with a crispy chicken let's just keep in mainly that way rather than submerge.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody:
Wonderful, delicious!
I added roasted  orzo to it. Also, instead of pine nuts, added roasted almonds just before serving.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Where in this recipe do you put in Cinnamon? Or is it cloves

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Gary’s suggestions sound very helpful.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Recipe was okay if a bit plain. The potatoes needed more time to be done.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I really wanted to like this. I followed the directions. I am an experienced cook. There are several disappointing things about this recipe. 1. Using expensive ingredients in ways that don't highlight their unique qualities. 2. the Potatoes:  timing doesn't work. Yes, I bought small potatoes. They were undercooked. 3. There was too much liquid at the end despite following the instructions. Overall, disappointing, and when I took steps to fix it, it was too much work and rather boring to eat.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight, substituting white wine for the vinegar.  Maybe this was a mistake, but the dish was so bland we didn't even finish it.  I won't be making this again, as it was a lot of work and truly lacked flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I read this recipe, got a few ideas (saffron, cloves, sherry vinegar), and made it intuitively. Read the comments later and they confirmed my doubts-  why put a lid on crispy skin? why so long? why potatoes at the end?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Needs to have excess chicken fat removed. A bit tasteless…

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Definitely avoid this recipe.  All of the problems/issues in other comments are true.  I followed the recipe exactly and the results are decidedly meh.  There are so many more delicious ways to turn these wonderful ingredients into something special.  Agree with others that NYT must not have adequately tested this one.  Steer clear.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody:
Okay, I tried.i read the comments, took the chicken out before cooking the potatoes, and tried to make sure everything was cooked.  Not quite cooked despite at least double the time.  I even broiled it at the end to make sure the skin was crispy.
The dish tasted sour to me. Threw out the remainder.
I even toasted the pine nuts and sprinkled them on.
Maybe a version without the vinegar and with the addition of some additional flavor would work.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This was so lovely. I ended up just removing the bones and it was more like a stew. I can only describe it as being French country kitchen, so tasty.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Very disappointing. Followed the recipe to the letter. After the initial the chicken & the potatoes were nearly raw. Turned the heat up for another 20 min just to get it done. Dish was finally done but very bland and boring. When i served it i asked my wife how it was while i finishing up in the kitchen and she said it didn't taste like anything. Don't serve this dish to any guest unless you try it first. I suppose it could be spiced up to make it more interesting but why bother.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Agreed. The nice crispy skin turns to mush after simmering for an hour-plus. The bay leaves and cloves may have added a touch of flavor but overall, the flavor was sour and bland. Not a keeper.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I thought this was bland and not worth the cooking time. I added saffron but maybe not enough. and as other reviewers mentioned why create wonderful crispy chicken skin and then braise the chicken?  I understand that this creates a flavor base but you end up with soggy chicken.  Adding kale is a negative in my book.  Spinach or arugula, please

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Bland and could be much simpler

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This was easy and very tasty. Didn't need the butter as the chicken schmaltz was enough so I left it out. Kale was a nice addition but also not necessary unless you need your greens. Not sure why the pine nuts were in it at the beginning; I toasted them and added at the end for flourish and a contrasting flavour. But...they too don't make the dish: the wine (I didn't have white so subbed a rosé porto) and the vinegar, spices distinguish it from a simpler chicken braise.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I did the oven route and without knowing what the recipe approach—all stovetop—would result it, I would use the oven if only because the chicken thighs have a great crispy skin that everyone adores!  I put the potatoes in with the veggies, laid the thighs on top, partially submerged, did 75 minutes at 325F.  Next, “tucked the Kale in” under the thighs (I used a fork to lift them up), and went another five minutes.  Finally, after melting the butter and sautéing with Parsley, spread on the thighs, and went another five minutes.  The result?  Chicken tender, kale cooked but not ‘mushy’, parsley/butter really made the chicken pop!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: I got a similar recipe from my favourite Italian cooking newsletter. They usually reduce the ingredients to the max. I add the potatoes at step 3 and let all simmer for at least 60 minutes. I only add 3 sticks of cinnamon, pine nuts, onion and lots of pepper / salt. And definitely no water, only wine.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: Delicious!  I used boneless skinless chicken thighs and substituted peas for the kale. I know it’s not the same, but we are not kale (or spinach) lovers and wanted something green in the mix.  As so often, I think recipes are underseasoned but that’s ok. It’s always easy to add seasoning but difficult (impossible?) to remove. A keeper!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: It was edible, but I question several aspects of this recipe. Why crisp the chicken skin if I just submerge it in water? I wish I had read the comments before making the recipe. Also, the potatoes were definitely not done after 20 minutes. I had to bring to a boil again to get them cooked

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This was very bitter. Probably the kale. So disappointing. Will not be trying again

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This was a big hit! I always read comments, I find they're helpful. Some folks found this bland. I used skinless, boneless thighs, some wine and mirin instead of the water, added in chopped carrots with the celery, and put the potatoes in at step 3. It was delicious, was a big hit, with lots of leftovers that were just as good. Not flavors that jump out at you, but with lots of depth.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Chicken Braised With Potatoes and Pine Nuts Recipe
commentBody: This was tasty. I would brown the skins longer than 10 minutes or perhaps brown them afterward as someone else suggested. Also, this was quite liquidy, so I served it with egg noodles, which my family enjoyed.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody:
This dish is a marvel of economy and unfussiness.  Do the beans (soaked overnight) in an Instant Pot (the standard "beans" setting: 1/2 hr + natural release) using the 1 quart/950 ml stock instead of water. (Paul Prudhomme: "Water's only for washing!") 
Rather than bouquet garni (good luck finding fresh herbs in Feb), I use them dried (1/2 tsp each thyme, sage, parsley & rosemary; 2 tsp chopped garlic) much later, at the start of Step 5, to maximize flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: not sure where you live but here in new england we can get fresh herbs year round at the grocery store. parsley, cilantro, and dill can be bought by the bunch, and things like chives, thyme, rosemary, sage, and fresh bay leaves can be had in clamshells.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody:
For many years I have been following the advice of America's Test Kitchen when soaking beans. Their advice: Brine the beans!

Dissolve 3 T salt in 4 Qt. cold water. Soak the beans overnight, 8-24 hours.

The result will be that the beans have a very creamy texture that is to die for. I highly recommend this any time you plan to use dried beans in a recipe like this.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody:
Water is a fine base for soups and stews, M.Prudhomme respectfully contradicted. It's better than many a package chicken stock with its list of ingredients.. There are fresh herbs all over the place in supermarkets! 

For me, with a Boston childhood of baked beans every Saturday night, I like them cooked slow and made thick. Also, like a Bostonian (Manhattan clam chowder, oi), find adding tomatoes to the beans ... unappetizing.  It's great to see this food given it's proper respect.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: Yes, I agree that adding tomatoes is unappetizing.  But I like that this version is not as sweet (no molasses or sugar!) as our Boston beans. I'll make these going forward and abandon the beans of childhood.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody:
RE: stock vs water: We can agree to differ, but this recipe uses 1 qt stock anyway: I merely suggest using it at step 1. 
RE: tomato: Worldwide, non-prosperous cultures that consume meat sparingly use tomato to add umami to legumes. Think black beans with salsa (Mexico); chickpea cholé (North India/Pakistan); Pasta Fazool (Southern Italy/Sicily). This recipe resembles the last somewhat: foodnetwork.com/recipes/jeff-mauro/pasta-fazool-3363378 .

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: The tomato adds acidity which affects the texture of the beans.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: Mostly agree with Joyce as to water v stock for this dish, and with Prakash as to soaking v Instapot.  An enameled cast iron stew-pot.  Vote for pancetta instead of salt pork, tho cut up pork jowl if you have access will do handsomely.  The recipe as written sounds delicious tho if you do use pork jowl or salt pork you'd do well to hold or at least minimize the salt. Finally and most importantly you'd do well to bake in lowish (300 degree) oven, and let it bake all afternoon if you have time.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: As a bean lover, I have adopted Diana Kennedy's foolproof frijoles de olla method: don't soak the beans, just cook them. The overnight soak is a disincentive to many a home cook and judging by the perfect results I get, thanks to Kennedy, completely unnecessary.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: I substituted dry large lima beans for the white beans, spooned it over thick-cut sourdough toast, and topped it with a poached egg and diced scallions — amazing.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody:
Do the beans (soaked overnight) in an Instant Pot (the standard "beans" setting: 1/2 hr + natural release) using the 1 quart/950 ml stock instead of water. (Paul Prudhomme: "Water's only for washing!")
Rather than bouquet garni, I use them dried (1/2 tsp each thyme, sage, parsley & rosemary; 2 tsp chopped garlic) much later, at the start of Step 5, to maximize flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: I am making this recipe today and will add a note after I have eaten the bean and bacon stew, but I wanted to mention to those like Maeve who don’t like the sweetness of Boston baked beans: Try Julia Child’s recipe in The Way To Cook. It has no brown sugar, just molasses and Dijon mustard so the beans are yummy but not sweet.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: Okay, gotta add my two bits.  Ditch step three.  To your bean cooking water, add the bouquet garni, some salt...Yes, and some aromatics (garlic, onion, celery, carrot, fennel, whatever you’ve got).  The resulting pot liquor is a fabulous stock for this dish.  Plus, the beans will already be so flavorful, you might have a hard time saving them for the rest of this heavenly recipe!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: I nod along in agreement to nanu's comment. :-)  "Ditch step three. To your bean cooking water, add the bouquet garni, some salt...Yes, and some aromatics (garlic, onion, celery, carrot, fennel, whatever you’ve got). The resulting pot liquor is a fabulous stock for this dish."

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: I also add toasted black cumin seeds finely ground and smoked pimento Vera region

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: Can imagine a teaspoon of Henderson's "Trotter Gear" going well in this

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: I don't get this. I assume the unpeeled garlic and whole herbs are removed from the stock at some point?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: You add the stock by the ladleful, leaving behind the garlic cloves and the bouquet garni.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: As a true blue NYer, I must put my two cents in on behalf of the yummy and superior Manhattan clam chowder. NE chowder is a boring and one note bowl of gloppy sludge. A spoonful or two and you’ll get all the flavor you’ll ever get. I’ll always opt for the briny herbaceousness of my Bowl of Manhattan. Go Yankees!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: Go Sox! Manhattan clam chowder should be named something other than chowdah; maybe then the arguments would stop. It's really more cioppino than chowdah, and, in its way, as good as genuine clam  chowdah, though not as good as fish chowdah made with "trash fish" (hake, cusk, etc.), so there!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: it's fine to prefer one dish to another (don't we all?) but if you're having a "one note bowl of gloppy sludge" then you aren't having real new england chowdah. as a rhode islander i turn my back on our namesake chowdah and think the new england variety is king. but it's also what most people imagine when they think of chowder, and thus is most widely emulated, which means there is a lot of gloppy sludge out there indeed. (just as all the bad pizza out there does not mean all pizza is bad ;))

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: I would just like to add my two cents since we are having a “chowdah” debate, and Rhode Island (clear) clam chowdah is the absolute best, the worst being tomatoey Manhattan clam chowder...yuck! Rhode Island chowdah with its briny and rich broth is to die for.  Boston baked beans are great as long as you do not ruin it with bacon, ketchup and regular mustard. Salt pork all the way with dried mustard is authentic and you must use the Yankee yellowed eyed beans.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: no need to "yuck" someone else's "yum".

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: Some of us like both styles of chowder and don’t denigrate either one.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: I used 3/4 pound of pancetta. I rendered it in the duck fat. It took about 45 minutes.  I think I cut the pancetta lardons too big. There was so much fat at the end: like over 1/2 cup. I used 5 tablespoons. It was plenty! Used water instead of stock. The herbs and garlic flavored it. The recipe makes a huge amount. I used dried sage but I had just purchased it. This is a deeply flavorful recipe. It’s special. I would serve it to my friends for dinner with crusty bread and a salad.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! So many layers of flavor! With 2 onions and 2 leaks, 6 cloves of garlic and all those herbs, it's a vegetable bean stew with tons of flavor and chunks of pork. (I used a meaty smoked hock because I had it instead of salt pork. Smokiness was lovely with all the herbs and alliums, but I want to try it with pancetta too.) I thought it needed just a little bump of acid, so added a glug of red wine vinegar to the pot before serving.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: This was a really good build of flavors.  I chopped the garlic from the broth and spread it on a hot, crusty baguette for a finishing touch.  Tip: make 5 cups of broth instead of 4, I did and it was just enough.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: One comment I would make is this dish really reheats well. It might even be better the second day.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: YUMMMMM! Simple, cheap, and utterly scrumptious. I only wish I had had the foresight to bake some fresh bread to go with it! I cooked the (soaked) beans in my instantpot (30 min on beans setting + natural release), but otherwise followed the recipe as is. This will be going into the comfort food arsenal for life. Yum!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: I just prepared this dish and it is very good.   My guess is that the prep is also very forgiving.  I though the beans were great with or without pancetta.   I think it would be great with bacon or sausage, too.  Next time, I might skip our use less of the stock.   I’d like the beans to be less soupy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: I have grown to really like this recipe.  The revelation for me is the stock, which is ramped up with lots of garlic and a large bouquet garni. I am thinking of doing this to my regular chicken stock-making.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: soak the beans overnight in the instant pot

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: You chowdah people are sumpin! I can’t wait til it’s travel time again and I can get my east coast relatives to drag me around for a scientificl controlled taste test. (California deaming here)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: When you’re in a “pot of Beans” mood, this recipe is an easy step above the ordinary and devious beyond the ordinary.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: Well we have a superb public market here in Rochester NY. We can source dried beans of almost any type and also any fresh beans as well as Wholesale canned. I often make with Ham Hocks or Ox tail as well as salt pork. This week I have Pheasant stock home made and as an experiment am using Smoked bacon and pheasant stock as well as fennel route. I'll let ya know how this turns out. but yes the pork is essential.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: Any recommendations on the best bean variety to use here?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: Maine soldier beans, or Maine yellow eye beans. Bought at your local farmers' market. And preferably that season's - beans that have been around for years can be used, but they never really soften, and who knows if they have any nutrient content at all.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody:
I personally prefer black beans, but any kind are OK. Cooking times vary, but you can't go wrong with an Instant Pot's default setting. Overcooking impacts only texture (soft vs meltingly soft); with undercooking, your bowels let you know it. 
Use beaninstitute.com/what-type-of-bean-should-i-use/ as a guide to bean choice. Better still, buy Harold McGee's "On Food and Cooking". (The legume chapter's sub-section "Fermented Legumes of India" blew me away, teaching me stuff I didn't know.)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: Made this in the Instantpot this evening. Used bacon, fresh herbs as mentioned as well as the leafy greens from the parsley.  Also added a dash of red pepper flakes.  I squeezed a little bit of fresh lemon juice when I served it.  Oh my goodness, it is delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody:
Hi Julia,

Very good flavor but one of the most complicated recipes I’ve ever done. But in the end very good flavor and result. But lots of pots to wash now.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: Excellent! Complex flavor!  Used the EVOO and 12 oz thick bacon, diced, because that was what I had. 14 oz. of canned plum tomatoes is definitely enough. If you open a 28 oz can, save the rest for something else.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: Made this as directed and really wonderful as is. I'll try the pancetta next time and agree it is worth holding the salt until you can gauge impact of the salt pork or bacon. I served it over some wilted arugula and was very satisfied.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: This was a perfect recipe. I left it a bit on the soupy side for this rainy day. And I used small diced pancetta bits from Trader Joe's. I tried it with the pumpkin corn bread I made but it cancelled out the complex notes of the beans. So I just left it for dessert. Delicate and perfect recipe for my palate. Love the tanginess of the lemon thyme and leeks.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: So, I don't like sage. Has anyone tried this with no sage?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: Peggy Carey, You ask if anyone has tried this w/out sage.  Yes.  Used only thyme and parsley for summer cook of this dish.  Worked well.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: A squeeze of lemon when done cooking really makes the flavors pop, IMHO.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody:
Made as per the recipe. Delicious! A few notes:
- This would be fine with less (expensive) pancetta, perhaps 3/4 lb. If you like it fairly crispy, allow for 45 minutes cooking instead of 20. Also don't need quite as much oil - try 1/8 cup instead of 1/4. 
- Finished this in the pot the beans cooked in, not the skillet the veggies cooked in; would not have been large enough. 
- Important! Cook the celery for a few minutes before adding the other veggies, otherwise, it will be too crunchy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: I made this recently and it was the best bean dish I’ve ever had. I used 2 cans of white beans, dried herbs, and smoked bacon lardons. I halved the time for most steps out of laziness, and it was still amazing!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: St. John Beans and Bacon Recipe
commentBody: Followed the advice to soak the beans in 4 quarts cold water with 3 tablespoons salt for 8 to 24 hours. Beans had a nice creamy texture after only 1 hour of simmering. Followed recipe as written, delicious flavor. I had never cooked with salt pork, next time I would cut the lardons smaller, and skip additional salt within the recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Three tries, three failures. 1. Couldn’t find mold, subbed in a trifle dish. This screwed up cooling time, led to runny mess. 2. Bought new mold but it was filled to the brim and sloshed on trip to fridge. Spooned out a cupful from top. Kitchen looked like a crime scene. Red jello never firmly set; lemon side crushed the red when overturned. 3. Cut quantities in half. Everything fit in mold, but the cherry side developed an eighth-inch barrier. Hard to cut. Reconsidering my life choices.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: To unmold you can place jello in mold upside down on serving plate, flip and cover mold with a small wet towel that has been heated in the microwave and wrung out.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Unless you have a giant mold, you will need to halve this recipe. Mine turned out to fit perfectly with exactly half in my regular sized Bundt cake mold. I followed advice to add gelatin to the cherry and this turned out perfectly. Also looks cool. I might try adding yogurt to both flavors since it turns out so nice and creamy that way, though the clear red with the cloudy yellow does look really great. A delightful dessert.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Dessert?! Where I grew up, a slice of this on a leaf of iceberg lettuce was called salad!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have made molds for 30+ years.  A few suggestions - The Tupperware jello mold is great for creating an attractive mold without unfolding problems.  You would probably have to cut the recipe in half to fit. Cooling the jello in part one as much as possible before adding sour cream, chilled cranberry/cherry juice in part two, and possibly adding some Knox jello to chilled juice may produce a firmer mold.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was simple & came out beautifully! I did 4 layers & had no trouble with it setting, though I took the advice to use chilled cherry juice & added a bit of Knox. The Knox prob helped the cherry layers, but the lemon layer didn’t need it. Also, I used my normal bundt pan (12 cups) & everything fit perfectly; those saying to halve the recipe must be using significantly smaller pans/molds. At the end of the day, though, this is just beautiful jello, so 4 stars only for boring texture.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Lemon less lemony than desired, kind of blah - next time will add some lemon zest - will also be adding some tart cherries to the other layer

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this for Christmas dinner.  Came out beautifully, and the family loved it.   Followed tip from comments to chill the cherry juice and added some extra plain gelatin to the black cherry jello mix.  Warm towel for release.  Success!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Followed the recipe, keep the lemon cream setting over night. Held together when i gently ladeled the lukewarm cherry jello over. Came out easily, broke seal as recommended with a knife, set into warm watter briefly, inverted and slid out. Such a treat. Surprisingly tart which was a great surprise but I used tart juice for the cherry jello with the 2 cups of water so Id do that again. Looked very pretty too.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was a big hit for my Thanksgiving dinner.  I couldn't find Black Cherry jello so used regular Cherry and a perfectly sized 32oz Ocean Spray Cranberry juice for the liquid on the Cherry layer.  I also bought an old-style lightweight bundt pan w/non-stick interior.  It was a perfect fit.  The whole thing set up perfectly and was gorgeous!  Everyone who tried it really enjoyed it.  That sour-cream layer cuts the sweet nature of typical jello dishes.  Love it!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Would greek yogurt work in lieu of the sour cream?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I've heard that the fat content of the dairy element helps with setting the gelatin, so if you use full fat greek yogurt it should be the same. Or, I imagine, you could use lower fat yogurt and add more plain gelatin.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: i will substitute  cream cheese and whipped cream for sour cream to give more body

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Added a packet of Knox to both cherry and lemon jello.  Bloomed in cold water and put it through a strainer.
