Used full fat Greek yogurt instead of sour cream.

Turned out well.  Maybe we will reverse the layers next time:  the cherry was struggling with the weight of the lemon.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I make this every year. It's a favorite! Even more fun to make it in vintage molds.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I tried this with pomegranate juice.  Failure, the lemon portion was perfect but 1 quart of Pom juice made a sloppy messy bottom layer. I have a photo.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
First: disregard the jello packet instructions.

For a 10 Cup bundt pan I used only one packet of cherry jello (plus one teaspoon of plain gelatin for firmness).

To release: I used PAM spray, and before inverting, let it sit at room temp. for 30 mins. warm towels, knife etc. it came out perfectly. 

To up the lemon flavor, I used 8oz lemon yogurt, 8 oz sour cream, and 1t lemon zest. Yum!

Next time: I might try doing the cherry jello first (fully set, then lemon) and omit the extra gelatin.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this and followed the recipe exactly, but it didn't work. There's too much liquid in the black cherry portion, and it won't set firmly enough. Despite waiting 24 hours to unmold, the black cherry was too soft. Total waste of time and money.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This turned out lovely! We couldn't eat the whole thing, but all the kids at our Thanksgiving dinner were delighted or intrigued (or both!) and the adults enjoyed the nostalgia. Next time I will make sure I have a platter big enough to hold this beaut. This time a Pyrex mixing bowl lid worked as an adequate substitute.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this for thanksgiving and it was a total hit. The cherry jello didn’t firm up as much as I would have liked so I will use the tip to add extra gelatin.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I was drawn in by the beautiful photograph, but encountered the same problem as many - lemon/sour cream mixture would not set when made as directed. Tried again with Knox as stabilizer, still would not set. I ended up making small parfait cups (no unmolding). Only the cherry layer truly firmed up. Flavor was ok, although Lemon Jello lends a distinctly chemical note.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: You must add extra gelatin to the red layer.  After unfolding my red layer seemed alive as it souped slowly out the sides.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this in a Bundt pan and it fit just right and appeared to have set up, although a thin layer of the lemon formed on the top while in the mold. I thought nothing of it. When I unmolded, the first thing I noticed was that the lemon layer was nowhere to be found! Suddenly, the ring split in places and a couple wedge-shaped pieces sloughed off and onto the counter…this revealed the lemon layer. We ate it and it tasted delicious. I’m just puzzled and disappointed.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I tried the recipe with tips from other commenters and it still didn’t work. A normal Bundt pan is big enough and the lemon layer was perfect but even after adding more Knox to the cherry mixture it never set after refrigerating overnight! I think a quart of extra juice requires a lot more gelatin

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I'm eager to try this but am timid after reading so many sliding, soupy failures. My aunt made a similar mold that I loved. It had three strawberry layers. The bottom was mostly strawberry jello, the middle a combination of jello, thawed frozen strawberries and sour cream, and the smallest top layer a beautiful light pink layer of strawberry-flavored sour cream. There were also some chopped walnuts mixed in somewhere.  If anyone has a recipe for this, please post!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I grew up in the desert every night era.  The fall back desert for my mother was what we called "Fluff".  Prepare a regular sized box of lemon jello.  Cool down a bit and stir in vanilla ice cream.  Pour into a pan or bowl and refrigerate.  Serve for dessert.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this exactly according to recipe, two days ahead, so lots of chilling time. Came out absolutely perfect. Ingredients fit in my Bundt pan, both layers were firm, and it slid out neatly. It didn’t require any of the extra tips or modifications others mentioned, unless you count the extra time in the fridge. I think I’ll try it with green jello on top next time for an extra Chrismassy effect.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This was a fun treat!  It turned out perfect with the following tips, many taken from other comments-

Disregard the instructions on the back of the jello boxes.

For the red layer, I used a mix of cranberry and raspberry jello with cranberry juice.  To make it more firm, I added one packet of Knox gelatin to the chilled cranberry juice, whisking it well, prior to adding it into the jello mix.  The lemon layer was solid and did not need Knox.

I served it with whipped cream.

Next time, I may reverse the layers because I think it may be prettier for red to be on top.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The ingredients say large 6 ounce package. Is that correct or do they mean .6 ounce package?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made for Christmas.  Used cherry jello as no black cherry to be found. Added some xtra knox geleton as recommended in other notes, which worked well, but couldnt get it all to dissolve so removed lumps with mesh strainer. Came out of mold beautifully and was fun addition to meal. Many wanted to know what it was.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Did you try "blooming" the gelatin? That's where you sprinkle the Knox gelatin into cool liquid and let it sit for 3-5 minutes before heating the liquid to dissolve the gelatin. I've been told this works well with Knox to avoid the lumps. Can't wait to try it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: wonder if I can substitute yoghurt for sour cream?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My son loved this recipe!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Can we please have the separated recipe for the lemon mix?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I brushed the mold with avocado oil (completely flavorless) and it slipped off the jello perfectly. 

Next time I will add whipped cream in the center!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I make this with cream cheese and canned pears….every one loves it!  Or maybe some “yuck” it!  Great recipe, I tried it…it was also a hit!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: What is a good substitute for the corn syrup? Maple syrup? Simple syrup? I just don't have any need otherwise for corn syrup.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: i've used honey in hot fudge when the recipe calls for corn syrup and it works well!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Lydia Sugarman Lyle’s Golden Syrup would work, and as a bonus it’s fabulous on waffles or pancakes.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Lydia Sugarman I find brown rice syrup to work well!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: You can make a corn syrup substitute with water, sugar and cream of tartar. Check online for a basic recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have made this three times lately with apples and then pears. Using individual small cut puff pastry squares from Lidle. They are delicious and can easily be reheated in the oven. Addictively good. I’ve baked them in muffin tins and custard ramekins both work well. They look gorgeous too. Don’t be afraid they are easier than you think once you get the hang of them!!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I would use Lyle’s Golden Syrup instead of corn syrup.  Love it in Pecan pie, too.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: If you're substituting Lyle's for corn syrup, would the measurements stay the same?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Amazing! This was a hit for Thanksgiving. We baked them in small ramekins with lids. Very easy to assemble and people love the presentation. Little do they know it's making your life easier making it this way. We used maple syrup instead of corn syrup.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: What fun this was and easy and delicious. I reduced all ingredients by a third for the two of us. Two individual ramekins. Took only a half hour of prep, including swirling the caramel. Expect to make this again and again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious!  I was worried when the caramel solidified to a rock as it cooled in the ramekins, but it was diluted by apple juices and absorbed into cooked apples; the finished caramel syrup remained soft, even when cooled.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I thought I had caramelized the sugar too long because it was hard and candied when cooling. I could barely get it out of the pot. I just gave it some heat to soften it and quickly scooped out the caramelized sugar into the greased cupcake tins and follow the recipe.  Once the Mini Apple Tartes Tatins were given the final reheating the candied sugar became a beautiful thick sauce dripping over the apples. Delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I never add corn syrup to my caramel and it's fine as long as you get to caramelization temperature (golden brown). Crystals will only form if the sugar is not fully caramelized.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: julia, would subbing honey for the caramel ingredients work for this recipe? thank you.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Thank you for including Nutritional Info.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used Agave syrup instead of corn syrup with nice results.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Pour the syrup in the cups just after adding the butter - it does solidify, but it will be the right consistency after baking. I thought I overcooked it the first couple of times- so I made another batch- so not necessary. I also tried it with sliced fresh figs- worked really well. I topped them with a spiced custard I had leftover from an apple pie experiment. If I try it with figs again, I might add some ginger to the syrup.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Warming the muffin tin prior to pouring in the caramel syrup will prevent it from hardening. Delicious recipe!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I used agave syrup, and it worked perfectly. I also used my own pie crust dough, which also worked perfectly (I make a big batch, form it into logs, and cut the logs into 1/2" discs, which I freeze. They keep for months. I pull out as many as I need for individual hand pies or enough to roll into a 9" crust.) The mini tartes were absolutely delicious. This is a keeper recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: First time making and it was much easier than anticipated. Fabulous results. Will make again!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Second time around: even better than I remembered! Made them this time with puff pastry in a muffin tin - made 5 mini tartes, but I think I could've crammed more caramel into each and made 4 (or even 3?). (I made half the recipe again, obvs.)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: lydia-- the main reason for using corn syrup is that the more kinds of sugar  molecules in a solution the less chance there is of the solution crystallizing. careful cooking of the caramel, washing down the sides of the pan at intervals will do the same thing. no need for any substitutions either.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I'm just now finding this recipe, and wondering if I can use a pre-made caramel sauce (thinking Trader Joe's which is very good) instead of making my own? Has anyone tried that?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I keep a bottle of corn syrup in the cabinet for one reason and one only: Pecan Pie.  I remember it going moldy in my childhood, so seldom used but so very important in but a few cherished recipes.  I wonder now if more practical syrups we might use more frequently are good substitutes? Barley syrup, rice syrup, malt etc.?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is a lovely recipe. Easy and impressive. Sprinkled bit of cinnamon over apples before adding pastry.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I have never made caramel desserts before. Can someone explain why you need the additional corn syrup or maple added to the sugar?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @LS - having multiple types of sugar (corn syrup, like honey, is an invert sugar) decreases the chance of crystallization

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: for anyone nervous about the corn syrup... adding multiple kinds of sugar crystals to a solution decreases the chances that the solution will crystallize. so go ahead and use honey, lyle's golden syrup, maple syrup. personally i would stay away from simple syrup as it is still sucrose and once the water is boiled off could possibly crystallize. or you could skip a second source of sugar altogether and be scrupulous in washing down the sides of the pan while boiling.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The caramel solidified into a solid mass after we made it and I was sure we’d done something wrong. We went with it though and it was perfect - the juice from the apples loosens everything up while it cooks and it was delicious. Just make sure to pour it into the ramekins/muffin tin while it’s still warm.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My partner and I loved this recipe. I used maple syrup instead of corn syrup. It took much longer for the caramel to brown than the time suggested but it went quicker once I resisted the urge to constantly swirl the pot. Not sure if it was technically wrong because as pouring the caramel into the baking dish it instantly hardened and had more of a fudge like consistency but I went with it and it turned out perfect after baking. I also didn’t have small dishes to bake it in so I used a 9” baking dish and just covered the whole thing with one sheet of pastry. Increased baking time about 5 minutes. Perfection!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Liz. So brave! A wonderful change from the commenters who ask permission for every tiny change!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I could have used a temperature reading for the caramel, perhaps soft ball stage. I ended up with sugardaddy on the bottom of the muffin tins.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I just made this! It worked out great. I made my own rough puff— I chose not to dock my pastry because I didn’t mind if I got some rise. Next time I’m also going to take the caramel off the heat a little sooner— I mimicked the color shown in the video and I feel my tarts were a little too bitter for my liking. You are going to get a fair amount of caramel stuck to the bottom of your baking vessel— I wonder if making a blonder caramel and increasing the warm up time at the end of the cook would help remedy this? I also added a little salt to my caramel.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Took three attempts to get the caramel right but smooth sailing after that and a hit with everyone. Will make again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The point of adding a tempered sugar (like honey or any kind of syrup) is to prevent the sugar from crystalizing. It doesn't matter what flavour as it's too little to alter the taste. Use what you have on hand: agave, golden syrup, maple ...

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This was pretty, tasty, and not too hard. The caramel was a little tricky, but if you leave yourself enough time (and butter) to make a second batch after you mess up the first one, it’s fine. I had to learn the hard way that inadequately browned sugar leads to a chalky crystallized chunk of buttery sugar instead of caramel. But second batch was perfect!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Followed the recipe exactly except upsized the recipe a tad to make 8. Did half in Pyrex ramekins/custard cups, half in a far too shallow muffin tin. Highly recommend the ramekins. The apples held together much better and turning the tarts out was as simple as flipping them over on each plate. Scooping apples and pastry out of the muffin tin mashed the apples. Next time, might reserve some of the caramel for drizzling on top. Not enough was left after turning them out. Recommend vanilla ice cream or maybe some crème fraiche for contrast.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: “Keep an eye on it” when making caramel is not a useful instruction! “Keep an eye on it” for what????? I now know having produced vile bitter caramel! Fortunately I caught it and re-did it before completing the dessert…

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Made the recipe as outlined. It was absolute perfection! A definite addition to my dessert repertoire. 

Someone in the comments asked as to watch to for when making caramel - one wants to make sure that once the swirling sugar-water mixture turns dark amber color, it needs to be taken immediately off the heat at that moment, otherwise the caramel will burn and start smoking.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Can I use silicone cupcake molds to bake these instead of a muffin tin?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I tried making the caramel twice- the first time after adding the butter the entire thing instantly crystallized (cooked too long apparently). The second time it remained syrupy (not cooked long enough?) The sugar I used was "natural" so maybe that made a difference- I'll try again with "less natural" sugar- it would be helpful if there would be a temperature point to aim for instead of color. The end result of the too syrupy batch was good - but not, I think, what was intended.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I don’t suppose one could use a high quality pre made caramel sauce in this recipe? Thoughts?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @les I had the same question

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The harder part is getting the puff pastry topping right, frankly. The puff pastry became too gooey while cutting up the apple (the choice was letting the apples brown or ignoring the temperature of the pastry dough). And be sure to match the apple size to your ramekin. Follow the low to high heat instructions for caramelization and it works well.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Am I the only  where the sirup was everywhere else in the oven, than in the muffin pan? For 6 tartes the amount of caramel was way too much. A third would’ve been enough.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My caramel mostly hardened so that even after warming them before turning tarts out of ramekin, caramel stuck to the bottom of ramekin. I warmed them up in microwave to liquify but as soon as it hit the tart and cooled it hardened like candy. Any tip? What did I do wrong at the stage of making caramel?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Keiko - It's likely that you kept it at a high heat for too long. The caramel reacted like candy because that's what you made! Remove it from the heat sooner?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: You can eliminate the corn syrup. It's not necessary when caramelizing sugar.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Would it be possible to make these through step 5 and then freeze the completed ramekins to bake on a later date?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I am wondering the same thing. I don't think uncooked apples freeze well, so I'd be more inclined to freeze them after completion of the recipe, then thaw before reheating (which the recipe says can be done). I'll probably try that!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe didn’t work for me. Too much syrup, and the water boiled off before it browned.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I found this tricky to get out of the muffin pan.  I really think the more classic method of drawing hot caramel up and over the apples repeatedly gives a richer flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Can you use pie crust instead of puff pastry? We need a gf version

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I wonder same thing, not a fan of dry puff pastry. I guess I’ll look at comparative baking times, if they are close, it should work .

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: These turned out OK, but the caramel did not melt completely when I re-heated them an hour or so after the initial bake. I was left with some rather thin caramel sauce, and a big lump of mostly hard caramel in the bottom of the cups. What did I do wrong? Cooked the caramel too long/hot?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The caramel sauce is horrible got hard so fast. I might try this recipe again but use my own caramel sauce. Might not make again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Help! Not sure what I did wrong. I didn’t have corn syrup, so maybe that was it, but I substituted honey for the teaspoon of corn syrup, as some suggested. The caramel mixture never darkened, even after the max time suggested, although it did look better when I whisked in the butter. But it completely stayed liquid, when it was baked, and when I turn them over, the caramel was just liquid and not very tasty, to be honest… Suggestions as to what I did wrong? Thank you!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Rose Casa that happened to me too. I did use the corn syrup and followed all the steps and measurements. This caramel recipe is not fool proof. The color was clear, not like the video that shows a beautiful caramelized syrupy mixture. I wish I knew what is missing. I might ask ChatGPT.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Rose Casa I was having a hard time too. We turned up the heat and waited, it took probably 10 minutes.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Rose Casa It took much longer than the suggested time for me to reach the correct color. I think this was due to the fact that I was swirling the pot too much. Once I let the pot just sit and resisted the urge to swirl often it finally changed color.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This didn’t work for me at all. When I tried to add the butter to the water, corn syrup and sugar the whole thing instantly hardened into a crust. Gave up. Made a rustic tart instead.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made a huge mistake by making the caramel the night before. It had hardened so well in the muffin tins that baking the next day failed to melt it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I left out the corn syrup completely and it turned out perfectly! I have never made it with the corn syrup so I don’t know what the difference is but if you don’t have any/don’t want to use it, it should be fine!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Maddie The reason for using corn syrup is to introduce an invert sugar, which prevents the sugar from re-crystallizing which can be disaster when making caramel. As others have noted, maple syrup, Lyle’s, or honey would work. It’s worth noting that corn syrup that is sold for baking is *not* the same product as the dreaded, industrial high fructose corn syrup.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Do we roll the pastry to a certain thickness or use it as is out of the box once unfolded?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: We make a batch of these on Sunday, then leave them on the counter.  We reheat them in a 350 degree oven for 12-15 minutes to melt the caramel. They're usually gone by Tuesday night...delicious for breakfast, too.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I love this recipe but the apples are coming out mushy. Should I shorten the cooking time at 400 or at 375 ... or both?
Maybe nix the 375 session altogether?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Had problems with the caramel.. it never got dark or think, and then it separated with the addition of the butter. Maybe needs more corn syrup or add sweetened condensed milk? Tasty in the end but not caramely.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’m going to make these for Thanksgiving for my 89 yo dad, who loves apple pie. Do these freeze well? Saw the comment re: waiting until baked. Does anyone have tips regarding how to/best time to freeze these? I have zero experience with puff pastry and freezing fruit desserts. Thanks in advance!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: how would it work without any covering ? no puff pastry-maybe oat crumble sprinkling ?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Eela I bet it would taste good but it wouldn’t look the same. The puff sits on top of the apple, which is cored-side up. If you made a crumble you would have to fill the cored center with crumble too. Also, I suspect when you turn it over it would not hold its shape, since the apples want to splay out when they come out of the molds

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Any problem getting these out of the muffin tins intact?  They look perfect in the video, but just like pot pie, if you can’t release them easily they could end up looking like hash.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Karen I made this today in a silicon muffin pan and it turned out perfectly.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This is an easy “mock” version: I can’t be bothered making pastry dough & I don’t think I would like store-bought. I have a quick biscuit recipe that I used instead. I fried them into English muffins, sliced them open, buttered them, & then poured the apple filling on top. I used maple syrup instead of corn syrup. Biscuit dough for 4 biscuits (also makes dumplings): 1 cup flour, 1/2 tsp salt, 1 tsp baking powder, 1/3 cup water, 2 Tbsp olive oil. Fry in pan. I use Pam spray on the pan. For savory biscuits I’ll add 1/2 tsp ground rosemary

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Looks so good! Would it work with vegan butter?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: terrible recipe.  caramel turned to a hockey puck apple( granny smith) to mush. and what’s with the corn syrup? why?  i am an experienced baker, tarte tatin is always good — this was an epic fail!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Not sure why this didn't work for me? The caramel never really melted into the apples and stayed fairly solid on the bottom of the pan. Even after reheating I had to dig it out, melt in the microwave to pour over the apples, then it just became like candy again. Maybe I heated it too long initially?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Can I make this recipe a day ahead? Is 12 hrs ahead a hard and fast rule? Is it an issue of apples breaking down? 

I have no time to make these beautiful tartes the day of my dinner party. Can I do everything but bake them (on the prior day) then finish them during dinner? Any expert advice would be appreciated.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @Julie don’t know if you did this 8 months ago, but I tested the reheating today and it worked well. Made them first thing in the morning and reheated towards the end of dinner.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made this for a seated four-course French dinner for six people last night and it was a huge hit to end the meal. Made it in the jumbo muffin tins- doubled it and did two tins in case of any mishaps. The only modification I made was that the discs of pastry were slightly smaller than the circumference of the openings of the muffin tins and I didn’t bother with tucking/crimping the pastry. All was perfect and each guest got to take one of the extras as a party favor. Home run!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Advice please? I do not understand about crimping the pastry dough up and then pushing it down. Why not just push the extra dough down to make a little cup that will catch the caramel when inverted?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: caramel sauce was watery

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made it exactly as it was written, but I find the caramel is too tough.. So I had leftovers (just the two of us) and I added cream to that caramel, and I plan to do it again tomorrow using  my version of caramel.  I won't say it wasn't good.  It was, but cleaning the leftover caramel is hard.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I’d like to try to make these again when I can devote a little more care to them. It was a bit much on top of the rest of my Thanksgiving menu and I didn’t get the caramel right, but they were still tasty.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: First time I made it, there was a super strong metallic taste that overwhelmed the rest.  So, I changed from muffin tin to ramekin but still had a strong but not as strong metallic taste

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My caramel is very watery after cooling. I assume it's the apple's liquid diluting it. I cooked the caramel to a nice amber, so it wasn't undercooked (and it hardened instantly). Is this right? I assumed it would be at least syrupy. Reheating to release caramel is completely unneeded.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, plenty of sauce for extra noodles.  Tamarind concentrate was not available; Google search suggested lime juice which worked very well.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: This is unique and delicious.  As made it was overly sweet, but next time I will cut the brown sugar by at least 1/3.  Other wise easy steps and tasty.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Despite the fact that I halved the sugar per suggestions here, I found this cloyingly sweet.  Perhaps eliminate the sugar altogether?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: This was wonderful. I crumbled up half a block of tofu and pan-friend until dry, it bulked the dish up a little and soaked up the extra sauce beautifully. Just the right amount of brown sugar is called for if using tamarind concentrate, I could see it needing a bit less sugar if made with lime juice.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I subbed lime for tamarind. This was better than the episode of RHONY when Kelly Bensimmon has a psychotic break on a yacht.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Wow! Amazing! A bit of a mess on my first try since I haven't worked with tamarind concentrate -- a bit sticky and it got all over the place! But what flavor. I used Roland rice vermicelli and the 8.8 oz bag seemed like too much after I cooked it so didn't put it all in. (Saved to throw in some soup.) Squeezed the lime right in at the end. This was enough for three hungry adults and leftovers. Big hit!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: First time didn’t have tamarind so used a bit of gochujong. Also learned to add much more veggies - carrots, julienned peppers, mushrooms. Added tofu on top as well to give some protein. Make sure you check whether you’ve got tamarind paste vs tamarind concentrate!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I also cut back on the sugar and found 8 oz of noodles to be too much; I held back about half the cooked noodles for another use. Next time, I will add carrots or red pepper for color and textural contrast. I didn't have sprouts, so the dish seemed to need something more. Even so, my 12-inch frying pan was overflowing. The dish is very good and might work just as well with cabbage in place of the sprouts. I'm thinking of adding shredded chicken to the leftovers.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: We loved this dish, but thought next time we will use more vegetables because of the generous amount of sauce recipe makes. Also, we might up the amount of tamarind concentrate and lower the amount of sugar.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Really interesting and tasty.  Overly sweet, should be easily adjusted by decreasing brown sugar by 1/3 to 1/2.   I only had half the required amount of miso, so not sure if that affected it.  I used a 14 oz box of rice noodles and it was perfect to the amount of sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody:
Have learned my lesson with tamarind on other recipes.
I used about 2 tablespoons of tamarind paste, didn’t have tamarind sauce. That was plenty for my family!
Thanks to the other reviewers only used a couple tablespoons of sugar. Reduced the tomato paste to a about a tablespoon and added chili crisp from Momofuko.
Also added a pinch of chili flakes to the Brussels sprouts.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Very good and very easy. Subbed lime juice for the tamarind.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I can’t figure out why you are proposing Brussels sprouts at this time of year.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I made this last night. Very satisfying and will make again. I made a few adaptations based on what was in my pantry. I used Napa cabbage instead of Brussels Sprouts, and added grated carrot. Plus Gochujang Korean chili paste and added a tablespoon of crunchy peanut butter!  I added a ton of fresh basil to the cilantro and it was delicious. I would add a few more veggies next time, but there will definitely be a next time!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody:
I swapped out rice noodles for Chinese wheat noodles and it was fantastic.
Also used 1 Tbsp of lime juice + 1 Tbsp fish sauce instead of tamarind concentrate. 
Next time I plan to double the brussel sprouts, and halve the sauce.
Overall it was really good. I loved how the taste of coconut oil really shone through this, and using a whole bundle of cilantro was delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Made it exactly as written (with tamarind paste, found at Whole Foods). I can’t wait to make this vegan dish again

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Add tempeh

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Owner of Chinese Grocery suggested Palm Sugar - it's the traditional sugar in Thailand - brown sugar is more readily available here, so that may be why it was included in the recipe.  Palm Sugar was NOT cloyingly sweet. This was delicious!!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Perfect just as written. Wasn't able to add bamboo shoots or Fresno pepper due to (un)availability. Requested to make this again!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Made as written, using Thai "forbidden" rice noodles. Really delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious—even with a few tweaks, some accidental. I found tamarind paste in small packs at Whole Foods, but actually forgot to add it and I still think this tasted great. I used red miso, because that’s what I had. And, I only used about two table spoons of brown sugar. It was plenty sweet. I’ll for sure make this again!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Flavors were delicious but I think the Brussels need to be broken down a little more- maybe seared in the sauce toward the end of cooking

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Tried it one night with brussels sprouts, and another night with eggplant, which worked really well, too.  Would love to try with tofu (in lieu of of brussels sprouts).

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Skip the first 2 ingredients of the sauce, add a little rice vinegar and you’ll be fine. Follow the recipe and you’ll get a salt-sugar shock. We opted for the shock and were granted a night full of laughter about what an intense and interesting experience that was. Seriously, I’m a huge NYT Cooking fan, I trust and love y’all, but what happened here?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody:
Tried this out tonight - it was delicious.

Read the comments beforehand and took note to use less sugar, and how I could use limes sans tamarind concentrate. Yum!

Curious, however, as to why coconut oil was the fat of choice, especially considering the use of a wok and coconut oil’s low smoke point?

It was all fun and games until the coconut oil, wok, and brussies came together. Smoke City. It’s okay, we figured out the smoke alarms and carried on. Good stuff!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: This recipe did not turn out great. We usually love all recipes from the NYT cooking, but I thought the overall flavor was quite bad...nothing could have really saved it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: This was too sweet and too salty for me. Yuck!  I will not make it again. I have a recipe for Pad Thai which is much better.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, but calls for way too much sauce! Mine was drowning in it— I would almost halve the amount of sauce next time.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and quick! Made mostly as directed (omitted cilantro!). Added 8oz cubed baked tofu (marinated in a bit of the sauce before baking). Made 12oz rice noodles, perfect ratio of sauce to noodles. Would def make again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious! Will make again. Made a few changes but keeping this one in the rotation! 

Used coconut aminos and chickpea miso and only 1 Tablespoon sugar (and was still really sweet). Tasted great!! The tamarind sauce was really good.

Added two large carrots thinly sliced and a pack of Trader Joe’s tempeh to the Brussels sprouts and it bulked things up. Will add even more veggies next time, perhaps a red bell pepper and the sprouts. Omitted the sprouts, pepper and peanuts but nobody noticed!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I learned the hard way from making this recipe that red miso should not replace white miso (at least not at a 1:1 ratio).  With red miso, this was pretty good after diluting 4:1 with freshly made un-sauced noodles (otherwise, inedible!) - don’t follow my approach.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Third time was the charm! For the sauce, I only used 2 Tbs. brown sugar, a tad of tamarind paste (that's all I really had, but was concerned about too sour) and used about 1/2 lime; all else the same as the recipe. For the veggies/noodles, I included a red bell pepper, thinly sliced which totally fixed the sauce to veggies/noodles ratio. Really good!!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I used baby bok choy instead of the Brussels sprouts and did not add any salt. Absolutely delicious and not too sweet at all!  I’m realizing this is really a personal thing given all the comments on this. This was perfectly balanced for me.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Definitely double noodles to sauce and add more of the veggies. Sauce is strong (and delicious). Added crispy Chile to the toppings and YUM!!!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: This was really tasty. I did not use sugar at all and if you enjoy a spicy meal, this is just right.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Any time I do saucy noodles I plate them and stick them under the broiler for just a few minutes to crisp up the top layer...umm, so good!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Unfortunately this is one of the most disappointing recipes I've ever made from NY Times Cooking. I halved the sugar according to suggestions from other readers and I don't know if that totally threw off the balance of the other ingredients, but it was terrible. Acidic, stodgy, underwhelming. Sad day for this hungry half Asian. Still ate it. Did not enjoy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: This was good with 1/2 -3/4 of the tamarind concentrate. Also good with all of it, but quite strong flavored.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Big Isa Chandra fan, big Thai food fan but sadly this was so gross. Too sweet, too mushy, too saucy..

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly as written, the sauce was disappointing and lacked balance, too much tamarind? Too much tomato paste? It was so far from the expected “loosely based on pad Thai” palate that it’s not even worth using the ingredients tweaking and trying again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Any particular reason why coconut oil was used over other oils?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: In the article about vegetarian cooking that accompanies this, Ms. Moskin writes, "Thanks to the recipes in 'I Can Cook Vegan,' a new book from the chef Isa Chandra Moskowitz, coconut oil is my new best friend."  She leaves it at that, so I suppose you'd have to get your hands on Ms Moskowitz's book to find out why.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I have a vegan family member who abhors cilantro but adores everything else in this dish. Any suggestions?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Thai basil works great!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Based on other people’s suggestions we decreased the sugar from 1/3 to 1/4. The sauce was very sour and salty. We only had red miso and perhaps that was the problem. Suggest using cauliflower with the brussel sprouts. We shaved them and they disappeared. Very disappointed.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: A tablespoon and a half of brown sugar is plenty!!!!  In Thailand most dishes are less sweet than their Americanized versions. Also subbed Thai basil for the Cilantro.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Delicious but mine came out too salty for our taste, though I used low sodium tamari sauce. Maybe because miso is quite salty on its own? I'd try it without the kosher salt.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Yum! Subbed lime juice for tamarind as suggested by others and cut the sugar entirely. This was a big hit for my whole fam of picky eaters.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I don't have tamari, can I sub soy sauce?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I did that, and it seemed to work fine!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I changed the proportions for this dish (based on my failure to read the recipe closely and wanting to use up ingredients, rather than any formal intention). Unexpectedly, what I did worked really well, and I would recommend it: double the brussels sprouts, use 3/4 of the sauce, and keep everything else the same. This was delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Can you use grapeseed oil or another oil in place of coconut?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Added some shiitake mushrooms to the mix.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I made this with a full lb. of rice noodles and more brussel sprouts. There was still plenty of sauce to cover. I didn’t have bean sprouts so I substituted snow peas. In place of the tamarind I used some sumac I had...in addition to lime juice. It tasted great, even with the improvisations.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody:
Double noodles,
Less sugar
Halved Brussels sprouts

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Gloppy, overly sweet (and used only half the sauce) - not enough vegetables.  Added fish sauce to liven it up a bit. Miso did not belong here, and neither did the tamarind.  I would stick to more traditional Thai flavors - being inventive here did nothing to elevate this dish.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Yummy dish. Added a little more tamarind concentrate, a little more veggies, and grilled tofu for more protein. An excellent one pot (wok) meal!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I used 1T tamarind paste and it was fine.  You could add cooked shrimp to the noodles.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Very tasty and satisfying, but far too salty for my taste. I might add more rice noodles or less miso next time.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I halved the recipe for just the two of us, added in some grated ginger, removed the brown sugar entirely, and added a bit of rotisserie chicken. Next time I'd decrease the sauce even more...it was still a bit overwhelming.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I will add more vegetables next time.  Maybe a whole head of steamed broccoli.  Also broiled tofu would be great stirred in.  I couldn’t find tamarind paste so just skipped it and the sauce was still really nice.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I  will add more vegetables next time.  Maybe a whole head of steamed broccoli.  Also broiled tofu.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I didn’t have tamarind it worked just fine. I added tofu and could easily add more veggies.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Good flavor but too sweet. I added extra veggies (bok Choy) and did not have peanuts. I think adding the peanuts and some tofu would have helped to balance out the sweet, but I will use only 1/4 of the sugar next time.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: This made an awesome savory breakfast for the week! I added a ton more veggies just to bulk it up and stretch it over the full week. I added mushrooms, broccoli florets, and diced squash and skipped the mung bean sprouts since my grocery store was out of them. The noodles did break apart a little, but not a big deal—the smaller pieces were easier to eat. The sauce was delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Subbed out gochujang for the tomato paste for extra kick with good results

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Nice flavor but do yourself a favor and only use a fraction of the sugar suggested here. Unbelievably sweet otherwise.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Sticky and delicious—will add chicken breasts next time since they'd work really nicely with the texture.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Over did it on the amount of noodles which made the dish to thick.  I’ll need to try again. Also, sauce was a little to tart got my taste.  Overall this is worth trying again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Loved the tamarind - use all the sugar if you're using tamarind. Added carrots which were great. I would only use one clove of garlic.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Reduce brown sugar to 1/4 C.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I used soy sauce instead of tamari, lime juice instead of tamarind, just a tablespoon of brown sugar, and a quarter teaspoon of red pepper flakes rather than a whole teaspoon. Otherwise, I followed the recipe. It was awful. The sauce was intensely salty, nearly inedible. I don’t think my substitutions would have that much impact, and normally I like more salt than others. Either I made an egregious mistake or the recipe is wonky.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. Used tamarind paste and it was very easy to add - comes in little tablespoon size packets. I bought a 14 oz box of “Thai rice noodles”. Since the recipe called for 8oz I only used half but then it didn’t seem like enough so I cooked the rest. The amount of sauce was perfect for the amount of noodles. Also I didn’t cook the scallions and sprouts separately as i thought that was unnecessarily cumbersome and it worked out fine. Must rinse the noodles really well. I would make this again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I doubled the recipe and found there was too much sauce for 16oz of noodles.  Also too sweet as others mentioned.  I think I’d use half the amount of sauce next time, add a protein (tofu) and cut down on the sugar.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Sweetness seemed fairly balanced when using the Tamarind as written. Feels like it was missing more veggies or other protein as others have noted.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: My grocery store was out of brussels sprouts so I used what I had instead - a small crown of broccoli, small head of cauliflower, and a small purple cabbage. After chopping the volume was way too much for my skillet so I tossed with a little olive oil on a sheet pan and roasted in a 400 degree oven for 20 min until they were well-crisped in parts. This replaced step 4 in the recipe. I felt like the balance of sauce to noodles and veg was good, and I appreciated the high vegetable-to-rice ratio.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Add tofu, no extra sauce needed (salty!)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Sorry to do this, but short on several ingredients (but even so, it was tasty).  Used a touch of fish sauce, no miso, lime juice in lieu of tamarind.  Jalapeno slices went in with garlic, scallion.  Almost doubled up on sprouts.  Good cheap meat free meal!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Used all the vegetables in my fridge (which did not include brussels sprouts) and worked just as well! The sauce and noodles are a great base to start any dish.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Agree with other comments that sugar could eliminated altogether or maybe reduced to just to a tablespoon. Next time would also reduce the amount of tomato paste which gave the sauce too much of a ketchup taste. Sauce was too much for amount of noodles. But the recipe has a lot of promise!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I made this without white miso (didn't have any) and it somehow had an overwhelming flavor of tamarind, to the exclusion of the other ingredients.  I still ate half a bowl and the next night made an extra batch of rice noodles and added a bit more tamaria and it was better.  I'll try it again with a quarter of the tamarind and see how it is...because it seemed so close to delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I followed recipe exactly; only problem was searing the Brussels sprouts which I had thinly sliced - they seared nicely but created quite a bit of smoke before they were softened. Perhaps should have added a little water after they browned a bit.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Tasty, but I guess I should have paid more attention to the notes that said too much sauce to noodles and veggies. I'll either have the sauce or increase noodles and veggies to even it out. Otherwise, thought it was great.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody:
Like others said, I cut the sugar in half and could have used about 3/4 the amount of sauce.  I also threw in the pepper with the garlic in Step 5.  

A yummy, quick dish that does well with substitutions.  I've cooked this multiple times during Shelter-in-Place when I'm overdue for a grocery trip: I have added bell pepper and spinach that I needed to get rid of, subbed shallot for scallions, or subbed ramen noodles for the rice noodles on different occasions.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody:
This was WAY salty. Made it pretty much as written the first time, except with 2 packs of drained and rinsed shiratake noodles, and I subbed brown Truvia for the sugar. Huh. Decent, but puckery.

The second time I used low-sodium tamari. Nope, still too salty. Wondering about changing the proportions of ketchup and tamarind.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody:
I made this exactly as written and it was excellent. I didn’t think it was too sweet— but maybe I’m used to sweeter pad Thais.

I didn’t think it needed the squeeze of lime, the tartness of the tamarind really comes through. I would actually add some seared sirloin to the fish next time to cut the acidity a little! And don’t skimp on the Serrano peppers!

I’ll be making this again and again!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody:
I followed recipe as is, except for the following: 
I substituted the brussels sprouts with broccoli and added tofu.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody:
As noted by others~too much sauce. Put in 1/2 to 3/4.
Needs much more Brussel sprouts and other green veggies
Delicious

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Made as directed, I didn’t find the recipe to be either too sweet or too saucy. We sautéed some sliced chicken breast between the Brussels sprouts and the scallions, which made the dish more filling. Broccoli could easily be swapped for the Brussels sprouts. Interesting flavor!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: The flavor was incredible, but way too saucy for me, and I really love sauce. Next time, I will not add the full amount of sauce in the cooking process! Otherwise fantastic.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Update. Make half the sauce following the sugar ratios as is. Then only use that amount (the entire half recipe) on 12 ounces of noodles. Extra veggies too.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: After following the advice of other folks, I did 1/3 of the sugar and thought the flavor was perfect. It took much longer to cook the quartered brussels sprouts, but everything else was great. I did cilantro on the side, a generous splash of lime, and added tofu at the end.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: The family loves this! I add some much needed protein to this dish by tossing cubes of tofu that have been pressed with a little soy sauce, grapeseed oil and cornstarch, roasting at 400 for about 30 min. Then I toss them into the pan when the rest of the sauce goes in.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: More effort than it was worth, honestly

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody:
Used broccoli instead of Brussels (worked great, got them browned and crispy) and udon noodles.
Subbed lime juice for tamarind, used only 1Tbsp of sugar.
Threw in some spinach at the end, topped with cucumber matchsticks. Basically, just used whatever I had and it still turned out tasty!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I used half of the sauce and still found it overly coated. I might try the lime juice substitute others suggested next time as the tamarind concentrate overpowered the dish. I added diced carrot and tofu which I think were great suggestions. This was not a big hit for me first time around, but perhaps will try again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: made this a couple more times. Most recent time made extra as usual, but the leftovers were mush and inedible. Only thing I did differently was that I used the hot water pour over method to cook the rice noodles instead of boiling them in the pot.  Maybe that was the problem?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: This is really delicious. I added a few drops of sesame oil to the sauce to warm up the flavor. I also added sliced yellow peppers, cubes of fried tofu and doubled the garlic. Everyone loved it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Yes!  Those are great ideas. I plan to add more veggies next time. Really satisfying.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Super yummy!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Oh, and I added 50 percent more veggies!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I definitely did not need to use all of the sauce for an 8oz package of noodles, I would stop after the first half and save the rest for another batch. Vegetables used are definitely interchangeable

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I followed recommendations here and only used 1T of brown sugar, and subbed lime juice for the tamarind concentrate. This dish was a huge hit! Would add some tofu next time - there would be plenty of sauce for it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Delicious but too sweet

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: This is so insanely delicious. I subbed lime juice for the tamarind paste like another person suggested and it was great. Don’t substitute anything for the coconut oil- it really adds to the dish. I simplified the steps by starting the Brussels and such halfway through the noodles cooking. I never took the Brussels out (I only quartered them) so they could keep cooking. Everything was done when the noodles were so I could skip the cold water rinse and toss in coconut oil. So delicious

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: This was awesome. Very tasty and aromatic, great sauce. My wife recommended using less sugar next time, so we will tweak it a bit.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Can you make this in advance and serve it at room temp?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: What is “tamari sauce”? Is it the same thing as tamari?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Where can I buy tamarind concentrate?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Whole Foods, Asian markets

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I LOVE salt but this sauce was inedible. Should make note of using low sodium tamari or low sodium soy sauce. Had to throw it out.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Any suggestions to use as a substitute for the miso?  Not worth buying a whole package to use only a couple of TBS. Thanks.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: It keeps forever - you'll use it-make more Thai stuff!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Sub 4 tamarind / lime

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Waaay too much sauce. I should have trusted my instincts and only used half. This is definitely enough for twice as much noodles.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: hmm, well this didn't work very well for me. I had Tamarind Paste that I have used many times, and this was just way too much, I only used 2 Tbls ad it was still over whelming all the other flavors, would probably be better with lime or/and only 2-3  tsps of Tamarind.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Loved this. For 2, cut the recipe in half and it was perfect. When I make it again, I will use more Brussels.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Really good. Used ketchup instead of tomato paste and lime juice instead of tamarind. Still turned out delish. Would add a protein next time to soak up extra sauce.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I thought the sauce was  overpowering and heavy even after diluting it with some water.  I didn't use all of the sauce, there was too much.  I reduced the brown sugar to 3 T.  I even added extra roasted broccoli and tofu but the sauce overpowers it all. And with the miso and tamari, no extra salt is needed.  I was disappointed with the overall taste and heaviness and will not make this again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: An amazing recipe! This one is a real winner! The amounts are perfect of everything if you are using tamarind concentrate.  Some reviewers subbed lime juice instead and they thought it was too sweet, which I could see. Consider that if you are using lime juice. Otherwise, if you are using the tamarind, just make it as written. You won't disappointed with this one. It's versatile as well. Carrots could be a good add on. It will make plenty of sauce in a good way.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: If I ever cook this again, I would cut the tamarind in half or less.  The sauce was very tart.  Again, too many noodles make this a dish of nood;les and sauce because the vegetables are lost in it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: We enjoyed this very much and it was fairly quick to make. I read the notes and reduced the sugar to 1 Tablespoon, which I think was just right. Will definitely make this again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody:
Holy smokes, Batman! Searing in coconut oil over high heat just filled my entire kitchen with smoke. Any tips? 

P.S. It turns out I need to change the battery in my smoke alarm.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: There was way too much sauce, and it was way too salty. These ingredients must vary in saltiness depending on brand, given that others actually like it. I followed the recipe exactly. (Unless tamari sauce is something other than just tamari) (which I used reduced sodium)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Per others’ notes: Use 2 tablespoons of lime juice in sub for tamarind. 2-3 tablespoons of brown sugar. Add 1-2 teaspoons of fish sauce—maybe more? Jalapeño sautéed with scallions. This needs protein like tofu cubed or egg: I can’t over the feeling that this needs shrimp. Toast peanuts and then crush in mortar. For the record, 8 oz rice noodles is on point; separate/loosen noodles before adding to the pan. This did not strike me as overly saucy as others bemoaned. Would try again!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Used less sugar than was stated in the recipe and it was still way too much

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Ok - so I am hearing what everyone is saying about this sauce and honestly I had to do a little revision to open it up enough to where it was not unbearably salty. I don’t think I can aptly review a recipe if I changed it, but my revision was like a yummy vegan version of pad Thai. I could see this with some cooked egg and shredded chicken breast, as well. (I used lime juice and some ketchup as I was out of tomato paste and had no tamarind, cut back on the sugar and added ginger and sesame oil.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Too saucy for me. If I made it again I would lessen sauce. A bit hoisin ish swwet

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: So very good.  I added some mushrooms and edamame for protein and cracked 2 eggs in it after the noodles as I do in my pad thai.  Excellent!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: If you double the recipe, don’t double the sauce. Just do 1 1/2 times. Add more than double the Brussels sprouts and scallions and bean sprouts. Also don’t need to double the peanuts.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: When we doubled this recipe we doubled the sauce and it was TOO MUCH — it overwhelmed everything else. So when doubling the recipe I would NOT double the sauce. Maybe not increase it at all. Otherwise this is an EXCELLENT recipe!!!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: When we doubled this recipe we also doubled the sauce but that was a mistake. It was TOO MUCH sauce! Next time we would not increase the sauce at all. It has so much flavor it can be overwhelming. Otherwise we LOVE this recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I say to skip this one. It was a little ketchupy. :(

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Watch out how concentrated your tamarind paste is! Mine is very dense (like a brick) and the recipe came out horrendously sour and overwhelming. I tried doctoring it up, but there is no fixing this.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I added some tofu to make a more substantial meal. Turned out pretty well, but next time I’ll try a little less tomato. My tamarind concentrate is quite strong, so I definitely didn’t have an issue with the dish being too sweet.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Good but next time cut tamarind by half. Used 1/4 br sugar. Add veggies!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: The sauce is very salty and rich. I would make less sauce next time and add more water when blended. I plan to use unsalted peanuts too as the garnish. The Brussel Sprouts were the favorite!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. Subbed 2 tsp fish sauce for the miso because I didn’t have any, and it worked well (not vegan, anymore, obviously). Rich, with multi-layered flavors. Will make again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: I read through the reviews before making this dish to ensure I was making any adjustments that previous cooks had called for. So I reduced the sugar and started with just a small smidge of tamarind concentrate, and still this sauce was SO ACIDIC. I think the tomato paste needs to be reduced by about half. Since I realized this too far into the process, I added more tamari and a  big glug of tahini which helped a lot- it even made it taste even more like pad thai than I think the recipe intended!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: This is a great meat free idea for people who love Thai flavors and brussels sprouts. I am wondering about the serving sizes though since my husband and I didn't even get what we felt were two full portions out of this, let alone 3-4. Next time I'll double it, but if this is the only component of your meal, just be aware you may want to grab some spring rolls or something to round out the meal.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: This would be better without the Brussels sprouts. Their bitterness detracts from the dish, imo. I’d also add some crispy cubed tofu next time. I used tamarind chutney in place of the concentrate, since I had it on hand. The sauce is okay; the tomato paste was too strong at first.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: Made this for lunch and had three seperate people ask me what smelled so good in the office. Can't recommend highly enough. Will sometimes include mushrooms, which are a nice addition.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious! I replaced the tamarind with a tablespoon of gochujang and used maple syrup instead of brown sugar and it was wow!

Added carrots and purple cabbage as toppings instead of sprouts and marinated air fried tofu!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Savory Thai Noodles With Seared Brussels Sprouts Recipe
commentBody: 4 tbsp brown sugar

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I thought the flavors were excellent but I struggle with my eggplant absorbing to much oil when I cook it so I decided to roast it until slightly tender in the  oven with a little olive oil and salt and pepper first and then added it to the onion mixture to finish cooking . I reduced the oil slightly with the onions and it came out delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Your pan and oil probably weren't hot enough and that's why the eggplant absorbed the oil

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Can this be cooked ahead and reheated?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: You can probably assemble everything through step 3 ahead of time. Then start with step 4 when you're ready to finish. But if you cook the couscous ahead of time and then reheat it will likely be mushy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: In a word, yes. The couscous will absorb more moisture but the spices will be more melded with each other.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Made recipe verbatim and it’s quite good. I’d been worried about that much cinnamon, but it all comes together nicely in the end. Next time I’ll add more eggplant; seems like you could add a cup, or even two.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Yummy, but agree with other opinions about adding more eggplant and liquid. I used 2 1/2 cups vegetable stock and about 4 cups of eggplant. I roasted the eggplant to cut down on the oil. With these minor modifications the dish came out great.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Simply delicious! I added another cup of eggplant and adjusted the seasonings accordingly, and it was amazing. I’ll definitely make this again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I agree about the eggplant--I found the recipe skimpy on eggplant and should have trusted my judgment and been more generous with it. The amount of eggplant could easily have been doubled without doubling the entire recipe as long as you use a big enough pot or pan.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Good, not great. Definitely easier mid-week type meal. Since so many commentators said there wasn’t enough eggplant, and the couscous needed more liquid, I reverse-engineered, and just cut back on the couscous to 1cup. That worked perfectly—plenty of eggplant, and the couscous was cooked through.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Also added extra eggplant! The yogurt is a must - I added some lemon zest to the yogurt which was delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Took advice of others and roasted unpeeled cubes of 3 Italian eggplants (~1-1/2") at 400 degrees x 30 min on 2 parchment-lined sheet pans with one toss halfway through while prepping aromatics. Added eggplant after onions softened. Used chicken broth instead of water; smoked chipotle powder instead of cayenne. Added 1/4 t ras el hanout to spice mix; 2 dashes of liquid smoke with paprika/parsley stir-in at end (should have doubled smoked pap -- we like smokey)! A hit, will definitely make again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: delicious!  I substituted a can of drained, rinsed garbanzos for the couscous and homemade tomato sauce for the paste and tomato called for.  Doubled the cumin and added 1/2 t more cinnamon.  It reminded me of a dish we had at the Peak Cafe in Hong Kong so I've renamed it Peak Cafe Special 😃

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Next time I would use twice as much eggplant and maybe another veggie. Too hot for us, less heat next time.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Used vegetable broth instead of water. Topped with yogurt and sliced avocados. Yum.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Made with ~5 cups eggplant and added spinach at the very end.  Tasty.  Needs the yogurt and some lemon at the end to add acid.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Would it be a sin to add 1/2 lb of ground lamb to this dish?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Sounds tasty!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I made it with whole wheat pearl couscous because that is what we had. The I used a medium-sized eggplant (~3c.) and added a zucchini I had lying around, but did not change the spices. The amount of vegetables was perfect this way! whole wheat flavor definitely came through, so if you don't like that, stick with regular. It needed an extra couple minutes to absorb the water. Served with plain Greek yogurt as suggested. The yogurt MADE the dish. Don't skip it!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I too upped the eggplant to 4 cups (1 med sized eggplant), which was the right call. In addition, here is a much easier way to get the eggplant ready for the pot than roasting in the oven: toss your chopped eggplant with 1 tsp salt and microwave for 10 min on a towel/paper towels. I learned this from an Americas Test Kitchen pasta alla Norma recipe I frequently make. It ensured they don’t just soak up the oil in this dish and tasted great.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Following suggestions, I doubled the eggplant, roasted it and upped the tomato and spices by about 50%, also using a large onion and more garlic. I don't get the concern with cinnamon--my experience is that it becomes rather subtle in cooking (as opposed to baking). If anything seemed a little too much, it was the cumin. I used a large cast iron pot--can't imagine toasting the couscous in anything smaller and actually it was difficult to get uniform browning. Cumin aside, it was delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I cannot eat carbs, so I roasted cauliflower instead of the couscous. Served it over the cauliflower. Left out the water. Was excellent.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This was tasty but I think the eggplant to couscous ratio is off.  I used twice as much eggplant (4 cups) as called for in the recipe and it still was too much couscous to eggplant.  If I make again, I will double the eggplant (4 cups) and cut the couscous to 1 cup!

author: Julia Moskin
