recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody:
This is quite lovely for a quick weeknight recipe! I agree with others about quantity adjustments: I used 4 cups eggplant, 2 cups water, and a full 14oz can of diced tomatoes. 

Some ground sumac sprinkled over the yogurt just before serving elevated this to something special!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This was terrific.  I made it exactly as printed, except I substituted farro for couscous.  I loved it and will make it again regularly!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I made this as written. Unless you want the result tasting like cinnamon buns, omit the cinnamon entirely and substitute smoked paprika. This is almost indelible with the cinnamon; I’m shocked by the positive reviews. Otherwise tasty.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Very tasty. Paying attention to other comments, I used 3/4 c. Israeli couscous and 1/4 c. Lebanese couscous for variety. We didn't have cayenne, so I subbed hot paprika. I also used the air fryer for the eggplant (sprayed with a little oil, 10 minutes at 400) to reduce the tendency of eggplant to suck up oil. A little salty. I think next time I'll add sweet Italian red peppers and chop the onions more coarsely. Parsley is a must. Maybe a little lemon juice?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This is wonderful! and comes together very easily I couldn't find Israeli couscous, so I used Orzo instead and it worked just fine. Also increased the eggplant by about two cups (one large eggplant). Delicious with a dollop of sour cream!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Like some others I wanted to cut back on the oil a bit, but instead of roasting the eggplant first, I microwave the eggplant pieces for a few minutes, just to collapse those air cells.  Easier than a separate roasting, the eggplant is beautifully flavored by the oil (less than half of what's called for in the recipe).

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Great flavors. I added the eggplant and some zucchini.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I agree with Eric M--I felt this recipe was skimpy on the eggplant, and I should have trusted my judgment and used more eggplant. I used a Dutch oven, so I could have fit more, or perhaps have used a wide, deep skillet. Skipped the cayenne as I find it overpowering, and the dish didn't lack any flavor. I use fragrant, farm-fresh eggplant as oppposed to strip-mined, so I didn't want to losr that flavor. Easy to make and good but should have used more eggplant.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: "strip mined" made me laugh! Perfectly descriptive...

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Made the recipe as written. Agree it could take more eggplant and maybe some other vegetables.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: As important as the taste -- which was lovely -- this is a good dish for folks who are sure they don't like eggplant.  (Cooked exactly as advised, including the addition of plain yogurt.)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I couldn’t find the couscous listed in the ingredients and used normal kind instead and it turned out great!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: As written plus an extra eggplant.  Really good flavor.  Added crumped tortilla chips and a sprinkle of Parmesan and served over arugula splashed with lemon.  Awesome easy weeknight meal that all ages will jump on.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: As written with additional eggplant.  Served with some crushed up tortilla chips and a sprinkle of Parmesan over arugula splashed with lemon.  Really good flavor and everybody jumped in.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Yoghurt helped cut the heat but also lemon juice too.  Next time, less cayenne.  Very tasty dish.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I wanted a more complex carb, so I used farro. It was delicious. I used more liquid than called for --veg. stock instead of water. We tried with both yoghurt and feta. Votes were evenly divided between the two. Will make great leftovers. Keeper for vegetarian meal!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Forgot to say: I added a can of drained, rinsed chickpeas to get some protein in there. It was a good addition. Served with spinach/arugula salad.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody:
I made this but added an equal amount of whole or cut in half mushrooms and then a bit more tomato paste as there was more liquid.  It gave it a bit more heft and was totally filling and delicious.

It's now on the rota!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I agree with everyone who said “more eggplant.”  I tripled it, and that seemed perfect. It definitely needed something at the end. I added lemon and some granulated dulse for umami. I love this recipe and will make often. If you do add eggplant be sure to add a lot more liquid. I used homemade vegetable broth, but I think it was a bit of waste of good broth as the other flavors overshadowed the broth. If you do add more eggplant, remember to jump up the spices as well.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious under any rating, but particularly for a weeknight meal. I doubled the eggplant, as other commenters suggested, and increased spices and salt a little to compensate. Instead of yogurt I added some bland grated cheese at the end. I think a poached egg would also top this well. This might now be my favorite way to prepare eggplant.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: made this last night ... perfect as is

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This was a hit just as written

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody:
I decided to make this because I have a ton of eggplant and tomatoes in the garden. Switched it a bit though.  I used a full teaspoon of freshly roasted and ground cumin and mild Chimayo chili powder with the cinnamon. Instead of tomato paste, I chopped up about 3 cups of fresh early girl tomatoes right off the vine.  I doubled the eggplant and backed the couscous to 1 cup.  I also doubled the garlic.  I didn't have yogurt so I crumbled sheep feta on top instead.
It was heavenly!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I agree with the others about adding more eggplant. Also, a little guidance about cube size would have been helpful as well. I made approximately one inch cubes and next time I will go a little bigger so the eggplant holds up better during searing. But absolutely delicious. Just made it two days ago and have already picked up another eggplant!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I made this with one eggplant and 1/2 a head of cauliflower, roasted instead of pan-seared. Also added more liquid as others suggested. Other than that I followed the recipe, and I thought it was delicious. The extra veggies were helpful to balance out the couscous. I may use farro or spelt next time as it would probably help the leftovers stay a little more separated - the couscous kind of solidified into a big (but still tasty!) noodle mass.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I had to make this for someone who is gluten-free.  I started the recipe at Step 2, separately cooked short gluten free pasta til almost al dente then combined the two at the end till the pasta was al dente.    (Following others, I also added more eggplant, which was a very good idea.)   It was fabulous - possibly better than the original, which I've also made.  Wonderful dish!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: 3/4 tsp of Aleppo pepper instead of cayenne

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This is quite tasty and really easy. Added more eggplant and doubled the garlic the recipe called for and it turned out very well.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Lowered the couscous to a cup. Used 28 ounce can of diced tomatoes. Added 1/2 teaspoon paprika and 1/2 teaspoon of pepper.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Used 1 whole eggplant - about 5 c. - and 2 c. Couscous. Cut cinnamon substantially and still had great flavor. Roasted eggplant in the oven. Sautéed onions and garlic then toasted couscous in the pan. Added more liquid. Served with yogurt one time. Leftovers were even better. Ate with avocado.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Loved it!  Forgot to put the yogurt on the table though. The only thing it needs is a bit of crunch for more texture.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I loved this. I used more eggplant than called for because I was trying to use it up. Because of that I browned my eggplant & onions split between two pans so it would actually brown and not steam. All back to one pan when adding garlic and continued from there. I did need to add a little more water and go a bit longer for the couscous to be fully cooked. I may like couscous less al dente than others tho. Was out of yogurt so I sprinkled with feta before serving and it was awesome.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Love the idea of adding zucchini, and roasting eggplant instead of stirring with onions to soften.  I followed the recipe exactly, using grape tomatoes instead (sliced and seeded) and I think I would like canned/diced tomatoes better.  As far as it "needing" more veggies or less couscous, I think that it changes it from a couscous dish to a vegetable dish which is fine if that's what you want.  I love pearled couscous and like that to be the main event of the dish.  It's a great dish both ways.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Use only 1cup of coos coos and roast the eggplant to reduce oil.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody:
Hahaha I did a quick read and then scanned the ingredients list puzzled looking for the toast I was supposed to add in step 2.
Sounds delicious (with or without toast) so I will try it tonight.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I would suggest adding boiling water and not tap water, as it cooks the Israeli couscous “ptitim” better. This is the instruction on the package and the way they are prepared in Israel.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This is a good tip - I found my dish wasn't hot enough to cook the couscous all the way through so it had to cook longer.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This is really great. I’m not a fan of pearled couscous so I just left that out and serve over the regular couscous- which I think is better since it provides a different texture

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Roasted eggplants w/ a little oil and salt at 425 for 15 minutes, then sautéed with the onions for the last 5 of the 10 minutes. Otherwise followed the recipe. Needed a little more water and would advise stirring occasionally so couscous doesn't stick. Added a little lemon juice at the end. Delicious. What vegetarian protein would work well with this?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I found this quite bland. I’d recommend adding a bit more cumin and some harissa, and a squeeze of lemon with the paprika and parsley. Don’t skip the yogurt

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe.  I added a cinnamon stick and a chopped red pepper to the onion/ eggplant step, otherwise cooked directed.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Delicious recipe. Only adjustment would be to roast the eggplant cubes for about 20 minutes before sautéing with the onion. Otherwise the eggplant is undercooked (to my taste).

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This was fine, but not great. I added more eggplant as other commenters suggested. I don't think the additional eggplant affected the recipe, but I have more so-so leftovers than I really want now.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I prepared this exactly as written - - except doubled the eggplant, as others have suggested. I also added slivered toasted almonds at the end for some crunch. Topped with a dollop of yogurt. Really good!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This dish was easy and very well seasoned. I added Samin Nosrat’s Basic Yogurt Sauce as a garnish and I think that added an essential element of richness and tang. The texture with the yogurt stirred in reminded me of a savory, chunky oatmeal. I’m excited to heat this up for lunch tomorrow.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: this is one of our weeknight go-tos! easy to make, and reheats beautifully through the week. i double the spices & eggplant   use broth for the couscous. yogurt or sour cream go perfectly on top, if i have lemons on hand, i usually zest one   add a bit of juice to it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Like other reviewers, roasted ( large) eggplant at 400 degrees for 20 min, added when onions were done. Otherwise followed recipe, very tasty

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I found this recipe was really missing a savoury/umami component. I added a touch of fish sauce but Worcestershire likely would have also worked. Topped it with crumbled feta and at was perfect. I do love the cinnamon in this dish

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Really really good!  I used 3-4 cups eggplant and one large tomato.  Also used vegetable stock instead of water.  I almost didn’t add the yogurt, but found some in the fridge and it was so much better with it!  Great weeknight, one dish recipe!!!!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody:
Like many I increased the eggplant and also reduced the couscous. I tend to scale way back on oil so I too roasted the eggplant partially before adding it to the sautéed onions. Two notes. First, for those of us with “swarthy” heritage- in my case Greek- the cinnamon will be increased not decreased! For those of you who are aghast, please try to accept the spicing as a cultural food-way.
Second to finish this dish I added the recommended lemon juice and also some golden raisins! So good.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Used whole eggplant, whole 14 oz can diced tomatoes

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I consistently make the mistake of following reviewers suggestions before making the recipe first as-is. Followed a suggestion to add more liquid. Don’t add more liquid. Mine became a sloppy mess. It was a delicious, perfectly spiced, sloppy mess. Would’ve preferred the cous cous to have the hardiness it should have. Other than that,flavors were wonderful. Did spices as-is, added the full eggplant as using partial felt like waste. Added a large handful of spinach, and a doll-up of lemony yogurt

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I've made this a couple of times; you can sub other grains but pearl couscous (I used Bob's Red Mill whole wheat) gives it a chewy bite (never got mushy) I used one long Japanese eggplant (probably about 3 c.), a heaping cup of pearl couscous, didn't measure the spices but I used less cinnamon, more cumin, pimentón, and hot red pepper than the recipe called for. 2 T. oil was sufficient. I added liquid as needed, cooked a bit longer than the recipe. Served w/vegan yogurt spiked w/salt and zaatar.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: doubled the eggplant. next time I would cook it in the oven first.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I used at least 1.5 lbs of eggplant and a 14 oz can of diced tomatoes.  The eggplant melted away and was just right.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This is a fairly simple and delicious way to prepare the abundance of eggplant we have this time of year. The spice mixture is very warming and balanced. I was out of cayenne, so I subbed in 1/2 t of Harissa spice. I used the eggplant that was in my CSA, which definitely provided around 4 cups. I also the a whole 14.5 oz can of diced tomatoes. I stirred in 2 T lemon juice with the parsley and paprika, which brightened it, and topped with yogurt and Za’atar. Served with pita and a green salad.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Used 5-6 cups of eggplant and added 1.5 cups of garbanzos with liquids.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I double the amount of eggplant.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I was out of tomato paste and used gochugaru as a substitute. It gave the dish a nice kick and I would definately recommend it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: For a more traditional flavor profile, use "seven spice" instead of cinnamon

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This was soooo good! I didn’t double the eggplant because I’m not the biggest fan so it was just the right amount for me! Added a touch less cinnamon initially and bumped it up as I went to adjust to my preferences. I also added some browned ground beef with similar seasonings to the mixture to amp up the protein along with the Greek yogurt topping/pinch of sumac as someone suggested here.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: One of my husband's favorite dishes.  I have made this multiple times and added garbanzo beans and red pepper to  make it "heartier".   Always on our rotation.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Where can I acquire the yellow pot in the photo?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This isn't the exact same, but it is similar: https://food52.com/shop/products/5678-limited-edition-dansk-kobenstyle-pastels

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Will this recipe work with regular couscous?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Regular couscous will end up turning to mush. It might taste fine but the texture will be off.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Add 1 cup more eggplant

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I misread the recipe and toasted couscous in the oil, so I used little oil to cook veggies. Turned out fine although the couscous was more brown than golden. Didn’t have cayenne and smoked paprika so subbed with chipotle powder and sweet paprika instead. It needed way more water than the recipe to cook the couscous. Next time I’ll try chicken broth for the liquid. Topped with yogurt and it was a great satisfying meal!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody:
Absolutely delicious with and without the yogurt. The flavor is spicy but not hot (I went easy on the cayenne). One medium eggplant was perfect. I served it with a roast duck and I imagine a roast chicken would also be wonderful with the dish.  As a side dish this could serve 8. Would be excellent for a buffet.
One note: it is very easy to burn the couscous when toasting it. I looked away for a couple of minutes and had to start all over again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Do Israelis use smoked paprika in this dish? Cumin and the smoked flavor seem to fight for attention instead of complementing each other. I loved it without the paprika. Thanks for the recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I would also like to know if this can be reheated

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Yes, make it ahead and reheat in the microwave.  I love this as a take-to-work lunch: healthy, economical, and *delicious*.  It's certainly better than anything frozen, anything in a cafeteria.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I made this pretty much as written, but without parsley as I didn't have it.  We ate it without the yogurt, but I'll have to try it with the leftovers.  I loved it, my husband didn't love the cinnamon in it, but he ate it so I guess he didn't hate it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: The pearl couscous did not absorb any of the flavour. Maybe toasting not a good idea. Any suggestions for alternative. Anybody tried orzo instead?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: My husband has celiac disease so I’d like to make a gluten free version. Can I just sub quinoa for the Israeli couscous?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This was really delicious.   One issue with the directions- not a pot, but a pan. I used a 8X3  with high sides. The eggplant and onions won't "brown and shrink" in a 2 or 3 quart pot.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This was okay but required more water/stock as others have noted.  Flavors come together surprisingly well—don’t forgo the yogurt.  Added a half cup more eggplant and it could have taken more.  Feeds 8 as a side.  Would be lovely with minced lamb included in the dish or as a side to a grilled leg.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly as directed, and it was terrific! Did top with yogurt. It's spicy, but we like some heat so we found it just right. After reading the other comments - yes, it could use more eggplant. But it's also very tasty just as it is. Add a green salad and enjoy!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I made significant changes because I was attempting this with what I had on hand. With that said, it was delicious. Used bok choy instead of eggplant (same amount of cook time except leafy tops went in with garlic). Used tomato sauce (instead of paste and chopped tomato) and used that as part of liquid - probably added 1 c. sauce and 1 c. water.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody:
Fantastic!  Loved this.  Cut the cayenne - don't like heat and added some
garlic powder.  Had to cook it longer to get couscous to al dente.

Otherwise, really easy.  This is a winner.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I couldn't believe how good this was, and such a simple preparation. I agree, there is room for more eggplant. The cinnamon and cumin plus smoked paprika is an unusual and delicious combination.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Loved this recipe! I didn’t have pearl couscous so I used 10 minute faro and I also added a can of rinsed chickpeas at the end. I’ve been asked by my family to add this to the weekly rotation.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: We really enjoyed this. I made it with more eggplant than called for, and added in some crumbled feta at the end instead of yogurt. It needed about half a cup more liquid than indicated, and I might use veggie broth instead of water next time for even more flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Really nice recipe. Made a keto-friendly version by adding skipping entirely the couscous and adding a can of black soybeans at the end. Made it breakfast by topping with a fried egg and half an avocado.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I made without the onions since they are objectionable to my husband.  I increased the eggplant to finish off what I had on hand and also increased the chopped tomato to an entire 15 oz can.  Delicious.  I found it to be a little salty (I am salt sensitive) but the rest of the family liked it as the recipe detailed.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I doubled the eggplant and bumped up the onion, tomato, spices & garlic by about 50%; all other ingredients stayed the samel this made it more of a stew than a pasta dish.  At the end, I also folded in a finely-chopped preserved meyer lemon half along with the smoked paprika and parsley.  Scrumptious!  And today at lunchtime I find out how well the leftovers reheat; I anticipate that if I stir in a little warm water before microwaving, it will be fine.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Perfect vegetarian dish for late summer/early fall when eggplant is plentiful at the farmers' market and it's still just a tad too warm outside to turn on the oven. I doubled the eggplant, not intentionally, but because I wanted to use the whole eggplant and not have any leftover eggplant go to waste. I, then, upped the spices. The only complaint I have, which was my own fault, not the recipe, is that I used too much cinnamon. Next time, I will use the amount as directed by the recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I halved this by using half the amount of couscous (and water) but keeping the amount of eggplant the same, and that ratio was just right for me. I used cilantro at the end because that's what I had in the fridge, and it was lovely. A spritz of lemon wasn't out of place either.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: We only had one eggplant, I cooked as directed and it was quite yummy as is!  I will try 2 eggplants next time as per everyone's advice.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Halved the recipe but kept the whole eggplant, tomato and onion. Came out delicious and I will make it again, next time with less cayenne. Topped mine with EBTB greek yogurt dip from Trader Joe’s.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Loved it! Used half the couscous, more onion, a can of fire-roasted tomatoes. Will make again!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I didn't have couscous, so I decided to try making this as a risotto with arborio rice. Of course, the preparation is different, since I added more liquid, a little at a time, and stirred until the rice was done. I also used a larger eggplant, so I increased the spices a bit. Turned out deliciously!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody:
Warning! Measurements are WAY off! I followed this recipe to a T but I got a humongous pot of couscous with hardly any eggplant. I had to add an additional 3 cups of water (4 1/2 cups total) plus 2/3 of a large 28 oz can of crushed tomatoes because, before these additions, when I added the couscous, it instantly became a pot of cement. There was no way it could've cooked without adding tons of liquid.
I think it should have been only a half cup of couscous or maybe a quart and a half of water?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody:
One star rating because of the couscous and water measurements being so far off. Got a pot of cement.
But if you adjust the recipe accordingly, it's a good 4 stars.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This was really delicious. I was skeptical about the amount of cinnamon, but it worked to offset the spiciness. I agree with the earlier comment about roasting the eggplant first to get it tender. I found that I had to cook everything longer than recommended because the eggplant wasn't tender enough, nor were the couscous. Also I used chicken stock in place of water for the liquid...more like 2+ cups and an extra tablespoon of tomato paste. This one surpassed my expectations.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: For all its simplicity, this was remarkably delicious.  Per recommendations, I did roast the eggplant but otherwise followed the directions.   Kudos!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Added some cooked chopped merguez sausage (1/per person) + can of cannelloni beans + bunch of bundled thyme (removed after everything cooked) - for super entree

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Great meatless dish and the warming spices are perfect for the cooler fall weather. With the exception of no fresh parsley at the end, I followed the recipe to the letter and it was easy to prepare so good for a weeknight. My only suggestion, like others, is for more eggplant. Other than that, I wouldn't change a thing. Looking forward to the leftovers today for lunch.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This was super! Yum. Thank you NYT! Thank you Julia Moskin! This one is a keeper! Great way to use garden produce. Total yum.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This recipe sounds delish.  I have one question though.  I live in Israel and eat both ptitim and Israeli couscous, but they are not the same.  Ptitim of the small round variety are called "couscous": but it is a pasta product. Real couscous of the Magrebi (North African) type can be bought here in "fine," "medium" and large or "Israeli."  Ptitim need to be boiled, but couscous no. Which is called for in this recipe?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! My couscous needed about another 1/2c of water to cook through.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: 1/4 t cinnamon, 1/8 t cayenne, 1 t cumin. No paprika. Serve with a meat and green veggie. Pork tenderloin or roast chix is good. Broccoli too.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Like others, I made a few adjustments and it was wonderful with those tweaks. 3 cups cubed eggplant / 1 cup couscous / 1.5 cup water to start and then added another .25 cup as the dish simmered. To speed up eggplant's cooking without dirtying another pan, I salted the cubes and microwaved them for 90 seconds. Added a diced red pepper when I added the couscous, plus 6 oz smoked tofu. Next time, I'll stir in baby spinach for color and nutrients just as I turn off heat.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Double the amount of all the veggies. I added half a head of garlic and extra cumin. We prefer spicy in my house so extra cayenne, too. Couscous cooks much faster than described in the recipe. I left it to simmer for about 3 minutes and took it off the heat for another 3. Would make again with these tweaks.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Loved this. Took the suggestion of other reviewers and doubled the eggplant. Made enough for both of us to take for lunch for a couple days. Absolutely delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Made pretty much as written, but used a small (400ml) can of tomatoes and a large eggplant that I roasted first. Also, more cumin and sm. paprika. Worked really well. Very tasty. Note: people who report their couscous turned to "cement" are probably using regular couscous instead of pearl. There's a big difference, and that simply shouldn't happen with pearl couscous. I suggest checking after the couscous has cooked for 7-8 minutes to see if it needs more water. (I added an additional ½ cup.)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: How would you adjust this if cooking with regular couscous (I have a lot on hand)?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Cook regular couscous as per box instructions. Skip step 2 and prepare the veggies in step 3, adding garlic for 1 minute and then fresh tomatoes for another 2 minutes.  Step 4 only add tomato paste and stir it through, simmer for another 2 minutes or so.  Add the mixture to already prepared regular couscous, along with paprika and parsley, and yogurt if desired.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Agreed with the need for more eggplant and liquid. I used dry white wine at the end and it added a nice complexity. I also used barely rather than couscous so cooked it almost completely and then added the eggplant mixture.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: any suggestions on a gree veggie dish to accompany?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Spicy!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I just made this today. It was fabulous! Per other notes, I added finely cut onion and minced garlic to cook with the mushrooms. I also floured the chicken before browning, rather than adding the 2Tbsp of flour to the mushrooms. I had marinated artichokes only, so used them.  Sprinkled some fresh thyme leaves over the top before putting it in the oven. Really the best chicken ever!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I’d like to make that too! Unfortunately, it ain’t this.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I picked my vegtables just before cooking. I think my tomatoes were not that juicy. Ended up adding 1/4 cup more water. Worried about cinnamon but the dish at the end tasted great.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I tried this recipe and last night and it was delicious.  I made it according to the directions and made no modifications.  I think the small amount of smoked paprika at the end really boosts the flavor so don't omit it.  I also have to say that leftovers warmed up the next day were maybe even tastier.  I'd make this again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Use extra eggplant, as much as desired.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Why the tight-fitting lid? It never says to cover the pot

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: You need to cover pot while cooking the couscous--step 4

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This is a great dinner, very healthy and tasty. As other cooks suggested, I increased the amount of eggplant to about 3 cups. It is good with parmesan cheese on top. An excellent way to use up all your extra eggplant from your garden!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Spice was wonderful. Agree with the commenter who said that it sounds like a lot of cinnamon,  but it all blended so well and was not noticeable, just tasty! Make sure your could cous is cooked though.  :)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I couldn’t get the couscous to cook with the amount of liquid suggested. Stuck to pan and it was hard.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: My mom and I made this the same night (different households). They liked it more than I did. We both roasted the eggplant in the oven first rather than in the pan. We both used a lot more eggplant than called for. I really liked it with a lot of Greek Yogurt and smoked paprika. My parents didn't use the yogurt. I ate mine with keilbasa and I thought it worked well with that.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I made this with Orzo instead and only used a cup instead of 1.5 but kept the water the same and it worked well. I loved the spice combo but will use a little less cayenne next time as it had a tiny bit too much kick for me. Served it with braised greens and seared salmon and it was delish.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: As with all NYT recipes, I read many of the comments and adopted several of them......doubled the eggplant and roasted it in the oven, reduced couscous to 1 cup, added spinach at the end.  Even with up the reduced amount of couscous, this recipe makes a large amount of food.  My husband and I ate it as a side dish for two nights, I had it for lunch one day and we still had leftovers.  The flavors were even better on the second serving....the cinnamon and cayenne really came through.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: How would you make this gluten free? Could you swap Israeli couscous for rice?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: May I substitute quinoa for couscous?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I thought this was a great recipe. Used it as a side to grilled chicken, and it would go with pretty much anything and as a standalone. Don't for get the yogurt with a squeeze of lemon in it!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with harissa to add some heat (in place of the cayenne)?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I am probably committing a mortal cooking sin but I make a great ratatouille by microwaving (yes, that's the sin) cubed eggplant, zucchini, green peppers, then draining them and putting them in a large pan with stewed italian tomatoes and their juices plus about 1/2 T of olive oil, dried thyme, couple of  cloves of garlic. I simmer to reduce it. I've gotten raves. It seems to me that you could do the same with just the eggplant in this dish and then not have to worry about oil!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, warm and spicy— perfect for this rainy evening. I used 4 cups of egg plant as Eric suggested. Will definitely make it again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: There's no step to add smoked paprika - is that a mistake? Should it go with the spices or be omitted?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody:
In step 4 it says 
Uncover, stir in paprika and parsley, then taste and adjust the seasoning with salt.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: delicious flavors, i would double the eggplant next time to make it a more substantial main course

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Fabulous and easy! I doubled the eggplant - used 4 cups of cubed eggplant - cubes were about 1/2 - 3/4". My couscous required more liquid than called for . . . I started with the recipe then had to add.  Check the directions for the couscous you use.  I needed a little more than 2 cups of liquid - about 1 1/2 times the amount of couscous.  I used some tasty bean broth I happened to have combined with a little water.  Served with a dollop of Greek yogurt - very yummy!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Just curious what to serve with this.  Side salad?  Rice?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: You dont  need rice as couscous serves same purpose. How about a nice lukewarm green bean salad? You need something slightly lemon-y with this....

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Soooooooo good. Went back for seconds till it was gone!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: For those who did not like the cinnamon in the recipe too much, a stick of cinnamon instead of powder while cooking is better.Sticks of cinnamon are frequently added to meat dishes in Sri Lanka and India to soften the smell and flavour of stews.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Made this last night and it was excellent.  Good spice and heat.  We doubled the eggplant and would add more next time.  This is a forgiving recipe and you could add other veggies, garbanzo beans or meat.  I think tiny meatballs would be excellent with it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I used double the egg plant which it needed. More fresh parsley and served with a wedge of lemon at the end would be a good idea.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Took advice of others and cubed and roasted my eggplants and added to softened onions. Used chicken broth instead of water; smoked chipotle powder instead of cayenne. This was AMAZING.  Yogurt is a must.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Made mostly as written. Like others, I roasted the eggplant first to cut back on oil. I also added feta at the end, which was a great addition. Chick peas would be wonderful here as well, if you wanted to make this an even heartier dish. Wonderful flavors, will definitely make again!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I would agree with upping the veg or lowering the couscous.  Roasting the eggplant would also reduce fat contents.  Was deliciously spiced

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This is extremely delicious -- flavors are perfect. Added another eggplant and made with regular couscous, which also worked nicely.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Ah! The aroma of this dish! I doubled the eggplant and cut back on the olive oil. A winner!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Guided by previous comments, I increased the eggplant to 3 cups and reduced the couscous and water to 1 cup each. I also pre-roasted the eggplant for about 10 minutes, and reduced the oil to 1/4 cup.  It came together deliciously. The commenter who said the yogurt really makes the dish was right on. Just finished the leftovers for lunch!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Excellent dish. Loved the flavours. More eggplant is a good idea. Really easy to cook.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious. I made it as directed in the recipe (without the yogurt because I didn’t have any). A noticeable heat, but warming, not intense. The eggplant was tender and juicy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody:
I was out of couscous but wanted to eat this ... turned out well with bulgar. Cooked the bulgar to “almost done” while preparing the the onion/eggplant etc then added the bulgar with a bit of water for a few minutes at the end. Yum!
Texture will be more interesting with couscous I think but was very flavorful :)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Hmmm, usually the NYT recipes have a high success rate with us, but we score this as a "miss". We added an extra cup of eggplant as many suggested, but left the spice mix amounts the same. Even then the spices seem to completely overpower everything else. We ate it, but were pretty disappointed. We were using fresh eggplant and tomatoes from our garden too. If I do make it again I'd cut the cinnamon a lot, the cayenne some as well.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody:
The smallest eggplant I found at the store ended up coming out to about 4 cups cubed, so I added so I threw in a bit more couscous and found that a ratio of 2:1 cups eggplant to couscous felt perfect. 

Someone else suggested roasting the eggplant in the oven to prevent it from soaking up too much oil. I also had this issue, so I plan to try that method next time.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Fabulous.  I diced about 3 cups of eggplant small and roasted it in the oven and increased the liquid -- a cup of chicken broth + water -- a small amount, but otherwise followed the recipe precisely.  Flavorful, unusual, satisfying.  Will make this one again and again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Easy to make. The balance of flavors from the spices was great, not overpowering. I added more eggplant.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Delish! I had roasted eggplant on hand so used that. Also had mushrooms to use up so added those with the onion. And cut the kernels off two ears of corn from last weeks farm box and added at the end, that added a little bit of crunch. Yogurt was perfect. This is a keeper recipe that lends itself to variation for sure. Even better the next night over fresh spinach and topped with grilled halibut. Next time I’ll add lemon as note suggest.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Only used a cup of couscous and a can of crushed tomatoes instead of fresh, no water, and about half the spice load, and it’s f’n delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Any suggestions on what to serve with this dish?  Side ?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I liked this very much but I agree with Eric M in that next time I will add more eggplant and maybe less couscous.  The spices were perfect!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. I used a whole eggplant. It was also great the next day. I will make it again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: The only thing that I did differently was cut up grape tomatoes because that’s what I had on hand. It’s an easy, quick, and delicious dish.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Great flavor! Perfectly versatile for a small dinner gathering - we served the yogurt and a pan-seared, oven-finished halibut as separate options so that the dish could be vegan, vegetarian or pescetarian.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I made this verbatim.  I was very skeptical about it because I'm not really a fan of eggplant in any form, but thought, perhaps with all of the spices, it might be a hit.  I was sadly mistaken.  Neither my husband, nor I ate very much of it, the rest went right into the garbage.  I think, if I were to make it again, I'd leave out the eggplant and replace it with chicken...

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody:
I did exactly as written. It’s kind of a tasty mush along the lines of a Minnesota hot dish. I’d add olives for something to put my teeth into. I served just this and a Caprese salad as Julia touted it as “a great weeknight one-dish dinner.” Fortunately, it’s only Monday. This is the kind of dish I used to take to a hippie potluck back when we had potlucks, that is.
Hot, warming, invisible pan of mush...

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Mine wasn't mushy at all! I cut back on the oil, and I used a smaller pot (a 2-qt cast iron Dutch oven), and the eggplant was still recognizable.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody:
I adjusted recipe based on other’s advice, and this dish was absolutely delicious. I used creme fraiche as a substitute for the yogurt. 

I used 4 cups of roasted (375 deg @20 min)eggplant. I used 1 3/4 cups of chicken broth, added a 1/4 tsp of el as ras el hanout and increased the smoked paprika to 1/2 tsp. Delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This is delicious! I made it at lunch (one of the perks of the new WFH normal, I guess) and it was very quick and easy to make. Perfect fall food!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I think the smoked paprika makes the dish- don't skip it or substitute with regular paprika.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This is one of my favorite recipes, and the fragrance it sends through my building is unparalleled. I like to use a medium eggplant or lots of baby eggplants and then add chopped red bell pepper to the eggplant and onion mixture. I serve it with fried halloumi and a salad (massaged dino kale is wonderful here) with lots of lemon juice to help balance the spice.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I made the recipe as written with a little extra eggplant (1 medium yielded a little over 2 cups) and, because I didn’t have tomato paste in the pantry, subbed 3 T tomato sauce. Turned out perfectly, and we loved the eggplant to couscous ratio.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Excellent. Easy quick and delicious, and a great way to enjoy eggplant, which I’m always looking for. And cheap! And beautiful! A great one pot dinner perfect for a vegetarian weeknight. Toasting the couscous gives it a new texture to me that was heavenly. This is a keeper.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I'd make a variation of this again as a way to jazz up Israeli couscous but I'd leave out the eggplant, which just became mushy. Also, it's definitely just a side dish, not a main.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I made it as a side dish for dinner and it was delicious!  I used fresh tomatoes (diced) which worked well.  The spice mixture is critical -- great flavors!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Increase spices considerably; increase eggplant

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: love this! i halved it tonight and made it without the tomatoes. added 1/4 c water and stirred in a lot of swiss chard at the end. yum.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Loved this dish. Made it with farro which required a bit more liquid and simmering time. Added spinach at the end as well. Really a lovely medley of North African flavors.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Used a bigger pot, added only 1/4 tsp cayenne and added 2 c of water.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Add to previous note. 2.5 cups water which get the couscous to a softer state. A bit spicy but the yogurt cuts that rather well. I would make it again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: More eggplant used,  needed more liquidor better, less couscous.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Used regular couscous and doubled the eggplant as other commenters did. Omitted cayenne for the toddler’s sake. Delicious, easy meal.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This is ok, definitely add lots more veggies. Served with preserved lemon.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious!!! I actually roasted my eggplant rather than frying. I also used more eggplant and a bunch of spinach. Best thing I’ve made in a while!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Any chance you can skip the toasting the cous cous part?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: You can for sure but it adds a nice nutty flavour and also depth. It really doesn't take much time so would recommend!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This was very good.  i agree with the comment that said the eggplant should be doubled.  I also suggest adding some feta sprinkled over the top at the end.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, easy.  Used Le Creuset.  I thought it might be too spicy but actually I might add more cayenne next time.  Also added a can of chick peas at the end.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Extra eggplant, more spicy, chicken broth

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This was a surprise hit.  SO easy.  My eggplant chunks were pretty big, which worked out well.  As others did, I tweaked the spicing for our palates: just a dash of cinnamon, WAY increased cumin, added ground coriander.  What put this over the top, though, was sautéed haloumi cubes.  I may also have added a knob of butter as the stew rested off heat.  I think it would be delicious with merguez sausage in it, too.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: For two the full recipe with 1/2 cup couscous worked well. Also added “coins” of merguez. Had no parsley but, fortuitously, mint was a great (and possibly superior) substitute. I had some tomato water on hand so used that instead of plain water.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody:
Liked quite a bit. I cut the couscous to one cup, and it was fine. Next time might do 1-1/4 cup. Added a cup or two of fresh baby spinach and it worked nicely. One smallish eggplant was plenty. Added pine nuts at end; next time might toast them.

To people who complained about proportions, think of this more as a couscous dish than an eggplant dish. I’ve spent a fair amount of time in Middle East, and this recipe captures the tastes and texture. 

Added bonus: twice as good the second day!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: extra eggplant

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I used less oil, 1/4 c and cut back a bit on the salt and pepper. The dish was still spicy enough. I also used  2 good sized Asian eggplants and just thickly sliced them. Came out great and yes it reheats well.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I made this tonight and my family and I loved it! I made it as is except to add additional eggplant. Because of that, I did need more water, but no big deal. The heat was just right and the texture of this dish is lovely. This comes together quickly and could easily be modified to any other softer veggies you have on hand (zucchini, mushrooms, other squash) I will definitely be adding this to my rotation! yum!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious. Made this according to recipe the first time.  Eggplant:couscous ratio was off.  Second time, I reduced couscous to 1 Cup and used 2 large eggplants.  Doubled tomato -used crushed.  Too much heat so I used 1/8 tsp cayenne.  Just before serving I added cubed feta for some protein and bite.  Added cilantro instead of parsley.  
Any leftover zucchini in the fridge can be added next time.  Greek yogurt finishes it off. This is a very forgiving recipe.  Amounts don’t have to be exact.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I think you can also double the amount of spices in this.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: At first, this was good. The next day I reheated leftovers: Even better! The flavors melded and were more related. I roasted the cubed eggplant, and added to the finished onions; easier and less messy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I loved this dish — and I agree with the other reviewers who said it was even better the next day. I roasted two eggplants (cubed) and used cherry tomatoes. Added spinach for some green goodness and chickpeas for protein. The yogurt definitely ties it all together — I added diced cucumber, dill, and lemon to mine. Served with feta and harissa. Lovely.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Decreased couscous a little, added bell pepper, mushrooms, and Chile Pequín. Added a little more liquid and garnished with basil. Very good.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Made the recipe exactly as noted - so, so tasty! My boyfriend and I devoured it. But I really wish I'd read the comments first. The eggplant shrunk quite a bit and the couscous dominated the dish. Will double the eggplant next time, and probably add a little more water and spices/salt to compensate. (An easy adjustment, since one cubed eggplant made 6-8 cups!) Might also suggest adding peas or some kind of easy green veggie for a more balanced meal.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I braise the eggplant separately at the start and add it to the tomato mixture. I’ve also swapped couscous for quinoa, which is equally flavorful

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I made this with quinoa instead of the couscous because of a gluten-intolerant family member. (I used 3/4 cup of quinoa and 1 1/2 cups of water, and just extended the cooking time by 8-10 minutes.) it was really delicious!  I may try adding chick peas next time for a complete vegetarian entree.  I also agree that the recipe can easily handle a bigger eggplant.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Also used quinoa instead of couscous. Cooked it separately and added it to the veg mixture at the end. Added about a cup of chickpeas for some protein. Absolutely delish!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: The spice mixture is spot on but I thought it needed more texture because everything was a bit soft - especially if eating it as the main dish.  The smoked paprika is a must.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: A great dish with wonderful complex flavors. Don’t worry about the exact amount of eggplant—one medium eggplant works well. I needed about a half cup more liquid than the recipe calls for. An excellent main course or side dish, and terrific as a leftover!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Made with cauliflower in place of eggplant. Next time reduce cinnamon by half and use chicken broth.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Very delicious. Added portobello mushrooms because I needed to use them up and it was a great add.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Used regular couscous and added a whole quart of mushrooms, added more stock. It was very yummy!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Excellent, delicious. Instead of sautéing the eggplant, I roasted (20 min. at 350) cubed eggplant (appx 1.25#) misted and tossed with olive oil spray. Then I added to the cooked onions. I made this dish the day before, toasting the couscous and making the veg / spice mix without yet adding the tomatoes, water and couscous, which I did next day, just before dinner. I used two high-quality tomatoes, chopped, instead of canned, and this worked very well. I used extra tomato paste, also a good idea.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Made this for the second time, omitting cinnamon, adding smoked paprika. Flavor is great, texture is... challenging (everything soft). Topped with some leftover roasted broccoli which helped. Next time will add roasted broccoli + some roasted chickpeas, crispy potatoes, and/or feta. When cooking onions and eggplant, crank the heat up to a true med/high so that the veg gets properly browned, to help with texture (stir every 1-2 min to avoid burning; it will soften later with the couscous).

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Mixed opinions here at our house. I thought it was bland, my partner (who at hers with Italian sausage) thought it was delicious. As a vegan meal it was very one note. I doubled the smoked paprika and tomato paste. Still needs something.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Use less cinnamon. It’s pretty spicy if you use the whole amount of spice mix for half the amount of couscous, so maybe use smoky piment d’ville instead of paprika at the end.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Use 1 cup couscous, 2 cups water (broth is better), and a large eggplant. Add lemon at the end. I also mixed the Greek yogurt with lemon juice, a lil olive oil, salt and pepper. Roast the eggplant in the over at 450 for about 10 minutes (lightly browned) before adding to onions for a more even browning.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Cut down on Cayenne Pepper

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: It is pretty good! Will make again. Next time, I'll try roasting the eggplant first because I didn't love the consistency. I wonder if there is a way to sneak some protein in there. Maybe toasted walnuts on top?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: The yogurt adds protein.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I wouldn’t make it again. As a novice cook trying to broaden my repertoire, my version was bland and uninteresting. It’s a waste of yummy couscous and eggplant. Don’t use a dutch oven bc it will absolutely burn the bottom.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I have made this recipe as is directed at least a dozen times and it is absolutely delicious. As a riff on the Sicilian caponata I did add toasted pine nuts and Castelvetrano olives at the end with the smoked paprika. Served over a bed of arugula - yum!!! The reheats rather well and makes an excellent lunch or dinner during the week.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Soo yum!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I added more eggplant, as others had suggested.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Good with summer squash, easier than EP

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Sounds delicious, but at only 11g of protein per serving I would definitely not consider it a “one-dish dinner.”   Any suggestions for doubling the protein while keeping it vegetarian?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: @PeaKay I added some ground beef it was fantastic!!!!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I generally top bowls of "warm spice" seasoned dishes with thick Greek yogurt.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: @Vanessa Parla Excep ground beef isn’t vegetarian…

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: What kind of eggplant do you recommend?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: I've been making this for several years now, especially during the winter - the spices bring a warming heat on a cold winter's night!  This dish can be reheated, too, for those nights that you want a really quick meal - just pop it in the microwave to warm it up.  i did cut back on the couscous, and generally add at least half of a 4 oz can of tomato paste, to give it flavor.  Get the ingredients ready ahead of time, and this will come together quickly.  Soooo good!!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly as written. Delicious and easy to make. I might add more eggplant next time.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: To make this dish GF, substitute Quinoa for the Couscous. Toast the Quinoa as you would the couscous but be careful not to burn. Use 1 1/2 cups of Quinoa to 3 cups of water or veg stock. Also, roasting the eggplant, as previously suggested by another commenter, makes for a less oily dish. Great flavors and we had for a day or two afterward as leftovers warmed in the uwave.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody:
Absolutely delicious.  I would adjust the cayenne down a little bit in case you're cooking for someone with low tolerance to spicy foods.  Also, I needed to add about a cup more of water than the recipe calls for in order to fully cook the couscous.
This one is definitely going on the rotation!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Add some chopped green bell peppers and finish off with a few sprinkles of Ras Al Hanout spice. This dish is lovely alone with a fresh green salad or served alongside slow cooker short beef ribs.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: This is comfort food for me -- I make a batch (doubling the eggplant and increasing the tomato) and freeze in single serving portions. In the midst of winter, I pull it out, pop it in the oven to reheat while I enjoy a glass of wine, and serve on a bed of arugula or spinach. A taste of summer in January.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody:
This recipe mirrors a recipe from Melissa Clark’s cookbook Dinner for one pg. 126. Unfortunately the photo does not represent the recipe…..cherry tomatoes in the photo are not in the recipe. It just irks me when recipes photos do not represent the ingredients in the recipe. 
The recipe on this post does present the actual look of the finished product. 
Very disappointed with Melissa Clark. 
I’m a retired professional chef so I expect honesty and yes professionalism.
Keep cooking Chef Suze

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Loved this recipe-- super savory, smokey and a filling meal. I think next time I'd add at least another cup of eggplant.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody: Good but it's Couscous with Eggplant, not the other way around as name suggests. The spices were not detectable except for the smoked paprika. Address toasted pine nuts for crunch along with yogurt and lemon juice. Makes a huge pot! Next time will listen to comments and cut couscous, up the spices and eggplant.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Seared Eggplant With Pearl Couscous Recipe
commentBody:
I followed this recipe to the T. It was delicious. I just wish I had some bread to go with it. 
I grew up in the same neighborhood as Adeena, the recipe curator. She’s an amazing person and I’ve never had any issues with any of her recipes. I know if I follow them, they’ll be delicious and this one was no exception.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Shrimp Bathed in Olive Oil and Lemon Recipe
commentBody: Our family restaurant, Santa Lucia, 160 West 54th St., served this with the addition of minced garlic and it was called Shrimp a la Ricky.  Impossible not to love.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Shrimp Bathed in Olive Oil and Lemon Recipe
commentBody: No, do not boil shrimp, poach them in plenty of salted water brought just to a boil then cut heat and sit with lid on until cooked.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Shrimp Bathed in Olive Oil and Lemon Recipe
commentBody: Simple dish, but very tasty. Obviously, high quality shrimp is essential. This was a side dish served with soup and salad. My hungry men were sated.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Shrimp Bathed in Olive Oil and Lemon Recipe
commentBody: Steam the shrimp instead of boiling them. More of the flavor will be retained this way.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Shrimp Bathed in Olive Oil and Lemon Recipe
commentBody: Following a similar recipe from elsewhere, I added 4 minced garlic cloves, and DID refrigerate/marinate for about 3 hours, before bringing back to room temp. Used defrosted colossal (13-15/#) Argentine red shrimp. Easy, attractive and tasty.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Shrimp Bathed in Olive Oil and Lemon Recipe
commentBody: I have no idea what your detractor, Richard, below is saying, or why. Refrigerating to marinate is one thing, freezing entirely another. I would suggest exhibiting no further interest in anything he has to say.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Shrimp Bathed in Olive Oil and Lemon Recipe
commentBody: We made this recipe tonight and we were not impressed by it. The suggestion to add garlic would have improved it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Shrimp Bathed in Olive Oil and Lemon Recipe
commentBody: I love this recipe. Super easy and so delicious. Quality shrimp  (and olive oil tbh) is going to be the make or break for this being good.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Shrimp Bathed in Olive Oil and Lemon Recipe
commentBody: And really extra virgin (early harvest) olive oil that's meant for drizzling (rather than cooking) is best.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Shrimp Bathed in Olive Oil and Lemon Recipe
commentBody: This is a lovely, simple meal

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Shrimp Bathed in Olive Oil and Lemon Recipe
commentBody: I created my own similar dish using the shrimp. But I use olive oil, lemon zest, garlic cloves, fresh thyme, fresh bay leaves squeezed couple of fresh lemons. Salt. Pink peppercorns. Toss all together and let it “marinade “. I serve with a garlic aoli  or just plain with a good bread to soak up !

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I just made this again, but tweaked it slightly. I used 1/2 c plus 1 T oil instead of 3/4 c because when I followed the recipe exactly, the bottom of the cake was a bit oily. The reduction did not sacrifice moistness and got rid of the oily residue. I also switched the rum from the batter to the glaze (1 T only) and added 1 t of cardamom rather than 3/4 t. Obviously this is all a matter of personal taste, but I thought the cardamom popped more without being overpowering; same for the rum.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I’d like to leave the rum out of the recipe. Do I need to replace it with something else? If so, any suggestions?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Rum extract or flavoring, available in the section with vanilla, lemon extract, etc.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: @Sally please note that rum extract may have alcohol.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: The recipe should specify the loaf pan size. I used an 8"x4" pan based on the amount of flour, sugar, and liquids, and it was perfect. Of the spices, I used cardamom, I used an olive oil labeled "fruity" (perhaps from Israel or Spain, but we bought it while on holiday in Zurich), and I used a spiced rum from a California distillery. Most importantly, I cut the sugar by 25%, and I'll cut it another 10% the next time I make it. The cake is delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: @Aviva Garrett does cutting sugar arbitrarily like that mar the texture?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: @scott D Typically you can cut out up to 1/3 of the sugar in a recipe without impacting the texture

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
I made these as little teacakes instead in a muffin tin. It makes about 15 cakes(filled 3/4 of the way), and I baked for 18 minutes at 375.
The little drizzle of glaze make them look so cute! I did adjust the glaze so that it was a touch firmer than the recipe so that it wouldn't flow down the sides too much.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
So perfect! Made it for my mom-law’s birthday and made another one a few days later as per request!! After having read the comments:
1 tsp cardamom
Between 1/2 and 3/4 cup oil
3 tbsp orange juice

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: This was easy to make and incredibly good. The cake is super moist and the cardamom and orange flavorings are very complimentary. Although I used the rum, I didn’t notice it in the final product. It might be better placed in the glaze.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone made this with a non-dairy milk? I’m hoping my oatmilk won’t comprise the flavor.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: When making the glaze do not use 2 tablespoons of orange juice use 2 teaspoons. 2 tablespoons will give you a very thin runny glaze that will soak into the cake, not pool on top like the photo.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: After reading many comments I made this with 5/8 cup olive oil and cut the sugar slightly; used cardamom; used Triple Sec instead of rum; also used Meyer lemon zest and juice instead of orange.  I used an 8 1/2 x 4 inch glass loaf pan and the cake almost overflowed the pan, darkened very quickly and was done in 45 min.
