If using glass, one should probably reduce the oven temperature by 25 degrees. I reduced the juice in the glaze for a thicker glaze.  A very lovely, tasty cake.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: This was a hit at a Solstice party!  Made as written with cardamom and a little bit less oil.  Beautifully moist and delicious.  I used my grandmother’s aluminum 9x5 loaf pan and it was done early at 32 or 33 minutes - not the first time for quick breads in this old pan.  Sharing in case this is helpful to anyone’s thought process.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Cooking temp/time too high.  Top almost burned.  350 for 45 minutes, then test, as another reviewer wrote. I found olive oil too strong and an odd flavor for a cake. Next time, I'll use a neutral oil, like grapeseed or avocado. I doubled the glaze recipe and mixed in chopped walnuts, for flavor & crunch. I'll try chopped candied orange peel in the batter to bump up the orange flavor, & Grand Marnier instead of rum plus 1 tsp. in the glaze.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I don't want to buy rum just to use two tablespoons; I will never use the rest. What would be a good substitute?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: What about those miniature bottles of liqueurs sold at liquor stores? While there I am also going to pick up a another small one of Cointreau to add to the glaze along with some grated orange zest.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: You could use rum extract. Or if you don't like rum flavor, try vanilla extract. One teaspoon should suffice.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: You could buy a nip-size bottle at a liquor store. But I expect it would be fine to just use a little extra orange juice and perhaps a bit of vanilla.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I made the glaze with Grand Marnier. Tasty.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Reduced amount of oil to 5/8 of a cup.  Used ground cardamom. Cooked in metal loaf pan. Cooked for 50 minutes, not an hour.  Otherwise followed recipe. Came out great!  Light, moist, flavorful. Next time I might increase just a bit the amount of cardamom, but not by much.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
delicious, easy, moist, packed with flavor! 
I used bob’s redmill 1to1 GF flour and oat milk instead of whole milk to make it Gluten Free & Dairy Free - and it worked wonderfully! Added an extra teaspoon of ginger and a tiny splash of vanilla for extra punch. Added Rum to the glaze as well. Perfect for the holidays!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Thank you so much for posting. I was just pondering if this cake could hold up under gluten and dairy free set of ingredients.  I am going to make this cake this weekend using your strategy.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I made this for a birthday, but doubled the measurements and put it in a bundt tin. Guests loved it, however I swapped the rum for Glenmorangie. Overall a beautiful cake when garnished with pistachios and candies oranges!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I used roasted corriander. Used a bunt pan. I put orange zest into the icing. Delicious! Not too sweet. Gets better as it ages.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I used a 9" X 5" loaf pan, but I would use an 8" X 4" next time. Cake was only 3" high (at the center).

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: 8 1/2" x 4 1/2" is the correct size for this cake and many other loaf cakes.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe! I dressed it up a bit by scaling up all ingredient weights by 1.5x and baking it in a 9” springform pan for about 45 minutes. Halfway through I added a loose layer of aluminum foil on top to prevent too much browning. Instead of a the glaze, I topped it with a blow-torched meringue. Pretty and delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Have made this twice now and it's absolutely delightful. Like others, I reduced the oil some because it was a little too oily the first time. I don't think the glaze works too well; it is very runny and sets clear, not white. Next time, I'm going to look for a different  glaze recipe...I think one other commenter suggested one. But the cake itself is divine!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Excellent, but much better a day, or even two, later. The second time around, I doubled all the spices and used both coriander AND cardamom, and it was far tastier and aromatic than the first time around with the quantities in the recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Delicious. Used 1 cup all purpose whole wheat flour and 3/4 cup almond meal. Cut sugar to 2/3 cup. Used lemons instead of oranges - these are Covid conditions. Cut olive oil to 1/2 cup. Used 1tsp fresh grated ginger, 3/4 tsp Garam masala and 1/4 tsp nutmeg. Would increase lemon zest next time. Used combo of whole milk Greek yoghurt and milk. Made buttermilk glaze and cut measurements 1/4 cup sugar with 2 Tbs lemon juice, 1tbs buttermilk.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I doubled the cardamom, added some ground cloves, ground ginger, and added about 1/4 cup chopped candied ginger and some dried cranberries. Used apple juice instead of rum and orange juice because that's what I had, but threw in some orange extract to compensate. Doubled the glaze ingredients, using apple juice and whatever was left in a bottle of ReaLemon. Used 3 small loaf pans, baked ~45 minutes. Very nice!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I made this cake exactly as directed, but didn't add the glaze. While the texture of the cake was pleasing, the cake didn't have any of the flavor notes of the ingredients. Hmmm.... When I make a loaf cake, I want the cake to stand on its own. This one was just OK. Will return to my other fave loaf cake recipes.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: in what recipe world is the size of the pan omitted?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I doubled the glaze! I am not a sugar fanatic, but I added first 1/2 while the cake was still hot and then let soak in. Then I added 2nd half after cake had cooled down. It made for a divinely most experience

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Worked well vegan! I used 2 tablespoons of apple sauce per egg as replacement and reduced fat coconut milk instead of the milk.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Try orange flower water or fiori di sicilia extract

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: This came out great! Really moist and nice flavors. Used Bacardi Black rum - the rum flavor is quite subtle after baking.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I used caramel monin syrup instead of the rum and it worked deliciously.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: What size loaf pan?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: 8.25 x 4.25 inches was fine for me.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I was a little disappointed.  Hardly tasted the orange or the rum flavor.  And it took far less time to cook - about 50 minutes and it is a little too brown on the edges.  I suspect I should have used a smaller loaf pan.  (No size specified in recipe.). It is moist so I suspect it will keep well.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: i second the question about non-dairy milk substitutions. Milk protein not allowed.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Does anyone else think it is a lot of oil for that quantity of flour and sugar? And that 375 is a little hot?  I used almond milk and reduced olive oil to 2/3 cup. plenty moist! Makes 4 cups batter, which fits in a standard 8 x 4 loaf pan or a 6 cup bundt pan. I used the bundt pan and baked 43 minutes, but had to reduce oven temp by 25 degrees after 30 minutes because the cake was browning too much.  made glaze with 1 Tbl each rum and orange juice.  Tasty enough that I think I will make again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I used orange liqueur in place of the rum and cinnamon in place of the cardamon. The cake was very moist and received huge accolades.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: What size is standard for loaf pan? It doesn't specify in recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this twice and everyone loved it.  The second time I doubled the orange zest and put quite a bit more powdered sugar in the glaze, which actually hardened on the surface.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I used a 9.3 x 5.2 x 2.7 and 1hour was too long. The cake burned a bit on the outside. Still moist inside though. The flavor is great.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: This cake is wonderful. My family and friends like the coriander the best. I use a bunt pan, it bakes in 30 minutes. Just left out the rum. I roast 2 tsp of coriander and one tsp of pepper. Sometimes I use dry orange peel and orange juice. Putting orange zest in the syrup is good as well.  Company loves this cake.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
Loved this with the cardamom (and the comment to sprinkle pistachios on the top)! So fragrant and lovely. This would be such a nice Thanksgiving weekend brunch item. 

My oven normally runs cold, but 60 min almost burned my loaf. The inside was fine, though. I am the type who loves crunchy brownie edges, so I happily ate the crunchy heels while everyone else enjoyed the rest.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Wow, what an interesting & delicious cake. Spicy, sweet, savory, & tart as I used lemon instead of orange which I did not have on hand. Added a dash of cayenne & toasted black peppercorns with the cardamom (plus juniper berries) to make it spicy. Enough sugar in the recipe to balance out the spice but even better when slathered in raspberry preserve. Olive oil added an earthy but not unpleasant depth. The lemon was fantastic; I'll bet orange is just as good. Not complicated, will def make again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Glass pan (45 minutes)   small aluminum pan (25 minutes). Don’t forget to lower temperature b/c glass pan

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
Very tasty. Made a half recipe and it fit nicely in my rectangular loaf pan. Did 20 minutes at 350, then turned, then another 20 minutes at 350. A little moist but I liked it like that.

If you're doing a half recipe you can take 1-2 teaspoons of EVOO off the recommended amount. I used coriander and cardamom and was very pleased. Also used a navel orange for juice and rind, worked out well.

Recommend this for morning to go with coffee or a potluck. Will make again!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
This was pure deliciousness!  I used cardamom (doubled) and 1.5x ginger.  Spiced rum  (also doubled).  I did not have an orange, so I used the zest of two tangerines instead.  The cake is quite flavorful and beautifully aromatic. 
 This bakes quickly, so turn after 20-22 minutes and not 30.  The glaze was nice, but not necessary.  I will definitely make this again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
This was a wonderful cake, and held up well to modifications for a vegan diet. I replaced the whole milk with an equal amount of unsweetened, original almond milk, and replaced the eggs with 2TB chia seeds and 6TB water mixed together until gelled.

I might play around with other possible stand ins for the eggs when I make this again in the future.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I enjoyed this cake. I did make a few modifications which still created a lovely cake: I used only a half cup of (olive oil) and a full tsp of cardamom. And baked the batter in three mini loaf pans and found them to be baked by 45 minutes. I also added 4tsp of poppy seeds to make an orange poppy seed cake. Lovely texture and I found the orange flavour to really deepen the second day. The cake stayed moist and very tender well into day three, at which point we finished it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Based on other comments, I reduced the amount of olive oil to about 140 ml. It seemed to me that the volume and weight measurements for the olive oil didn't match up - when I measured out 180 ml, it was closer to 1 cup, so I decided to scale back. It turned out great! Next time, I'd increase the amount of confectioners' sugar in the glaze so that it's a little thicker (my glaze was runny, as suggested, but it just ran right off of the cake).

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: an hour in the oven is too long. I think for our oven the optimum time would be about 50-55 minutes. Also I used about 3/5 of a cup. It could do with more spices and that's after adding about an extra half a teaspoon of the spice mix. Regardless, it tastes great!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: 8x4 pan

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
Only 1 tsp ginger
Cardamom not others and no pepper. 
Amazing

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
1 tsp ginger
8x4 pan

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Way too oily and bland - spice flavour didn’t come through at all. Won’t make again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Made this cake and it was a hit! I reduced the oil to 5/8c and reduced the sugar to 3/4c per recommendations. I had a dull microplane so wasn't able to get as much zest as I wanted, but the cake still tastes sufficiently like orange (the glaze helps). Added about 6oz chocolate chips to make a chocolate chip orange cake and would absolutely do this again! I used 2T OJ for the glaze plus the juice of a few tiny calamansi fruits for extra brightness. The glaze was thin but tasted great.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
Ok be like me and do these 2 things because this cake was delish: 1 teaspoon EACH coriander seeds and fennel seeds ground + 1 tsp ground cardamom since I saw comments that the cake was underspiced, halve  the sugar and the glaze 

Don't be like me and DO read comments and use full olive oil amount (greasy), DO use parchment paper (what possessed me???) the cake broke in the middle as I was trying to get it off the pan RIP it seemed like the excess oil had fried the bottom.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Try baking at 350. Got browned too early at 375

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: This cake is so delicious, and so spiced and flavorful. Other people in comments are saying its not spiced enough and too oily… like any recipe, the quality of each individual ingredient will determine the outcome. Also if you don’t like oiliness why are you making an olive oil cake? The olive oil is the whole point! I think its perfect the way it is

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this several times over the years, exactly according to the recipe, using cardamom, ginger, pepper as the spices. It is always wonderful, and the batter is not thick, I’ve also baked it in a Bundt pan, which has worked well and is pretty for serving.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I basically used this as a template to make a different cake but it was really good so hear me out. I didn't want the olive oil flavor to be two strong so I subbed in 1/3 c melted butter and I scanted the olive oil a bit as other comments said too oily. I then added chopped pear that I had sauteed in red wine, and a tablespoon and a half of port. The spices I used were ginger, cardamom, and cinnamon. I left out all the other spices and orange. It's good! (I had some pears I needed to use)

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Please pass along this suggestion to the recipe editors. I've greatly appreciated the use of grams and mL in your recipes. I'd like to suggest for viscous liquids, such as olive oil, grams be used. I suppose I could just use the same number of grams as mL, however, the problem with that is oil is not the same as water. The specific gravity of olive oil according to the USDA is 0.9143 (though it varies from 0.80 to 0.92). The amount of olive oil for the spiced olive oil cake would be 165 grams.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this cake twice now, the first time fully following directions and I found it to have a slight aftertaste. The second time I made it I used 1/2 c of sugar instead of the full cup, as I suspected that was my issue, and now it’s exactly to my liking! (I also used 1/4 c of citrus olive oil from my favorite small olive oil company and then 1/2 c regular olive oil). If you’re looking for something to go with a cup of tea or coffee I definitely recommend halving the sugar!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Super adaptable and delicious. I used yogurt in lieu of milk and added a little Diaspora Co. turmeric along with the coriander and ginger. Replaced 1/2 cup of flour with chopped walnuts. Delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried using almond flour in this recipe?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly as directed, using ground cardamom and black rum. It was delicious!! It took only 42 minutes to bake completely, however. (I'm not sure if this made the difference, but I baked it in a metal pan.) I also glazed it on a wire rack over a cookie sheet, so I was able to catch and reapply the glaze that ran off the cake.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Followed others recommendations for slightly less olive oil (used 1/2 cup + 1 Tbsp) using orange infused olive oil and used 3 Tbspn (vs 2 Tbspon) of OJ in the cake. I might use Cointreau or Grand Marnier instead of rum to enhance the orange flavor. The cake was fantastically moist and flavorful using KA GF flour. Made an orange glaze (4 Tbspn granulated sugar + 2 Tbsp OJ, boiled for a couple minutes) and poured that over the loaf once out of the oven. Skipped the powdered sugar glaze.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Delicious, and so fast and easy. The only change I made was to add a pinch of ground clove to the batter and a small dollop of orange marmalade in the glaze. I love the cake's soft interior texture, less dense that I had expected. Will definitely make again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I was worried about how quickly the loaf was browning around the 30 minute mark, so I put a piece of foil on top and it turned out fine cooking to the full hour.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious as written and we talked over experimenting with other options like lemon and almond flavor. Big hit at a dinner party, I'll make it again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone substituted raw sugar for the granulated sugar?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Not a fan of orange - would lemon work?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I think lemon would be awesome. I was wondering the same thing when I tasted mine as written.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Not sure exactly what it meant by "ground ginger."  Does he mean the powdery kind you buy in a jar in the spice aisle?  Or freshly grated?  Thanks!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Ground ginger is the powdered dry spice in the jar.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Yes, he means the powdered kind found in spice sections.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
Does the recipe call for using only 1 of the 3 spices noted :
ground fennel, cardamom or coriander 
OR does it mean Fennel with either cardamom or coriander?  
Each time i read it I think something different

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Ah, a good case for the Oxford comma. From reading the tip, I think it's one of the three.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I made it last night with only cardamom and it was fine.  I thought about mix/match but came to the cardamom bottle first and dropped that thought.  I think a mixture would be interesting, but possibly you need to choose only one for the entire amount just for impact.  It you divide among two or three spices they may be lost. But play around with it because imho it's not going to ruin the cake no matter how you go.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried substituting wheat flour with gluten free flour?  I'd hate to waste all those ingredients if the g.f. flour doesn't work.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I'm wondering how strong an olive oil people are using.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I will soon need a new bottle anyway, so I bought Pompeian Extra Virgin Smooth so I would have a nice fresh  oil for this recipe.  I don't pick up  any particular olive oil taste in the cake.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Can you use fresh ginger instead of powdered ginger? I don't particularly care for powdered ginger.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Other olive oil cakes I’ve made have had too heavy of an olive oil aftertaste. Does this have one?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I just got done making it.  I do not tasted the olive oil, at least now while it is still a bit warm.  I used a very neutral extra virgin olive oil not one of the “spicy” dipping ones like an arbequina.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I second the question on pan size. Looks like a low volume of batter overall. And 1 cup sugar to 1 3/4 cups flour seems like it would make a pretty sweet cake even though it is billed as not too sweet. Has anyone made this with less sugar?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: recipe does not specify size of loaf pan..I assume 8 1/2 x 4

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: That's what I used and it was perfect.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Does anyone have ideas about making this vegan? I have a vegan friend coming for dinner, and would like to try baking something!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: By baking this in a round cake pan, should I allow for extra cooking time?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Makes a moist cake - next time would cut down a bit on the sugar, and add a bit more salt

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I followed the recipe except for omitting the rum, which I replaced with a teaspoon of vanilla. The cake is very moist, but the spices don't come through that well. Maybe it's just that my spices are a little bit old. I wasn't sure how I was going to like the cake with olive oil, and I have to say I'm not a huge fan of the olive oil scent or taste in sweet baked goods. Having said that, I would gladly make this again with a neutral oil.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Great cake...moist, nice flavor

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Follow up: I tried making this vegan, and it was pretty good, a bit sticky. I used almond milk and flaxseed egg replacer. A bit nutty, had some trouble getting it out of the pan. I'm eager to try this as written too.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I really liked the cake, but next time I will use the glaze from Melissa Clark’s Orange Marmalade Cake instead of the glaze in the recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I made this in a bundt pan without issue, using ground spices, which I would  have preferred to be more pronounced in flavor. Nor did the flavor of the rum come through; it might have worked better in the glaze than the batter. But still, it's  a fast and easy dessert: ideal if you're one of those people who tends to forget leaving butter out to soften.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I ended up making this without the rum, and didn't substitute anything else for it - the cake still turned out very well.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: For those who don't imbibe, always remember the "airplane" mini bottles of rum for this and other recipes. Great way to add cognac and kirsch to recipes without buying large quantities!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
I had to use fresh ginger instead of powdered and wow it made the cake so vibrant!

I also cooked it for about 40-45m, not the full hour, and it turned out perfectly - but then again, my oven seems to run hot. But keep an eye on the cake after the 30m mark!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Becca I used unsweetened soy milk for nearly everything.  Fine in this recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Lovely cake but glaze recipe is way off.  I followed it exactly and the glaze was way too runny and sunk into the cake immediately while poured on warm as the recipe notes. Looked nothing like the photo.  I let it cool completely then found a different glaze recipe that called for double the confectioner’s sugar and the same amount of juice. Turned out much better and much more like the photo.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: This was amazing!!!  I added some rum to some fresh whipped cream too...actually wasn't necessary...cake was just fine all alone.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I sifted the flour and subbed Triple Sec for the rum- so tasty. Not too sweet, great texture.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Excellent! I reduced sugar to 2/3 cup and it was still plenty sweet and it was done after 40-45 minutes although my oven may run a little hot. Definitely check sooner than one hour! Perfect crumb, moist, nice flavor with the citrus and cardamom.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I made it exactly as instructed using cardamom as the main spice. I was a bit worried as the recipe indicates a stiff batter but I found the batter to be surprisingly runny. Still baked perfectly. With all the moisture from the olive oil it seems like a hard cake to over-bake. I took it out of the oven after 55 minutes and it was perfect. I also very lightly draped a piece of foil over the top part way through baking to avoid it getting too browned on top. Would definitely try again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Has anyone tried this with almond milk or another non-dairy "milk"?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: This was absolutely delicious! I used oat milk with zero issues and my oven runs HOT so using a standard loaf pan I baked it for a little over 40 minutes total and it cooked perfectly.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
I made this using a crazy mutant orange from my back yard (not a sour orange but some weird hybrid the previous owner grew from seed - not good to eat but great for cooking).  I used cardamom and substituted white pepper for the black pepper.  I also was out of dark rum and used gin instead.  It was great!  Nice bite. 

I used a wider bread pan and the loaf was done in 50 minutes.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I wonder if I can replace the orange with a lemon. You know, we're living in the age of Covid... use what you have!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
My favorite NYT baking recipe ever. I didn't have rum
so added 1/2 tbps each of vanilla & almond extract and 1 tbps bourbon. Also instead of 1c granulated sugar I did 3/4c and 1/4c light brown. Needed much more confectioners' sugar to the glaze - was way too thin otherwise. I'd wait a full 30 min after removing cake from oven before glazing.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Just made this and its excellent. Substituted Amaro di Angostura for the rum, cos I didn't have any rum. Also, 3/4 sourdough for regular flour cos I have so much. Subtly spicy, moist, delicious. Trying not to eat it all at once.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I used brown sugar and baked for 40 minutes.  Used 1 T lemon juice + 1 T simple syrup instead of orange juice. Next time would use all lemon juice.  Glazed with lemon juice and zest of 1 lemon. Luscious and delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
No pepper
Used - cinnamon, cardamon & nutmeg as 3 spices. Then added a couple dashes of ground cloves. 
Zested a 1/2” piece of ginger in the wet ingredients 

Was tasty. Did half orange and half blueberry glaze

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Made this but didn’t have rum and used a tablespoon of almond extract and a tablespoon of Jack Daniels and it turned out great! Great texture. The glaze soaked in to the cake however it set ok when I tried it the next morning. If you plan on serving the day if I would use a more trusted glaze recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I made this cake again for the second time and it was excellent. This time I used toasted fennel seed as the spice. One of the big pluses for this cake is that it is tremendously quick to make. We had dinner guests and I forgot dessert so I quickly threw this together in the 30mins before they arrived and it was the perfect accompaniment to coffee after dinner. One note, the recipe warns that the batter will be stiff. I found the complete opposite. It is a very loose and pourable batter.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
Such a great riffable base. I made the following modifications:
- Decreased olive oil to 1/2 cup 
- Made up for the decrease in oil by subbing in an equal amount of heavy cream for milk.
- Used 1/2 cup brown sugar and 1/4 cup maple syrup. 
- Increased spices to: 1 tbsp ginger, 1 tsp cardamom powder, 1 tsp garam masala, about 1/2 tsp allspice, and 1 tsp vanilla extract to round off the flavors.
- Had no oranges so used the zest and juice of 1 lemon.
- Glazed with warmed up marmalade   rum.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: It was okay but won’t make it again.  The orange added barely a hint of flavor and the rum none at all.  It was pleasantly spicey and had a nice texture but nothing special.  I baked it in a metal loaf pan and checked it with 8 minutes to go.  It was clearly done. My electric oven is very predictable so I was surprised to see it done.  Maybe the metal cooks faster than glass.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Cut down the sugar, added rum to both the cake and the glaze, and threw candied orange peel in with the zest. Delicious and excellent texture.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: This cake was pretty good, though I would like more orange flavor in an orange cake. Perhaps next time I will use a bit less oil, as it was oily on the bottom, and use additional orange rind in the glaze, along with a bit of rum. The tip about toasting the spices is a good one that will implement next time.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! Added fresh grated ginger. Only needed 50 minutes baking in an aluminum 9x5 loaf pan.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I ended up adding another half cup of powdered sugar to the glaze, and it was still too runny. Disappeared on top of the cake — not as shown in the recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I think I added 5-7 more minutes to the fifteen

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Definitely watch position in oven. Followed another commenter and covered in foil halfway, but should have lowered temp slightly and covered from the start!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I made this switching the run with vanilla extract, and put the cake batter into mini Bundt moulds, to make little cakes. I reduced temp and time in oven a bit to accomodate that switch, and they turned out great.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this a few times. It vanishes quickly in a brunch buffet and is a often requested for the pre-zoom Scrabble goodie exchange. My fav variation is 1/4’tsp coriander, 1 tsp cardamom, 1 Tbl  vanilla, skip the rum and oat milk rather than dairy.   But, because coriander tastes like soap to many of my friends I usually skip the coriander I subbed zest from one lemon and two tangerines out of necessity once and it rocked. Works well as tea cakes too. Very flexible recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Take out 20 mins early with convection oven

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I halved the recipe and made cupcakes.  Cooking time is reduced.  Works out very well.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I made the cake using cardamom, and Cointreau instead of rum (didn’t have rum).  The texture was perfect but I found the flavor lacking. Next time I will up the spices and orange zest. I will also put zest in the glaze.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: 2 tbsp muffin. 20 min. Reduced 1/4 cup sugar could do more

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Added a mix of cloves, cinnamon and nutmeg because I didn’t have ground ginger and it worked really well!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Delicious and quick.  Used less oil as others did.  Making it again now.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: This got RAVE reviews when I made it for a dinner party. I doubled the recipe and made it as a bundt cake, and used extra cardamom because I love the flavor (1 tsp instead of 3/4). I used vanilla extract instead of rum and it came out great.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Use the glaze for the Orange Marmalade Cake. Double it and add 2T OJ instead of the glaze used in this recipe. You’ll not regret it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I accidentally ground both fennel and coriander seeds and the cake was perfect!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I made this exactly to the recipe and it was delicious!! Absolutely a keeper, and I will make this often. It actually gets better after a day or so.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
I doubled the recipe and yielded 3 loaves (200mm X 73mm).
The neighbors who received the extras raved about the great flavor.
I used just 80ml oil plus 1/4c grated overripe pear; I reduced sugar to 3/4c, using 1/2c white sugar  1/4c packed brown sugar.
I used grapefruit instead of orange for zest&juice, opted for coriander and just a dash of cardamom.
Will definitely make this again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Very greasy

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Wonderful spicy cake for a rainy day. I used coconut sugar and 1/2 cup oil. Came out perfect. We ate with tea.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Yes the glaze is runny and I assume the purpose is so it will soak in a bit.  But why does the accompanying photo show the opposite type of glaze? It’s almost frosting in the photo! Messy editing.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Lovely in a 6” x 3” round cake pan. 375, 45 min. 2T homemade limoncello, 120 ml olive oil, 120g sugar, zest rubbed into sugar. Warm wet ingredients. Glaze was too subtle so added a little lemon juice.  Next time I’ll reduce or omit black pepper and sub in 1/4t sea salt.  Did not care for bursts of salt from medium coarse kosher salt.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Moist, rich and interesting flavor. Love the crunchy outer layer that forms. Watch the cooking time, for me this cooked way faster than I expected, next time I’ll check for doneness at 45 min.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Used 1/2 tsp ras el hanout and 1/4 tsp cardamom for the ground spices and it was lovely. Will bake at 350 next time cuz it got far too browned, and may double the glaze for added sweetness.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Didn’t use rum—instead, I added about a tbsp of vanilla and a splash more of orange juice to the wet ingredients. Turned out perfectly and was a huge hit with my hallmates.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Cardamom, orange extract and zest in the glaze

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Took A LOT of creative liberties here based on what I had on hand and it actually turned out lovely. Used almond milk instead of whole milk, toasted my own whole fennel/pepper and then crushed it in a nutribullet, used amaretto instead of rum, and subbed lemon juice instead of orange in the batter. I also threw in some homemade candied ginger and it added a fun crunch.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Yes, I used oat milk and it turned out great

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Sub quatre epice and 5 spice, lemon juice, vanilla

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Made this for dessert last night. Really lovely for the end of a dinner with friends, interesting and not too sweet. I didn’t glaze it in the end, served with cream. Used cardamom as my additional spice. I’d use fennel if I had a less fruity olive oil. I didn’t use rum as I didn’t have any, but just added a little more orange juice.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I did not add rum or make the glaze. Delicious!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Lessened sugar, more cardamom, Cointreau instead of rum, in a loaf pan at 375 it was done at 40 mins or would have been burnt !! :) absolutely incredible cake

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: This was outstanding! Still learning how to manage high altitude (& a new oveb!) cooking so it was a bit dry. I'll use a bit more powdered sugar & orange juice in the glaze next time-& there will be!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I followed this recipe exactly and used a light-colored metal loaf pan. It came out burnt, despite the oven being accurately at 375 degrees F. I had to cut away the burnt edges before glazing. Has anyone else had this happen?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
Delicious! Ideal paired with coffee and Autumn leaves.

I made it with a couple small tweaks. I swapped rum for cognac (I used Courvoisier). Instead of using the booze in the batter, as suggested by other commenters, I opted to add 1 tbsp. to the glaze. The cognac played beautifully with the orange. I also used a smidge less sugar than is called for in the batter and I found it plenty sweet. I'll definitely make it again!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I substituted coconut milk instead of whole milk for someone allergic to milk, turned out great!  Also sprinkled pomegranate seeds on top, yum!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Made exactly as written, came out perfectly!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: A French chef we knew who thought bourbon was a great flavoring agent  put me onto using bourbon in deserts like flan, and I will try bourbon in this recipe without trepidation.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: This cake is the real deal. Balanced and moist. Definitely carve off 10 min from cook time. I served with a dollop of labneh, roast apricots and a sesame toffee shard.  If anything, go harder on spice! I think I could’ve done with the added heat.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Such a delightful recipe! I'm sober so I used an alcohol-free aperitif instead of rum. I'd think almost any liquid at that volume would do. I also used whole wheat for 100 g of the flour. I freehand the spices each time and use more orange zest since I can't really taste it otherwise.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Baked it in a metal bread pan in a toaster oven air fryer (on the plain bake setting, not convection) and it only needed 30min.  Moist and light but surprisingly bland (couldn't really taste the orange or rum).  I only put in half the sugar, so it was somewhere between a cake and a breakfast bread.  I would be interested to try honey yogurt instead of the milk.  Candied ginger or currants might also be interesting.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Anyone tried any other good sub for all purpose flour? The recipe looks great! Can’t wait to try it out.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: has anyone made this with GF flour?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Bland. It tastes like a greasy cake donut. I followed the recipe as written except I used rum extract instead of rum. Baked in a metal loaf pan and it was done at 50 minutes. Bottom was crunchy and greasy. This was easy to mix up but I won’t make it again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Wondering if anyone has made this cake in a round 9” cake pan, and if so, how long did you cook it??

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I made it in a round and it was done in 40 minutes. I'll continue to make it in the round - feels more like a cake than a tea loaf.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody:
Too
Much cardomon flavor for me. Next time less than 1 tsp. Doubled glaze - wonderful.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I subbed the fennel, cardamom, or coriander for Chinese Five Spice. Otherwise, followed the recipe exactly. Gorgeous dessert -- skipped the glaze (we're not glaze/frosting people) - delicious, spicy, orange-y, and moist!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: The orange cake of my dreams! The citrus flavor, though delicate, shone through, and the cake responded well to modifications: I used flax eggs, reduced the olive oil to 135 ml, and replaced rum with almond & vanilla extract (realized too late I had no rum). Highly recommend toasting and grinding fresh whole spices!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: Made this for a dinner party and my guests - who know me and my cooking - said "I didn't think you were a baker!!? This is delicious!" In other words, easy enough for a non-baker to make a delicious impact. Used rum extract as I was out of dark rum, coriander for the spice, and baked closer to 50 minutes than the full hour. Will definitely make again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Spiced Olive Oil Cake With Orange Glaze Recipe
commentBody: I'd definitely increase spices and zest. Some chopped nuts would be a nice addition. It keeps well for several days.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody:
From one home cook to another ---

Delicious. 

But there are quite a few steps that can be cut. If you aren't into salting your tomatoes AND cucumbers separately/do not want to clean up more dishes, do it together, or don't do it at all. Similarly, icing the cucumbers was a bit frivolous. Put them in the freezer for a few minutes if you are looking for "firm" cucumbers. 

Another note, I was considering making crostini instead of croutons, but croutons are most definitely the way to go.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: icing the cucumbers was a bit frivolous, no kidding, especially if they are fresh. Also I happen to like the bitter skin of normal cukes and this adds another flavor note to the salad.  Sheeps milk feta would be a classic option here

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: I already had most of the ingredients except for the halloumi, but I breaded mozzarella slices (flour, beaten egg, panko) and they worked just fine!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Because I had delicious late summer cherry tomatoes, I saved their juice and whisked it into the vinaigrette (using a Serious Eats tip from their panzanella recipe). Pre-salting the tomatoes is still important but then incorporating the juice back into the salad by letting the crunchy croutons soak it up - brilliant. This was a fantastic side to roast chicken.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody:
I LOVED this but here are some things I'd do differently -

1) I didn't ice the cucumbers - not necessary
2) Salt the tomatoes in a colander 
3) WAY too much bread - I'd use half as much bread next time as we only used half of it
4) I used ¼ cup of pickled red onions 
5) Just slice the halloumi in slabs and sear, then cut up.  

This was about 10 servings as a side.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody:
This is a nice chopped salad and agree with the description of it as a mix of a Greek salad and panzanella.  If I made it again, I'd eliminate the halloumi (or mozzarella) as well as the icing of the cucumbers and the addition of vinegar -- instead just use the salty, fresh juices from the tomatoes. I might, however, add a crumble of feta.

Because they are so evident as ingredients, the quality of olive oil and bread really matter here.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody:
Won't say I made this (no Halloumi and many shortcuts employed) but I took it as inspiration and made a great no-meat-on-Friday lunch salad with available crusts of various breads, cannellini beans and mozzarella bites for my Mom.  It was the perfect September salad with great tomatoes, cucumbers and red onion. 
Thank you! 
PS -  I will get some Halloumi and try again with the real recipe. Can't wait to get a bite of that toasted cheese in my mouth with a bite of ripe tomato.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: do not drain off liquid from salted tomatoes. Taste and add EVOO judiciously at the end and toss, taste again until you like the result.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Love this when the tomatoes and cucumbers are fresh and abundant! Our store usually carries halloumi but they were out, but they DID have a packaged paneer so I tried that. It fried up nicely. So I’m anxious to try this again w halloumi, but it’s good to know paneer can work in a pinch (at least texturally).

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Allow the halloumi to really come to room temp before you sauté it;  you will lose less whey if you do.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: 5th time and still amazing. This should be a “no recipe, recipe”. Just use the ingredient list and assemble, balancing quanties according to what is on hand. Put all veggies in bowl (including chopped garlic), use plenty of basalmic vinegar. Do NOT drain any of the lucious juices. Dont bother draining cucumbers (tried that previously with no impact for the effort). DO toss bread cubes with olive oil and salt and toast. DO sear halumi in pan until golden brown. Mix in and enjoy. Yum.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: This was excellent; imagine if Greek salad and panzanella had a baby. I would err on the side of usingfewer croutons.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: So glad I hunted down the halloumi cheese, my family loves cheese, it was such a wonderful savory and chewy addition to the crisp bright salad. This was part of a 3 part meal ambitious meal of corn chowder and shrimp bathed in olive oil. My olive oil was good, but with dishes like this, really need a super fruity oil. My men who don’t crave veggies, loved this (it was the cheese!).

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Don't eat bread so added warm faro - which absorbed all the delicious liquid from the tomatoes-  and the arugula. Will make again- everyone loved.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Did not have halloumi cheese and crumbled smoked feta cheese into salad instead.   Used basil and some parsley as herbs along with the crushed garlic from the infused oil.   Delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Consider grilling halloumi instead. It using feta or ricotta salata?

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Added radish and did not make croutons. The croutons were not missed. Delicious.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: I followed the advice of other commenters and used the salted tomato juice instead of vinegar in the dressing. It was good enough to drink. I also didn’t ice my cucumbers. I had some leftover rye bread from the farmers market and they made great croutons. Loved the seared halloumi and could imagine using feta for a cheaper option (maybe adjusting salt elsewhere). Great, easy dinner as late summer straggles on in New York.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: I make a version of this that I learned from Chef Neil O'Malley. I roast the cherry tomatoes whole and add them with the juices to the salad, tear in a variety of fresh herbs--dill, mint, cilantro, and include some arugula.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: I love this salad!!!! I've made it a million times and it's delish. Sometimes I add radish, sometimes I use whatever random vinegar I have in my pantry. But let me tell you, I ain't never chilled those cucumbers. And it's still AMAZING!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Way overly-complicated for what it produces. The flavors are meh and the texture was nice. Cut the croutons smaller than directed if you plan to add them at the end. Or don’t continually drain the juices (why on earth would you do that?) if you leave the croutons large.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Added avo and peach chunks!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Delicious as is (although yes, icing the cucumbers is probably unnecessary), but try adding in a chopped avocado! We make it this way all the time at home and just eat the salad as our entire dinner.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Good ingredients. Good dish. Bad recipe. Excessive steps, confusing order of steps, and doesn't take varieties of produce into account. Doesn't seem tested or well thought out. Recommend as a guideline more than a recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: I had never used halloumi before I made this. We were blown away by the great flavor of this salad, and especially by the rich, meaty taste and texture of the halloumi--totally worth the $9 or so that it cost. I changed nothing except for quantities (we only needed 4 hearty servings) and used sherry vinegar instead of red wine vinegar, as it was what I had to hand. Will definitely be making this again.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious. We had buttermilk biscuits with jam as a side. My only issue is with halloumi. I love it, but it is just way too salty!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Really delicious salad. I misread Step 5 and "combined" the halloumi strips with the garlic and oil. Not a problem as it turned out. Just didn't add oil to the fry pan.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe. Did not chill cucumbers.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody:
Loved this! Salt is included in many steps so keep an eye on the total amount of salt you're using. 

I wish haloumi was more readily available in the US. Trader Joe's carries it seasonally while most other groceries don't have it at all. Bodegas to the rescue!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Every Whole Foods I've ever shopped at in CA carry the Kradjian brand and now my market in Washington state carries the same one. It's in a  little plastic tub for storing in the fridge.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: all kind of perfect summer time dinner yes

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Oh so good with heirloom tomatoes and fresh picked cucumbers! I used less bread for a full meal salad and would leave most of it out if served as a side. Like others, I just stuck cucumbers in the freezer for a few minutes and I’m not even sure they needed that. I also used the tomato juices from about five types of tomatoes— it was sweet so still needed a splash of vinegar. The halloumi is perfect with it.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: So so. A bit fussy in preparation. Better is the pita and cucumber salad on this site, just at halloumi.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: I make a salad of chopped cucs with cut up same size tomatoes or cherry tomatoes cut in half plus red onion thin slices and basil.  Salt the tomatoes and cucs and add red wine vinegar (tablespoon or more as desired)and small amount (1tablespoon) of evoo Let stand for everything to meld Delicious

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Very nice. Refreshing and attractive. I used Vidalia onion, regular cucumber (seeds mostly cut out) and a combo of cherry tomatoes and regular. Croutons were terrific, adding a fantastic crunch. Much easier to pull this salad together than it looks.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: I’ve made this salad so many times because it is just so good when cherry tomatoes are good. I’ve never salted the cucumbers; I tear the bread into smallish crumbs rather than croutons; and I’ve used bread cheese when I can’t find halloumi which I just cube and bake. Super easy and super yummy!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Just saved this recipe.  I love bread salads and all the suggestions from your readers.  Thank you.

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Use first note to skip some steps

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Delicious

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: This was delicious! I simplified like others suggested and used a huge beautiful heirloom tomato instead of cherry.  Yummy!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Good!! Recipe is written just okay.. this isn’t much different than the Taverna Salad recipe on NYT that i love very much!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Enjoyed this with good dollops of burrata on top of the primary ingredients, subbed for halloumi.  An easy relatively no cook meal (if you time the baking of your croutons before a hot weather day!), very tasty and easy summer dish!

author: Julia Moskin
recipeTitle: Cucumber-Tomato Salad With Seared Halloumi and Olive Oil Croutons Recipe
commentBody: Sprinkle some zaa'tar on this and we're in business.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Like Gyros or Doner Kebab, Al Pastor refers to meat stacked onto a vertical spit, which is then sliced off the outside as it cooks. Put the pork loin in the freezer for 20 minutes or so to firm it up. You'll have an easier time slicing it thin, then put it in the marinade. A dash of liquid smoke in the marinade is a nice cheat, if you like smoky meat.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I am also wondering if the meat is cooked first before marinating, and how this should happen, if later does it absorb all the marinade? .

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Marinate before and sauce after. Marinate >> Cook >> Sauce. Marinate the meat reserving a small portion (this recipe says 1/2c) to help additionally flavor the meat during cooking. Using the marinade after cooking would do very little in getting flavor penetration and also ruining the crispiness that you're trying to achieve on the meat.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The meat is raw while marinating. The cooking happens in the hot skillet in Step 6.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: You don't cook the meat first. Just sprinkle with salt and add the marinade from Step 1, reserving half a cup. Store meat and remaining marinade in the fridge overnight.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I make this similarly but pound the pork after slicing the disks. Then cut two 1-inch slices of pineapple for the top and bottom supports. I set 3-4 skewers through a pineapple and then layer the pork, finishing off with the final slice of pineapple to hold the skewers together. I cook at 275F for two to three hours. It doesn't have to be cooked all the way. Let it rest until manageably cool. Slice thinly and mix with pineapple then saute on high in cast iron until crisp/cooked.
Bon appetit!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Excellent recipe. Here’s the lazy version with what I had at home. Instead of the dried peppers, I used gochugang powder, Aleppo pepper flakes, plenty of smoked pimentón de la vera and a splash of La Morena chipotle sauce. I am a purist from Mexico City and the pastor flavor came through. You gotta use corn tortillas and all the trimmings for real al pastor delight. Also excellent for fish al pastor tacos, like Chef Camara serves at Contramar, her fabulous restaurant in Mexico City.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Thanks, these are great tips! I live in Germany and may not get all the different chiles. It's still great to know that I can produce a close result here. Puchasing the spices/condiments you mentioned are not a problem here.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Awesome!!! Would have also appreciated a home-made tortilla recipe & simple green sauce recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Go to the top of the Cooking page and use the search tool. Or you could always use Google.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious and pleasing. Marinade is similar to a homemade adobo sauce; used 2 pasilla chilis instead of cascabel, 1 tsp ancho chili powder instead of dried ancho chili (this all is what was available locally). A little extra fresh orange juice for the sweetness to cut down spiciness of the red chilis de arbol. Pineapple takes longer to caramelize (20 minutes). Used red onion instead of white. Didn't add marinade when sauteeing the meat, but people used the reserved marinade as sauce on tacos.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Does this recipe assume you will be buying corn tortillas?  I make my own - honestly they're not that hard to make. But would be interested on recommendations for store bought CORN, not horrors of horrors flour, tortillas.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Messed up by marinating with all the sauce, I didn’t pat the meat dry, I didn’t have all the right spices. Still turned out great. Saved it by draining off the extra liquid and fat then continued to fry till charred. I used two smaller fry pans—one cast iron and one nonstick. The cast iron produced a better char, and a second batch in the cast iron was even better because it loosened the tasty char bits in the first batch. Best tacos I ever.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Great recipe! To get more of a traditional cook on the pork, I placed the tenderloin in the freezer for 25 minutes then rinsed and dried it before slicing into 1/2” medallions that I then placed in the marinade overnight. An hour before cooking, I removed the tenderloin and folded them into stacks on skewers (to emulate a tromp) and gas grilled on high heat 500 degrees to sear both sides then lowered to medium temp until pork registered 145 degrees internal temp.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I would like more detail on the preparation of the meat If using chicken say boneless skinless thighs I would assume best to cut up or shred after cooking Likewise I assume the instruction to shred the pork  would apply after cooking

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I think it was just a way of describing the not so neat pieces of extra meat.  When I've bought raw al pastor from Mexican butchers, it's always already cut up into little pieces and mixed in the marinade.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I think the opposite is correct. First I think "shred" is the wrong term for al pastor. When I hear "shred" I think pulled pork or ropa vieja. Al pastor is all about the crispy bits from the trompo or in this case the griddle/skillet.

But in any case, I think you cut the meat before cooking so you can get a more complete "crustification" on the meat. If you slice afterwards, you'll miss a side.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: i have bought chicken pastor from the local Latino market, and it is always chopped up raw and sitting in the marinade. I would say that a smaller rough chop is what you want, to get the crisp ends. Or keep whole, marinate for a long time, and BBQ/grill until crisped and cooked, then chop.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The flavor and texture imparted the "spintop" broiler (like a shawarma grill) in taquerías is impossible to replicate in the kitchen. This special grill gives a crispy crust while the interior is kept relatively rare. As the taquero slices the exterior of the meat spintop he catches it in the tortilla and also includes onion/pineapple that are being roasted simultaneously. I've found just a few of places in the US where you can find the real thing. Most places offer this kind. Ain't the same!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Shelley, the meat should be marinated raw. The longer you marinate, the deeper the flavor will penetrate.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: What kind of tortilla basket can be put in a 225º oven? Does it have to be clay?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Unbelievably good marinade even with substitutions (I didn't have any of these specialty chilies so just used jalapeno); great meal.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Stacking large thin slices of marinated meat and pineapple slices in the air fryer and keeping the temperature at 140c to 150c created a similar effect as a giros stand oven. The top charred a lot more than the sides and it kept the insides quite moist. Sliced them thin after letting the pile of meat rest.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: And the middle was also cooked? Or did you have multiple air-frying rounds?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I've made this in two different ways. Once was the easiest, basically "seared" on the weber kettle. Next was a little more complex, again using the kettle, but I used my rotisserie. As it's a horizontal one (as apposed to vertical), I had to use extra prongs to hold the place. I also left the marinade on the meat, and let it run offset for quite a while. Both were a success, with the 2nd being the slight winner.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The al pastor marinade is also splendid for grilling. Just keep your tenderloins whole, cook on your gas or charcoal grill, and then slice and serve with accoutrements.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Why blot off the marinade between towels only to add the same reserved marinade before cooking?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Turned out lovely. When we served it at our restaurant, we used too many cloves. This recipe is better balanced and the tomato provides that lovely umami that was missing from our recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Marinade is very good. My pineapple (canned) didn’t brown in the slightest at medium heat. On high heat, it caramelized in 10-15 minutes. Perhaps fresh pineapple caramelizes more readily.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
The recipe touts “the combination of sweet pineapple, spicy meat and fragrant corn.”  
But there is no corn in the recipe?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Corn in the corn tortillas.  :-)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: probably the corn tortillas

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: My husband and our guest loved them! I would make it again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Made with chicken thighs and had to substitute some of the chile's because they aren't available in New England...will definitely make again...

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: When is the pineapple sugar mix added?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: It's one of the toppings.  It is served at the table with the onion and cilantro.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Really good-but difficult to get the pastor patted dry enough to avoid steaming instead of growing when goes into hot pan, particularly when it calls for adding sauce while cooking.  We let the sauce cook off then browned it, and it got excellent reviews from adults and kids alike.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I was too lazy to pat mine dry but cooking it in a 12" cast iron pan created the char I was looking for that reminded me of when I had Al Pastor in Puerto Vallarta. As long as you have a searing hot pan and don't overcrowd it, it should do the trick. :)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made the pork/marinade sous vide at 140 degrees for 14 hours or so (overnight and beyond).  I finished the pork and marinade in my cast iron Lodge skillet.  It was beyond the beyond.  The pork melted in my mouth.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: A bit of expresso in the marinade can hint the taste of smoke without additives. Looks like weekend tacos! ;)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This would be very good for informal entertaining. I used chicken breasts because that’s what I had on hand.
If you don’t have pineapple, I think nectarines or mangoes would work just as well.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Not a huge fan of this recipe. It lacked flavor, even after 24 hours of marinating, and saucing. Adding hot sauce to tacos was a must.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this tonight and we really enjoyed it. My only issue was getting the pineapple to caramelize. I cooked it as directed, and waited for it to brown a little. And waited and waited and waited. Finally, I gave up and stopped. It tasted good, but looked nothing like the picture. It was still pale. Any idea what I did wrong?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: used 1 tsp of guajillo paste and chiles de arbol flakes

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The very best tacos al pasture I have ever had were from an appropriately ranked, five-star taco truck in Taos, NM (Carittas?); beautiful little delights of crispy pork, perfectly seasoned, sauced and juicy, with just a nice dotting of pineapple bits, on a lightly crisped, yet soft and chewy flour tortilla. I will forever be looking for them in every al pasture I come across.  So I'm ready (and hopeful) for this recipe!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Wow. Absolutely delicious. Marinating overnight is worth it. Butter-tender and crispy.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Delicious but a little dry. Next time I’ll add a little more pepper water to the marinade and set aside 3/4 cup instead of 1/2 cup. To make more like what I’m used to at taquerias, I’d use 3/4 cup pineapple instead of 1/2 cup, cut into smaller chunks than shown in the photo (1/3-1/2 the size), and mix in pineapples and reserved marinade after meat has cooked, rather than pineapple and marinade as topping.
Used canned pineapple and tomato puree, didn’t salt meat before marinade or pat dry after marinade w paper towels. Still came out great. Agree w other commenter that freezing the meat makes it much easier to thinly slice. I froze mine ~45 min.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Authentic even without the actual “char of the grill”.  We have made this numerous times making changes here and there yet the chilis and spices make this el pastor sing!

Brings visceral memories back of el rancho cookouts…

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Seems like it would be better to sear/char the meat when it’s dry then add the bit of marinade…

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Use a tortilla press to make the topping circles.  Does a great job and creates a uniformly thick layer.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The directions mention eggs but the ingredients don’t.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: “2  eggs, at room temperature” is noted in the dough between the salt and flour

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made these this morning and they are fabulous despite some troubles. I used a scale to weigh the ingredients and had to add more flour to the dough to have it come together. The topping did not come together like a stiff cookie dough for me. The topping was very soft and could not be rolled out but I was able to shape it enough and drape the circles over the dough balls. I also weighed the dough balls and ended up with 15 instead of 12 (not complaining about extras). Overall, quite successful!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I had these exact same problems. I don't think my butter was cold enough.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I'm making these right now, and I came to the comments to see if I was crazy. I had to add a ridiculous amount of flour to get the dough to come together. At least 1/2 cup, but probably more. I, too, used a scale to measure. ??? We'll see how they turn out.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: These came out great! For the tops, make during the first rise! Shape at room temp, place on wax/parchment paper, and pop in the fridge/freezer for a minute or two. Lightly brush the tops with vegetable oil. Then place on the buns and score the decorations; the top will soften and drape naturally over the buns during the second rise. The veg oil helps the topping adhere without poking and pressing. 375 is too hot, I recommend 325 or 350.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Glad to see this recipe, which takes me back to the panaderías we used to visit in Tijuana.  Ah, for one of those bolillos!  BUT I do not have a standing mixer, don’t want a standing mixer, and am tired of recipes that assume I have one.  Big, heavy, expensive.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Oops, I meant, yes it's there, between the flour and the salt...

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Whenever I have had pastries in Mexico I've not really been super impressed, they seem a little less flaky, delicate than say a French pastry and more like a heavy piece of bread with sugar on top. That said maybe the trick is to enjoy them with hot milky coffee and described above. (Or maybe I just haven't had the right ones) I'd be willing to give these a try!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Give them a try. They aren't pastries, your description is pretty accurate!, Not difficult, and ye, good with a milky coffee.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I plan to try this using an osmotolerant yeast like SAF Gold.
