Osmotolerant yeast are designed for breads with higher sugar content and will rise much more rapidly than traditional yeast in high sugar dough. Basically sugar loves water and the normal yeast struggle when the sugar sucks up all the water in a high sugar dough.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: So delicious and so easy! I did have to add more flour for the rolls, and I used twice as much butter in the topping as I should have (by mistake) but even so, these were a knockout. Will definitely make again!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Eggs are mentioned in step 2.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The topping can be difficult to work with if the ingredients warm up even just a little. Try making the topping during the first rise and placing it in the refrigerator to cool. This should allow the topping to be easily shaped after the dough is done rising.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious and easy to make. I substituted regular milk with vanilla soy milk and they came out wonderful. I used a tortilla press  and parchment paper to flatten the topping as suggested. Just be sure not to press to hard and be gentle when peeling it off. Heat from your hands can cause the topping to break if you are too rough.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Backed these for the first time yesterday so we could have some ready for a-mad morning. Followed the recipe exactly. Everything came out perfect. The topping is hard work work with warm. Just do your best to pinch the ends to the dough. Will make a gain for sure!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Tastes amazing. Love them. Better than a doughnut. I had to add quite a bit more flour (~1/2 cup? One heaping tablespoon at a time) to get a sticky dough that wasn’t way too sticky. I also struggled with making the topping into discs. Refrigerated little balls of topping to help it shape better, but still was challenging to get thin disc without tearing /butter melting.

author: Julia Moskin
Julia Moskin
commentBody: These were everywhere when we stayed for three months in Northern Mexico this winter. The buns had a cheesy aroma, I did not like them at all. Perhaps this recipe would be different. I do not see cheese in the ingredient list. It is possible that I am thinking of another type of bun. I am so confused.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: They definitely don't include cheese, but the bakeries that cook them also cook several other pastries with cheese. Maybe that's where you got the smell? I have tried several times to replicate the recipe but yeast based dough doesn't agree with me.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Cinnamon, butter and almond flower on the topping? That's new.
Traditionally conchas' topping is vanilla or chocolate and the topping made with flour, confectioner's sugar,  shortening and vanilla or cocoa. 
Also, you normally don't eat them warm. You dunk them on milk, latte or hot cocoa.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
My favorite thing when I'm back home is to wait until 6 pm, when the local panaderia (bakery) near my mom's house  will pull out of the oven a fresh batch of conchas. I would pay and eat it before I even got in my car. Best thing ever. 

And while you're right about the topping, I'm glad this one substitutes it. I have no qualms on eating the original recipe, but can't bring myself to cook with shortening.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: What on earth is a "flattened topping circle"?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Hi, I don't understand the following parts of the instructions:
Part 4
...One by one, use your fingers to press and pinch each ball into a thin circle about 3 inches across. (You could also do this on a work surface, placing the ball between two pieces of parchment paper).
Part 5
Drape a flattened topping circle over the top of each bun, completely covering it. 

Can anyone give me some insight into these instructions?  Thanks!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: The topping circle is the topping, which has the consistency of cookie dough. You top each bun with a flat circle of topping.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: can this dough be made the night before and baked in the AM?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: What is a “topping circle”?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: the thing you make in step 4. :)

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I just made these this morning, and brought them to work.  Rave reviews!  I'd never done a topping like that before, but with a little flour on my hands, it was pretty easy.  And thanks so much for the gram amount for each bun!  It made my little pastry chef heart happy!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Crumb a tad harder and doughy than traditional conchas available in Mexico city bakeries.  Good flavor, will do again.  Thank you for the recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Hey! these came out great! early brunch for family and everyone loved them.   One question (but not critical), is there a way to make the topping adhere better to the bun?  I tried to 'fasten' it around the edges before baking, but some of that deliciousness crumbled off (the top) when cutting.  Will make these again for sure - thanks Julia!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
This recipe needs a video!

Crumb toping was so crumbly, could not be shaped at all.  Fell off the sides of the concha balls.  and forget trying to make an impression with my "concha cutter" mold.  They do taste good, though!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Didn't seem to a sufficient amount of dough for the topping, which is really quite good. Next time I'll increase ingredients by 1/4 - 1/3.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Found it useful to roll the topping into the 12 balls and then to chill while the dough was rising. Made rolling out much easier.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Has anyone tried overnighting the dough in the fridge for the first rise?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Can I skip the almond flour?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I had some leftover conchas (everything goes stale here instantly) so I made the NYT "Simple Bread Pudding" recipe with them. Turned out great!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: As many have noted, the topping is impossible to work with as intended. I even tried refrigerating it first to make it firmer, but still had difficulties. Better off looking for a different recipe.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Has anyone had an issue with the baking temp? Mine came out super dark. I’m wondering if my oven was too hot though... I made a pie at 350 right before, and then turned it up to 375 for these guys.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I spaced on the first proof and left it in for an hour longer - made up for this by proofing it an hour more for the second proof and punching out alll the gas in the dough before rolling out into ball.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Delicious!!!! I was afraid the topping wouldn’t stick but it did! I used a tortilla press to flatten them. I do agree that the topping should be done in advance and refrigerated after every step (making it, balling it, flattening it) and kept in the fridge until ready to use as it’s very fragile.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I really loved these, and the profess was simpler than I anticipated. However, the bottoms of mine burnt slightly. Not sure if I need to turn down the temp, or avoid using the bottom rack... I didn’t even make it the full 20 minutes. Maybe a silpat instead of a buttered pan?

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
I cooked these according to the directions. My first round of yeast didn't proof in the mixer bowl, probably because the bowl itself was cold. I remixed in a Pyrex measuring cup and that worked. 

The topping only came together when I used my hands (a pastry cutter didn't do the trick), but it was very soft and difficult to slice patterns into. I'd let them chill in the fridge before putting them on the buns next time.

20 minutes was a little too long. That said, these taste like I remember.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Had some trouble with the active yeast at the beginning (not a baker). Didn’t have a thermometer, but read if you put your finger into the water and it stings but you can keep it in the water, that’s the temperature you want. The yeast proofed and the dough rose well. Only issue was the topping. I wish we would’ve double the ingredients. It’s just not a concha if there isn’t that sugary topping in every bite. Oh, and our oven slightly burned some of the buns. 20 min was way too long.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: I made this substituting the yeast for instant yeast, and the almond flour for hazelnut flour. 20 minutes was definitely to long so I took it out earlier. Turned out amazing would definitely make again.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: This recipe gave me great results. I tried a different recipe once before but this attempt turned out far better. As people have said, the topping is a little tricky to work with but I think that’s just the nature of the thing, not a flaw in the recipe. I just tried to keep the topping cold as I worked with it. Other than adding 1/4 tsp of cardamom to the topping I stuck to the recipe as written. Loved it.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody:
Made this exactly to spec. 20 minutes was maybe 3 minutes too long. I should have kept a more careful eye but I was too busy sitting on the sofa drinking Prosecco. 

The topping definitely stumped me cuz I couldn’t get it to come together. I ended up using the paddle attachment on the mixer and voilà problem solved. I used my hands to make the topping circles. I didn’t have enough to completely cover the rolls but they still turned out really pretty. 

The consistency of the rolls was perfect.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Can I make the dough tonight and, after shaping, put in the fridge so I can put the tops on an bake in the morning? I want to have fresh conchas with my coffee in the morning? Or do I fridge after the first proof before I shape them? Thanks!

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Great recipe, chilling the topping made it much easier to work with. The flatten, passing back and forth between floured palms (similar to making tortillas) resulted in more reliable discs that didn’t break as often.

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: Didn't use the topping instead brushed with heavy cream and sprinkled with swedish pearl sugar

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: @laurie Then you didn’t make Conchas

author: Julia Moskin
commentBody: yeah i also had to add in more flour. i looked up similar recipes elsewhere, and none of them mention an additional 120mL of water (in addition to the milk), but the flour amount is the same (~500g). i wonder if calling for water was a typo.
